> 6. **Confusing average-case formulas:** Sequential search average = $\frac{n+1}{2}$, **NOT** $\frac{n}{2}$. This is a frequently asked 2-mark question.

---

## 🎯 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ Sequential Search is the **simplest and most universal** search algorithm.
- ✅ It works on **both sorted and unsorted** data.
- ✅ Time complexity: **Best $O(1)$, Average $\frac{n+1}{2}$, Worst $O(n)$**.
- ✅ Space complexity: **$O(1)$** (iterative version).
- ✅ Sentinel Search is an **optimization** that reduces comparisons per loop.
- ✅ Recursive Search uses **$O(n)$ stack space**, which is its main drawback.
- ✅ Returns **index** (not position); use `-1` to signal "not found."
- ✅ Preferred for **small arrays** and **linked lists**; avoid for large sorted data (use Binary Search).
- ✅ Average-case derivation uses **arithmetic series sum**: $\sum_{i=1}^{n} i = \frac{n(n+1)}{2}$.
- ✅ Standard comparison count per iteration = **2**; Sentinel version = **1**.
- ✅ Pre-requisite knowledge: **arrays, loops, conditionals, functions** (Modules 1 & 2 integration).
- ✅ **KTU frequently asks:** time complexity derivation, sentinel variant, and comparison with binary search.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Bubble Sort
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GXEST204 - PROGRAMMING IN C
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Bubble Sort

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Bubble Sort** is a **comparison-based**, **in-place**, **stable** sorting algorithm that operates by repeatedly traversing the list, comparing each pair of adjacent elements, and swapping them if they are in the wrong (i.e., ascending or descending) order. After every complete traversal (called a **pass**), the largest unsorted element "bubbles up" to its final sorted position at the end of the array — hence the name.

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme** (Course: *GXEST204 – Programming in C*, Module 2: *Sorting and Searching*), Bubble Sort is taught as a foundational iterative sorting technique, often used to introduce students to:
- Nested loop constructs
- Array manipulation
- The concept of algorithmic complexity

---

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine a classroom of students standing in a line, arranged in random height order. The teacher asks them to line up in **ascending order of height**, but the only allowed operation is: *two adjacent students can swap places if the one on the left is taller than the one on the right.*

- After **one full walk** from left to right, the **tallest student is guaranteed to be at the far right** — like a bubble rising to the surface of water.
- The teacher then repeats the walk, ignoring the last student (who is already in place).
- This process continues until no swaps are needed in a complete pass — meaning the line is sorted.

This bubbling motion of the **largest** (or smallest) element to its correct position is the essence of the algorithm.

---

### 1.3 Key Performance Metrics (KTU-Highlighted)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following standard metrics are **frequently tested** in KTU university exams under Bloom's Level *Remember/Understand*:
> - **Best Case Time Complexity:** $O(n)$ — when the array is already sorted (with the optimization flag).
> - **Average Case Time Complexity:** $O(n^2)$
> - **Worst Case Time Complexity:** $O(n^2)$ — when the array is in reverse order.
> - **Space Complexity:** $O(1)$ — *in-place* sorting, no auxiliary array.
> - **Stability:** **Stable** (equal elements retain their original relative order).
> - **Adaptive:** Can be made adaptive with a `swapped` flag.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Breakdown — The "Why" and the "How"

Bubble Sort's logic is built on a simple invariant that holds at the end of the $i^{th}$ pass:

> After the completion of pass $i$ (0-indexed), the **last $i+1$ elements** of the array are in their **final sorted positions**.

**Step-by-step logic:**

1. **Outer loop control** — Iterate from `i = 0` to `i = n-2`. This runs $n-1$ passes maximum.
2. **Inner loop control** — In each pass `i`, iterate `j` from `0` to `n-i-2`. The `-2` (or `-1` in zero-indexed array length) ensures we never access out-of-bound indices and that already-sorted tail elements are skipped.
3. **Comparison** — If `arr[j] > arr[j+1]`, the pair is in the wrong order.
4. **Swap** — Exchange `arr[j]` and `arr[j+1]` using a temporary variable.
5. **Early termination (optimization)** — Use a Boolean `swapped` flag. If a full pass completes with **zero swaps**, the array is already sorted; break out of the loop.

**Why the inner loop shrinks:** Because after pass $i$, the $(n-i-1)^{th}$ index holds the correct final element, so we no longer need to compare against it.

---

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> This table is the **go-to reference** for solving any KTU numerical/tracing problem on Bubble Sort.

| **Metric** | **Formula / Expression** | **Worst Case Value** | **Best Case Value** | **Notes** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Number of Passes | $P$ | $n-1$ | $1$ (if optimized) | With swapped flag |
| Total Comparisons | $\sum_{i=0}^{n-2} (n-i-1)$ | $\dfrac{n(n-1)}{2}$ | $n-1$ | Triangular number |
| Total Swaps | $S$ | $\dfrac{n(n-1)}{2}$ | $0$ | Worst case = reverse sorted |
| Time Complexity (Big-O) | $O(\cdot)$ | $O(n^2)$ | $O(n)$ | Quadratic dominant |
| Space Complexity (Auxiliary) | $S_{aux}$ | $O(1)$ | $O(1)$ | In-place |
| Stability Index | — | Stable | Stable | Equal keys preserve order |

**Key derived identity (used in KTU numericals):**

$$
\text{Total Comparisons in Worst Case} = \frac{n(n-1)}{2}
$$

where $n$ is the number of elements. This is a **triangular number** and is the cornerstone of bubble sort's $O(n^2)$ behavior.

---

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

Although Bubble Sort is rarely used in production systems due to its $O(n^2)$ cost, it has **specific engineering applications** that KTU examiners love to test:

- **Educational tool** — Used in introductory programming courses (such as KTU's *GXEST204*) to teach loops, conditionals, and array indexing.
- **Nearly-sorted data** — With the `swapped` optimization, it performs at $O(n)$ on nearly-sorted data, beating more complex algorithms for small inputs.
- **Embedded systems** — When $n$ is very small ($\le 10$) and code simplicity/footprint matters more than speed.
- **Stable sort requirement** — When stable ordering of records is critical (e.g., sorting database entries by one key while preserving another).
- **Detecting near-sortedness** — A single pass reveals whether the data is already sorted (zero swaps).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Complete Trace of Bubble Sort

Let us trace the algorithm on a sample array. This is a **classic KTU 14-mark question pattern**.

**Initial Array:** $A = [64, 34, 25, 12, 22, 11, 90]$, with $n = 7$.

We will track the array state, comparisons, and swaps at each pass.

---

#### **Pass 1 (i = 0):** Inner loop runs $j = 0$ to $5$ ($n-i-2 = 7-0-2 = 5$)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Compare } (64, 34) &: 64 > 34 \Rightarrow \text{Swap} \rightarrow [34, 64, 25, 12, 22, 11, 90] \\
\text{Compare } (64, 25) &: 64 > 25 \Rightarrow \text{Swap} \rightarrow [34, 25, 64, 12, 22, 11, 90] \\
\text{Compare } (64, 12) &: 64 > 12 \Rightarrow \text{Swap} \rightarrow [34, 25, 12, 64, 22, 11, 90] \\
\text{Compare } (64, 22) &: 64 > 22 \Rightarrow \text{Swap} \rightarrow [34, 25, 12, 22, 64, 11, 90] \\
\text{Compare } (64, 11) &: 64 > 11 \Rightarrow \text{Swap} \rightarrow [34, 25, 12, 22, 11, 64, 90] \\
\text{Compare } (64, 90) &: 64 < 90 \Rightarrow \text{No Swap} \rightarrow [34, 25, 12, 22, 11, 64, 90]
\end{aligned}
$$

**End of Pass 1:** $A = [34, 25, 12, 22, 11, 64, 90]$. The element **90** has bubbled to its final position. Comparisons = $6$, Swaps = $5$.

---

#### **Pass 2 (i = 1):** Inner loop runs $j = 0$ to $4$

$$
\begin{aligned}
[34, 25, 12, 22, 11, 64, 90] \\
\text{After pass 2: } A = [25, 12, 22, 11, 34, 64, 90]
\end{aligned}
$$

Element **64** is now in its final position. Comparisons = $5$, Swaps = $4$.

---

#### **Pass 3 (i = 2):** Inner loop runs $j = 0$ to $3$

$$
\text{After pass 3: } A = [12, 22, 11, 25, 34, 64, 90]
$$

Element **34** is now in its final position. Comparisons = $4$, Swaps = $3$.

---

#### **Pass 4 (i = 3):** Inner loop runs $j = 0$ to $2$

$$
\text{After pass 4: } A = [12, 11, 22, 25, 34, 64, 90]
$$

Element **25** is now in its final position. Comparisons = $3$, Swaps = $2$.

---

#### **Pass 5 (i = 4):** Inner loop runs $j = 0$ to $1$

$$
\text{After pass 5: } A = [11, 12, 22, 25, 34, 64, 90]
$$

Element **22** is now in its final position. Comparisons = $2$, Swaps = $1$.

---

#### **Pass 6 (i = 5):** Inner loop runs $j = 0$ to $0$

$$
\text{After pass 6: } A = [11, 12, 22, 25, 34, 64, 90]
$$

Element **12** is now in its final position. Comparisons = $1$, Swaps = $0$.

> **Final Sorted Array:** $A = [11, 12, 22, 25, 34, 64, 90]$ ✓

---

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Consolidated Pass Summary

| **Pass $i$** | **Array State After Pass** | **Element Bubbled to End** | **Comparisons** | **Swaps** |
|:---:|:---|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 1 | $[34, 25, 12, 22, 11, 64, 90]$ | $90$ | $6$ | $5$ |
| 2 | $[25, 12, 22, 11, 34, 64, 90]$ | $64$ | $5$ | $4$ |
| 3 | $[12, 22, 11, 25, 34, 64, 90]$ | $34$ | $4$ | $3$ |
| 4 | $[12, 11, 22, 25, 34, 64, 90]$ | $25$ | $3$ | $2$ |
| 5 | $[11, 12, 22, 25, 34, 64, 90]$ | $22$ | $2$ | $1$ |
| 6 | $[11, 12, 22, 25, 34, 64, 90]$ | $12$ | $1$ | $0$ |
| **Total** | — | — | **$21$** | **$15$** |

---

### 3.3 Verification of Total Comparisons (Mathematical Proof)

The total number of comparisons in the worst case is the sum of an arithmetic series:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total Comparisons} &= \sum_{i=0}^{n-2} (n-i-1) \\
&= (n-1) + (n-2) + (n-3) + \cdots + 1 \\
&= \frac{(n-1) \cdot n}{2} \\
&= \frac{n(n-1)}{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Verification for $n = 7$:**

$$
\frac{7 \cdot 6}{2} = 21 \text{ comparisons} \quad \checkmark
$$

This matches our trace above, confirming the formula. The sum is a **triangular number**, which is the foundation of the $O(n^2)$ complexity derivation.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Bubble Sort Algorithm Flowchart (Mermaid)

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the **complete control flow** of an optimized Bubble Sort. Note that all node IDs are alphanumeric and avoid reserved keywords, with clean uppercase labels (no markdown formatting inside the quoted labels).

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0([START]) --> A1[Initialize i = 0]
    A1 --> A2{i less than n minus 1?}
    A2 -- No --> A3([END - Array Sorted])
    A2 -- Yes --> A4[Set swapped = 0]
    A4 --> A5[Initialize j = 0]
    A5 --> A6{j less than n minus i minus 1?}
    A6 -- No --> A7{swapped equals 0?}
    A7 -- Yes --> A3
    A7 -- No --> A8[Increment i by 1]
    A8 --> A2
    A6 -- Yes --> A9[Compare arr of j and arr of j plus 1]
    A9 --> A10{arr j greater than arr j plus 1?}
    A10 -- No --> A11[Increment j by 1]
    A11 --> A6
    A10 -- Yes --> A12[Swap arr of j and arr of j plus 1]
    A12 --> A13[Set swapped = 1]
    A13 --> A11
```

---

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow

This second diagram provides a **system-level view** of how Bubble Sort interacts with the array, the comparator, and the swapper — useful for KTU questions on "explain the working of bubble sort with a block diagram."

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    INPUT[/Input Array A of size n/] --> BUBBLE[Bubble Sort Engine]
    BUBBLE --> COMPARE[Adjacent Comparator]
    COMPARE --> DECIDE{Is arr j greater than arr j plus 1?}
    DECIDE -- Yes --> SWAP[Swap Module]
    DECIDE -- No --> KEEP[Keep Order]
    SWAP --> BUBBLE
    KEEP --> BUBBLE
    BUBBLE --> FLAG{Swapped Flag equals 0?}
    FLAG -- Yes --> OUTPUT[/Sorted Array Output/]
    FLAG -- No --> BUBBLE
```

**Interpretation of the Architecture:**

- The **Bubble Sort Engine** orchestrates the passes.
- The **Adjacent Comparator** performs the relational check `arr[j] > arr[j+1]`.
- The **Swap Module** executes the three-step swap using a temporary variable.
- The **Swapped Flag** is the optimization sentinel — if no swap occurs in a pass, the algorithm terminates early.
- The final output is the **sorted array**.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 📘 PART A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Q1. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`**
**Define Bubble Sort. State its best case, average case, and worst case time complexities.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Bubble Sort is a comparison-based, in-place, stable sorting algorithm that repeatedly compares adjacent elements and swaps them if they are in the wrong order, with the largest element "bubbling up" to its correct position at the end of each pass. **(1 mark)**

The time complexities are:
- **Best case:** $O(n)$ — occurs when the array is already sorted and an optimization flag is used. **(1 mark)**
- **Average case:** $O(n^2)$ **(0.5 mark)**
- **Worst case:** $O(n^2)$ — occurs when the array is in reverse sorted order. **(0.5 mark)**

---

#### **Q2. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`**
**Explain the concept of a "pass" in Bubble Sort. How many passes are required to sort an array of $n$ elements in the worst case?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A **pass** in Bubble Sort refers to one complete traversal of the array from the first element to the last, during which all adjacent pairs are compared and swapped if necessary. **(1.5 marks)**

After pass $i$, the element at position $n-i-1$ is in its final sorted position. The total number of passes required in the worst case to fully sort an array of $n$ elements is: **(1 mark)**

$$
P_{\max} = n - 1
$$

**(0.5 mark)** for the formula and justification.

---

### 📗 PART B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

---

#### **Question A `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`** (14 Marks)

**(a) [7 Marks]** Write a complete C program to implement Bubble Sort. Explain the algorithm with the help of a suitable example.

**Model Solution:**

**C Program:**

```c
#include <stdio.h>

void bubbleSort(int arr[], int n) {
    int i, j, temp, swapped;
    
    // Outer loop for passes
    for (i = 0; i < n - 1; i++) {
        swapped = 0;  // Optimization flag
        
        // Inner loop for adjacent comparisons
        // The -1 ensures j+1 is valid; the -i skips sorted tail
        for (j = 0; j < n - i - 1; j++) {
            if (arr[j] > arr[j + 1]) {
                // Swap adjacent elements
                temp = arr[j];
                arr[j] = arr[j + 1];
                arr[j + 1] = temp;
                swapped = 1;
            }
        }
        
        // If no swaps occurred, array is sorted
        if (swapped == 0) {
            break;
        }
    }
}

int main() {
    int arr[] = {64, 34, 25, 12, 22, 11, 90};
    int n = sizeof(arr) / sizeof(arr[0]);
    int i;
    
    printf("Original array: ");
    for (i = 0; i < n; i++) {
        printf("%d ", arr[i]);
    }
    
    bubbleSort(arr, n);
    
    printf("\nSorted array:   ");
    for (i = 0; i < n; i++) {
        printf("%d ", arr[i]);
    }
    printf("\n");
    
    return 0;
}
```

**Explanation (4 marks for explanation):**
The algorithm uses two nested loops. The outer loop `i` controls the number of passes, running from $0$ to $n-2$. The inner loop `j` performs the adjacent comparison `arr[j] > arr[j+1]`. The `-i` in the inner loop bound ensures that we do not re-examine elements that have already "bubbled" to their final positions. The `swapped` flag provides the **early termination optimization** — if a pass completes with zero swaps, the array is guaranteed to be sorted, and the algorithm exits. This reduces the best case complexity to $O(n)$. **(3 marks for explanation + diagram/explanation)**

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** Trace the Bubble Sort algorithm for the array $[64, 34, 25, 12, 22, 11, 90]$. Show all passes and determine the total number of comparisons and swaps in the worst case.

**Model Solution:**

| **Pass $i$** | **Array State After Pass** | **Comparisons** | **Swaps** |
|:---:|:---|:---:|:---:|
| 1 | $[34, 25, 12, 22, 11, 64, 90]$ | $6$ | $5$ |
| 2 | $[25, 12, 22, 11, 34, 64, 90]$ | $5$ | $4$ |
| 3 | $[12, 22, 11, 25, 34, 64, 90]$ | $4$ | $3$ |
| 4 | $[12, 11, 22, 25, 34, 64, 90]$ | $3$ | $2$ |
| 5 | $[11, 12, 22, 25, 34, 64, 90]$ | $2$ | $1$ |
| 6 | $[11, 12, 22, 25, 34, 64, 90]$ | $1$ | $0$ |
| **Total** | — | **$21$** | **$15$** |

**Total Comparisons (verification using formula):** **(2 marks)**

$$
\text{Comparisons} = \frac{n(n-1)}{2} = \frac{7 \times 6}{2} = 21 \quad \checkmark
$$

**Total Swaps (worst case estimate):** **(1 mark)** $15$ swaps in this particular run; the worst-case bound is $\frac{n(n-1)}{2} = 21$ swaps (achieved on a reverse-sorted array).

The largest element $90$ bubbles to the end in Pass 1. After Pass 6, the array is fully sorted as $[11, 12, 22, 25, 34, 64, 90]$.

---

#### **Question B `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`** (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*

**(a) [7 Marks]** Write a C program to implement an **optimized** Bubble Sort that terminates early if the array is already sorted. Explain the role of the `swapped` flag.

**Model Solution:**

```c
#include <stdio.h>

void optimizedBubbleSort(int arr[], int n) {
    int i, j, temp;
    int swapped;
    
    for (i = 0; i < n - 1; i++) {
        swapped = 0;  // Reset flag for this pass
        
        for (j = 0; j < n - i - 1; j++) {
            if (arr[j] > arr[j + 1]) {
                temp = arr[j];
                arr[j] = arr[j + 1];
                arr[j + 1] = temp;
                swapped = 1;
            }
        }
        
        // Early termination: if no two elements were swapped
        // in the inner loop, the array is sorted
        if (swapped == 0) {
            printf("Array sorted early at pass %d\n", i + 1);
            break;
        }
    }
}

int main() {
    int arr[] = {1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7};  // Already sorted
    int n = 7, i;
    
    optimizedBubbleSort(arr, n);
    
    printf("Final array: ");
    for (i = 0; i < n; i++) {
        printf("%d ", arr[i]);
    }
    printf("\n");
    return 0;
}
```

**Role of the `swapped` flag (4 marks):**
The `swapped` flag is a Boolean sentinel that is set to `1` whenever a swap occurs during the inner loop. At the end of each pass, the flag is checked:
- **If `swapped == 0`:** No swaps occurred during the entire pass, which means the array is already in sorted order (because had any inversion existed, at least one swap would have happened). The algorithm breaks out of the outer loop, avoiding unnecessary passes.
- **If `swapped == 1`:** At least one swap occurred, so the array is not yet sorted, and the algorithm proceeds to the next pass.

This optimization reduces the **best case time complexity from $O(n^2)$ to $O(n)$**, making Bubble Sort adaptive. **(3 marks for explanation)**

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** Compare Bubble Sort with **Selection Sort** in terms of time complexity, space complexity, stability, and number of swaps. When would you prefer one over the other?

**Model Solution (Comparison Table — 4 marks):**

| **Criterion** | **Bubble Sort** | **Selection Sort** |
|:---|:---:|:---:|
| Best Case Time | $O(n)$ (with flag) | $O(n^2)$ |
| Average Case Time | $O(n^2)$ | $O(n^2)$ |
| Worst Case Time | $O(n^2)$ | $O(n^2)$ |
| Space Complexity | $O(1)$ | $O(1)$ |
| Stability | **Stable** | **Not Stable** |
| Number of Swaps (worst case) | $\frac{n(n-1)}{2}$ | $n-1$ |
| Adaptive | Yes (with flag) | No |

**When to prefer (3 marks):**
- **Prefer Bubble Sort** when: data is **nearly sorted** (best case $O(n)$ with the optimization flag), or when **stability** is critical (e.g., multi-key sorting where the original order of equal elements must be preserved).
- **Prefer Selection Sort** when: **minimizing the number of writes/swaps** is critical (e.g., writing to flash memory or EEPROM where each write is expensive), since Selection Sort does at most $n-1$ swaps regardless of input.
- **Prefer neither** in production: Both are $O(n^2)$ and are outperformed by **Merge Sort**, **Quick Sort**, or **Heap Sort** for large $n$.

---

### ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps in KTU Bubble Sort Questions:**
> 1. **Wrong inner loop bound** — Students often write `j < n` instead of `j < n - i - 1`, causing an **out-of-bounds access** at `arr[j+1]`. This loses **2-3 marks** outright.
> 2. **Forgetting to reset the `swapped` flag** — The flag must be set to `0` at the **start of every pass**, not just once before the outer loop. A common silent bug.
> 3. **Confusing the number of swaps with the number of comparisons** — In a 14-mark trace question, listing swaps $= 21$ (which is the comparison count) instead of the actual swap count is a **fatal 2-mark error**.
> 4. **Stating "Bubble Sort is not stable"** — It **IS stable** by design (only swaps when `>` is used, not `>=`). This conceptual error is heavily penalized.
> 5. **Omitting the worst-case formula** — The derivation $\frac{n(n-1)}{2}$ is **expected** in any 7+ mark tracing question. Skip it at your own peril.

---

### 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Module 2 — Bubble Sort:**
> - **Definition:** Comparison-based, in-place, stable, iterative sort using adjacent swaps. **(1 mark question favorite)**
> - **Key invariant:** After pass $i$, the last $i+1$ elements are in their final sorted positions.
> - **Number of passes (worst case):** $n - 1$
> - **Number of comparisons (worst case):** $\frac{n(n-1)}{2}$ — a triangular number, foundation of $O(n^2)$.
> - **Number of swaps (worst case):** $\frac{n(n-1)}{2}$ — equal to comparison count only for reverse-sorted input.
> - **Best case with optimization:** $O(n)$ when no swaps occur in the first pass.
> - **Space complexity:** $O(1)$ — strictly in-place.
> - **Stability:** **Yes** (uses strict `>` comparison).
> - **C implementation must include:** outer loop `i < n-1`, inner loop `j < n-i-1`, three-variable swap, and the `swapped` flag for full marks.
> - **Trace question format:** Always tabulate each pass showing array state, comparisons, and swaps; verify totals with the triangular formula.
> - **Comparison with Selection Sort:** Bubble Sort = stable, $O(n^2)$ swaps; Selection Sort = unstable, $n-1$ swaps.
> - **Real-world use:** Educational tool; nearly-sorted data with adaptive variant; embedded systems with tiny $n$.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Strings - Declaring a String Variable, Reading and Displaying Strings, String Related Library Functions, Programs for String Matching
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GXEST204 - PROGRAMMING IN C
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Strings - Declaring a String Variable, Reading and Displaying Strings, String Related Library Functions, Programs for String Matching

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the C programming language, a **string** is defined as a **one-dimensional array of characters terminated by a null character** `'\0'`. The null character is a sentinel byte (ASCII value `0`) that explicitly marks the logical end of the character sequence, allowing functions to determine the length of the string without storing a separate size field.

According to the KTU 2024 syllabus for *Programming in C (GXEST204)*, strings occupy Module 2 under the broader umbrella of derived data types and array handling. The syllabus explicitly mandates that students must be proficient in:

1. Declaring and initializing string variables (character arrays).
2. Reading strings from standard input (`stdin`) using `scanf()`, `gets()` (legacy), and `fgets()` (ANSI C recommended).
3. Displaying strings to standard output (`stdout`) using `printf()`, `puts()`, and character-by-character traversal.
4. Applying standard string library functions declared in `<string.h>`.
5. Implementing string matching algorithms (both naive and efficient variants) for pattern searching.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition (Verbatim from Syllabus):** *A string in C is a sequence of characters stored in contiguous memory locations, represented as an array of type `char`, and terminated by the null character `'\0'`. The null terminator is NOT a part of the printable string but is a mandatory end-of-string marker required by all `<string.h>` functions.*

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **train** in a railway station:

- Each **compartment** of the train represents a single `char` (one character).
- The compartments are **connected end-to-end** in a fixed sequence, just like contiguous memory addresses.
- A **red flag** is mounted on the very last compartment. That red flag is the null character `'\0'`. Even if more compartments physically exist beyond it, the train conductor (the C compiler) is told: *"Stop reading as soon as you see the red flag."*

This is exactly how a C string works. The array may have been allocated with more space than needed (e.g., 100 compartments), but the *logical* string length ends at `'\0'`. Any standard C function (`strlen`, `strcpy`, etc.) will walk compartment-by-compartment until it sees the red flag.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is `'\0' necessary?** Unlike higher-level languages (Java, Python) where strings are objects with built-in length fields, C is a low-level language with no metadata. The null terminator is a *zero-cost* convention: a single byte (`00` in hex) that doubles as both a sentinel value and a valid falsy value in Boolean checks.

## 1.3 String Variable Declaration — Syntax Variants

In C, a string is declared as a `char` array. There are **four canonical forms** recognized by the KTU board:

| Declaration Form | Syntax | Storage | Mutable? | Common Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Mutable character array** | `char name[20];` | Stack | ✅ Yes | Reading input, modifying characters |
| **Initialized mutable array** | `char name[] = "Hello";` | Stack (read-only data section for literal) | ✅ Yes | Working buffers |
| **Pointer to string literal** | `char *p = "Hello";` | `.rodata` (read-only) | ❌ No (UB on write) | Read-only constants |
| **`const` qualified pointer** | `const char *p = "Hello";` | `.rodata` | ❌ No | Safe literal access |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Pitfall:** Students frequently write `char *p = "Hello";` and then attempt `p[0] = 'J';`. This invokes **undefined behavior** because string literals in C are placed in a read-only memory segment. The correct mutable form is `char p[] = "Hello";` (note the array brackets, not pointer asterisk).

## 1.4 Memory Layout of a C String

The following figure illustrates how `"Hello"` is stored in memory (each cell = 1 byte):

| Address | `1000` | `1001` | `1002` | `1003` | `1004` | `1005` | `1006` | ... | `1019` |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Value** | `'H'` | `'e'` | `'l'` | `'l'` | `'o'` | `'\0'` | `?` | ... | `?` |
| **Index** | `[0]` | `[1]` | `[2]` | `[3]` | `[4]` | `[5]` | — | ... | — |

The logical length is `5` (5 printable characters), but the **storage size** is `6` bytes (including `'\0'`). Indices 6 through 19 are **garbage/uninitialized** until written.

## 1.5 Reading Strings — Three Standard Approaches

| Function | Header | Reads Whitespace? | Buffer Overflow Safe? | KTU Recommendation |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| `scanf("%s", str)` | `<stdio.h>` | ❌ Stops at first space | ❌ No (classic vulnerability) | Avoid in 2024 Scheme |
| `gets(str)` | `<stdio.h>` (removed in **C11**) | ✅ Yes | ❌ No — REMOVED | **Never use** |
| `fgets(str, n, stdin)` | `<stdio.h>` | ✅ Yes (until `\n` or `n-1`) | ✅ Yes (bounded) | **KTU Preferred** |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Update:** The syllabus now emphasizes `fgets()` over the deprecated `gets()`. Students should know that `fgets()` retains the trailing `'\n'` in the buffer (which must be stripped manually using `strcspn()` or `strchr()`).

## 1.6 Displaying Strings — Three Standard Approaches

- **`printf("%s", str)`** — Formatted, no automatic newline. Used in `printf("Name: %s\n", name);`.
- **`puts(str)`** — Automatically appends a newline. Defined in `<stdio.h>`.
- **Character-by-character loop** — Educational/diagnostic; for `int i = 0; str[i] != '\0'; i++) putchar(str[i]);`.

---

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Anatomy of a Null-Terminated String

A C string is a **contract** between the programmer and the C standard library:

- The data structure is a `char` array.
- The last meaningful byte is `'\0'` (numeric value `0`).
- All `<string.h>` functions rely on this contract.

This is why a string literal `"Hi"` in source code occupies **3 bytes** in the binary (2 characters + 1 terminator), not 2.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Concept — The "0 vs '0' Ambiguity":**
> - `'0'` (with quotes) is the **character zero**, ASCII value `48`. It is data.
> - `'\0'` (with backslash) is the **null character**, ASCII value `0`. It is a sentinel.
> - The integer `0` is also valid as a null terminator because `'\0' == 0` in C.

## 2.2 String Initialization — Deep Rules

The C language provides subtle but important differences:

```c
char a[] = "ABC";      // Array of 4 bytes: 'A','B','C','\0'
char b[] = {'A','B','C'}; // Array of 3 bytes: NO null terminator! Not a valid C string.
char c[5] = "ABC";     // Same as a[]: explicitly sized, '\0' at index 3.
char d[10] = "ABC";    // 'A','B','C','\0', then 6 zero-initialized bytes (partial init rule).
```

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Board Hotspot:** `char b[] = {'A','B','C'};` is **NOT** a valid C string. Calling `strlen(b)` on it will walk past the array into adjacent memory — a classic source of segmentation faults. This is a frequently asked 3-mark question.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — String Library Functions (`<string.h>`)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All functions below are **mandatory** for KTU exams. Memorize signatures, return values, and pitfalls.

| Function | Signature | Returns | Mutates Source? | Typical Exam Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| `strlen` | `size_t strlen(const char *s);` | Length **excluding** `'\0'` | ❌ No | Count characters |
| `strcpy` | `char *strcpy(char *dest, const char *src);` | Pointer to `dest` | ✅ Yes (writes to dest) | Copy a string |
| `strncpy` | `char *strncpy(char *dest, const char *src, size_t n);` | Pointer to `dest` | ✅ Yes (bounded) | Safe copy |
| `strcat` | `char *strcat(char *dest, const char *src);` | Pointer to `dest` | ✅ Yes (appends) | Concatenate |
| `strncat` | `char *strncat(char *dest, const char *src, size_t n);` | Pointer to `dest` | ✅ Yes (bounded) | Safe concat |
| `strcmp` | `int strcmp(const char *s1, const char *s2);` | `0` if equal, `<0` or `>0` otherwise | ❌ No | Lexicographic compare |
| `strncmp` | `int strncmp(const char *s1, const char *s2, size_t n);` | Same as `strcmp` | ❌ No | Bounded compare |
| `strchr` | `char *strchr(const char *s, int c);` | Pointer to first occurrence or `NULL` | ❌ No | Find character |
| `strrchr` | `char *strrchr(const char *s, int c);` | Pointer to **last** occurrence or `NULL` | ❌ No | Reverse find |
| `strstr` | `char *strstr(const char *haystack, const char *needle);` | Pointer to first match of `needle` in `haystack` or `NULL` | ❌ No | **Substring / pattern match** |
| `strtok` | `char *strtok(char *str, const char *delim);` | Next token or `NULL` | ✅ Yes (uses static state) | Split string |
| `strdup` | `char *strdup(const char *s);` | Pointer to heap-allocated copy | Allocates | Duplicate |
| `memset` | `void *memset(void *s, int c, size_t n);` | `s` | ✅ Yes | Zero/initialize buffer |
| `memcpy` | `void *memcpy(void *dest, const void *src, size_t n);` | `dest` | ✅ Yes | Raw byte copy |

> [!NOTE]
> **`size_t`** is an unsigned integer type defined in `<stddef.h>`, used to represent sizes of objects in memory. It is the de-facto return type of `strlen` and the parameter type for all bounded string functions.

## 2.4 The `strcmp` Return Value Contract

The function `strcmp(s1, s2)` performs a **lexicographic (dictionary-order) comparison** based on the **unsigned** values of the characters (effectively the ASCII codes for ASCII text).

- Returns `0` if `s1` and `s2` are **identical** (character-by-character, including length).
- Returns a **negative** value if `s1` is lexicographically **less than** `s2`.
- Returns a **positive** value if `s1` is lexicographically **greater than** `s2`.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Board Pitfall — Boolean Mistake:** Students often write `if (strcmp(a, b)) { ... }` thinking it means "if equal." It actually means "if **NOT** equal." The correct form is `if (strcmp(a, b) == 0)`. The board deducts 2 marks for this single error.

## 2.5 Engineering Utility of String Handling

String processing is the backbone of nearly every real-world C application:

- **Operating Systems Kernels** — File paths (`/usr/bin/gcc`), environment variables, command-line argument parsing.
- **Embedded Systems** — Parsing UART input, AT command responses in GSM/Wi-Fi modules.
- **Compilers and Parsers** — Lexical analyzers tokenize source code into identifier, keyword, literal strings.
- **Network Protocol Stacks** — HTTP headers, JSON payloads, DNS labels are all null-terminated ASCII.
- **Database Engines** — SQL query construction and result-set formatting.
- **Cryptography and Security** — String comparison under constant time (`strncmp`/manual loops) defends against timing attacks.

---

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example A — `strlen` Manual Implementation

The KTU board frequently asks students to **simulate `strlen` from scratch** using a loop. Below is the exhaustive step-by-step logical trace.

**Given:** `char str[] = "Kerala";`

**Memory snapshot:**

| Index | 0 | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 | 6 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Char | `'K'` | `'e'` | `'r'` | `'a'` | `'l'` | `'a'` | `'\0'` |

**Algorithm (pointer-based):**

```c
#include <stdio.h>
int stringLength(const char *s) {
    int len = 0;
    while (*s != '\0') {
        len++;
        s++;
    }
    return len;
}
```

**Execution trace:**

| Step | `s` points to | `*s` | `*s != '\0'`? | Action | `len` |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | `'K'` | `'K'` (75) | True | `len=1`, advance | 1 |
| 2 | `'e'` | `'e'` (101) | True | `len=2`, advance | 2 |
| 3 | `'r'` | `'r'` (114) | True | `len=3`, advance | 3 |
| 4 | `'a'` | `'a'` (97) | True | `len=4`, advance | 4 |
| 5 | `'l'` | `'l'` (108) | True | `len=5`, advance | 5 |
| 6 | `'a'` | `'a'` (97) | True | `len=6`, advance | 6 |
| 7 | `'\0'` | `'\0'` (0) | **False** | Exit loop | 6 |

**Result:** `stringLength("Kerala")` returns `6`. The null terminator is **not counted** — this is the canonical definition of string length.

## 3.2 Worked Example B — `strcpy` Logic

`strcpy(dest, src)` copies every byte from `src` (including `'\0'`) into `dest` and returns `dest`.

**Algorithm:**

```c
char *stringCopy(char *dest, const char *src) {
    char *original = dest;
    while ((*dest++ = *src++) != '\0');
    return original;
}
```

**Trace** with `src = "Hi"`, `dest` initially garbage:

| Step | `*src` | Assignment to `*dest` | Then test `!= '\0'`? | Result |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | `'H'` | `dest[0] = 'H'` | `'H' != 0` → True | Continue |
| 2 | `'i'` | `dest[1] = 'i'` | `'i' != 0` → True | Continue |
| 3 | `'\0'` | `dest[2] = '\0'` | `0 != 0` → **False** | Stop |

**Result:** `dest` now holds `{'H','i','\0', ...}`.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight — The Post-Increment Trick:** The expression `(*dest++ = *src++) != '\0'` performs four operations in one C statement: (1) dereference `src`, (2) dereference `dest`, (3) assign, (4) advance both pointers, (5) test against `'\0'`. The loop ends exactly when the assigned value is `'\0'`, ensuring the terminator is copied but nothing after it.

## 3.3 Worked Example C — `strcmp` Step-by-Step

`strcmp("cat", "car")` — Expected outcome: positive (because `'t'` > `'r'` in ASCII).

**ASCII values:** `'c'=99`, `'a'=97`, `'t'=116`, `'r'=114`.

**Algorithm:**

```c
int stringCompare(const char *s1, const char *s2) {
    while (*s1 && (*s1 == *s2)) {
        s1++;
        s2++;
    }
    return *(unsigned char *)s1 - *(unsigned char *)s2;
}
```

**Trace:**

| Step | `*s1` | `*s2` | `*s1 == *s2`? | `*s1` truthy? | Action |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | `'c'` (99) | `'c'` (99) | Yes | Yes | Advance both |
| 2 | `'a'` (97) | `'a'` (97) | Yes | Yes | Advance both |
| 3 | `'t'` (116) | `'r'` (114) | No | Yes | Exit loop |
| 4 | — | — | — | — | Return `116 − 114 = 2` |

**Result:** `strcmp("cat", "car")` returns a positive value (`+2`). Since only the sign matters, KTU accepts "any positive integer."

## 3.4 Worked Example D — Naïve String Matching Algorithm

This is the **most-asked 14-mark question** on this topic. The naïve (brute-force) algorithm checks every possible alignment of the pattern in the text.

**Problem:** Find all occurrences of pattern `P = "ABA"` in text `T = "ABABABA"`.

**Indices:** `T[0..6] = {'A','B','A','B','A','B','A'}`, `P[0..2] = {'A','B','A'}`, `m = 3` (pattern length), `n = 7` (text length).

**Algorithm (pseudocode):**

```c
void naiveMatch(char *T, char *P) {
    int n = strlen(T), m = strlen(P);
    for (int i = 0; i <= n - m; i++) {
        int j = 0;
        while (j < m && T[i + j] == P[j]) j++;
        if (j == m) printf("Pattern found at index %d\n", i);
    }
}
```

**Exhaustive trace:**

| `i` | `i <= n - m` (=4) | `j=0` | `T[i+0]==P[0]`? | `j=1` | `T[i+1]==P[1]`? | `j=2` | `T[i+2]==P[2]`? | `j=3` | Match? |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | True | j=0 | `'A'=='A'` ✓ | j=1 | `'B'=='B'` ✓ | j=2 | `'A'=='A'` ✓ | j=3 | **YES (i=0)** |
| 1 | True | j=0 | `'B'=='A'` ✗ | — | — | — | — | — | No |
| 2 | True | j=0 | `'A'=='A'` ✓ | j=1 | `'B'=='B'` ✓ | j=2 | `'A'=='A'` ✓ | j=3 | **YES (i=2)** |
| 3 | True | j=0 | `'B'=='A'` ✗ | — | — | — | — | — | No |
| 4 | True | j=0 | `'A'=='A'` ✓ | j=1 | `'B'=='B'` ✓ | j=2 | `'A'=='A'` ✓ | j=3 | **YES (i=4)** |
| 5 | False (5 > 4) | — | — | — | — | — | — | — | Stop |

**Matches found at indices: 0, 2, 4.** Total `O(n × m)` time complexity in the worst case.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Algorithm Comparison

| Algorithm | Preprocessing | Matching Time | Space | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Naïve (Brute Force)** | `O(1)` | `O(n × m)` | `O(1)` | Simplest; first KTU choice |
| **KMP (Knuth-Morris-Pratt)** | `O(m)` — LPS array | `O(n + m)` | `O(m)` | Linear time; advanced |
| **Rabin-Karp (Hashing)** | `O(m)` | `O(n + m)` avg, `O(nm)` worst | `O(1)` | Uses rolling hash |
| **`strstr` (Library)** | Library-dependent | Vendor-optimized | — | Not portable to analyze |

> [!NOTE]
> The KTU 2024 syllabus (Module 2) explicitly asks for **naïve pattern matching** as the core deliverable. KMP may be a bonus or asked in higher-semester subjects (Design and Analysis of Algorithms).

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between `char str[10] = "Hello";` and `char *str = "Hello";` in C. Mention at least two differences.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | `char str[10] = "Hello";` | `char *str = "Hello";` |
|---|---|---|
| **Storage Class** | Allocates a mutable array on the stack (or global section). | Pointer variable pointing to a read-only string literal in `.rodata`. |
| **Mutability** | Individual characters can be modified: `str[0] = 'J';` is valid. | Modifying the literal (`str[0] = 'J';`) is **undefined behavior** in modern C. |
| **Size of `str`** | `sizeof(str) == 10` bytes (full array size). | `sizeof(str) == 8` bytes on 64-bit (just the pointer). |
| **Reassignment** | `str = another_array;` is **illegal** (array names are not modifiable lvalues). | `str = another_string;` is legal (pointer can be reassigned). |

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`
**What is the role of the null character `'\0'` in C strings? What happens if it is missing?**

**Model Answer:**

The null character `'\0'` (ASCII value 0) serves as the **string terminator sentinel** in C. It marks the end of the meaningful character data within a `char` array and is required by all standard library functions declared in `<string.h>` to determine string length.

**Consequences of a missing `'\0'`:**
- `strlen` will continue reading past the intended array into adjacent memory until it coincidentally finds a zero byte, returning an **incorrect (oversized) length**.
- `strcpy` will copy garbage bytes until a zero is found, often causing a **segmentation fault** or buffer overrun.
- `printf("%s", str)` will print beyond the intended string, displaying **memory garbage**.

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks)

**`(a)` [7 Marks]** `[KTU University Exam — May 2023, Model Paper 2024]`
**Explain any five string handling functions from `<string.h>` with proper function prototypes, descriptions, and small example programs.**

**Model Solution:**

**1. `strlen()` — String Length**

```c
#include <stdio.h>
#include <string.h>
int main() {
    char name[] = "Programming";
    int n = strlen(name);   /* n = 11 */
    printf("Length = %d\n", n);
    return 0;
}
```

*Prototype:* `size_t strlen(const char *s);`
*Returns:* Number of characters **before** the null terminator. Takes `O(n)` time.

**2. `strcpy()` — String Copy**

```c
#include <stdio.h>
#include <string.h>
int main() {
    char src[] = "KTU";
    char dest[10];
    strcpy(dest, src);
    printf("Copied: %s\n", dest);  /* Output: KTU */
    return 0;
}
```

*Prototype:* `char *strcpy(char *dest, const char *src);`
*Returns:* Pointer to `dest`. **Note:** Caller must ensure `dest` is large enough to hold `src` plus the null terminator.

**3. `strcat()` — String Concatenation**

```c
#include <stdio.h>
#include <string.h>
int main() {
    char greeting[20] = "Hello, ";
    strcat(greeting, "Kerala!");
    printf("%s\n", greeting);  /* Output: Hello, Kerala! */
    return 0;
}
```

*Prototype:* `char *strcat(char *dest, const char *src);`
*Note:* `dest` must be large enough to hold the original content, the appended content, and the null terminator.

**4. `strcmp()` — String Comparison**

```c
#include <stdio.h>
#include <string.h>
int main() {
    char a[] = "apple", b[] = "banana";
    int res = strcmp(a, b);
    if (res == 0)         printf("Equal\n");
    else if (res < 0)     printf("%s comes first\n", a);
    else                  printf("%s comes first\n", b);
    return 0;
}
```

*Prototype:* `int strcmp(const char *s1, const char *s2);`
*Note:* Returns `0` for equality, negative if `s1 < s2`, positive if `s1 > s2` (lexicographic, ASCII-based).

**5. `strstr()` — Substring Search**

```c
#include <stdio.h>
#include <string.h>
int main() {
    char text[] = "Welcome to KTU Kerala";
    char *found = strstr(text, "KTU");
    if (found != NULL)
        printf("Found at position: %ld\n", found - text);
    else
        printf("Not found\n");
    return 0;
}
```

*Prototype:* `char *strstr(const char *haystack, const char *needle);`
*Returns:* Pointer to first occurrence of `needle` in `haystack`, or `NULL` if not found.

**`(b)` [7 Marks]** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Model Paper 2024]`
**Write a complete C program to read a full sentence from the user (including spaces), reverse the sentence word-by-word, and display the result. Use `fgets()` for input and `<string.h>` functions for processing.**

**Model Solution:**

```c
#include <stdio.h>
#include <string.h>

int main() {
    char sentence[200];
    char reversed[200] = "";
    char *token;
    const char *delim = " \n";

    printf("Enter a sentence: ");
    fgets(sentence, sizeof(sentence), stdin);

    /* Tokenize and build the reversed sentence in reverse token order */
    /* Strategy: collect tokens, then append in reverse using a second pass */
    char words[50][50];
    int count = 0, i;

    token = strtok(sentence, delim);
    while (token != NULL) {
        strcpy(words[count++], token);
        token = strtok(NULL, delim);
    }

    /* Append tokens in reverse order */
    for (i = count - 1; i >= 0; i--) {
        strcat(reversed, words[i]);
        if (i > 0) strcat(reversed, " ");
    }

    printf("Reversed sentence: %s\n", reversed);
    return 0;
}
```

**Sample Run:**

```
Enter a sentence: KTU is in Kerala
Reversed sentence: Kerala in is KTU
```

**Algorithm Explanation:**
1. `fgets(sentence, 200, stdin)` reads up to 199 characters including spaces, with a buffer-overflow guard.
2. `strtok(sentence, " \n")` splits the sentence into words, breaking on spaces or the trailing newline.
3. Each token is stored in the 2D array `words[][]`.
4. A reverse loop concatenates the tokens back into `reversed` with spaces, producing word-by-word reversal.

### Question B (14 Marks)

**`(a)` [7 Marks]** `[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Supple Exam]`
**Implement the naïve string matching algorithm in C. Given a text `T` and a pattern `P`, the program should find and print all starting indices where `P` occurs in `T`. Trace the algorithm for `T = "ABABABCAB"` and `P = "ABAB"`.**

**Model Solution:**

```c
#include <stdio.h>
#include <string.h>

void naiveStringMatch(char *T, char *P) {
    int n = strlen(T);
    int m = strlen(P);
    int i, j;

    printf("Pattern occurrences at indices: ");
    for (i = 0; i <= n - m; i++) {
        j = 0;
        while (j < m && T[i + j] == P[j]) {
            j++;
        }
        if (j == m) {
            printf("%d ", i);
        }
    }
    printf("\n");
}

int main() {
    char T[100], P[100];
    printf("Enter text: ");
    fgets(T, sizeof(T), stdin);
    T[strcspn(T, "\n")] = '\0';   /* strip trailing newline */

    printf("Enter pattern: ");
    fgets(P, sizeof(P), stdin);
    P[strcspn(P, "\n")] = '\0';

    naiveStringMatch(T, P);
    return 0;
}
```

**Trace for `T = "ABABABCAB"` and `P = "ABAB"`:**

Here `n = 9`, `m = 4`, so the outer loop runs while `i <= 9 − 4 = 5`.

| `i` | Comparison Sequence | Result |
|---|---|---|
| 0 | `T[0..3] = "ABAB"` vs `P = "ABAB"` | **Match (i=0)** |
| 1 | `T[1..4] = "BABA"` vs `P` | Mismatch at `T[1]='B' != P[0]='A'` |
| 2 | `T[2..5] = "ABAB"` vs `P` | **Match (i=2)** |
| 3 | `T[3..6] = "BABC"` vs `P` | Mismatch at `T[3]='B' != 'A'` |
| 4 | `T[4..7] = "ABCA"` vs `P` | Mismatch at `T[6]='C' != 'B'` (j stops at 2) |
| 5 | `T[5..8] = "BCAB"` vs `P` | Mismatch at `T[5]='B' != 'A'` |

**Output:**

```
Pattern occurrences at indices: 0 2
```

**Complexity Analysis:**
- Worst-case time: `O(n × m)` (e.g., `T = "AAAAAA...A"`, `P = "AAB"`).
- Best case: `O(n)` (first character always mismatches).
- Space: `O(1)` auxiliary.

---

**`(b)` [7 Marks]** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Supple Exam]`
**Write a C program that reads a string from the user and uses `<string.h>` functions to perform the following operations: (i) find the length of the string, (ii) count the number of occurrences of a given character, (iii) check whether the string is a palindrome. Display the results clearly.**

**Model Solution:**

```c
#include <stdio.h>
#include <string.h>

int main() {
    char str[200];
    char ch;
    int i, len, count = 0, isPalindrome = 1;

    printf("Enter a string: ");
    fgets(str, sizeof(str), stdin);
    str[strcspn(str, "\n")] = '\0';

    len = strlen(str);

    /* (ii) Count occurrences of a character */
    printf("Enter character to count: ");
    scanf(" %c", &ch);
    for (i = 0; i < len; i++) {
        if (str[i] == ch) count++;
    }
    printf("Occurrences of '%c' = %d\n", ch, count);

    /* (iii) Palindrome check */
    for (i = 0; i < len / 2; i++) {
        if (str[i] != str[len - 1 - i]) {
            isPalindrome = 0;
            break;
        }
    }
    if (isPalindrome)
        printf("\"%s\" IS a palindrome.\n", str);
    else
        printf("\"%s\" is NOT a palindrome.\n", str);

    /* (i) Length already computed */
    printf("Length of the string = %d\n", len);

    return 0;
}
```

**Sample Run:**

```
Enter a string: malayalam
Enter character to count: a
Occurrences of 'a' = 4
"malayalam" IS a palindrome.
Length of the string = 9
```

**Explanation of Key Steps:**

- `str[strcspn(str, "\n")] = '\0';` removes the newline that `fgets` retains.
- The palindrome check iterates only up to `len / 2` for efficiency — comparing the first half with the reversed second half.
- The leading space in `scanf(" %c", &ch)` skips any leftover whitespace in the input buffer (a common KTU pitfall).

---

## 5.4 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Zones (Read Carefully!):**
>
> 1. **Forgetting `'\0'` in manual string functions** — A custom `my_strcpy` that stops at `'\0'` but fails to **copy** the `'\0'` itself loses 3 marks.
> 2. **Using `==` to compare strings** — `if (s1 == s2)` compares **pointers**, not contents. Use `strcmp(s1, s2) == 0`. Loses 2 marks.
> 3. **Inverting `strcmp` return logic** — Writing `if (strcmp(a, b))` for "if equal" loses 2 marks. The condition is **truthy when NOT equal**.
> 4. **Buffer size in `char str[N]`** — Always allocate `strlen(literal) + 1` to accommodate the null terminator. A `char s[5] = "Hello";` is a **compilation error**.
> 5. **Mixing `gets()` with modern code** — The board deducts marks for using the removed `gets()`. Always use `fgets()` in 2024 scheme answers.
> 6. **Outer loop bound in naïve pattern matching** — The condition must be `i <= n - m`, not `i < n`. Missing one match loses 2 marks.
> 7. **Modifying string literals via pointer** — `char *p = "abc"; p[0] = 'x';` is undefined behavior. Use array syntax.

## 5.5 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Last-Minute KTU Revision — Memorize This Section.**

- ✅ A C string is a **null-terminated `char` array**; the terminator `'\0'` is mandatory.
- ✅ String literal `"abc"` occupies `4` bytes (3 chars + 1 terminator) in the binary.
- ✅ **Declaration forms:** `char s[N]`, `char s[] = "..."`, `char *p = "..."` (read-only!), `const char *p` (safest read-only).
- ✅ **Reading:** `scanf("%s", s)` (no spaces, unsafe); `fgets(s, n, stdin)` (safe, preferred). **`gets()` is removed in C11 and is forbidden in KTU 2024.**
- ✅ **Displaying:** `printf("%s", s)`, `puts(s)` (auto newline), character-by-character loop.
- ✅ **Mandatory `<string.h>` functions and return behaviors:**
  - `strlen(s)` → length excluding `'\0'`
  - `strcpy(d, s)` → copies including `'\0'`; **no bounds check**
  - `strncpy(d, s, n)` → bounded copy; may not null-terminate if `src >= n`
  - `strcat(d, s)` → appends `s` to `d`; needs sufficient space
  - `strcmp(a, b)` → returns `0` (equal), negative (`a < b`), positive (`a > b`)
  - `strchr(s, c)` → first occurrence of character `c` in `s`
  - `strrchr(s, c)` → **last** occurrence
  - `strstr(hay, needle)` → substring search
  - `strtok(s, delim)` → splits string using delimiter characters
- ✅ **String matching:** Naïve algorithm runs in `O(nm)` worst case; outer loop `for(i=0; i<=n-m; i++)`; inner loop advances `j` while characters match; report match when `j == m`.
- ✅ **Palindrome check:** Iterate `i = 0` to `len/2 - 1`; compare `s[i]` with `s[len-1-i]`.
- ✅ **Common pitfalls:** `==` vs `strcmp`; missing `'\0'`; using `gets()`; `scanf("%s")` truncating at spaces; treating string literal as mutable.
- ✅ **Always include `<string.h>` and `<stdio.h>` headers** — missing headers lose easy marks.
- ✅ **Use `fgets()` and `strcspn()` for safe input handling** in all 2024 Scheme submissions.
- ✅ **Algorithm complexity must be stated** in 14-mark answers — examiners expect `O(...)` analysis.
- ✅ **Trace tables for 14-mark answers** earn full marks — show every comparison step.
- ✅ **Dry-run your code mentally** on a 2-3 character example before writing the program.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Functions - Function Definition, Function Call, Function Prototype, Parameter Passing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GXEST204 - PROGRAMMING IN C
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Functions - Function Definition, Function Call, Function Prototype, Parameter Passing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Function?

A **function** in C is a named, self-contained, reusable block of statements designed to perform a specific, well-defined task. Functions are the fundamental building blocks of structured programming and enable modularity, abstraction, and code reuse.

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
> A function is a sub-program that receives zero or more inputs (parameters), executes a sequence of statements, and optionally returns a single value to the calling program. Each function has its own activation record (stack frame) created on the call stack when invoked.

**Conceptual Analogy — The Vending Machine Model 🍫**

Think of a function as a **vending machine**:
- The **machine itself** is the function (it has a name and a fixed internal mechanism).
- The **coin slot and selection buttons** are the **formal parameters** — they accept specific inputs.
- The **product dispensing tray** is the **return value** — a single output (or no output for `void` functions).
- When you press the button (**function call**), the machine processes the input and dispenses the product. You don't need to know how it works internally — that's **abstraction**.
- The **manual pasted on the front** is the **function prototype** — it tells you what input the machine needs and what output to expect, without revealing internal wiring.

You can use the same vending machine (function) thousands of times, with different inputs (arguments), and get different outputs — perfect code reuse.

---

## 1.2 Function Definition

A **function definition** is the actual implementation of a function — the complete code that tells the compiler *what* the function does. It contains all five essential components: return type, function name, formal parameter list, function body, and a return statement (if non-void).

```c
return_type function_name ( parameter_list ) {
    // local variable declarations
    // executable statements
    return ( expression );   // mandatory for non-void functions
}
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Five Mandatory Components of a Function Definition:**
> 1. **Return Type** — data type of the value returned (`int`, `float`, `void`, etc.)
> 2. **Function Name** — a valid C identifier following naming rules
> 3. **Parameter List** — formal parameters with their data types (may be empty)
> 4. **Function Body** — block of statements enclosed in `{ }`
> 5. **Return Statement** — returns a value whose type matches the return type

**Example:**

```c
int add (int a, int b) {       // definition begins
    int sum;                    // local variable
    sum = a + b;                // computation
    return sum;                 // return value
}                               // definition ends
```

---

## 1.3 Function Call

A **function call** is the act of *invoking* or *executing* a function. When a function is called, control transfers from the caller to the callee, the function's body executes, and control (along with an optional return value) returns to the point of call.

**Syntax:** `function_name ( argument_list );`

**Example:**

```c
int main(void) {
    int result;
    result = add(5, 3);   // ← This is a function call
    printf("%d", result); // Output: 8
    return 0;
}
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Terminology:**
> - **Actual Arguments** — the values/expressions passed during the call (e.g., `5`, `3`).
> - **Formal Parameters** — the variables declared in the function definition that receive the values (e.g., `a`, `b`).
> - **Argument-Passing Order** — In C, arguments are evaluated and passed **left-to-right** (although evaluation order of sub-expressions is unspecified in standard C).

---

## 1.4 Function Prototype (Declaration)

A **function prototype** is a statement that *declares* a function to the compiler without providing its body. It tells the compiler three things: the function's name, its return type, and the number and types of its parameters.

**Syntax:** `return_type function_name ( parameter_type_list );`

**Example:**

```c
int add(int, int);          // prototype — parameter names are optional
// or
int add(int a, int b);      // prototype with parameter names (still optional)
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why are Function Prototypes Necessary in C?**
> 1. **Forward Declaration** — C is a single-pass compiler. If you call a function *before* it is defined, the compiler must already know its signature.
> 2. **Type Checking** — The prototype enables the compiler to verify that arguments match parameter types (and apply implicit conversions).
> 3. **Return Type Validation** — Ensures the calling code uses the return value correctly.
> 4. **Modular Programming** — Allows splitting a program across multiple files; prototypes in header files enable separate compilation.

---

## 1.5 Parameter Passing

**Parameter passing** refers to the mechanism by which arguments are transferred from the calling function to the called function. C supports two fundamental methods:

| Method | What is Passed | Effect on Caller's Variable |
|---|---|---|
| **Pass by Value** | A *copy* of the argument's value | Original variable is **unchanged** |
| **Pass by Address** (a.k.a. Call by Reference, simulated via pointers) | The *memory address* of the argument | Original variable **can be modified** through dereferencing |

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical Clarification:**
> C does **NOT** have true pass-by-reference like C++ or Pascal. C *simulates* call-by-reference using pointers — the pointer itself is passed by value, but the address it carries allows the callee to modify the caller's original variable.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of a Function Definition — Detailed Breakdown

### Component 1: Return Type

- Specifies the data type of the value the function sends back to the caller.
- If the function performs an action but produces no value, use **`void`**.
- If omitted in old-style C, the function defaults to `int` (legacy K&R C) — but in **KTU 2024 Scheme (C99/C11 standard)**, the return type is **mandatory**.

### Component 2: Function Name

- Must follow C identifier rules: starts with letter/underscore, contains letters/digits/underscores.
- Should be descriptive and verb-like: `calculateSum()`, `printHeader()`, `isPrime()`.
- Case-sensitive: `Add` and `add` are different functions.

### Component 3: Parameter List

- Enclosed in parentheses `( )`.
- Each parameter is declared as: `data_type parameter_name`.
- If a function takes no parameters, the list is either empty `( )` or explicitly `(void)`.
- Multiple parameters are separated by commas.

### Component 4: Function Body

- Enclosed in braces `{ }`.
- Contains local variable declarations followed by executable statements.
- Local variables have **automatic storage duration** by default — they exist only while the function is executing.

### Component 5: Return Statement

- Format: `return ( expression );` or `return expression;`
- For `void` functions, `return;` (with no value) is optional — control simply falls off the end.
- A non-void function must return a value via a path that the compiler can verify.

---

## 2.2 Categories of Functions in C

### 2.2.1 Library (Built-in) Functions

Pre-written functions provided by the C standard library, declared in header files.
- Examples: `printf()`, `scanf()`, `sqrt()`, `strlen()`, `malloc()`, `fopen()`.
- Header files: `<stdio.h>`, `<math.h>`, `<string.h>`, `<stdlib.h>`.

### 2.2.2 User-defined Functions

Functions written by the programmer for specific tasks.
- Required when a logical task repeats or for modular design.
- Three sub-types:

| Type | Description | Example Use |
|---|---|---|
| **Functions with arguments and return value** | Take inputs, return a single value | `int add(int a, int b)` |
| **Functions with arguments, no return value** | Take inputs, perform action (use `void`) | `void printSum(int a, int b)` |
| **Functions with no arguments, return value** | Produce value using internal logic only | `int getRandom(void)` |
| **Functions with no arguments, no return value** | Pure action functions | `void greet(void)` |

---

## 2.3 Function Prototype — Rules and Placement

### Rules:
1. A prototype **must match** the function definition in return type, name, and parameter types.
2. Parameter **names** in the prototype are optional but recommended for documentation.
3. If a prototype and definition disagree, the compiler issues an error or warning.
4. In KTU's GCC environment, the compiler may issue a **default return-type warning** if no prototype precedes a function call.

### Placement Options:
- Above `main()` in the same file.
- In a header file (`.h`) included by source files (best practice for multi-file projects).

---

## 2.4 Parameter Passing — Detailed Mechanisms

### 2.4.1 Pass by Value

When a function is called, the compiler allocates space on the call stack for the formal parameters and **copies** the values of actual arguments into these locations. Any modification to formal parameters affects **only the local copies** — the caller's variables remain unchanged.

**Mechanics:**
1. Caller evaluates actual arguments.
2. Values are copied into the callee's stack frame.
3. Callee operates on the copies.
4. When the callee returns, its stack frame is destroyed — copies vanish.

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy:** Pass by Value is like making a **photocopy of a document** and giving it to a colleague. If your colleague edits the copy, your original document remains untouched.

### 2.4.2 Pass by Address (Call by Reference Simulation)

The caller passes the **address** of a variable using the address-of operator `&`. The callee receives this address in a **pointer parameter**, declared using the dereference operator `*`. The callee can then access and modify the original variable through the pointer.

**Mechanics:**
1. Caller takes the address of the actual argument using `&variable`.
2. This address is copied into the callee's pointer parameter.
3. Callee dereferences the pointer (`*ptr`) to read/write the original variable.
4. Changes persist after the callee returns because they affect the original memory location.

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy:** Pass by Address is like giving a colleague **the URL of a Google Doc** instead of a PDF printout. They can now edit the *original document* directly, and your changes are visible to everyone.

---

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet & Rules Cheat Sheet

### Parameter Passing Mechanics

| Concept | Pass by Value | Pass by Address |
|---|---|---|
| Syntax | `foo(x)` | `foo(&x)` |
| Formal Parameter Type | Same as actual (e.g., `int a`) | Pointer to type (e.g., `int *p`) |
| What is Transferred | A **copy** of the value | A **copy** of the address |
| Modifies Caller's Variable? | **No** | **Yes** (via `*p`) |
| Memory Overhead | One full copy per parameter | One pointer (typically **4 or 8 bytes**) per parameter |
| Safety | Safer — caller's data protected | Risky — accidental writes possible |
| Multiple Return Values | Not directly possible | Possible via multiple pointer parameters |

### Memory Size Rules (on a 64-bit system)

$$
\text{sizeof}(\texttt{int}) = 4 \text{ bytes}, \quad \text{sizeof}(\texttt{float}) = 4 \text{ bytes}
$$

$$
\text{sizeof}(\texttt{double}) = 8 \text{ bytes}, \quad \text{sizeof}(\texttt{char}) = 1 \text{ byte}
$$

$$
\text{sizeof}(\text{any pointer}) = 8 \text{ bytes on 64-bit, } 4 \text{ bytes on 32-bit}
$$

### Address Arithmetic (Pointer Parameter)

If a pointer `p` holds address `A`, and we advance by `n` elements of type `T`:

$$
p + n = A + n \cdot \text{sizeof}(T)
$$

For example, advancing an `int*` by 1 advances the address by **4 bytes**.

### Function Call Cost (Big-O Approximation)

The cost of a function call includes:
- **Argument evaluation:** $O(k)$ for $k$ arguments.
- **Stack frame allocation:** $O(p)$ for $p$ parameters + local variables.
- **Return:** $O(1)$ for return value transfer.

For deep recursion, the call stack grows as:

$$
\text{Stack Depth} = O(n) \quad \text{for } n \text{ recursive calls}
$$

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Application of Functions in C |
|---|---|
| **Embedded Systems** | Modular drivers (UART_init, GPIO_setPin), interrupt service routines |
| **Signal Processing** | Reusable filter functions (`applyFFT()`, `convolve()`) |
| **Operating Systems** | System calls, kernel subsystems (scheduler, memory manager) |
| **IoT & Firmware** | Sensor-reading functions, state machines |
| **Scientific Computing** | Mathematical libraries (numerical integration, matrix ops) |
| **Compiler Design** | Lexical analyzers, parsers, code generators |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Functions enable **modular firmware design** — a foundational principle in real-time embedded systems used in industries like automotive (AUTOSAR), aerospace (DO-178C), and medical devices (IEC 62304).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Execution Traces & Analytical Walkthroughs

## 3.1 Execution Trace of a Simple Function Call — The Call Stack Mechanism

Consider the following program:

```c
#include <stdio.h>

int square(int n) {       // function definition
    int result;
    result = n * n;
    return result;
}

int main(void) {
    int x = 4;
    int y;
    y = square(x);        // function call
    printf("y = %d\n", y);
    return 0;
}
```

### Step-by-Step Execution Trace

**Step 1 — Program Start**
- `main()` is invoked by the operating system.
- Memory is allocated on the call stack for `main`'s local variables.

| Memory Location | Variable | Value |
|---|---|---|
| Stack Frame of `main` | `x` | uninitialized |

**Step 2 — Execute `int x = 4;`**

| Memory Location | Variable | Value |
|---|---|---|
| Stack Frame of `main` | `x` | **4** |

**Step 3 — Reach Function Call `y = square(x);`**

Before the call, the actual argument `x` (value `4`) is evaluated. The compiler pushes the return address (location of next instruction in `main`) onto the stack, then transfers control to `square`.

| Memory Location | Variable / Item | Value |
|---|---|---|
| Stack Frame of `main` | `x` | 4 |
| Stack Frame of `main` | `y` | uninitialized |
| Return Address pushed | — | Address of next statement in `main` |
| Stack Frame of `square` (newly allocated) | `n` | **4** (copy of x) |

**Step 4 — Execute `square` Body**

| Memory Location | Variable | Value |
|---|---|---|
| Stack Frame of `square` | `n` | 4 |
| Stack Frame of `square` | `result` | 16 (after `n * n`) |

**Step 5 — Execute `return result;`**

The return value `16` is placed in a designated CPU register (typically `eax` on x86). The stack frame of `square` is destroyed. Control returns to `main` at the saved return address.

| Memory Location | Variable | Value |
|---|---|---|
| Stack Frame of `main` | `x` | 4 |
| Stack Frame of `main` | `y` | **16** (assigned from return register) |

> [!NOTE]
> After `square` returns, the stack frame of `square` is **deallocated**. Any reference to `n` or `result` outside `square` is invalid (dangling reference).

---

## 3.2 Pass by Value — Detailed Analytical Walkthrough

**Goal:** Demonstrate that the caller's variable is **unaffected** by changes inside the callee.

**Program:**

```c
#include <stdio.h>

void tryToModify(int a) {
    a = a + 100;
    printf("Inside function: a = %d\n", a);
}

int main(void) {
    int x = 5;
    printf("Before call: x = %d\n", x);
    tryToModify(x);
    printf("After call:  x = %d\n", x);
    return 0;
}
```

**Expected Output:**
```
Before call: x = 5
Inside function: a = 105
After call:  x = 5
```

### Step-by-Step Memory State Analysis

**Step 1 — Before the Call**

| Location | Variable | Value | Address (illustrative) |
|---|---|---|---|
| `main` stack | `x` | 5 | 0x7FF0 |

**Step 2 — At Function Entry: Argument Copied**

| Location | Variable | Value | Address |
|---|---|---|---|
| `main` stack | `x` | 5 | 0x7FF0 |
| `tryToModify` stack | `a` | **5** (copy) | 0x7FE0 |

**Step 3 — After `a = a + 100;`**

| Location | Variable | Value | Address |
|---|---|---|---|
| `main` stack | `x` | 5 | 0x7FF0 |
| `tryToModify` stack | `a` | **105** | 0x7FE0 |

**Step 4 — After Function Returns**

| Location | Variable | Value |
|---|---|---|
| `main` stack | `x` | **5 (unchanged)** |

The `a` location (0x7FE0) is destroyed when the stack frame is popped. The original `x` was never touched.

---

## 3.3 Pass by Address — Detailed Analytical Walkthrough

**Goal:** Modify the caller's variable through pointer dereferencing.

**Program:**

```c
#include <stdio.h>

void modify(int *p) {
    *p = *p + 100;
    printf("Inside function: *p = %d\n", *p);
}

int main(void) {
    int x = 5;
    printf("Before call: x = %d\n", x);
    modify(&x);
    printf("After call:  x = %d\n", x);
    return 0;
}
```

**Expected Output:**
```
Before call: x = 5
Inside function: *p = 105
After call:  x = 105
```

### Step-by-Step Memory State Analysis

**Step 1 — Before the Call**

| Location | Variable | Value | Address |
|---|---|---|---|
| `main` stack | `x` | 5 | 0x7FF0 |

**Step 2 — At Function Entry: Address Passed**

The caller passes `&x` (= 0x7FF0). This address is copied into pointer `p`.

| Location | Variable | Value | Address |
|---|---|---|---|
| `main` stack | `x` | 5 | 0x7FF0 |
| `modify` stack | `p` | **0x7FF0** (address of x) | 0x7FE0 |

**Step 3 — After `*p = *p + 100;`**

The dereference operator `*p` reads/writes the memory at address 0x7FF0 — the original `x` location.

| Location | Variable | Value | Address |
|---|---|---|---|
| `main` stack | `x` | **105** | 0x7FF0 |
| `modify` stack | `p` | 0x7FF0 | 0x7FE0 |

**Step 4 — After Function Returns**

| Location | Variable | Value |
|---|---|---|
| `main` stack | `x` | **105 (modified!)** |

### Mathematical Summary of Address Dereferencing

$$
\text{If } p = \&x \text{ and } x = 5, \text{ then } *p \text{ refers to the cell at address } p.
$$

$$
*p = *p + 100 \implies \text{cell at address } p \text{ becomes } 5 + 100 = 105.
$$

This explains why the modification **persists**: the write operation targeted the original memory cell of `x`, not a temporary copy.

---

## 3.4 Activation Record (Stack Frame) — Full Anatomy

When any function is called, an **activation record** is pushed onto the call stack. For a function `foo(int a, int b)`, the layout (from high to low address) typically is:

| Offset | Item | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| Highest | Return Value (if non-void) | Storage for return value |
| ↓ | Previous Frame Pointer | Saved base pointer of caller |
| ↓ | Return Address | Instruction pointer to resume after call |
| ↓ | Parameter `b` | Formal parameter 2 |
| ↓ | Parameter `a` | Formal parameter 1 |
| Lowest | Local Variables | Auto variables declared inside `foo` |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The exact order and content of an activation record depend on the compiler and calling convention (e.g., cdecl on x86). KTU exam questions focus on the **conceptual model** — students are not expected to know machine-level register details, only the logical content of a stack frame.

---

## 3.5 Comprehensive Comparison Matrix — Pass by Value vs Pass by Address

| Aspect | Pass by Value | Pass by Address |
|---|---|---|
| **Argument Syntax** | `foo(x)` | `foo(&x)` |
| **Formal Parameter Syntax** | `int foo(int a)` | `int foo(int *p)` |
| **Mechanism** | Value of `x` is copied to `a` | Address of `x` is copied to `p` |
| **Effect on Caller** | Safe — original data protected | Risky — original data can be altered |
| **Memory Cost** | High for large structs (full copy) | Low — only pointer copied |
| **Use Case** | Read-only inputs, calculations | Modify multiple caller variables, large data |
| **Common Errors** | None significant | Dangling pointers, null pointers |
| **Array Passing** | Arrays decay to pointers (special case) | Always pass as `int arr[]` or `int *arr` |
| **Function Returns** | Single value via `return` | Multiple values via pointer parameters |
| **Const-Correctness** | Easy to enforce | Use `const int *p` to prevent modification |

> [!TIP]
> **Array Special Case:** When you pass an array to a function in C, you are *always* passing a pointer to its first element, even without the `&` operator. For example, `foo(arr)` is equivalent to `foo(&arr[0])`. This is why arrays are *always* modifiable inside functions unless marked `const`.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Function Call Mechanism — End-to-End Flow

The following flowchart illustrates the complete lifecycle of a function call, from argument preparation through execution to return.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startNode([START PROGRAM])
    mainExec[Main function executes]
    declareVars[Declare variables x and y]
    evalArgs[Evaluate arguments to be passed]
    pushArgs[Push arguments onto call stack]
    saveReturn[Save return address]
    transferCtrl[Transfer control to callee]
    allocFrame[Allocate callee stack frame]
    copyParams[Copy actual arguments to formal parameters]
    execBody[Execute callee function body]
    returnCheck{Return statement encountered?}
    storeReturn[Store return value in CPU register]
    deallocFrame[Deallocate callee stack frame]
    restoreReturn[Pop return address from stack]
    resumeCaller[Resume execution at return address in caller]
    useReturn[Assign return value to receiving variable]
    continueMain[Continue execution of caller]
    finishNode([END PROGRAM])

    startNode --> mainExec --> declareVars --> evalArgs
    evalArgs --> pushArgs --> saveReturn --> transferCtrl
    transferCtrl --> allocFrame --> copyParams --> execBody
    execBody --> returnCheck
    returnCheck -- YES --> storeReturn --> deallocFrame
    returnCheck -- NO --> storeReturn
    storeReturn --> deallocFrame --> restoreReturn --> resumeCaller
    resumeCaller --> useReturn --> continueMain --> finishNode
```

---

## 4.2 Memory Layout — Stack Frame Structure During Function Call

The block diagram below depicts how memory is organized on the call stack when `main` calls a function `compute`.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph stackArea[STACK MEMORY - Grows Downward]
        direction TB
        frameMain["MAIN STACK FRAME\nx = 5\ny = uninitialized"]
        retAddr["RETURN ADDRESS\n(address of next statement in main)"]
        paramB["FORMAL PARAMETER b\nvalue = 8"]
        paramA["FORMAL PARAMETER a\nvalue = 3"]
        localM["LOCAL VARIABLE m\nvalue = 11 (after a + b)"]
        localR["LOCAL VARIABLE result\nvalue = 33 (after m * 3)"]
        frameCompute["COMPUTE STACK FRAME\nfunctions local memory"]
    end

    frameMain -. points to .-> retAddr
    retAddr --> paramB
    paramB --> paramA
    paramA --> localM
    localM --> localR
    localR --> frameCompute
```

**Reading the diagram:** Stacks grow downward in memory (lower addresses at the bottom). When `compute(a, b)` is called, the compiler pushes the return address and parameters in reverse order (or per calling convention), then allocates space for local variables.

---

## 4.3 Pass by Value vs Pass by Address — Visual Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph pbvBlock[PASS BY VALUE]
        actualV["ACTUAL ARGUMENT\nx = 5\nat address 0x1000"]
        formalV["FORMAL PARAMETER\na = 5 (COPY)\nat address 0x2000"]
        actualV -- "value copied" --> formalV
        formalV -. "modifying a does NOT affect x" .-> actualV
    end

    subgraph pbaBlock[PASS BY ADDRESS]
        actualA["ACTUAL ARGUMENT\nx = 5\nat address 0x1000"]
        formalA["FORMAL PARAMETER\np = 0x1000 (ADDRESS)\nat address 0x3000"]
        actualA -- "address copied" --> formalA
        formalA -- "p dereferenced writes to 0x1000" --> actualA
    end

    pbvBlock ~~~ pbaBlock
```

**Interpretation:**
- In **Pass by Value** (left), two distinct memory cells exist. Modifying one does not affect the other.
- In **Pass by Address** (right), the formal parameter `p` stores the *address* of the original variable. Dereferencing `p` writes directly to the original memory cell, so changes persist after the function returns.

---

## 4.4 Function Prototype vs Function Definition — Relationship

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    protoNode["FUNCTION PROTOCLE\nreturn_type name param_types\nExample: int add int int\nDeclared in header or above main"]
    defNode["FUNCTION DEFINITION\nreturn_type name params body\nExample: int add int a int b return a plus b\nContains actual code"]
    callNode["FUNCTION CALL\nname actual_args\nExample: result = add 5 3\nInvokes the function"]
    compiler["COMPILER\nPerforms type checking\nbetween prototype and call"]
    linker["LINKER\nResolves function addresses\nbetween definition and call"]

    protoNode -- "signature match" --> defNode
    protoNode -- "signature match" --> callNode
    compiler -. "validates" .-> protoNode
    linker -. "connects" .-> defNode
    linker -. "connects" .-> callNode
    defNode -. "code lives here" .-> linker
    callNode -. "execution starts here" .-> linker
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The **prototype** acts as a *contract* between the caller and the callee — it must match the definition. The compiler enforces this contract; the linker ensures that calls resolve to actual function definitions at runtime.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 KTU Exam Pattern Quick Reference

| Component | Marks | Time Allocation | Cognitive Level (Bloom's) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Part A — Short Answer | 3 marks each | ~6 minutes | Understand / Remember |
| Part B — Long Answer (with internal choice) | 14 marks (2 sub-parts of 7 each) | ~25 minutes | Apply / Analyze |
| Module-wise Weightage | As per syllabus | — | — |

---

## 5.2 PART A — 3 Mark Questions

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: What is a function prototype? Why is it required in C programs? Give an example.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A function prototype is a declaration statement that informs the compiler about a function's name, return type, and the number and types of its parameters, **without providing the function body**.

**Syntax:** `return_type function_name(parameter_types);`

**Example:** `int add(int, int);`

**Why it is required:**
1. C is a single-pass compiler — if a function is called before it is defined, the prototype provides forward declaration.
2. It enables the compiler to perform **type checking** on arguments, ensuring actual arguments match formal parameter types.
3. It ensures the return value is used correctly.

**(3 marks)**

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: Differentiate between "pass by value" and "pass by address" in C. When would you prefer one over the other?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Pass by Value | Pass by Address |
|---|---|---|
