| What is passed | A copy of the value | A copy of the address |
| Modifies caller? | No | Yes (via dereference) |
| Syntax at call | `foo(x)` | `foo(&x)` |
| Memory cost | Full copy | Pointer copy only |

**When to prefer:**
- **Pass by value** is preferred when the function should not modify the original data (read-only inputs) or for small data types.
- **Pass by address** is preferred when the function must modify multiple caller variables, when passing large structures (avoid expensive copy), or when working with arrays.

**(3 marks)**

---

## 5.3 PART B — 14 Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Paper]`

**(a)** **[7 marks]** Explain the different elements of a function definition with a suitable example. Also explain the concept of function prototype and state why it is necessary.

**(b)** **[7 marks]** Write a C program to swap two integers using (i) pass by value and (ii) pass by address. Explain why only one of them successfully swaps the values.

---

### Model Solution for Question A

#### Part (a) — Elements of Function Definition & Function Prototype

**Elements of a Function Definition (5 components):**

```c
return_type function_name ( parameter_list ) {
    // body
    return ( expression );
}
```

1. **Return Type** — Data type of value returned (e.g., `int`, `float`, `void`).
2. **Function Name** — Valid C identifier (e.g., `add`).
3. **Parameter List** — Formal parameters with types (e.g., `int a, int b`).
4. **Function Body** — Block of statements inside `{ }`.
5. **Return Statement** — Returns a value (mandatory for non-void functions).

**Example Definition:**

```c
int add(int a, int b) {     // return_type name params
    int sum = a + b;         // local variable
    return sum;              // return statement
}
```

**Function Prototype:**

A prototype is a function declaration without the body, ending with a semicolon.

```c
int add(int a, int b);     // prototype
// or
int add(int, int);          // prototype without parameter names
```

**Why Prototypes are Necessary:**
- C is a single-pass compiler; without a prototype, calling a function before its definition causes an implicit declaration warning (C99 treats return type as `int` by default — risky).
- The prototype enables **argument type checking** and **implicit conversions**.
- It is essential for **separate compilation** in multi-file projects (prototypes go in `.h` files).

**(7 marks)**

#### Part (b) — Swap Using Pass by Value vs Pass by Address

**Program:**

```c
#include <stdio.h>

// (i) Pass by value - WILL NOT SWAP
void swapByValue(int a, int b) {
    int temp = a;
    a = b;
    b = temp;
}

// (ii) Pass by address - WILL SWAP
void swapByAddress(int *a, int *b) {
    int temp = *a;
    *a = *b;
    *b = temp;
}

int main(void) {
    int x = 10, y = 20;

    printf("Before swapByValue: x=%d, y=%d\n", x, y);
    swapByValue(x, y);
    printf("After  swapByValue: x=%d, y=%d\n\n", x, y);

    printf("Before swapByAddress: x=%d, y=%d\n", x, y);
    swapByAddress(&x, &y);
    printf("After  swapByAddress: x=%d, y=%d\n", x, y);

    return 0;
}
```

**Output:**
```
Before swapByValue: x=10, y=20
After  swapByValue: x=10, y=20

Before swapByAddress: x=10, y=20
After  swapByAddress: x=20, y=10
```

**Explanation:**

In `swapByValue`, the formal parameters `a` and `b` are **local copies** of `x` and `y`. Swapping them affects only the copies, which are destroyed when the function returns. Therefore, `x` and `y` in `main` remain unchanged.

In `swapByAddress`, the formal parameters are **pointers** that receive the **addresses** of `x` and `y`. By dereferencing them with `*a` and `*b`, the function writes directly to the original memory locations of `x` and `y`. Therefore, the swap persists after the function returns.

**(7 marks)**

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model Paper]`

**(a)** **[7 marks]** What are the different methods of parameter passing in C? Explain each with an example program.

**(b)** **[7 marks]** Explain the function call mechanism in C with the help of an activation record. Trace the execution of the following program and show the contents of the stack at each step:

```c
#include <stdio.h>
int multiply(int a, int b) {
    int p = a * b;
    return p;
}
int main(void) {
    int m = 4, n = 5, r;
    r = multiply(m, n);
    printf("%d", r);
    return 0;
}
```

---

### Model Solution for Question B

#### Part (a) — Methods of Parameter Passing

C supports two parameter-passing methods:

**1. Pass by Value:**
A copy of the actual argument's value is passed to the formal parameter. Changes inside the function do not affect the caller.

```c
#include <stdio.h>
void increment(int n) {
    n = n + 1;
    printf("Inside: n = %d\n", n);
}
int main(void) {
    int x = 10;
    increment(x);
    printf("Outside: x = %d\n", x);   // x is still 10
    return 0;
}
```

Output: `Inside: n = 11` then `Outside: x = 10`.

**2. Pass by Address (Call by Reference Simulation):**
The address of the actual argument is passed. The callee uses a pointer to access and modify the original variable.

```c
#include <stdio.h>
void increment(int *n) {
    *n = *n + 1;
}
int main(void) {
    int x = 10;
    increment(&x);
    printf("x = %d\n", x);   // x becomes 11
    return 0;
}
```

Output: `x = 11`.

**Comparison summary:** Pass by value is safer but cannot return multiple values; pass by address is more powerful but requires careful handling to avoid bugs like dangling or null pointers.

**(7 marks)**

#### Part (b) — Function Call Mechanism & Stack Trace

**Function Call Mechanism:**

When a function is invoked, the following steps occur:
1. The actual arguments are evaluated.
2. A new **activation record (stack frame)** is pushed onto the call stack.
3. The return address (location of next instruction in caller) is saved in the frame.
4. Values (or addresses) are copied into the formal parameter slots.
5. Control transfers to the callee.
6. The callee executes its body, possibly creating local variables.
7. The return value (if any) is stored in a designated location.
8. The callee's stack frame is destroyed (popped).
9. Control returns to the caller at the saved return address.

**Activation Record for `multiply(int a, int b)`:**

| Component | Description |
|---|---|
| Return value storage | Holds `p` (to be copied to `r` in main) |
| Saved frame pointer | Base pointer of `main`'s frame |
| Return address | Address of statement after `multiply(m, n)` in `main` |
| Formal parameter `b` | Holds copy of `n` (= 5) |
| Formal parameter `a` | Holds copy of `m` (= 4) |
| Local variable `p` | Holds `a * b` (= 20) |

**Execution Trace:**

| Step | Action | Stack Contents (top-of-stack focus) |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | `int m = 4, n = 5, r;` | `m=4, n=5, r=?` in main's frame |
| 2 | `multiply(m, n)` called | Push return address; push `b=5`, `a=4`; allocate `p` |
| 3 | `int p = a * b;` | `p = 4 * 5 = 20` |
| 4 | `return p;` | Return value 20 stored; frame destroyed |
| 5 | Back in main: `r = 20;` | `r = 20` in main's frame |
| 6 | `printf("%d", r);` | Output: `20` |

**(7 marks)**

---

## 5.4 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks in This Topic:**
> 1. **Confusing `*` in declaration vs dereference** — `int *p` declares a pointer; `*p` dereferences it. Mixing these up costs marks.
> 2. **Forgetting the semicolon after the prototype** — `int foo(int);` ← semicolon mandatory. The body has braces, not a semicolon.
> 3. **Passing `&x` to a pass-by-value parameter** — Compiler error: type mismatch. Address must go to a pointer parameter.
> 4. **Believing C has true pass-by-reference** — State clearly that C *simulates* it via pointers.
> 5. **Not drawing the stack diagram in long answers** — KTU examiners award marks for **diagrammatic representation** of activation records. Always include a labeled stack frame in 7-mark answers.
> 6. **Confusing formal parameters with actual arguments** — Formal parameters are in the *definition*; actual arguments are in the *call*.
> 7. **Forgetting to declare a function before calling it** — Causes "implicit declaration" warnings in GCC; risky in modern C standards.
> 8. **Modifying a pass-by-value parameter and expecting the caller to see it** — A classic conceptual error worth 1–2 marks lost.

---

## 5.5 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

### Rapid Revision Checklist

- [ ] A **function** is a named, reusable block of statements that performs a specific task.
- [ ] A **function definition** has **five components**: return type, name, parameter list, body, and return statement.
- [ ] A **function call** invokes the function using `function_name(arguments);`.
- [ ] A **function prototype** is a declaration (signature only) used for forward declaration and type checking.
- [ ] In **pass by value**, a copy is passed; caller is unaffected.
- [ ] In **pass by address**, an address is passed via pointer; caller can be modified.
- [ ] C does **not** have true pass-by-reference — pointers simulate it.
- [ ] Arrays always decay to pointers when passed to functions.
- [ ] An **activation record (stack frame)** contains: return address, parameters, local variables, saved frame pointer.
- [ ] Formal parameters and actual arguments must match in **number, order, and compatible types**.
- [ ] The `void` keyword means "no value" (return type) or "no parameters" (in `(void)`).
- [ ] Prototypes should be placed in **header files** for multi-file projects.

### Quick-Reference Code Templates

```c
// Definition template
return_type function_name(type1 param1, type2 param2) {
    // body
    return value;
}

// Prototype template
return_type function_name(type1, type2);

// Call template
variable = function_name(arg1, arg2);

// Pass by value template
void byValue(int x) { /* changes to x are local */ }

// Pass by address template
void byAddress(int *x) { *x = newValue; /* persists */ }
```

### Mnemonic for Function Call Steps

> **"E-P-P-E-R-D-P-R"**
> 1. **E**valuate arguments
> 2. **P**ush arguments onto stack
> 3. **P**ush return address
> 4. **E**nter function body
> 5. **R**un statements
> 6. **D**eal with return value
> 7. **P**op stack frame
> 8. **R**esume caller

### Key Differences at a Glance

| Term | One-Line Definition |
|---|---|
| Function | Reusable named block of code |
| Definition | Full implementation with body |
| Call | Invocation of a function |
| Prototype | Signature-only forward declaration |
| Pass by Value | Copy of value is passed |
| Pass by Address | Address of variable is passed via pointer |
| Activation Record | Stack frame storing call-time data |
| Formal Parameter | Variable in function definition |
| Actual Argument | Expression passed during call |

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Recursion
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GXEST204 - PROGRAMMING IN C
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Recursion

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Recursion** is a programming technique in which a **function calls itself**, either **directly** or **indirectly**, to solve a problem. Each recursive call works on a **smaller sub-problem** of the original problem, until a **base case** (terminating condition) is reached.

In the C programming language (KTU GXEST204), recursion is implemented using **self-referential functions**. The C run-time system maintains a **call stack** to manage active function invocations, where every call pushes a new **stack frame** (activation record) containing parameters, local variables, and the return address.

### The Two Mandatory Pillars of a Recursive Function

| Pillar | Purpose | Consequence of Missing It |
|---|---|---|
| **Base Case** | Terminates recursion | **Infinite recursion** → Stack Overflow |
| **Recursive Case** | Reduces problem size; invokes the same function | Algorithm never converges |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Highlight:** Examiners specifically check whether the student has clearly stated *both* the base case and the recursive case. Marks are awarded only when both are unambiguously defined.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain-English Intuition

Imagine a set of **Russian Matryoshka (nesting) dolls**.

1. You pick up the *largest* doll and open it.
2. Inside, you find a *slightly smaller* doll — same shape, same type, just smaller.
3. You open that one, and find an even smaller one… and so on.
4. Eventually, you reach the **smallest, solid doll** that **cannot be opened** — this is your *terminating point*.
5. Now you start **closing them back up** in reverse order.

This is *exactly* how recursion works:
- The **outer doll** = the original function call with the *largest* problem.
- Each **inner doll** = a recursive call with a *smaller* problem.
- The **innermost solid doll** = the **base case**.
- **Closing the dolls in reverse** = the **unwinding phase** where values are returned and combined.

> A second classic analogy: a person standing between **two parallel mirrors** sees an infinite chain of reflections — each reflection *contains* the next, and the chain continues until it fades (the visual "base case").

## 1.3 Why Use Recursion? (Engineering Relevance)

- Naturally models problems with **self-similar structure** (trees, fractals, divide-and-conquer).
- Produces **elegant, compact** code for problems that are cumbersome iteratively (e.g., Tower of Hanoi, QuickSort, DFS).
- Foundational to advanced topics: **linked-list/tree traversals, dynamic programming, backtracking, compiler design (recursive descent parsers)**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of a Recursive Function (Operational Breakdown)

A well-formed recursive function in C contains **three structural elements**:

1. **Function Signature** — declares parameters, return type.
2. **Base Case(s)** — explicit conditional(s) that return a hard-coded value without further recursion.
3. **Recursive Case** — returns an expression that **combines** a simpler recursive call (with reduced input) using an operator.

### Worked Skeleton

```c
return_type recursive_function(parameters) {
    if (base_condition_is_true)        /* BASE CASE     */
        return trivial_value;          /* stops recursion */
    else
        return combine(                /* RECURSIVE CASE */
            some_operation,
            recursive_function(smaller_input)  /* self-call */
        );
}
```

---

## 2.2 Classification of Recursion (KTU High-Yield)

| Type | Definition | Canonical Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Direct Recursion** | A function invokes itself directly inside its own body. | `int f(){ ... f(); ... }` |
| **Indirect Recursion** | Function A calls B, and B (or a chain) eventually calls A. | `A() → B() → A()` |
| **Tail Recursion** | The recursive call is the **last statement** executed in the function. | Factorial (tail version) |
| **Non-Tail (Head) Recursion** | Operations remain to be performed **after** the recursive call returns. | Standard factorial, Fibonacci |
| **Tree Recursion** | A single call triggers **multiple** recursive calls (branching). | Fibonacci, Tower of Hanoi |
| **Linear Recursion** | Each call triggers **at most one** further recursive call. | Factorial, sum of digits |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Tail Recursion Optimization (TRO):** Modern compilers (GCC with `-O2`) can convert tail-recursive functions into iterative loops, eliminating stack growth. However, KTU examiners expect the **standard recursive form** unless explicitly asked for an iterative conversion.

---

## 2.3 How the Call Stack Manages Recursion

Every invocation of a function (recursive or otherwise) pushes a **stack frame** containing:
- **Parameters** (passed values)
- **Local variables**
- **Return address** (where to resume after the call completes)

When the base case returns, frames **pop in LIFO (Last-In-First-Out) order**, and each popped frame computes its final result using the returned value from its callee.

### Memory Cost Equation

For a recursion of depth $d$, the auxiliary space consumed is:

$$S_{aux} = O(d)$$

Because the call stack holds $d$ activation records simultaneously at peak depth.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Problem | Recurrence Relation | Base Case(s) | Closed-Form / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Factorial: $n!$ | $T(n) = n \cdot T(n-1)$ | $T(0) = T(1) = 1$ | $n! = \prod_{k=1}^{n} k$ |
| 2 | Sum of first $n$ naturals | $S(n) = n + S(n-1)$ | $S(0) = 0$ | $S(n) = \dfrac{n(n+1)}{2}$ |
| 3 | Fibonacci | $F(n) = F(n-1) + F(n-2)$ | $F(0) = 0,\ F(1) = 1$ | $F(n) = \dfrac{\phi^{n} - \psi^{n}}{\sqrt{5}}$ |
| 4 | Power: $x^{n}$ | $P(n) = x \cdot P(n-1)$ | $P(0) = 1$ | Use fast-exponentiation for $O(\log n)$ |
| 5 | Sum of digits | $D(n) = D(\lfloor n/10 \rfloor) + (n \bmod 10)$ | $D(0) = 0$ | Linear in $\log_{10} n$ |
| 6 | GCD (Euclidean) | $\gcd(a,b) = \gcd(b, a \bmod b)$ | $\gcd(a, 0) = a$ | $O(\log(\min(a,b)))$ |
| 7 | Tower of Hanoi moves | $H(n) = 2H(n-1) + 1$ | $H(1) = 1$ | $H(n) = 2^{n} - 1$ |
| 8 | Reverse a string | $R(s) = R(s_{rest}) + s[0]$ | empty string = "" | $O(n)$ time, $O(n)$ stack |

> **Legend:** $\phi = \dfrac{1+\sqrt{5}}{2}$ (golden ratio) and $\psi = \dfrac{1-\sqrt{5}}{2}$.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** For Fibonacci, naive recursion gives time complexity $O(2^{n})$ (exponential), not $O(n)$. Examiners often test whether students recognize this. Memoization or iteration reduces it to $O(n)$.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Applications

- **Operating Systems:** Recursive directory traversal (`ls -R`, `dir /S`).
- **Compilers:** Recursive-descent parsers, AST (Abstract Syntax Tree) construction.
- **Graphics:** Fractal generation (Sierpinski triangle, Mandelbrot set), ray tracing.
- **AI / Search:** Depth-First Search, minimax in game trees.
- **Data Structures:** Traversals and operations on trees, linked lists, graphs.
- **Mathematics:** Numerical methods (Newton–Raphson), combinatorial generation.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations, Trace Tables & Analytical Solutions

## 3.1 Example 1 — Factorial of a Number (Complete Derivation)

### Mathematical Definition (Recursive)

$$n! = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } n = 0 \text{ or } n = 1 \quad \text{(base case)} \\[4pt] n \times (n-1)! & \text{if } n > 1 \quad \text{(recursive case)} \end{cases}$$

### Step-by-Step C Program

```c
#include <stdio.h>

long long factorial(int n) {
    /* BASE CASE: terminating condition */
    if (n == 0 || n == 1)
        return 1;

    /* RECURSIVE CASE: self-call with reduced input */
    return n * factorial(n - 1);
}

int main(void) {
    int num = 4;
    printf("Factorial of %d = %lld\n", num, factorial(num));
    return 0;
}
```

### Exhaustive Trace for `factorial(4)`

The recursion tree below shows every activation and the resulting flow of values:

| Call # | Argument `n` | Action | Returned Value |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | `factorial(4)` | $4 \times \texttt{factorial(3)}$ → suspends | — |
| 2 | `factorial(3)` | $3 \times \texttt{factorial(2)}$ → suspends | — |
| 3 | `factorial(2)` | $2 \times \texttt{factorial(1)}$ → suspends | — |
| 4 | `factorial(1)` | **base case hit** → returns 1 | $1$ |
| — | unwind to call 3 | $2 \times 1$ | $2$ |
| — | unwind to call 2 | $3 \times 2$ | $6$ |
| — | unwind to call 1 | $4 \times 6$ | $24$ |

**Final Output:** `Factorial of 4 = 24`

### Mathematical Verification

$$4! = 4 \times 3 \times 2 \times 1 = 24 \quad \checkmark$$

---

## 3.2 Example 2 — Fibonacci Sequence

### Mathematical Definition

$$F(n) = \begin{cases} 0 & \text{if } n = 0 \\[2pt] 1 & \text{if } n = 1 \\[2pt] F(n-1) + F(n-2) & \text{if } n \geq 2 \end{cases}$$

### C Implementation

```c
#include <stdio.h>

int fibonacci(int n) {
    if (n == 0) return 0;        /* base case 1 */
    if (n == 1) return 1;        /* base case 2 */
    return fibonacci(n - 1) + fibonacci(n - 2);
}
```

### Trace for `fibonacci(4)`

| Call | Computation | Returns |
|---|---|---|
| `F(4)` | `F(3) + F(2)` | `2 + 1 = 3` |
| → `F(3)` | `F(2) + F(1)` | `1 + 1 = 2` |
| → → `F(2)` | `F(1) + F(0)` | `1 + 0 = 1` |
| → → `F(1)` | base case | `1` |
| → → `F(0)` | base case | `0` |
| → → `F(1)` | base case | `1` |
| → `F(2)` | `F(1) + F(0)` | `1 + 0 = 1` |

**Result:** `fibonacci(4) = 3`   →   Sequence: `0, 1, 1, 2, 3, 5, 8, 13, ...`

### Time Complexity Analysis

The number of calls $C(n)$ satisfies the recurrence:

$$C(n) = C(n-1) + C(n-2) + 1$$

Solving this yields the exponential bound:

$$C(n) = O(\phi^{n}) \approx O(1.618^{n})$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is **why** naive recursive Fibonacci is impractical for large $n$. The memoized/iterative version brings it down to $O(n)$ time and $O(1)$ space.

---

## 3.3 Example 3 — Sum of Digits of a Number

### Mathematical Recurrence

$$D(n) = \begin{cases} 0 & \text{if } n = 0 \\[2pt] D\!\left(\left\lfloor \dfrac{n}{10} \right\rfloor\right) + (n \bmod 10) & \text{if } n > 0 \end{cases}$$

### C Program

```c
#include <stdio.h>

int sumDigits(int n) {
    if (n == 0) return 0;                       /* base case */
    return sumDigits(n / 10) + (n % 10);        /* recursive case */
}
```

### Trace for `sumDigits(123)`

| Call | `n` | `n / 10` | `n % 10` | Returns |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | `123` | `12` | `3` | → suspends |
| 2 | `12`  | `1`  | `2` | → suspends |
| 3 | `1`   | `0`  | `1` | → suspends |
| 4 | `0`   | —    | —   | `0` (base) |
| unwind → 3 | | | | `0 + 1 = 1` |
| unwind → 2 | | | | `1 + 2 = 3` |
| unwind → 1 | | | | `3 + 3 = 6` |

**Result:** `sumDigits(123) = 6`   ✓   (since $1+2+3 = 6$)

---

## 3.4 Example 4 — Power Function ($x^{n}$) — Fast Exponentiation

### Naïve Recurrence (Linear)

$$P(x, n) = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } n = 0 \\[2pt] x \cdot P(x, n-1) & \text{if } n > 0 \end{cases}$$

Time: $O(n)$, Stack depth: $O(n)$.

### Efficient Recurrence (Logarithmic — *KTU favourite*)

$$P(x, n) = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } n = 0 \\[2pt] P(x, n/2) \cdot P(x, n/2) & \text{if } n \text{ even} \\[2pt] x \cdot P(x, n/2) \cdot P(x, n/2) & \text{if } n \text{ odd} \end{cases}$$

### C Implementation

```c
#include <stdio.h>

double power(double x, int n) {
    if (n == 0) return 1;                    /* base case */
    double half = power(x, n / 2);
    if (n % 2 == 0)
        return half * half;
    else
        return x * half * half;
}
```

### Trace for `power(2, 5)`

| Call | `n` | `half` | n even? | Returns |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 5 | → call 2 | odd | `2 * 2 * 2 = 8` |
| 2 | 2 | → call 3 | even | `2 * 2 = 4` |
| 3 | 1 | → call 4 | odd | `2 * 1 * 1 = 2` |
| 4 | 0 | base | — | `1` |

**Result:** `power(2, 5) = 32`   ✓   (since $2^{5} = 32$)

**Time Complexity:** The recurrence solves to $T(n) = T(n/2) + O(1) \Rightarrow O(\log_{2} n)$.

---

## 3.5 Example 5 — Tower of Hanoi (Classic KTU Long-Answer Problem)

### Problem Statement

Move $n$ disks from **Source (S)** to **Destination (D)** using **Auxiliary (A)**, obeying:
1. Only one disk moved at a time.
2. A larger disk may never sit atop a smaller one.

### Recursive Solution Logic

The genius of recursion is that the problem for $n$ disks is expressed **in terms of the same problem for $n-1$ disks**:

1. **Move top $n-1$ disks** from `S → A` (using `D` as auxiliary).
2. **Move the largest disk** from `S → D`.
3. **Move the $n-1$ disks** from `A → D` (using `S` as auxiliary).

### C Program

```c
#include <stdio.h>

void hanoi(int n, char S, char D, char A) {
    if (n == 1) {                              /* base case */
        printf("Move disk 1 from %c to %c\n", S, D);
        return;
    }
    hanoi(n - 1, S, A, D);                     /* step 1 */
    printf("Move disk %d from %c to %c\n", n, S, D); /* step 2 */
    hanoi(n - 1, A, D, S);                     /* step 3 */
}
```

### Trace for `hanoi(3, A, C, B)` (i.e., 3 disks, A→C using B)

The recursive unfolding produces exactly $2^{3} - 1 = 7$ moves:

| Step | Move |
|---|---|
| 1 | `A → C`  (disk 1) |
| 2 | `A → B`  (disk 2) |
| 3 | `C → B`  (disk 1) |
| 4 | `A → C`  (disk 3) |
| 5 | `B → A`  (disk 1) |
| 6 | `B → C`  (disk 2) |
| 7 | `A → C`  (disk 1) |

### Closed-Form Number of Moves

Derivation of $H(n) = 2^{n} - 1$ from the recurrence $H(n) = 2H(n-1) + 1$ with $H(1)=1$:

$$H(n) = 2H(n-1) + 1$$

Substitute $H(n-1) = 2H(n-2) + 1$:

$$H(n) = 2\bigl[2H(n-2) + 1\bigr] + 1 = 2^{2} H(n-2) + 2 + 1$$

Continue unrolling for $n-1$ steps:

$$H(n) = 2^{n-1} H(1) + \sum_{k=0}^{n-2} 2^{k} = 2^{n-1} \cdot 1 + (2^{n-1} - 1)$$

$$\therefore \boxed{H(n) = 2^{n} - 1}$$

Verification: $H(3) = 2^{3} - 1 = 7$ ✓

---

## 3.6 Example 6 — GCD Using Euclidean Algorithm

### Recurrence

$$\gcd(a, b) = \begin{cases} a & \text{if } b = 0 \\[2pt] \gcd(b,\ a \bmod b) & \text{if } b \neq 0 \end{cases}$$

### C Program

```c
#include <stdio.h>

int gcd(int a, int b) {
    if (b == 0) return a;                 /* base case */
    return gcd(b, a % b);                 /* recursive case */
}
```

### Trace for `gcd(48, 18)`

| Call | $a$ | $b$ | $a \bmod b$ | Returns |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 48 | 18 | 12 | suspends |
| 2 | 18 | 12 | 6  | suspends |
| 3 | 12 | 6  | 0  | suspends |
| 4 | 6  | 0  | —  | `6` (base) |

**Result:** `gcd(48, 18) = 6`   ✓
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Recursion Tree — Factorial(4)

The following Mermaid tree visualizes the **descent (calls)** and **ascent (returns)** phases for `factorial(4)`.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    call4["factorial 4 returns 24"]
    call3["factorial 3 returns 6"]
    call2["factorial 2 returns 2"]
    call1["factorial 1 returns 1 base case"]
    ret1["1"]
    ret2["2"]
    ret3["6"]
    ret4["24"]

    call4 --> call3
    call3 --> call2
    call2 --> call1
    call1 --> ret1
    ret1 --> ret2
    ret2 --> ret3
    ret3 --> ret4
```

---

## 4.2 Recursion Tree — Fibonacci(4) Showing Branching

The Fibonacci recursion is *binary* — each call spawns **two** further calls, forming a binary tree.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    f4["F of 4 returns 3"]
    f3a["F of 3 returns 2"]
    f2a["F of 2 returns 1"]
    f1a["F of 1 returns 1 base"]
    f0a["F of 0 returns 0 base"]
    f2b["F of 2 returns 1"]
    f1b["F of 1 returns 1 base"]
    f0b["F of 0 returns 0 base"]

    f4 --> f3a
    f4 --> f2b
    f3a --> f2a
    f3a --> f1a
    f2a --> f1a2["F of 1 returns 1"]
    f2a --> f0a
    f2b --> f1b
    f2b --> f0b
```

*(Simplified for clarity; the same call to `F(2)` may be invoked twice.)*

---

## 4.3 Stack Frame Evolution — `factorial(3)`

The diagram below depicts the **call stack** at maximum depth (just before the base case returns).

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph peak["Maximum Stack Depth for factorial 3"]
        dir1["TOP FRAME factorial 3 n equals 3 pending return 3 times factorial 2"]
        dir2["factorial 2 n equals 2 pending return 2 times factorial 1"]
        dir3["BOTTOM FRAME factorial 1 n equals 1 returns 1 BASE CASE"]
    end
    dir1 --> dir2
    dir2 --> dir3
```

After the base case returns `1`, frames pop **bottom-up**: `factorial(1)` returns `1` → `factorial(2)` returns `2` → `factorial(3)` returns `6`.

---

## 4.4 Block Diagram — Recursion Execution Pipeline (Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    entry["Entry Call main invokes f of n"]
    check{"Is base case n equals 0 or n equals 1"}
    basepath["Execute base case return 1"]
    recurse["Execute recursive case return n times f of n minus 1"]
    stack["Push new stack frame onto call stack"]
    compute["Compute n times returned value"]
    output["Final result returned to main"]

    entry --> check
    check -- YES --> basepath --> output
    check -- NO --> recurse --> stack --> recurse
    stack -- "frame unwinds" --> compute --> output
```

---

## 4.5 Comparison Table — Recursion vs Iteration

| Aspect | Recursion | Iteration |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | Function calls itself | Loop repeats a block |
| **State Storage** | Implicit on the call stack | Explicit loop variable |
| **Memory** | $O(d)$ stack space | $O(1)$ auxiliary |
| **Speed** | Function-call overhead | Generally faster |
| **Readability** | Elegant for tree/graph problems | Cleaner for linear scans |
| **Termination** | Base case | Loop condition |
| **Risk** | Stack overflow | Infinite loop |
| **Convertibility** | Tail-recursive forms can be converted | Cannot easily express tree recursion |
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper 2]**
**Define recursion. Differentiate between direct and indirect recursion with one example each.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Recursion** is a technique in which a function calls itself (directly or through a chain of other functions) to solve a problem that can be broken down into smaller instances of the same problem. Every recursive function must specify a **base case** (which terminates the recursion) and a **recursive case** (which invokes the function with a smaller input).

**Direct Recursion:** A function calls **itself** directly.

```c
void f() {
    f();   /* direct self-call */
}
```

**Indirect Recursion:** Function `A` calls function `B`, and `B` calls `A` (forming a cycle).

```c
void A();   /* prototype */
void B() { A(); }      /* B calls A */
void A() { B(); }      /* A calls B */
```

---

### Question A2
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**What is a base case? Why is it essential in recursive functions? What happens if it is missing?**

**Model Answer:**

> The **base case** is the terminating condition in a recursive function — the input value(s) for which the function returns a hard-coded result **without making any further recursive call**.
>
> It is **essential** because it halts the chain of self-invocations. Without a base case, the function would call itself indefinitely, each call consuming a new stack frame. This leads to **stack overflow** (a runtime error where the program runs out of stack memory) and eventual program termination. Hence, the base case is the **safety brake** of every recursive algorithm.

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU's End Semester Examination (ESE) pattern for Part B requires an **internal choice**: students must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B. Both questions below are modeled on KTU past papers.

---

### Question A — (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 / Module 3 Compulsory]**

**(a)** Write a recursive C function to compute the **factorial of a given non-negative integer**. Trace the execution for `factorial(5)` showing the contents of the call stack at each stage. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Write a recursive C function to compute the **n-th Fibonacci number**. Explain why the naive recursive implementation is computationally inefficient and state its time complexity. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — (a)

**Recursive Definition:**

$$n! = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } n \leq 1 \\ n \cdot (n-1)! & \text{if } n > 1 \end{cases}$$

**C Program:**

```c
#include <stdio.h>

long long factorial(int n) {
    if (n == 0 || n == 1)          /* BASE CASE */
        return 1;
    return n * factorial(n - 1);   /* RECURSIVE CASE */
}

int main(void) {
    int n = 5;
    printf("Factorial of %d = %lld\n", n, factorial(n));
    return 0;
}
```

**Trace Table for `factorial(5)`:**

| Call # | Argument `n` | Operation | Returned Value |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 5 | $5 \times \texttt{factorial(4)}$ | suspends |
| 2 | 4 | $4 \times \texttt{factorial(3)}$ | suspends |
| 3 | 3 | $3 \times \texttt{factorial(2)}$ | suspends |
| 4 | 2 | $2 \times \texttt{factorial(1)}$ | suspends |
| 5 | 1 | base case → returns 1 | $1$ |
| unwind | — | $2 \times 1 = 2$ | $2$ |
| unwind | — | $3 \times 2 = 6$ | $6$ |
| unwind | — | $4 \times 6 = 24$ | $24$ |
| unwind | — | $5 \times 24 = 120$ | $120$ |

**Output:** `Factorial of 5 = 120`

#### Model Solution — (b)

**Recursive C Function:**

```c
#include <stdio.h>

int fibonacci(int n) {
    if (n == 0) return 0;                         /* base case 1 */
    if (n == 1) return 1;                         /* base case 2 */
    return fibonacci(n - 1) + fibonacci(n - 2);   /* recursive case */
}
```

**Why It Is Inefficient — Explanation:**

The naive recursive Fibonacci re-computes the **same sub-problems** repeatedly. For instance, `F(2)` is computed twice when computing `F(4)`, three times for `F(5)`, and exponentially many times for larger `n`. The recursive tree grows like a binary tree, producing a massive number of redundant calls.

**Number of Calls Recurrence:**

$$C(n) = C(n-1) + C(n-2) + 1, \quad C(0)=C(1)=1$$

Solving yields:

$$C(n) = O(\phi^{n}) \approx O(2^{n})$$

Thus, even computing `F(50)` requires on the order of $2^{50} \approx 1.12 \times 10^{15}$ calls — completely infeasible.

**Remedies (briefly):**
- **Memoization** (top-down DP) → $O(n)$ time, $O(n)$ space.
- **Tabulation** (bottom-up DP) → $O(n)$ time, $O(1)$ space.
- **Matrix exponentiation** → $O(\log n)$ time.

---

### Question B — (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 / Module 3 Internal Choice]**

**(a)** Write a recursive C function to find the **sum of digits of a positive integer**. Provide the complete trace for the input `n = 4321`. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **Tower of Hanoi** problem. Write a recursive C program to solve it for `n = 3` disks and show the sequence of disk moves. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — (a)

**Recursive Definition:**

$$D(n) = \begin{cases} 0 & \text{if } n = 0 \\ D(\lfloor n/10 \rfloor) + (n \bmod 10) & \text{if } n > 0 \end{cases}$$

**C Program:**

```c
#include <stdio.h>

int sumDigits(int n) {
    if (n == 0) return 0;
    return sumDigits(n / 10) + (n % 10);
}

int main(void) {
    int n = 4321;
    printf("Sum of digits of %d = %d\n", n, sumDigits(n));
    return 0;
}
```

**Trace Table for `sumDigits(4321)`:**

| Call | `n` | `n / 10` | `n % 10` | Returns |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 4321 | 432 | 1 | suspends |
| 2 | 432  | 43  | 2 | suspends |
| 3 | 43   | 4   | 3 | suspends |
| 4 | 4    | 0   | 4 | suspends |
| 5 | 0    | —   | — | `0` (base) |
| unwind → 4 | — | — | — | `0 + 4 = 4` |
| unwind → 3 | — | — | — | `4 + 3 = 7` |
| unwind → 2 | — | — | — | `7 + 2 = 9` |
| unwind → 1 | — | — | — | `9 + 1 = 10` |

**Output:** `Sum of digits of 4321 = 10`

---

#### Model Solution — (b)

**Problem Statement:** Move $n$ disks from the **Source** peg to the **Destination** peg using one **Auxiliary** peg, with the rule that a larger disk may never rest on a smaller one.

**Recursive Strategy:**

$$H(n, S, D, A) = \begin{cases} \text{print move S → D} & \text{if } n = 1 \\ H(n-1, S, A, D) \\ \text{print move disk } n \text{ from S → D} \\ H(n-1, A, D, S) & \text{if } n > 1 \end{cases}$$

**C Program:**

```c
#include <stdio.h>

void hanoi(int n, char S, char D, char A) {
    if (n == 1) {
        printf("Move disk 1 from %c to %c\n", S, D);
        return;
    }
    hanoi(n - 1, S, A, D);
    printf("Move disk %d from %c to %c\n", n, S, D);
    hanoi(n - 1, A, D, S);
}

int main(void) {
    int n = 3;
    printf("Solution for %d disks:\n", n);
    hanoi(n, 'A', 'C', 'B');
    return 0;
}
```

**Output Trace for `hanoi(3, A, C, B)`:**

| Move # | From | To | Disk |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | A | C | 1 |
| 2 | A | B | 2 |
| 3 | C | B | 1 |
| 4 | A | C | 3 |
| 5 | B | A | 1 |
| 6 | B | C | 2 |
| 7 | A | C | 1 |

**Total moves = $2^{3} - 1 = 7$ ✓**

**Closed-Form Justification:**

From the recurrence $H(n) = 2H(n-1) + 1,\ H(1)=1$, repeated substitution yields:

$$H(n) = 2^{n} - 1$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Missing base case** → automatic 2-mark deduction. Always write the base case explicitly, even if "obvious".
> 2. **Incorrect return type** → if `factorial(20)` is computed using `int`, **integer overflow** occurs silently; declare `long long`.
> 3. **Fibonacci time complexity** → many students write $O(n)$ for the naive version — the correct answer is $O(2^{n})$; only memoized versions are $O(n)$.
> 4. **Confusing parameter roles in Tower of Hanoi** → label source/destination/auxiliary clearly; swapping them mid-trace loses marks.
> 5. **No trace table for Part B** → KTU examiners *expect* a step-by-step call-stack trace; omitting it can cost up to 3 marks.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Recursion (KTU Module 3)**

- ✅ **Recursion** = a function calling itself directly or indirectly.
- ✅ Every recursive function **must** have a **base case**; otherwise → stack overflow.
- ✅ Every recursive function **must** have a **recursive case** that reduces the problem size.
- ✅ **Direct recursion**: function calls itself.
- ✅ **Indirect recursion**: cycle of calls through two or more functions.
- ✅ **Tail recursion**: recursive call is the *last* operation (compiler-optimizable).
- ✅ **Non-tail recursion**: work remains after the recursive call (most common).
- ✅ **Tree recursion**: one call spawns multiple recursive calls (e.g., Fibonacci).
- ✅ **Call stack**: each invocation creates a new stack frame containing parameters, locals, and return address.
- ✅ **Space complexity** of recursion = $O(d)$ where $d$ is recursion depth.
- ✅ **Classic recursive algorithms:** Factorial, Fibonacci, Sum of Digits, Power, GCD, Tower of Hanoi, Reverse a string, Binary Search (recursive), Tree Traversals.
- ✅ **Tower of Hanoi**: minimum moves = $2^{n} - 1$.
- ✅ **Naive Fibonacci** is $O(2^{n})$ — exponential; use DP/iteration for efficiency.
- ✅ **GCD Euclidean algorithm**: $\gcd(a, b) = \gcd(b, a \bmod b)$, terminates at $\gcd(a, 0) = a$.
- ✅ **Convert tail recursion → loop** to save stack space.
- ✅ **Always** include a clear trace table in KTU exam answers for full marks.
- ✅ For very deep recursion, prefer iteration to avoid stack overflow.
- ✅ Recursion forms the conceptual foundation for **divide-and-conquer**, **dynamic programming**, **backtracking**, and **graph/tree algorithms** — all higher-semester KTU topics.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Passing Array to Function
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** GXEST204 - PROGRAMMING IN C > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Passing Array to Function # 📚 MODULE 3 — PASSING ARRAY TO FUNCTION ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus) In the C programming language, **passing an array to a function** is the mechanism by which the *base address* (the memory address of the first element) of an array is transferred to a function's formal parameter. According to the C standard (ISO/IEC 9899), array arguments are *not* copied into the function; instead, they undergo a process called **"array-to-pointer decay"**, where the array name implicitly converts to a pointer to its first element. As a consequence, any modifications performed by the called function are reflected back in the original array in the calling function — this is the default **call-by-reference** behavior for arrays in C. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Style):** > *"When an array is passed to a function, only the name of the array (i.e., the base address) is passed. The receiving function uses this base address to access and manipulate the elements of the original array."* ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Library Card" Intuition Imagine you own a large **bookshelf** with 100 books at your home. A friend wants to read those books. You have **two options**: | Method | Action | What Friend Can Do | Cost | |--------|--------|-------------------|------| | **Photocopying** (Call by Value) | Make 100 copies, hand them over | Friend can scribble on copies; originals safe | High memory cost | | **Sharing your home address** (Call by Reference — what C does with arrays) | Just tell the friend: *"My house is at #42, MG Road"* | Friend can visit and modify the original books | Zero extra memory | When you pass an array in C, you are essentially **handing over the house address** (the base address `&arr[0]`). The function does *not* receive a copy; it receives a *pointer* that lets it walk into the same memory and rearrange the books. This is why any change inside the function is permanent. ### 1.3 Key Terminology (KTU Board-Standard Vocabulary) - **Base Address**: The memory address of the first element, `&arr[0]`, which is equal to the array name itself, `arr`. - **Array Decay**: Implicit conversion of an array type to a pointer type during function calls. - **Formal Parameter**: The variable declared in the function header (e.g., `int x[]` or `int *x`). - **Actual Parameter / Argument**: The array name passed during the function call. - **Pointer Arithmetic**: The mechanism used inside the called function to traverse the array. - **const Qualifier**: A safety modifier preventing accidental modification of the received array. > [!NOTE] > 🔑 **Memorize This:** The name of an array, in most contexts, evaluates to the address of its first element. Therefore, `arr == &arr[0]` is universally true in C. --- # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Operational Mechanism — Step-by-Step When you write a function call like `display(marks, 5);`, the following sequence occurs in memory: 1. **Step 1 — Call Initiation**: The C compiler evaluates the array name `marks`. Through the *array-to-pointer decay* rule, `marks` is converted to a pointer of type `int *`, pointing to `marks[0]`. 2. **Step 2 — Argument Push**: Only this 8-byte (on 64-bit systems) address is pushed onto the call stack — **NOT the entire array contents**. 3. **Step 3 — Parameter Binding**: The formal parameter `int x[]` (or equivalently `int *x`) in the function header receives this address. The parameter becomes a *local pointer variable* inside the function. 4. **Step 4 — Element Access**: Inside the function, `x[i]` is interpreted by the compiler as `*(x + i)`, performing pointer arithmetic to walk through original memory. 5. **Step 5 — Modification Visibility**: Since the function is working on the *original* memory, any write operation like `x[i] = value;` permanently alters the caller's array. ## 2.2 Function Prototype Syntax (All Three Equivalent Forms) The C language offers three syntactically different but semantically identical ways to declare and define a function accepting an array: ```c /* Form 1: Using array notation (Most KTU-preferred) */ void display(int arr[], int size); /* Form 2: Using pointer notation (Compiler internally equivalent) */ void display(int *arr, int size); /* Form 3: Using array notation with explicit size (Size is IGNORED by compiler) */ void display(int arr[100], int size); ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **The size inside the brackets `[100]` in Form 3 is purely documentary.** The C compiler completely ignores it. The function will happily accept an array of any size. The actual size must be communicated via a separate `size` parameter. ## 2.3 Passing 1-D, 2-D, and Multi-Dimensional Arrays ### A. One-Dimensional Array Only the base address is passed. A separate `int n` parameter carries the length. ### B. Two-Dimensional Array The compiler must know the **number of columns** to compute row offsets correctly. Therefore, the column size is *mandatory* in the formal parameter. ```c /* Function prototype for 2D array */ void printMatrix(int mat[][4], int rows, int cols); ``` The first dimension (`[]`) may be left blank, but the second dimension (`[4]`) is **compulsory**. ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Concept | Formula / Rule | Notes | |---|---------|----------------|-------| | 1 | Array name equivalence | `arr == &arr[0]` | Both produce the base address | | 2 | Pointer arithmetic distance | `&arr[i] - &arr[0] = i` | Distance in *elements*, not bytes | | 3 | Address of `i`-th element | `&arr[i] = base_address + i × sizeof(type)` | In raw byte arithmetic | | 4 | Element access via pointer | `arr[i] ≡ *(arr + i) ≡ *(i + arr) ≡ i[arr]` | All four are identical in C | | 5 | Sizeof pitfall | `sizeof(arr)` in function → `sizeof(pointer)` | Loses array size info; always pass `n` | | 6 | Bytes per pointer | `sizeof(int*)` = **8 bytes** (64-bit) / **4 bytes** (32-bit) | Constant regardless of array size | | 7 | 2D row-major offset | `&a[i][j] = base + (i × cols + j) × sizeof(type)` | C stores 2D arrays in row-major form | | 8 | Function call by value | A copy of the **address** is sent | Not a copy of the array | | 9 | const-correct signature | `void print(const int arr[], int n)` | Function promises not to modify | | 10 | Returning array | Return type must be a `pointer`, not an array | Arrays cannot be C return values | > [!NOTE] > 📌 **Engineering Utility:** Array passing is the foundation of all numerical libraries in C (e.g., BLAS, LAPACK used in MATLAB/NumPy backends), signal processing routines (FFT, convolution), image processing kernels, and embedded firmware for sensor data handling. --- # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Examples ## 3.1 Derivation: Why `arr[i] ≡ *(arr + i)`? The C language specification states that subscript notation is *defined* in terms of pointer arithmetic. We will prove this equivalence step-by-step. **Given:** A 1-D array declared as `int arr[5] = {10, 20, 30, 40, 50};` starting at base address **B = 1000**. Assume `sizeof(int) = 4` bytes. **Derivation:** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Step 1: } & \text{The array name } arr \text{ decays to a pointer to its first element.} \\ & arr \equiv \&arr[0] \equiv B = 1000 \\[6pt] \text{Step 2: } & \text{By C language definition, subscript operator } [] \text{ is defined as:} \\ & arr[i] \equiv *(arr + i) \\[6pt] \text{Step 3: } & \text{The address of } arr[2] \text{ is computed as:} \\ & \&arr[2] = B + (2 \times \text{sizeof(int)}) = 1000 + (2 \times 4) = 1008 \\[6pt] \text{Step 4: } & \text{The value at this address is fetched using dereference:} \\ & *(1008) = 30 \quad \text{(the third element)} \\[6pt] \text{Step 5: } & \text{Confirming that } arr[2] = *(arr + 2) = 30. \end{aligned} $$ > **Conclusion:** Every element access inside a function receiving an array is essentially pointer arithmetic disguised as array indexing. ## 3.2 Worked Example 1 — Sum of Array Elements **Problem:** Write a C program using a function to compute the sum of `n` integers stored in an array. **Model Code:** ```c #include /* Function prototype — array notation form */ int computeSum(int arr[], int n); int main(void) { int marks[] = {85, 90, 78, 92, 88}; int n = 5; int total; total = computeSum(marks, n); /* Only base address is passed */ printf("Total = %d\n", total); printf("Average = %.2f\n", (float)total / n); return 0; } int computeSum(int arr[], int n) { int i, sum = 0; for (i = 0; i < n; i++) { sum = sum + arr[i]; /* Equivalent to sum += *(arr + i) */ } return sum; } ``` **Step-by-Step Trace:** | Call | Action | Stack Frame | |------|--------|-------------| | `computeSum(marks, 5)` | Pushes address of `marks[0]` (= 2000) and `n=5` | `arr` = 2000, `n` = 5 | | `i=0` | Access `*(2000 + 0×4) = 85` | sum = 85 | | `i=1` | Access `*(2000 + 1×4) = 90` | sum = 175 | | `i=2` | Access `*(2000 + 2×4) = 78` | sum = 253 | | `i=3` | Access `*(2000 + 3×4) = 92` | sum = 345 | | `i=4` | Access `*(2000 + 4×4) = 88` | sum = 433 | | Return | Returns 433 to `main` | total = 433 | **Output:** ``` Total = 433 Average = 86.60 ``` ## 3.3 Worked Example 2 — In-Place Modification (Call by Reference in Action) **Problem:** Write a function that doubles every element of an array. The changes must persist in `main`. ```c #include void doubleArray(int *ptr, int n) { int i; for (i = 0; i < n; i++) { *(ptr + i) = *(ptr + i) * 2; /* Permanent modification */ } } int main(void) { int data[] = {1, 2, 3, 4, 5}; int n = 5, i; doubleArray(data, n); /* Pass base address */ printf("Modified array: "); for (i = 0; i < n; i++) { printf("%d ", data[i]); } return 0; } ``` **Output:** ``` Modified array: 2 4 6 8 10 ``` > [!NOTE] > This program **does not return the array**. The array was modified *in place* because the function held a direct pointer to the original memory. This is a hallmark of array passing in C. ## 3.4 Worked Example 3 — Passing a 2-D Array to a Function ```c #include void printMatrix(int mat[][3], int rows, int cols) { int i, j; for (i = 0; i < rows; i++) { for (j = 0; j < cols; j++) { printf("%4d", mat[i][j]); } printf("\n"); } } int main(void) { int A[2][3] = {{1, 2, 3}, {4, 5, 6}}; printMatrix(A, 2, 3); return 0; } ``` **Output:** ``` 1 2 3 4 5 6 ``` **Memory Layout (Row-Major Order):** | Index | Address | Value | |-------|---------|-------| | `A[0][0]` | 2000 | 1 | | `A[0][1]` | 2004 | 2 | | `A[0][2]` | 2008 | 3 | | `A[1][0]` | 2012 | 4 | | `A[1][1]` | 2016 | 5 | | `A[1][2]` | 2020 | 6 | > **Verification of offset formula:** `&A[1][1] = 2000 + (1×3 + 1) × 4 = 2000 + 16 = 2016` ✓ ## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Common Pitfalls in Array Passing | # | Common Mistake | What Happens | Correct Practice | |---|----------------|--------------|------------------| | 1 | `int arr[10]; func(arr)` where `func(int a[])` forgets size | Function reads garbage beyond bound | Always pass `n` as parameter | | 2 | `int arr[5]; func(arr)` calling `func(int a[10])` | No error — size `[10]` is ignored | Treat bracket size as documentation only | | 3 | `int arr[3][3]; func(arr)` calling `func(int a[][])` | **Compilation Error** | Column size `[3]` is mandatory | | 4 | `return arr;` from a function with array return type | **Compilation Error** | Return a `pointer` instead | | 5 | `sizeof(arr)` inside function | Returns `sizeof(int*)` = 8 (not 20) | Compute size before call, pass as `n` | --- # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Memory & Stack Flow Diagram — How an Array Call Works ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph caller["CALLING FUNCTION (main)"] direction TB A["Stack Frame of main()"] A1["int marks[5] = {85, 90, 78, 92, 88}"] A2["marks = Base Address = 2000"] A3["int n = 5"] A1 --> A2 end subgraph stackcall["FUNCTION CALL MECHANISM"] direction TB S1["Pass marks (decays to int*)"] S2["Pass n (int value)"] S1 --> S3["Stack push: pointer 2000"] S2 --> S4["Stack push: 5"] end subgraph callee["CALLED FUNCTION (computeSum)"] direction TB B["Stack Frame of computeSum()"] B1["arr (int *) = 2000"] B2["n (int) = 5"] B3["Loop: sum += arr[i]"] B4["Return sum = 433"] B1 --> B3 B2 --> B3 B3 --> B4 end caller -->|call| stackcall stackcall --> callee callee -->|returns 433| caller ``` ## 4.2 Block Diagram — Three Equivalent Function Signatures ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph mainblock["Caller Side"] M["int data[5] = {1,2,3,4,5};
process(data, 5);"] end subgraph f1["Form 1: Array Notation"] F1["void process(int arr[], int n)
{ arr[i] accessed }"] end subgraph f2["Form 2: Pointer Notation"] F2["void process(int *arr, int n)
{ *(arr+i) accessed }"] end subgraph f3["Form 3: Sized Array (Ignored)"] F3["void process(int arr[100], int n)
{ arr[i] accessed }"] end mainblock --> F1 mainblock --> F2 mainblock --> F3 F1 -.->|"Compiler treats identically"| SHARED["All 3 forms receive a pointer
of type int*"] F2 -.-> SHARED F3 -.-> SHARED ``` ## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Array Passing in a Real Engineering Pipeline ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph sensors["Sensor Array (Hardware Layer)"] S1["Temp Sensor 1"] S2["Temp Sensor 2"] S3["Temp Sensor 3"] S4["Temp Sensor 4"] end S1 --> BUF["int readings[4] = {28, 30, 27, 29}"] S2 --> BUF S3 --> BUF S4 --> BUF BUF -->|passes base addr| AVERAGE["computeAverage()"] BUF -->|passes base addr| MAXMIN["findMaxMin()"] BUF -->|passes base addr| FILTER["applyFilter()"] BUF -->|passes base addr| DISPLAY["displayArray()"] AVERAGE --> OUT["Result on LCD / Serial Monitor"] MAXMIN --> OUT FILTER --> OUT DISPLAY --> OUT ``` > **Engineering Interpretation:** In embedded systems (KTU ECE/EEE students), a single sensor buffer is shared across multiple processing functions — averaging, peak detection, digital filtering, and display — by passing the same base address. This avoids memory duplication, which is critical on resource-constrained microcontrollers like 8051, Arduino, or STM32. --- # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Q: What happens when an array is passed to a function in C? Explain with an example.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** When an array is passed to a function in C, the array name decays into a pointer to its first element. Only the base address of the array is passed to the function, not a copy of the entire array. As a result, any modification made to the array elements inside the function will be reflected in the original array in the calling function. **Example:** ```c void modify(int x[], int n) { x[0] = 100; /* Modifies original array */ } int main() { int a[3] = {1, 2, 3}; modify(a, 3); printf("%d", a[0]); /* Prints 100 */ } ``` **Mark Split (1 + 1 + 1):** - 1 mark: Concept of address passing / decay - 1 mark: Effect of modification on original array - 1 mark: Working example --- ### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Q: Compare passing an array to a function vs passing a single variable. Mention the keyword "decay".** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** | Aspect | Single Variable | Array | |--------|----------------|-------| | What is passed | A *copy* of the value | The *base address* (pointer) | | Modification visible in caller? | No (call by value) | Yes (effectively call by reference) | | Memory transferred | `sizeof(type)` bytes | `sizeof(pointer)` = 8 bytes | | Concept of "decay"? | Not applicable | Array name decays to `int *` | **Mark Split (1 + 1 + 1):** Distinction in passing mechanism + memory concept + "decay" explanation. --- ## 5.2 📝 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Part B questions carry **internal choice**. Solve **EITHER** Question A **OR** Question B. Each Part B question has two sub-parts: **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**. --- ### ✍️ QUESTION A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Q: (a)** Explain with a neat diagram how a one-dimensional array is passed to a function in C. What is "array decay"? Why must we pass the size as a separate parameter? **[7 Marks]** **(b)** Write a C program to read an integer array of size `N` and find the **largest** and **second largest** element using a user-defined function. Display the result in `main`. **[7 Marks]** #### Model Solution (a) — 7 Marks **Diagram (3 marks):** ``` main() memory: computeSum() memory: +-----------+ +-----------+ | marks[0] |<--2000--| arr (ptr) | | marks[1] | +-----------+ | marks[2] | | n = 5 | | marks[3] | +-----------+ | marks[4] | | i, sum | +-----------+ +-----------+ ``` **Array Decay Explanation (2 marks):** The expression `marks` used as a function argument is implicitly converted from type `int[5]` to `int *`. The numerical value of the pointer is the address of `marks[0]`. This implicit conversion is called **array-to-pointer decay** and is mandated by the C standard. **Need for size parameter (2 marks):** Inside the function, the parameter `arr` is a *pointer*, and `sizeof(arr)` returns only the size of a pointer (8 bytes), not the array. Therefore, the function has no way to know how many elements exist. We must pass `n` explicitly so the loop bounds can be set correctly. #### Model Solution (b) — 7 Marks ```c #include void findTwoLargest(int arr[], int n, int *largest, int *second) { int i; if (arr[0] > arr[1]) { *largest = arr[0]; *second = arr[1]; } else { *largest = arr[1]; *second = arr[0]; } for (i = 2; i < n; i++) { if (arr[i] > *largest) { *second = *largest; *largest = arr[i]; } else if (arr[i] > *second && arr[i] != *largest) { *second = arr[i]; } } } int main(void) { int N, i, largest, second; int data[100]; printf("Enter N: "); scanf("%d", &N); printf("Enter %d elements:\n", N); for (i = 0; i < N; i++) { scanf("%d", &data[i]); } findTwoLargest(data, N, &largest, &second); printf("Largest = %d\n", largest); printf("Second Largest = %d\n", second); return 0; } ``` **Sample Run:** ``` Enter N: 5 Enter 5 elements: 12 45 7 89 23 Largest = 89 Second Largest = 45 ``` **Mark Distribution (b):** - 2 marks: Correct function signature with array & size - 2 marks: Correct algorithm to track largest / second largest - 2 marks: Proper loop and conditional logic - 1 mark: Correct `main` function and output format --- ### ✍️ QUESTION B (Choice 2) — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Q: (a)** Differentiate between passing a 1-D array and a 2-D array to a function in C. Why is the **column size** mandatory in 2-D array parameters? **[7 Marks]** **(b)** Write a C program using a function to add two 3×3 matrices. The matrices should be read in `main`, the addition should be performed in the function `addMatrices()`, and the result should be displayed using another function `displayMatrix()`. **[7 Marks]** #### Model Solution (a) — 7 Marks | Feature | 1-D Array | 2-D Array | |---------|-----------|-----------| | Function signature | `void f(int a[], int n)` | `void f(int a[][C], int r, int c)` | | Size info needed | Length `n` | Rows `r` and **columns `C`** (mandatory) | | Decays to | `int *` | `int (*)[C]` (pointer to row array) | | Reason for mandatory column | N/A | Compiler needs column count to compute `a[i][j]` offset | **Why Column Size is Mandatory (3 marks):** To compute the address of element `a[i][j]`, C uses the row-major formula: $$ \text{Address of } a[i][j] = \text{Base} + (i \times \text{cols} + j) \times \text{sizeof(type)} $$ If `cols` is unknown, the compiler cannot translate `a[i][j]` into the correct pointer arithmetic. Hence, the column size **must** be a compile-time constant in the function parameter. #### Model Solution (b) — 7 Marks ```c #include void addMatrices(int A[][3], int B[][3], int C[][3], int n) { int i, j; for (i = 0; i < n; i++) { for (j = 0; j < n; j++) { C[i][j] = A[i][j] + B[i][j]; } } } void displayMatrix(int M[][3], int n) { int i, j; for (i = 0; i < n; i++) { for (j = 0; j < n; j++) { printf("%5d", M[i][j]); } printf("\n"); } } int main(void) { int A[3][3], B[3][3], C[3][3]; int i, j; printf("Enter elements of Matrix A (3x3):\n"); for (i = 0; i < 3; i++) for (j = 0; j < 3; j++) scanf("%d", &A[i][j]); printf("Enter elements of Matrix B (3x3):\n"); for (i = 0; i < 3; i++) for (j = 0; j < 3; j++) scanf("%d", &B[i][j]); addMatrices(A, B, C, 3); printf("Resultant Matrix C = A + B:\n"); displayMatrix(C, 3); return 0; } ``` **Sample Run:** ``` Enter elements of Matrix A (3x3): 1 2 3 4 5 6 7 8 9 Enter elements of Matrix B (3x3): 9 8 7 6 5 4 3 2 1 Resultant Matrix C = A + B: 10 10 10 10 10 10 10 10 10 ``` **Mark Distribution (b):** - 2 marks: Correct function prototypes with proper column size `[3]` - 2 marks: Correct addition logic using nested loops - 2 marks: Correct `displayMatrix` implementation - 1 mark: Input/output and overall program flow --- > [!WARNING] > ⚠️ **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:** > 1. **Forgetting column size in 2-D array parameters** — `int a[][]` causes a *compilation error*. Always specify the second dimension. > 2. **Confusing `int arr[]` with `int *arr` in sizeof** — `sizeof(arr)` inside a function returns pointer size, not array size. KTU examiners frequently test this. > 3. **Trying to return an array from a function** — `int func() { int a[5]; return a; }` is **illegal**. Use pointer return with caution (avoid dangling pointers). > 4. **Mixing up `arr[i]` and `&arr[i]`** — Only the *value* of elements should be passed for call-by-value behavior on individual elements. Passing `&arr[i]` passes a *single element's address*. > 5. **Writing `void f(int a[100], int n)` and expecting size enforcement** — The `100` is silently ignored by the compiler. > 6. **Forgetting `#include `** — A surprisingly common deduction (½–1 mark lost) in KTU valuation. --- ## 5.3 🧠 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST | # | Key Takeaway | Status | |---|--------------|--------| | 1 | In C, `arr == &arr[0]` — array name IS the base address | ✅ | | 2 | Array passing passes only the **base address** (8 bytes), not a copy | ✅ | | 3 | The decay rule converts `int[5]` → `int *` automatically at function call | ✅ | | 4 | `int a[]` and `int *a` in function parameters are **identical** to the compiler | ✅ | | 5 | The size inside `[ ]` in function parameter is **ignored** by the compiler | ✅ | | 6 | For 2-D arrays, the **column size is mandatory** for pointer arithmetic | ✅ | | 7 | Always pass array length `n` as a **separate parameter** | ✅ | | 8 | Modifications inside the function **persist** in the caller's array | ✅ | | 9 | Use `const int arr[]` to **protect** the array from accidental modification | ✅ | | 10 | `sizeof(arr)` inside a function gives pointer size, **not** array size | ✅ | | 11 | You **cannot** return an array from a function — only a pointer | ✅ | | 12 | The element access `arr[i]` is defined as `*(arr + i)` in the C standard | ✅ | | 13 | 2-D arrays are stored in **row-major order** in memory | ✅ | | 14 | Address formula: `&a[i][j] = base + (i × cols + j) × sizeof(type)` | ✅ | | 15 | This is the **foundation of all numerical libraries** (BLAS, NumPy internals) | ✅ | > 🎯 **Final Exam Tip:** KTU examiners love questions that combine `const` correctness, the `sizeof` pitfall, and 2-D array column-size rules. Memorize the equivalence `arr[i] ≡ *(arr + i)` — it appears in at least 30% of array-related questions on KTU papers.

topics.title: Macros - Defining and Calling Macros
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GXEST204 - PROGRAMMING IN C
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Macros - Defining and Calling Macros

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the **C Programming Language**, a **Macro** is a fragment of code that has been given a name by the programmer. Whenever that name is used in the program, the **C Preprocessor** (a text substitution tool that runs *before* the actual compilation) replaces the macro invocation with the macro's body. This substitution mechanism is governed by the `#define` preprocessor directive and is formally classified by the ISO C Standard as part of **"Conditional and Inclusion Directives"** within the *Preprocessing Directives* phase of translation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition to Memorize:** A macro is a **name given to a piece of code** using the `#define` preprocessor directive. It is a pure text-substitution mechanism handled by the **Preprocessor** (not the compiler), which conceptually operates in translation phases 1–4 of the C Standard before actual compilation (translation phase 7) begins.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (Plain English)

Think of a **macro** like a **"shortcut" or "nickname"** that you create for a longer piece of text.

**Real-world analogy:** Imagine you are writing a letter and at the top you write:
> *"ASAP = As Soon As Possible"*

Now, every time you type **"ASAP"** in the rest of the letter, your reader (who already knows the definition) automatically substitutes it with "As Soon As Possible". You didn't change the meaning of the word — you just made typing faster and the document more readable.

The C **preprocessor** works exactly like this:
1. **You define** a shortcut (`#define PI 3.14159`).
2. **You "call" (use)** the shortcut anywhere in your code (`area = PI * r * r`).
3. **Before the compiler even sees the code**, the preprocessor *physically replaces* every occurrence of `PI` with `3.14159`. The compiler never even knows `PI` existed!

> [!NOTE]
> **Crucial Insight:** A macro is **not a function call**, and it is **not a variable**. It is **purely textual replacement** done by the preprocessor. The compiler sees the *expanded* code, not the original.

## 1.3 Types of Macros in C

KTU 2024 syllabus classifies macros into two fundamental categories:

| Macro Type | Syntax Pattern | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| **Object-Like Macro** | `#define NAME replacement` | Defines a constant or a piece of code with no parameters |
| **Function-Like Macro** | `#define NAME(args) replacement` | Defines a parameterized code fragment that *looks* like a function call |

## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standards Mentioned

- **ANSI C / ISO C9899 Standard** — Governs the formal definition of preprocessor behavior.
- **Translation Phase 3** — The exact phase where macro expansion occurs (per ISO C standard).
- **No standard physical constants** are defined by default, but commonly used ones like `PI = 3.14159` and `E = 2.71828` are typically created by the programmer.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 The C Preprocessor Pipeline (Why and How)

Before the C compiler produces any machine code, your source file passes through **8 translation phases** as defined by the ISO C standard. Macros operate in **Phase 3 and Phase 4**:

1. **Phase 1** — Physical source file characters are mapped to source character set.
2. **Phase 2** — Lines are spliced (backslash-newline removed).
3. **Phase 3** — The source file is decomposed into **preprocessing tokens** and **sequences of white-space characters**. Each comment is replaced by one space character. **Macro invocations are expanded** at this stage.
4. **Phase 4** — `#include` directives are processed, and macro expansion continues recursively.
5. **Phases 5–7** — Syntax/semantic checking, translation to assembly.
6. **Phase 8** — Linking.

> [!TIP]
> **Why does this matter for KTU exams?** Because if asked "Which phase expands macros?", the correct answer is **Phase 3 (and 4)** of translation, not the compiler itself.

## 2.2 Object-Like Macros — Deep Dive

### 2.2.1 Syntax
```c
#define IDENTIFIER replacement-list
```

The `#define` directive tells the preprocessor: *"Every time you see `IDENTIFIER` followed by something that is **not** a parameter parenthesis, replace it with `replacement-list`."*

### 2.2.2 Defining Symbolic Constants (Most Common Use)
```c
#define PI        3.14159
#define MAX_SIZE  100
#define NEWLINE   '\n'
```

### 2.2.3 Defining Code Snippets
```c
#define PRINT_HELLO  printf("Hello, World!\n");
```

> [!WARNING]
> Trailing semicolons in macros are a classic source of bugs. If used inside an `if` without braces, the semicolon becomes a *null statement*, breaking logic. KTU examiners often test this.

## 2.3 Function-Like Macros — Deep Dive

### 2.3.1 Syntax
```c
#define IDENTIFIER(arg1, arg2, ...) replacement-list
```

The left parenthesis **must immediately** follow the macro name (no space allowed). This distinguishes a function-like macro from an object-like macro.

### 2.3.2 Defining a Function-Like Macro
```c
#define SQUARE(x)   ((x) * (x))
#define MAX(a, b)   ((a) > (b) ? (a) : (b))
#define PRINT_INT(x) printf("Value = %d\n", (x))
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Parentheses Rule (Critical for KTU):** Always wrap the **entire macro body** in parentheses **AND** wrap **each argument** in parentheses. This prevents the famous operator-precedence bug:
> ```c
> #define SQUARE(x) x * x
> int y = SQUARE(2 + 3);  // Expands to: 2 + 3 * 2 + 3 = 11 ❌ (Expected: 25)
> #define SQUARE(x) ((x) * (x))
> int y = SQUARE(2 + 3);  // Expands to: ((2 + 3) * (2 + 3)) = 25 ✅
> ```

## 2.4 Advanced Macro Operators

### 2.4.1 The Stringification Operator (`#`)
The `#` operator, when placed before a macro parameter, converts the argument into a **string literal** (wrapped in double quotes) *at the point of expansion*.

```c
#define PRINT_VAR(x)  printf(#x " = %d\n", x)

int age = 20;
PRINT_VAR(age);   // Expands to: printf("age" " = %d\n", age);
                  // Output: age = 20
```

### 2.4.2 The Token-Pasting Operator (`##`)
The `##` operator concatenates two tokens into a single token during macro expansion. It is widely used to build identifiers programmatically.

```c
#define CONCAT(a, b)  a##b

int xy = 100;
printf("%d", CONCAT(x, y));   // Expands to: printf("%d", xy);  → Output: 100
```

### 2.4.3 The Variadic Macro (`...` and `__VA_ARGS__`)
C99 introduced variadic macros for variable argument lists.

```c
#define LOG(fmt, ...)  printf("[LOG] " fmt "\n", __VA_ARGS__)

LOG("Value: %d", 42);   // Expands to: printf("[LOG] " "Value: %d" "\n", 42);
```

> [!NOTE]
> For compilers that pre-date C99 variadic support, the **GCC extension** `args...` (with a named parameter before `...`) is used:
> ```c
> #define LOG(fmt, args...)  printf("[LOG] " fmt "\n", args)
> ```

## 2.5 Calling (Invoking) a Macro

A macro is "called" simply by **using its name in the source code**. There is no special call syntax — the preprocessor performs substitution **everywhere** the macro name appears in tokens, **after** its definition.

**Example showing full definition + invocation:**
```c
#include <stdio.h>

#define PI       3.14159
#define SQUARE(x)  ((x) * (x))
#define AREA(r)    (PI * SQUARE(r))

int main(void) {
    double radius = 5.0;
    printf("Area = %.2f\n", AREA(radius));
    return 0;
}
```

**After preprocessor expansion (conceptually):**
```c
int main(void) {
    double radius = 5.0;
    printf("Area = %.2f\n", (3.14159 * ((radius) * (radius))));
    return 0;
}
```

## 2.6 Predefined Macros (Standard Library)

The C standard provides several built-in macros that the preprocessor automatically defines:

| Macro | Meaning | Example Output |
|---|---|---|
| `__FILE__` | Current source file name as a string | `"main.c"` |
| `__LINE__` | Current line number as an integer | `42` |
| `__DATE__` | Compilation date | `"Mar 15 2024"` |
| `__TIME__` | Compilation time | `"14:30:00"` |
| `__func__` | Current function name (C99) | `"main"` |
| `__STDC__` | `1` if compiler conforms to ISO C | `1` |

## 2.7 KTU High-Yield Comparison Table: Macros vs Functions

| Feature | Macro | Function |
|---|---|---|
| **Handled by** | Preprocessor (Phase 3) | Compiler (Phase 7) |
| **Execution** | Text substitution (no call) | Actual function call with stack frame |
| **Speed** | Faster (no call overhead) | Slower (call/return overhead) |
| **Type checking** | ❌ None | ✅ Yes |
| **Debugging** | Harder (sees expanded code) | Easier |
| **Side effects** | ❌ Arguments may be evaluated multiple times | ✅ Arguments evaluated once |
| **Code size** | May bloat (inlined at every call) | One copy in memory |
| **Recursion** | ❌ Not possible | ✅ Yes |

## 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

Macros are heavily used in:

- **Embedded Systems Programming:** Defining hardware register addresses (`#define PORTA (*(volatile uint8_t *)0x4000)`).
- **Operating Systems Kernels (e.g., Linux):** The kernel is *built* largely from macros (`container_of`, `likely`, `unlikely`).
- **Assertion Libraries:** `assert()` itself is implemented as a macro so it can be disabled by `#define NDEBUG`.
- **Conditional Compilation:** `#ifdef DEBUG` blocks control whether debugging code is compiled.
- **Cross-Platform Code:** Macros detect OS/compiler and include platform-specific code.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Matrix: Macro Expansion Walkthrough

> [!NOTE]
> This section demonstrates the *exact* step-by-step textual expansion the preprocessor performs. Every intermediate stage is shown explicitly.

### 3.1.1 Problem: Trace the expansion of a nested macro

**Original Source Code:**
```c
#include <stdio.h>

#define PI          3.14159
#define SQUARE(x)   ((x) * (x))
#define AREA(r)     (PI * SQUARE(r))

int main(void) {
    float r = 2.0;
    float a = AREA(r);
    printf("Area = %f\n", a);
    return 0;
}
```

### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Preprocessor Expansion

**Step 1 — Identify the macro call:**
The preprocessor scans the program and finds the first use of a defined macro identifier.

$$\text{Target Expression: } a = \text{AREA}(r)$$

**Step 2 — Look up the macro definition for `AREA`:**

$$\text{Definition found: } \text{\#define AREA}(r) \;\; (\text{PI} * \text{SQUARE}(r))$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the actual argument `r` into the body:**

$$\text{After substitution: } a = (\text{PI} * \text{SQUARE}(r))$$

*(Note: The parameter `r` is replaced textually with the literal argument `r`. The parentheses around the body are preserved.)*

**Step 4 — Recursively expand any sub-macros in the body:**

The body contains `PI` and `SQUARE(r)`, both of which are defined macros. The preprocessor **rescans** the result.

**Step 4a — Expand `PI`:**
$$\text{PI} \rightarrow 3.14159$$
$$\text{Partial result: } a = (3.14159 * \text{SQUARE}(r))$$

**Step 4b — Expand `SQUARE(r)`:**
$$\text{SQUARE}(r) \rightarrow ((r) * (r))$$
$$\text{Final result: } a = (3.14159 * ((r) * (r)))$$

**Step 5 — Final code passed to the compiler:**

```c
int main(void) {
    float r = 2.0;
    float a = (3.14159 * ((r) * (r)));
    printf("Area = %f\n", a);
    return 0;
}
```

**Step 6 — Runtime evaluation:**
$$a = 3.14159 \times (2.0 \times 2.0)$$
$$a = 3.14159 \times 4.0$$
$$a = 12.56636$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Takeaway for KTU:** The preprocessor does **NOT** evaluate the math. It only substitutes text. The actual arithmetic is performed by the compiled code at runtime.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Derivation: The Double-Evaluation Problem

### 3.2.1 Problem Statement
Demonstrate why naive macros with side-effects produce incorrect results.

**Naive Macro:**
```c
#define INC(x)  ((x) + 1)
```

**Invocation:**
```c
int a = 5;
int b = INC(a++);
```

### 3.2.2 Step-by-Step Trace

**Step 1 — Substitute the argument `a++` into the body:**

$$\text{INC}(a++) \rightarrow ((a++) + 1)$$

*(Here, `a++` is the argument `x`. The preprocessor copies it verbatim — it does not know it has a side effect.)*

**Step 2 — Compiler now sees the expanded expression:**

$$b = (a++) + 1$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate `a++`:**
- The current value of `a` (which is `5`) is yielded for the addition.
- The side effect of `++` increments `a` **after** its value is used.

$$\text{Expression evaluates to } 5 + 1 = 6$$
$$\text{After evaluation, } a = 6$$
$$\text{Therefore, } b = 6$$

**Step 4 — What the programmer probably *expected*:**
The programmer probably thought `INC(a++)` would mean "increment `a` first, then assign to `b`". They likely expected `b = 6` and `a = 6`. By coincidence, this is what happened here, but the result is **unpredictable** in general.

### 3.2.3 A Worse Example

```c
#define SQR(x)  (x * x)
int a = 3;
int b = SQR(a++);
```

**Expansion:** `b = (a++ * a++)`

**Result:** This invokes **undefined behavior** because `a` is modified twice between two sequence points. Different compilers may produce different results (e.g., 9, 12, 16, etc.). This is a **classic KTU viva question** and a frequent source of marks lost in exams.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Macro vs Function Performance

| Aspect | Macro `SQUARE(x)` | Function `square(x)` |
|---|---|---|
| **Source line** | `#define SQUARE(x) ((x)*(x))` | `int square(int x){return x*x;}` |
| **Call syntax** | `y = SQUARE(5);` | `y = square(5);` |
| **After preprocessing** | `y = ((5)*(5));` | *(unchanged)* |
| **Compiler output** | Computed inline at call site | Function call instruction + return |
| **Cycle count (typical)** | ~1 cycle (single `imul`) | ~10–20 cycles (call/ret + push/pop) |
| **Code size impact** | Expands at every call site → may bloat binary | One copy in `.text` section |
| **Type safety** | Accepts any type (int, float, double) | Only the declared type |

---

## 3.4 Worked Example: Building a `DEBUG` Logger with Stringification

**Goal:** Print both the variable name and its value.

**Source Code:**
```c
#include <stdio.h>

#define DEBUG_PRINT(x)  printf(#x " = %d\n", x)

int main(void) {
    int count = 42;
    int limit = 100;
    DEBUG_PRINT(count);
    DEBUG_PRINT(limit);
    DEBUG_PRINT(count + limit);
    return 0;
}
```

**Step-by-step expansion:**

| Source Line | After `#` Stringification | After Macro Expansion |
|---|---|---|
| `DEBUG_PRINT(count);` | `"count"` | `printf("count" " = %d\n", count);` |
| `DEBUG_PRINT(limit);` | `"limit"` | `printf("limit" " = %d\n", limit);` |
| `DEBUG_PRINT(count + limit);` | `"count + limit"` | `printf("count + limit" " = %d\n", count + limit);` |

**Runtime Output:**
```
count = 42
limit = 100
count + limit = 142
```

> [!TIP]
> Notice the clever use of the C string-literal concatenation rule: `"a" "b"` is treated by the compiler as the single string `"ab"`. This is why the `#x` string and the format string `" = %d\n"` combine seamlessly.

---

## 3.5 Worked Example: Token-Pasting for Register Access

In embedded C (which KTU ECE/EEE students often encounter), `##` is used to build hardware register names dynamically.

```c
#define SET_BIT(reg, bit)   (reg |= (1U << bit))
#define PORT(port, pin)     PORT##port##_PIN##pin

// Define port-pin combinations
SET_BIT(PORT(A, 5), 3);
```

**Expansion of `PORT(A, 5)`:**
$$\text{PORT}(A, 5) \rightarrow \text{PORT} \#\# A \#\# \_ \text{PIN} \#\# 5 \rightarrow \text{PORTA\_PIN5}$$

**Full expansion:**
$$\text{SET\_BIT}(\text{PORTA\_PIN5}, 3) \rightarrow \text{PORTA\_PIN5} \, \vert= \, (1U << 3)$$

This is the foundation of HAL (Hardware Abstraction Layer) libraries in STM32, Arduino, etc.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The C Compilation Pipeline — Where Macros Live

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Source File main.c] --> B[Phase 1: Trigraph Replacement]
    B --> C[Phase 2: Line Splicing]
    C --> D[Phase 3: Tokenization AND Macro Expansion]
    D --> E[Phase 4: Include Directive Processing]
    E --> F[Phase 5: String Literal Concatenation]
    F --> G[Phase 6: Translation to Assembly]
    G --> H[Phase 7: Linking]
    H --> I[Executable Binary]

    subgraph M[Preprocessor Phases 3 and 4]
        D
        E
    end

    K[define Directive in Code] -->|Triggers| D
    L[Macro Invocation NAME] -->|Replaced| D
```

**Reading the diagram:** The macro definitions (`#define` lines) and macro invocations are both consumed by **Phase 3** of translation. The preprocessor outputs a *new* source file where every macro call has been textually replaced. The compiler proper (Phases 6–7) never sees the original macros.

---

## 4.2 Macro Expansion Algorithm (Block-Level Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S[Start: Scan Token Stream] --> Q{Is next token a macro name?}
    Q -- No --> N[Pass token through unchanged] --> S
    Q -- Yes --> T{Is it function-like or object-like?}
    T -- Object-like --> O[Replace with replacement-list] --> R
    T -- Function-like --> A[Match opening parenthesis next] --> B[Parse comma-separated arguments] --> C[Substitute each argument textually into body] --> R{Does body contain more macros?}
    R -- Yes --> S
    R -- No --> X[Output expanded token]
    X --> S
    S --> Z[End of file reached]
```

**Explanation of the loop:** The preprocessor does *not* expand a macro only once. It **rescans** the result repeatedly until no more expansions are possible. This is why nested macros (like `AREA` containing `SQUARE`) work correctly.

---

## 4.3 Macro vs Function Call Stack (Architectural Comparison)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
```
