```mermaid
    subgraph MacroPath[Macro Call Path]
        M1[Caller Code] -->|Token Substitution| M2[Inlined Code at Same Address]
        M2 --> M3[Result Computed Inline]
    end

    subgraph FunctionPath[Function Call Path]
        F1[Caller Code] -->|CALL instruction| F2[Push Return Address]
        F2 --> F3[Jump to Function Body]
        F3 --> F4[Function Body Executes]
        F4 -->|Return value via EAX or register| F5[Return to Caller]
    end
```

**Architectural implication:** A macro is **zero-cost** at runtime — there is no call, no return, and no stack frame. A function call involves at minimum a `CALL` instruction, saving/restoring the return address, and (usually) a `RET` instruction.

---

## 4.4 Macro Category Hierarchy

```mermaid
graph TD
    Macro[Macros in C] --> ObjLike[Object-Like Macros]
    Macro --> FuncLike[Function-Like Macros]
    Macro --> Predef[Predefined Standard Macros]

    ObjLike --> SymConst[Symbolic Constants e.g. PI]
    ObjLike --> CodeFrag[Code Fragments e.g. PRINT_HELLO]

    FuncLike --> Simple[Simple Parameterized e.g. SQUARE]
    FuncLike --> Advanced[Advanced with Operators]
    Advanced --> Stringify[Stringification Operator hash]
    Advanced --> Paste[Token Pasting Operator hashhash]
    Advanced --> Variadic[Variadic Macros with VA_ARGS]

    Predef --> FILE[underscore FILE underscore]
    Predef --> LINE[underscore LINE underscore]
    Predef --> DATE[underscore DATE underscore]
    Predef --> TIME[underscore TIME underscore]
    Predef --> FUNC[underscore func underscore]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 3]`
**Q: What is a macro in C? Differentiate between object-like and function-like macros with one example each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **macro** in C is a named piece of code defined using the `#define` preprocessor directive. The preprocessor performs textual substitution of the macro name with its definition *before* compilation, so the compiler never sees the original macro syntax.

**Object-like macro** — has no parameters; it is a simple name-to-text substitution.

```c
#define PI 3.14159
```

**Function-like macro** — accepts a parameter list (parentheses immediately after the name); the parameters are substituted textually into the body.

```c
#define SQUARE(x)  ((x) * (x))
```

The key difference is that function-like macros take arguments and allow parameterization, whereas object-like macros are essentially named constants.

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 3]`
**Q: Explain the role of the `#` (stringification) and `##` (token-pasting) operators in C macros with examples.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The `#` and `##` operators are only meaningful inside the body of a function-like macro.

- **`#` (Stringification):** Converts its argument into a string literal by surrounding it with double quotes at the point of macro expansion.
  ```c
  #define PRINT(x)  printf(#x)
  PRINT(Hello);  // Expands to: printf("Hello");
  ```

- **`##` (Token-Pasting):** Concatenates two adjacent tokens into a single token during expansion. It is used to construct identifiers dynamically.
  ```c
  #define VAR(n)  int var##n
  VAR(1);  // Expands to: int var1;
  ```

These operators are heavily used in building debug macros and in hardware abstraction layers for embedded systems.

---

## 5.2 Part B: Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper]`

**Q (a)** Define a macro `SQUARE(x)` that returns the square of a number. Explain why every argument and the entire macro body must be enclosed in parentheses. Show, with an example, what happens if the parentheses are omitted. **(7 marks)**

**Q (b)** Write a C program that uses macros to define a structure for a `Circle` (with radius) and computes its area and circumference using the macro `PI = 3.14159`. Use `#define` for `PI` and a function-like macro `AREA(r)` and `CIRCUMFERENCE(r)`. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Q (a) — 7 marks**

**Definition (2 marks):**
```c
#define SQUARE(x)  ((x) * (x))
```

**Why parentheses are mandatory (3 marks):**

Macros are *textually* expanded by the preprocessor. Without parentheses, the C operator-precedence rules will produce incorrect results when the argument is itself an expression.

**Example showing the bug (2 marks):**

```c
// WRONG version:
#define WRONG_SQUARE(x)  x * x
int y = WRONG_SQUARE(2 + 3);
```

**Expansion:** `y = 2 + 3 * 2 + 3;`

**Evaluation by compiler (operator precedence: `*` before `+`):**

$$y = 2 + (3 \times 2) + 3 = 2 + 6 + 3 = 11$$

The programmer expected `25`, but got `11`. The fix is to wrap both the **body** and the **argument** in parentheses:

```c
#define SQUARE(x)  ((x) * (x))
int y = SQUARE(2 + 3);
```

**Expansion:** `y = ((2 + 3) * (2 + 3)) = 25` ✅

---

#### **Model Solution for Q (b) — 7 marks**

```c
#include <stdio.h>

#define PI               3.14159
#define AREA(r)          (PI * (r) * (r))
#define CIRCUMFERENCE(r) (2 * PI * (r))

int main(void) {
    float radius = 5.0;
    float a, c;

    a = AREA(radius);
    c = CIRCUMFERENCE(radius);

    printf("Radius       = %.2f\n", radius);
    printf("Area         = %.4f\n", a);
    printf("Circumference= %.4f\n", c);
    return 0;
}
```

**Output:**
```
Radius       = 5.00
Area         = 78.5398
Circumference= 31.4159
```

**Preprocessor expansion of `AREA(radius)`:**

$$\text{AREA}(\text{radius}) \rightarrow (\text{PI} * (\text{radius}) * (\text{radius})) \rightarrow (3.14159 * (5.0) * (5.0)) = 78.5398$$

**Explanation (for full marks):**
- `PI` is an *object-like macro* (a symbolic constant).
- `AREA(r)` and `CIRCUMFERENCE(r)` are *function-like macros* that take one argument and produce inline mathematical expressions.
- All parentheses are correctly placed to avoid precedence issues.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023, Supplementary]`

**Q (a)** Explain the concept of **predefined macros** in C. List any five predefined macros and write a C program that uses `__FILE__`, `__LINE__`, and `__DATE__` to print debugging information. **(7 marks)**

**Q (b)** Differentiate between **macros and functions** in C with respect to: (i) handling phase, (ii) type checking, (iii) execution speed, (iv) side effects, (v) code size. Provide one example where a macro is preferred over a function and one where a function is preferred. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Q (a) — 7 marks**

**Concept (2 marks):**
Predefined macros are macro names that the C standard (and the compiler) automatically defines. The programmer does not define them with `#define`. They provide information about the *compilation environment* — the file being compiled, the current line, the date, etc.

**Five predefined macros (2 marks):**

| Macro | Description |
|---|---|
| `__FILE__` | Name of the current source file (string) |
| `__LINE__` | Current line number in the source file (integer) |
| `__DATE__` | Date of compilation (string in `"Mmm dd yyyy"` format) |
| `__TIME__` | Time of compilation (string in `"hh:mm:ss"` format) |
| `__func__` | Name of the current function (C99 onwards) |

**C Program (3 marks):**

```c
#include <stdio.h>

int main(void) {
    printf("Source File : %s\n", __FILE__);
    printf("Line Number : %d\n", __LINE__);
    printf("Compiled On : %s at %s\n", __DATE__, __TIME__);
    printf("Function    : %s\n", __func__);
    return 0;
}
```

**Sample Output:**
```
Source File : main.c
Line Number : 6
Compiled On : Mar 15 2024 at 14:30:00
Function    : main
```

---

#### **Model Solution for Q (b) — 7 marks**

| S.No. | Aspect | Macro | Function |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Handling Phase** | Preprocessor (Phase 3) | Compiler (Phase 7) |
| 2 | **Type Checking** | None — arguments can be any type | Strict — arguments must match parameter types |
| 3 | **Execution Speed** | Faster (no call overhead, inline expansion) | Slower (call/return + stack frame) |
| 4 | **Side Effects** | Arguments may be evaluated multiple times → dangerous | Arguments evaluated exactly once |
| 5 | **Code Size** | May bloat binary (expanded at every call) | Single copy in memory |

**Example where a macro is preferred (1 mark):**
Defining a hardware register access in embedded systems:
```c
#define PORTA  (*(volatile uint8_t *)0x4000)
```
Here, a function call would be too slow and the inline dereference is essential.

**Example where a function is preferred (1 mark):**
Computing the factorial of a large number using recursion:
```c
long long factorial(int n) {
    if (n <= 1) return 1;
    return n * factorial(n - 1);
}
```
Macros cannot be recursive, and a function provides type safety and stack-based execution.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students lose marks in Macro questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting parentheses** around the macro body or its arguments — costs 2–3 marks instantly. The examiner specifically looks for `((x) * (x))` style, not `x * x`.
> 2. **Confusing macro expansion with runtime evaluation.** The preprocessor does *text* replacement, not *math* replacement. Students often write "macro computes the square" — wrong wording; the compiler computes it after expansion.
> 3. **Confusing `#include` (file inclusion) with `#define` (macro definition).** These are different directives! KTU often tests this with MCQs.
> 4. **Using `=` inside a macro definition body** — `#define X = 5` is wrong; the correct form is `#define X 5`. The `=` is part of the replacement text, not a directive.
> 5. **Treating macros like functions for debugging** — breakpoints cannot be set inside a macro call (debugger sees expanded code).
> 6. **Side-effect arguments in `MAX(a++, b++)`** — both arguments may be evaluated multiple times. The C Standard permits this, and it leads to undefined behavior.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ✅ A **macro** is a name-to-text substitution defined via `#define`, processed by the **preprocessor in translation Phase 3**.
- ✅ **Object-like macros** have no parameters (`#define PI 3.14`). **Function-like macros** have a parameter list (`#define MAX(a,b) ((a)>(b)?(a):(b))`).
- ✅ The opening parenthesis of a function-like macro must **immediately** follow the macro name (no space).
- ✅ **Always** parenthesize both the **entire body** and **each argument** of a function-like macro to avoid operator-precedence bugs.
- ✅ The `#` operator **stringifies** its argument; the `##` operator **pastes tokens together**.
- ✅ C99 **variadic macros** use `...` and `__VA_ARGS__` to accept variable argument lists.
- ✅ Predefined macros (`__FILE__`, `__LINE__`, `__DATE__`, `__TIME__`, `__func__`) are auto-defined by the compiler.
- ✅ Macros are **faster** than functions (no call overhead) but have **no type checking** and may **evaluate arguments multiple times** (danger of side effects).
- ✅ Macros are **not recursive**, cannot be debugged normally, and can **bloat code size** if used in large codebases.
- ✅ A semicolon at the end of a macro definition is part of the replacement text — be cautious when using such macros inside `if` statements without braces.
- ✅ Conditional compilation (`#ifdef`, `#ifndef`, `#if`) works hand-in-hand with macros to build portable and debuggable code.

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GXEST204 - PROGRAMMING IN C
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Union; Storage Class - Storage Classes Associated with Variables: Automatic, Static, External and Register

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is a Union in C?

A **Union** is a user-defined data type in C (introduced in the ANSI C standard) that allows the storage of **different data types in the same memory location**. Unlike a structure (where each member has its own distinct memory slot), a union allocates a single shared memory block whose size is equal to the size of its **largest member**. All members of a union therefore **overlap** in memory, and only **one member can hold a meaningful value at any given instant**.

**Formal KTU Definition:**
> A *union* is a derived data type that groups variables of different data types under a single name, where the compiler allocates memory sufficient to hold the largest member, and all members share the same starting address.

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**
Imagine a **multi-purpose toolbox drawer** in a workshop. The drawer has only **one physical space** of a fixed size (large enough to fit the biggest tool). Depending on the job:
- If you are fixing a screw, you place a *screwdriver* in the drawer.
- If you are hammering a nail, you replace the screwdriver with a *hammer*.
- The drawer never holds *both* the screwdriver and the hammer simultaneously — placing a new tool physically overwrites the previous one.

The drawer is the **union**; the tools (screwdriver, hammer, pliers) are the **members**. The total drawer size is the size of the largest tool. Only one tool can be validly in use at a time.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The keyword `union` is used to declare a union. Its size is determined using the `sizeof` operator, which returns the size of the largest member (often rounded up for alignment purposes).

**General Syntax of Union Declaration:**

```c
union UnionName {
    data_type member1;
    data_type member2;
    ...
    data_type memberN;
} variable_list;
```

A working example:

```c
union Number {
    int   i;
    float f;
    char  c;
} num;
```

Here, the union `Number` will occupy memory equal to the size of its largest member (in most 32/64-bit systems, the `float` or `int` member dictates size — typically **4 bytes**). The variables `i`, `f`, and `c` all share the **same starting memory address**.

---

### 1.2 What are Storage Classes in C?

A **Storage Class** in C defines four important attributes of a variable:
1. **Scope** — where the variable is accessible (visibility).
2. **Lifetime / Storage Duration** — how long the variable exists in memory.
3. **Default Initial Value** — what value the variable holds if not explicitly initialized.
4. **Storage Location** — whether it is stored in CPU registers, stack, or the program's data segment.

**Formal KTU Definition:**
> A *storage class specifier* in C is a keyword that determines the visibility (scope), lifetime, default value, and storage location of a variable or function. The four standard storage classes in C89/C90 are `auto`, `register`, `static`, and `extern`.

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**
Think of storage classes as **rental agreements for memory space**:

| Class | Rental Analogy |
|-------|---------------|
| `auto` | A **short-term hotel room** — given to you when you enter, taken back when you leave the function. |
| `register` | A **premium pocket notepad** — fastest access but limited in number, cannot be pointed to. |
| `static` | A **permanent personal locker** — survives between function calls; value is retained. |
| `extern` | A **shared public notice board** — accessible across multiple files of the same program. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module-3 Core Concept:** Every C variable possesses two inherent properties — its **Data Type** (int, float, etc.) and its **Storage Class** (auto, register, static, extern). Together, these fully describe the variable's behavior in memory.

---

### 1.3 Why Study Unions and Storage Classes?

- **Unions** enable **memory-efficient programming**, critical in embedded systems, hardware register mapping, and variant data records.
- **Storage Classes** give the programmer fine-grained control over **memory usage, performance optimization, and variable visibility** — a frequent KTU examination topic because it directly tests understanding of the C runtime memory model.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metric Used in KTU Problems:**
> - 1 Byte = 8 bits
> - `sizeof(int)` ≈ **4 bytes** (on 32/64-bit GCC compilers for KTU labs)
> - `sizeof(char)` = **1 byte**
> - `sizeof(float)` = **4 bytes**
> - `sizeof(double)` = **8 bytes**
> - `sizeof(pointer)` = **4 bytes (32-bit) or 8 bytes (64-bit)**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Deep Analysis of Unions

#### 2.1.1 Memory Allocation Rule

The C standard guarantees the following for unions:

1. The size of a union is **at least** large enough to hold the **largest member**.
2. The compiler may add **padding bytes** for **alignment** purposes.
3. **All members share the same starting address** (offset 0 from the base address).

**Mathematical Representation:**

$$\text{sizeof}(\text{union } U) = \max_{i=1}^{N} \left( \text{sizeof}(member_i) \right) + \text{Padding}_{\text{aligned}}$$

Where $\text{Padding}_{\text{aligned}}$ accounts for byte-alignment rounding (e.g., to a multiple of 4 bytes).

#### 2.1.2 Accessing Union Members

Members are accessed using the **dot operator (`.`)** for union objects and the **arrow operator (`->`)** for pointers to unions — exactly the same syntax as structures.

```c
union Number num;
num.i = 42;       // Access integer member
printf("%d", num.i);

num.f = 3.14f;    // Overwrites the same memory with float
printf("%f", num.f);   // Valid
printf("%d", num.i);   // UNDEFINED BEHAVIOR — memory now holds float bits
```

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Valuation Pitfall:** Reading a different member than the one most recently written results in **undefined behavior** under the C standard. Examiners frequently award zero marks to students who claim unions "store all values simultaneously."

#### 2.1.3 Key Differences — Union vs Structure

| Property | Structure (`struct`) | Union (`union`) |
|----------|---------------------|-----------------|
| Memory Allocation | Sum of sizes of all members | Size of the **largest member** |
| Member Storage | Each member has its own memory | All members **share the same** memory |
| Simultaneous Access | All members can be active at once | Only **one member is valid** at a time |
| Keyword | `struct` | `union` |
| Total Size Formula | $\sum_{i=1}^{N} \text{sizeof}(member_i) + \text{Padding}$ | $\max_{i=1}^{N} \text{sizeof}(member_i) + \text{Padding}$ |
| Use Case | Grouping related but distinct attributes | Memory-efficient variant data |

---

### 2.2 Deep Analysis of Storage Classes

#### 2.2.1 The Four Standard Storage Classes

| Storage Class | Keyword | Scope | Lifetime | Default Value | Storage Location | Declaration Place |
|---------------|---------|-------|----------|---------------|------------------|-------------------|
| Automatic | `auto` | Local to the block | Until block ends | Garbage (unpredictable) | Stack (RAM) | Inside a function/block |
| Register | `register` | Local to the block | Until block ends | Garbage | CPU Registers (if available) | Inside a function/block |
| Static (local) | `static` | Local to the block | Entire program duration | **Zero (0)** | Data Segment | Inside a function/block |
| Static (global) | `static` | File-scope only (single file) | Entire program duration | **Zero (0)** | Data Segment | Outside all functions |
| External | `extern` | Global (multi-file) | Entire program duration | **Zero (0)** | Data Segment | Outside functions (or as declaration) |

#### 2.2.2 Operational Logic of Each Class

**1. Automatic Storage Class (`auto`)**
- This is the **default** storage class for any variable declared inside a function or block.
- Memory is allocated on the **stack** when the block is entered and deallocated when the block exits.
- Variables are **not initialized automatically** — they contain garbage values.
- The `auto` keyword is **rarely used explicitly** because it is the default.

**2. Register Storage Class (`register`)**
- Requests the compiler to store the variable in a **CPU register** for faster access.
- Cannot be applied to **floating-point** values in older C standards.
- The address-of operator (`&`) **cannot** be used on a register variable because registers do not have memory addresses.
- Modern compilers **ignore** the `register` hint and perform their own optimization; C++17 and C23 deprecate this keyword.

**3. Static Storage Class (`static`)**
- A static local variable retains its value **between function calls** — its lifetime is the entire program.
- Only the function that declared it can access it (scope is still local).
- Default initialized to **zero (0)** if no explicit initializer is provided.
- A static global variable/function has **internal linkage** — visible only within the file where it is declared.

**4. External Storage Class (`extern`)**
- Used to declare a global variable/function that is **defined in another file** or elsewhere in the same file.
- It does **not allocate** memory; it merely **refers** to an existing variable.
- Enables **multi-file C programs** by sharing variables across translation units.
- Default initialized to **zero** if not explicitly initialized.

#### 2.2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Unions** are heavily used in:
  - **Embedded Systems** (mapping hardware registers where the same bits can be interpreted as flags, integers, or bit-fields).
  - **Network Protocol Parsing** (a packet's payload can be parsed as different structures).
  - **Variant Data Types** (storing values whose type is determined at runtime).
- **Storage Classes** are essential in:
  - **Operating Systems Kernel Development** (managing kernel variables with `static`, sharing data via `extern`).
  - **Performance-Critical Code** (using `register` to suggest register allocation for hot loops).
  - **Modular C Programming** (`extern` for inter-module communication, `static` for encapsulation).

---

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Rule | Notes |
|---------|----------------|-------|
| Union Size | $\text{sizeof}(U) = \max(\text{sizeof}(member_i)) + \text{Pad}$ | Padding rounds up to alignment |
| Structure Size | $\text{sizeof}(S) = \sum \text{sizeof}(member_i) + \text{Pad}$ | Includes internal padding between members |
| Default `auto` Value | Garbage (uninitialized) | Compiler does not zero it |
| Default `static` Value | $\mathbf{0}$ | Zero for integers, `\0` for chars, `NULL` for pointers |
| Default `extern` Value | $\mathbf{0}$ | Same as static |
| `auto` storage | Stack segment | Lifetime = block scope |
| `static` storage | Data segment (BSS for zero-init) | Lifetime = entire program |
| `register` max count | Limited by CPU registers (typically $\leq 6$ to $\leq 16$) | Compiler-dependent |
| `extern` declaration | Does NOT allocate memory | Pure reference |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example 1: Calculating Union Size

**Problem:** Determine the size of the following union on a 32-bit GCC system (alignment to 4 bytes):

```c
union Data {
    char  c;     // 1 byte
    int   i;     // 4 bytes
    float f;     // 4 bytes
    double d;    // 8 bytes
    char  str[20]; // 20 bytes
};
```

**Step-by-Step Solution:**

We compute the size of each member:

$$\text{sizeof}(c) = 1 \text{ byte}$$
$$\text{sizeof}(i) = 4 \text{ bytes}$$
$$\text{sizeof}(f) = 4 \text{ bytes}$$
$$\text{sizeof}(d) = 8 \text{ bytes}$$
$$\text{sizeof}(str) = 20 \text{ bytes}$$

We then take the **maximum** of these sizes:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{MaxSize} &= \max(1, 4, 4, 8, 20) \\
&= 20 \text{ bytes}
\end{aligned}
$$

Now we apply **alignment padding**. On a 32-bit system, double and int often require 4-byte or 8-byte alignment. The largest member is `str[20]` (20 bytes), but for proper alignment the compiler may round the total size to a multiple of the **strictest alignment requirement** (typically 4 or 8 bytes).

For 8-byte alignment of `double`:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{sizeof}(Data) &= \left\lceil \frac{20}{8} \right\rceil \times 8 \\
&= 3 \times 8 \\
&= 24 \text{ bytes}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answer:** $\text{sizeof}(Data) = \mathbf{24 \text{ bytes}}$ (typical on GCC 32-bit).

> If alignment is only 4 bytes: $\text{sizeof}(Data) = \lceil 20 / 4 \rceil \times 4 = 20$ bytes.

---

### 3.2 Worked Example 2: Static vs Automatic Variable Behavior

**Problem:** Predict the output of the following C program:

```c
#include <stdio.h>
void counter() {
    int  a = 0;
    static int s = 0;
    a++;
    s++;
    printf("a = %d, s = %d\n", a, s);
}
int main() {
    counter();
    counter();
    counter();
    return 0;
}
```

**Step-by-Step Logical Evaluation:**

**Call 1** to `counter()`:
- A new stack frame is created.
- `a` is initialized to 0, then incremented → `a = 1`.
- `s` is initialized to 0 (only on the **first** call), then incremented → `s = 1`.
- Output: `a = 1, s = 1`

**Call 2** to `counter()`:
- A new stack frame is created. `a` is **re-initialized** to 0, then incremented → `a = 1`.
- The static variable `s` **retains its previous value (1)** because it is stored in the data segment, not the stack. So `s` becomes `2`.
- Output: `a = 1, s = 2`

**Call 3** to `counter()`:
- `a` is re-initialized to 0, then incremented → `a = 1`.
- `s` retains value 2, then increments → `s = 3`.
- Output: `a = 1, s = 3`

**Final Program Output:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Output} &= \begin{cases} a = 1, s = 1 \\ a = 1, s = 2 \\ a = 1, s = 3 \end{cases}
\end{aligned}
$$

This example is a **classic KTU examination question** that tests the difference in lifetime between `auto` and `static` variables.

---

### 3.3 Worked Example 3: Union Member Overlap Demonstration

**Problem:** Predict the output of the following program and explain:

```c
#include <stdio.h>
union Value {
    int   i;
    float f;
    char  c[4];
};
int main() {
    union Value v;
    v.i = 0x41424344;  // ASCII: 'A','B','C','D' (little-endian interpretation may differ)
    printf("As int:   %d\n", v.i);
    printf("As float: %f\n", v.f);
    printf("As chars: %c %c %c %c\n", v.c[0], v.c[1], v.c[2], v.c[3]);
    return 0;
}
```

**Step-by-Step Explanation:**

1. The integer `0x41424344` is written to the union's shared memory in hexadecimal form.
2. Reading the integer member `v.i` gives back `0x41424344` = **1,094,795,588** in decimal (signed) or the same value in unsigned.
3. Reading the float member `v.f` reinterprets the same 4 bytes as IEEE 754 floating-point. The result is a small positive number ($\approx 12.078431$).
4. Reading individual bytes as chars shows the ASCII representation of the bytes (subject to endianness):
   - On a **little-endian** machine (most PCs): `v.c[0]='D'`, `v.c[1]='C'`, `v.c[2]='B'`, `v.c[3]='A'`.
   - On a **big-endian** machine: `v.c[0]='A'`, `v.c[1]='B'`, `v.c[2]='C'`, `v.c[3]='D'`.

This demonstrates how union members **physically overlap** in the same memory region.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: When to Use Each Storage Class

| Requirement in Program Design | Recommended Storage Class | Justification |
|------------------------------|---------------------------|---------------|
| Temporary loop counter that is destroyed after the loop | `auto` (default) | Minimal overhead, lives only during the loop |
| Function call counter that persists across calls | `static` | Retains value between invocations |
| Frequently accessed loop variable in performance-critical code | `register` | Hint for CPU register storage |
| Global variable shared between two C files | `extern` | Provides external linkage |
| Global variable used only within one file (encapsulation) | `static` (global) | Restricts visibility to that file |
| Constant lookup table | `static const` | Read-only, persistent throughout program |

---

### 3.5 Comparison: `static` Global vs `extern` Global

| Property | `static` Global | `extern` Global |
|----------|-----------------|-----------------|
| Linkage | Internal (file-local) | External (multi-file) |
| Visibility outside the file | **Not visible** | Visible from any file |
| Default value (uninitialized) | 0 | 0 |
| Lifetime | Entire program | Entire program |
| Storage | Data segment | Data segment |
| Common use | Helper functions, file-private globals | Shared data between modules |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Memory Layout of a C Program

This diagram shows where each storage class resides in the classic C runtime memory model:

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Program Memory Layout] --> B[Code Text Segment]
    A --> C[Data Segment]
    A --> D[Stack Segment]
    A --> E[Heap Segment]

    C --> C1[Initialized Data: extern global, static global with value]
    C --> C2[Uninitialized Data BSS: extern global, static global zero init, static local]

    D --> D1[auto variables]
    D --> D2[register variables logical placement]
    D --> D3[function parameters]
    D --> D4[return addresses]
```

> Note: The `register` storage class requests the compiler to place the variable in a **CPU register** rather than the stack. The diagram above shows the *logical* location when register allocation is not possible.

---

### 4.2 Mermaid Flowchart — Storage Class Decision Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start[Declare a Variable] --> q1{Declared inside a function/block?}
    q1 -- No --> q2{Needed in other files?}
    q1 -- Yes --> q3{Need to retain value between calls?}
    q2 -- Yes --> ext[Use extern]
    q2 -- No --> sg[Use static global]
    q3 -- Yes --> st[Use static local]
    q3 -- No --> q4{Performance critical?}
    q4 -- Yes --> reg[Use register]
    q4 -- No --> aut[Use auto default]
```

---

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Union Memory Overlap Visualization

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph Union_U["union U { int i; float f; char c; }  — Total 4 bytes"]
        M0[Byte 0: shared by i, f, c]
        M1[Byte 1: shared by i, f, c]
        M2[Byte 2: shared by i, f, c]
        M3[Byte 3: shared by i, f, c]
    end
    M0 --> labelA[Same starting address for all members]
    M1 --> labelA
    M2 --> labelA
    M3 --> labelA
```

> All members `i`, `f`, and `c` begin at the same memory offset (offset 0). Writing to one member overwrites the others.

---

### 4.4 Mermaid Comparison Tree — Structure vs Union

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Derived Data Types in C] --> B[struct]
    A --> C[union]
    B --> B1[Sum of all members]
    B --> B2[Each member has unique memory]
    B --> B3[All members active simultaneously]
    C --> C1[Size of largest member]
    C --> C2[All members share memory]
    C --> C3[Only one member valid at a time]
```

---

### 4.5 Mermaid Sequence — Static Variable Lifetime

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Caller as main function
    participant Func as counter function
    participant Mem as Data Segment
    participant Stack as Stack Memory

    Caller->>Func: Call 1
    Func->>Stack: Allocate auto a
    Func->>Mem: Initialize static s to 0, then s++ to 1
    Note over Func: a=1, s=1
    Func->>Stack: Destroy a (end of block)

    Caller->>Func: Call 2
    Func->>Stack: Allocate fresh auto a
    Func->>Mem: s retains value 1, then s++ to 2
    Note over Func: a=1, s=2
    Func->>Stack: Destroy a

    Caller->>Func: Call 3
    Func->>Stack: Allocate fresh auto a
    Func->>Mem: s retains value 2, then s++ to 3
    Note over Func: a=1, s=3
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (2 Marks each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**Differentiate between a structure and a union in C with respect to memory allocation.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Structure | Union |
|--------|-----------|-------|
| Memory Size | Sum of sizes of all members (plus padding) | Size of the **largest member** (plus alignment padding) |
| Member Storage | Each member has its own unique memory location | All members share the **same** memory location |
| Simultaneous Use | All members can hold values at the same time | Only one member can hold a valid value at a time |
| Keyword | `struct` | `union` |

(2 marks — comparison table form is preferred in KTU valuation.)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**List the four storage classes in C and state the default value of variables for each.**

**Model Answer:**

The four storage classes in C are:

1. `auto` — default value: **garbage (uninitialized)**
2. `register` — default value: **garbage (uninitialized)**
3. `static` — default value: **zero (0)**
4. `extern` — default value: **zero (0)**

(2 marks — awarded for correct listing of all four classes and identification of default values.)

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks each — with Internal Choice)

---

#### QUESTION A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** Explain the concept of a union in C with a suitable example. How is a union different from a structure with respect to memory? Write a C program to demonstrate that all members of a union share the same memory location. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the four storage classes in C with their scope, lifetime, default value, and storage location. Write a C program that uses a `static` local variable to count the number of times a function is called. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Q A (a):**

A union is a user-defined data type in C that allows multiple members of different types to **share the same memory location**. The size of a union is equal to the size of its **largest member**. Only one member can hold a meaningful value at any given time.

**Program to demonstrate shared memory:**

```c
#include <stdio.h>
union Sample {
    int   i;
    float f;
    char  c;
};

int main() {
    union Sample s;
    s.i = 65;
    printf("Address of s.i: %p\n", &s.i);
    printf("Address of s.f: %p\n", &s.f);
    printf("Address of s.c: %p\n", &s.c);
    printf("All addresses are identical.\n");
    return 0;
}
```

**Memory Diagram (textual):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Address} &= 1000 \quad (\text{base}) \\
\text{s.i (4 bytes)} &: 1000 \to 1003 \\
\text{s.f (4 bytes)} &: 1000 \to 1003 \\
\text{s.c (1 byte)} &: 1000
\end{aligned}
$$

All three members start at the same address 1000. The total size of the union is **4 bytes** (size of the largest member).

**Difference in Memory:**

- A **structure** allocates separate memory for each member; total size = sum of all members + padding.
- A **union** allocates memory equal to the largest member; all members overlap.

---

**Model Solution for Q A (b):**

The four storage classes in C are summarized below:

| Storage Class | Keyword | Scope | Lifetime | Default Value | Storage |
|---------------|---------|-------|----------|---------------|---------|
| Automatic | `auto` | Local (block) | Block execution | Garbage | Stack |
| Register | `register` | Local (block) | Block execution | Garbage | CPU register |
| Static | `static` | Local (block) | Entire program | 0 | Data segment |
| External | `extern` | Global (file) | Entire program | 0 | Data segment |

**Program using static local variable as a counter:**

```c
#include <stdio.h>
void countCalls() {
    static int count = 0;   // Initialized only once
    count++;
    printf("Function called %d time(s)\n", count);
}
int main() {
    countCalls();
    countCalls();
    countCalls();
    return 0;
}
```

**Output:**

```
Function called 1 time(s)
Function called 2 time(s)
Function called 3 time(s)
```

**Explanation:** The `static` variable `count` retains its value across function calls because it is stored in the data segment, not on the stack. Hence, the counter increments correctly on each call.

---

#### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** Define a union. Write a C program using a union that stores either an integer or a floating-point number (decided by a tag variable) and prints the appropriate value. Explain how memory is utilized. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the differences between `auto`, `static`, `extern`, and `register` storage classes. Demonstrate with a C program how an `extern` variable can be shared between two C files. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Q B (a):**

**Definition:** A union is a user-defined data type that allows different types of data to occupy the **same memory location**. The total size of a union equals the size of its largest member.

**Program with tagged union:**

```c
#include <stdio.h>
union Value {
    int   i;
    float f;
};
struct Variant {
    int           type;   // 0 for int, 1 for float
    union Value   v;
};
int main() {
    struct Variant var;
    var.type = 0;
    var.v.i = 100;
    if (var.type == 0)
        printf("Integer value: %d\n", var.v.i);
    var.type = 1;
    var.v.f = 3.14f;
    if (var.type == 1)
        printf("Float value: %.2f\n", var.v.f);
    return 0;
}
```

**Memory Analysis:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{sizeof}(\text{union Value}) &= \max(\text{sizeof int}, \text{sizeof float}) \\
&= \max(4, 4) = 4 \text{ bytes} \\
\text{sizeof}(\text{struct Variant}) &= \text{sizeof type} + \text{sizeof union Value} + \text{padding} \\
&= 4 + 4 + 0 = 8 \text{ bytes (typical)}
\end{aligned}
$$

Memory is efficiently used because the union component only takes 4 bytes regardless of whether it stores an integer or a float.

---

**Model Solution for Q B (b):**

**Differences between storage classes:**

| Feature | `auto` | `register` | `static` | `extern` |
|---------|--------|------------|----------|----------|
| Scope | Local block | Local block | Local block (or file for global) | Global (multi-file) |
| Lifetime | Block | Block | Entire program | Entire program |
| Storage | Stack | CPU register | Data segment | Data segment |
| Default Value | Garbage | Garbage | 0 | 0 |
| Address (`&`) | Yes | **No** | Yes | Yes |

**Multi-file extern example:**

**File 1: `file1.c`**

```c
#include <stdio.h>
int counter = 0;          // Definition (memory allocated)
void increment() {
    counter++;
}
```

**File 2: `file2.c`**

```c
#include <stdio.h>
extern int counter;        // Declaration (no memory allocated)
extern void increment();
int main() {
    printf("Initial: %d\n", counter);
    increment();
    printf("After increment: %d\n", counter);
    return 0;
}
```

Compile and link:

```
gcc file1.c file2.c -o program
```

**Output:**

```
Initial: 0
After increment: 1
```

**Explanation:** The `extern` keyword in `file2.c` does not allocate new memory; it merely informs the compiler that `counter` is defined in another translation unit (`file1.c`). The linker resolves the reference at link time.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cause Mark Deductions in KTU Exams:**
> 1. **Confusing union size with the sum of all members** — students frequently write "union size = sum of members" which is wrong. Correct: union size = size of the **largest member**.
> 2. **Forgetting alignment padding** — always mention that the compiler may add padding for alignment, even though the C standard only requires the size to be ≥ the largest member.
> 3. **Claiming that all union members are valid simultaneously** — this is a critical conceptual error. Only the most recently written member is valid.
> 4. **Using `&` operator on a register variable** — the `&` operator is **not allowed** on a register variable, and the compiler will throw an error.
> 5. **Treating `auto` and `static` as identical** — many students incorrectly assume static variables are re-initialized every call. The defining feature of `static` is **value persistence** across calls.
> 6. **Defining `extern` variables with initializers in multiple files** — multiple definitions cause linker errors. Use `extern` only for **declarations**, not definitions.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Module 3 (Union & Storage Classes)**

**Unions:**
- ☐ Union keyword is `union`; declared similar to `struct`.
- ☐ All members share the **same starting address**.
- ☐ Size of union = size of **largest member** + alignment padding.
- ☐ Only **one member** holds a valid value at a time.
- ☐ Access operators are `.` (for objects) and `->` (for pointers).
- ☐ Unions are widely used in **embedded systems, variant data, and protocol parsing**.

**Storage Classes:**
- ☐ Four storage classes: `auto`, `register`, `static`, `extern`.
- ☐ `auto` — default for local variables, lives on the **stack**, garbage default.
- ☐ `register` — requests **CPU register** storage; `&` operator is forbidden.
- ☐ `static` — value **persists** across function calls; default value is **0**.
- ☐ `static` global — internal linkage (file-scope only).
- ☐ `extern` — declares a variable defined **elsewhere**; enables multi-file sharing.
- ☐ `extern` declaration does **not** allocate memory; only references it.
- ☐ Default value of `static` and `extern` is **zero**; of `auto` and `register` is **garbage**.
- ☐ Storage location: `auto`/`register` → stack/registers; `static`/`extern` → data segment.
- ☐ Lifetime: `auto`/`register` → block; `static`/`extern` → entire program.

**Common Pitfalls to Avoid in KTU Valuation:**
- ☐ Do not confuse `static` local with `static` global — they share the keyword but have different scopes.
- ☐ Do not write `&` before a register variable.
- ☐ Do not assume union members are independent in memory.
- ☐ Always mention padding and alignment when calculating sizes.

> **End of Module 3 Notes — Union & Storage Classes**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Pointers - Declaration
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GXEST204 - PROGRAMMING IN C
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Pointers - Declaration

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Pointer** in the C programming language is a **derived data type** (also classified as a *scalar data type*) that stores the **memory address** of another variable instead of holding a direct data value. A **Pointer Declaration** is the formal C statement that introduces a pointer variable into the program, specifying two critical properties: (1) the **data type** of the object the pointer will point to, and (2) the **name** by which the pointer will be referenced.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 4):** "Pointers – Declaration, Initialization, Accessing variables through pointers, Pointer arithmetic, Pointers and arrays, Pointers and strings, Pointers and functions." This section covers the *Declaration* sub-topic exclusively.

The general syntax of pointer declaration as mandated by the **ISO C11 / C18 standard** (followed by KTU) is:

```c
data_type *pointer_name;
```

The asterisk (`*`) is called the **indirection operator** or **dereference operator** during declaration, though it performs a different role at runtime. The `data_type` is **not** the type of the pointer itself — it indicates the type of the data residing at the memory address the pointer will hold.

## 1.2 Real-World Analogy — The "House Number" Model

Imagine a city where every house has a unique number (e.g., House #104, #105). The house itself holds the *family living inside* (the data value), while the house number is a *reference* to that house. If you want someone to visit your family, you don't carry your family around — you simply give them the house number.

- The **house** = an ordinary variable (e.g., `int age = 25;`)
- The **family inside the house** = the data value (`25`)
- The **house number** = the memory address (e.g., `0x7FFC5A3E`)
- The **piece of paper on which the house number is written** = the **pointer variable**

So, a pointer is essentially a *piece of paper* that has a *house number scribbled on it* — the paper doesn't contain the family, but it tells you *where to find the family*.

## 1.3 Key Terminology (Bolded for KTU Emphasis)

- **Pointer Variable**: A variable that holds a memory address as its value.
- **Memory Address**: A unique hexadecimal (or numeric) identifier assigned by the OS to every byte of RAM.
- **Indirection Operator (`*`)**: Used at declaration to mark a variable as a pointer; used at runtime to fetch the value stored at the pointed address.
- **Address-of Operator (`&`)**: A unary operator that returns the memory address of any ordinary variable.
- **NULL Pointer**: A pointer initialized to `0` (or `NULL` macro), indicating it points to *nowhere* — a safe sentinel to prevent dangling references.
- **Void Pointer (`void *`)**: A generic pointer that can hold the address of *any* data type — must be cast before dereferencing.
- **Pointer Size**: On modern 64-bit systems, every pointer occupies **8 bytes**; on 32-bit systems, **4 bytes** — regardless of the data type it points to.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pointer Initialization vs. Declaration (Common Confusion):**
> - **Declaration** = Telling the compiler "I need a pointer variable named `ptr`." (No memory address is assigned yet.)
> - **Initialization** = Assigning an actual valid memory address to that pointer at the time of declaration (e.g., `int *ptr = &x;`).
> In KTU exams, students frequently lose marks by mixing these two distinct concepts.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Breakdown of a Pointer Declaration

A pointer declaration is processed by the C compiler in three logical phases. Understanding these phases is essential for KTU's *Application-level* questions.

### Phase 1 — Type Specification
The compiler must know what kind of data the pointer will eventually reference. This determines how many bytes will be read/written when dereferencing and how pointer arithmetic will scale (step size).

### Phase 2 — Asterisk Placement
The `*` symbol binds to the variable name *immediately following* it, **not** to the entire declaration line. This is the source of the most common KTU exam pitfall — discussed in detail in Section 5.

### Phase 3 — Name Registration
The pointer name is added to the compiler's **Symbol Table** with attributes: identifier, type (pointer-to-T), scope, and initial value (uninitialized / `NULL` if specified).

## 2.2 Pointer Declaration Examples — Valid and Invalid Forms

| # | C Statement | Status | KTU Explanation |
|---|-------------|--------|------------------|
| 1 | `int *p;` | ✅ Valid | Declares `p` as a pointer to an integer. |
| 2 | `float *q, *r;` | ✅ Valid | Declares **both** `q` and `r` as pointers to float. |
| 3 | `float *q, r;` | ⚠️ Subtle | `q` is a pointer-to-float, but `r` is a **plain float** — not a pointer! The `*` binds only to `q`. |
| 4 | `int *p = NULL;` | ✅ Valid | Declares and initializes `p` to NULL — best practice. |
| 5 | `int *p = &x;` | ✅ Valid | Declares `p` and initializes it with address of `x`. |
| 6 | `int *p = 100;` | ❌ Invalid (Warning/Error) | Integer literal `100` is not a typed address — produces a compiler warning about "initialization makes pointer from integer." |
| 7 | `void *vp;` | ✅ Valid | Generic pointer; cannot be dereferenced without casting. |
| 8 | `int *p, *q, *r;` | ✅ Valid | All three are pointers-to-int. |
| 9 | `*int p;` | ❌ Invalid Syntax | The `*` must come *after* the type, before the name. |
| 10 | `int p*;` | ❌ Invalid Syntax | The `*` must precede the variable name. |

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **single most important reference** for Module 4 — Pointer Declaration. Memorize it before any KTU evaluation.

| Concept | Formula / Rule | Notes |
|---------|---------------|-------|
| Pointer Declaration Syntax | `type *ptr;` | `type` = base type; `ptr` = pointer name |
| Size of Pointer (64-bit) | `sizeof(ptr) = 8 bytes` | Independent of base type |
| Size of Pointer (32-bit) | `sizeof(ptr) = 4 bytes` | Independent of base type |
| Size of Pointed Data | `sizeof(*ptr) = sizeof(type)` | Determined by base type |
| Address-of Operator | `&variable` | Returns starting memory address |
| Dereference Operator | `*pointer` | Returns the value at the address |
| Pointer Arithmetic Step | `ptr + n → ptr + (n × sizeof(type))` | Type-aware scaling |
| NULL Pointer | `ptr == NULL` or `ptr == 0` | Sentinel for "no valid address" |
| Generic Pointer | `void *vp;` | Cannot dereference without cast |
| Asterisk Binding Rule | `*` binds to the variable name, not the type | Applies to multi-variable declarations |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Pointers are **not** an academic luxury — they are the backbone of every major engineering software system:

- **Embedded Systems (KTU ECE/EEE relevance):** Microcontroller registers are memory-mapped; pointers are used to read/write hardware registers directly (e.g., `*(volatile uint32_t *)0x40021000`).
- **Data Structures:** Linked lists, trees, graphs, and hash tables rely on pointers to chain nodes dynamically.
- **Operating Systems:** Memory management, process control blocks, and file descriptor tables are pointer-heavy.
- **High-Performance Computing:** Passing large arrays/structures to functions by reference (using pointers) avoids expensive data copies.
- **IoT & Robotics:** Sensor data buffers are manipulated via pointer arithmetic in firmware.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Outcome Mapping:** This topic primarily serves **CO2 (Apply pointer concepts to solve computational problems)** and **CO3 (Develop modular programs using pointers and dynamic memory).**

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Memory-Level Walkthrough of a Pointer Declaration

Let us trace, byte-by-byte, what happens when the following C statements execute on a typical **64-bit Linux/x86-64** system:

```c
int x = 25;
int *ptr = &x;
```

### Step 1 — Declare the integer variable `x`

The compiler reserves a memory block of `sizeof(int) = 4` bytes (on most modern systems) in the **stack** segment. The address of the *first byte* of this block is the address of `x`. Assume the OS assigns the address `0x7FFC5A30` (illustrative).

$$
\text{Address of } x \rightarrow 0x7FFC5A30
$$

The value `25` is stored in binary as `0001 1001` (using little-endian: `19 00 00 00`).

### Step 2 — Declare the pointer variable `ptr`

The compiler reserves `sizeof(int *) = 8` bytes in the stack for the pointer. Since `ptr` is not yet initialized, it contains a **garbage value** (an unpredictable address from a prior stack frame) — this is a *wild pointer* hazard.

### Step 3 — Evaluate the right-hand side `&x`

The `&` operator does not allocate memory; it merely returns the **r-value** (the actual numeric value) of the address of `x`.

$$
\&x \rightarrow 0x7FFC5A30
$$

### Step 4 — Initialize `ptr`

The value `0x7FFC5A30` is copied into the 8 bytes reserved for `ptr`. Now `ptr` "points to" `x`.

### Step 5 — Verify the dereferencing relationship

$$
\text{Value of } ptr = 0x7FFC5A30
$$

$$
\text{Dereference: } *ptr = \text{value at address } 0x7FFC5A30 = 25
$$

## 3.2 Multi-Variable Declaration — Exhaustive Trace

Consider the *trickiest* declaration form, which KTU examiners love to test:

```c
int *p, q;
```

### Step 1 — Tokenization by the compiler

The compiler reads this as two distinct declarations because the `*` is treated as a *prefix unary operator* attached to the identifier that follows it.

### Step 2 — Binding of the `*` operator

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Declaration 1:} \quad & \text{int } \boxed{*p}; \\
\text{Declaration 2:} \quad & \text{int } \boxed{q}; \\
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Interpretation

- `p` is of type `int *` (pointer to integer) — occupies 8 bytes (on 64-bit), holds an address.
- `q` is of type `int` (plain integer) — occupies 4 bytes, holds a numeric value.

### Step 4 — Memory size calculation

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total memory reserved} &= \text{sizeof}(p) + \text{sizeof}(q) \\
&= 8 \text{ bytes} + 4 \text{ bytes} \\
&= 12 \text{ bytes (before any padding)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Contrast this with:** `int *p, *q;` — in this case *both* `p` and `q` are pointers, and `8 + 8 = 16 bytes` are reserved. KTU examiners specifically test this distinction in Part A (3-mark) questions.

## 3.3 Size Derivation — Why All Pointers Have the Same Size

On a system with a **N-bit address bus**, the maximum number of uniquely addressable memory locations is $2^N$. Therefore, every address — and hence every pointer — must be exactly **N bits** wide.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Address width in bits} &= N \\
\text{Pointer size in bytes} &= \frac{N}{8} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

For the two dominant architectures in 2024–2025:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{32-bit system: } \frac{32}{8} &= 4 \text{ bytes per pointer} \\
\text{64-bit system: } \frac{64}{8} &= 8 \text{ bytes per pointer} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

This explains why `sizeof(char *) == sizeof(int *) == sizeof(double *)` on the *same* machine, even though `sizeof(char) ≠ sizeof(int) ≠ sizeof(double)`.

## 3.4 Pointer Arithmetic Step-Size Derivation

When you add an integer `n` to a pointer `ptr`, the compiler automatically scales `n` by the size of the base type:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{ptr} + n \quad &\equiv \quad \text{ptr} + (n \times \text{sizeof}(\text{base\_type})) \\
\text{ptr} - n \quad &\equiv \quad \text{ptr} - (n \times \text{sizeof}(\text{base\_type})) \\
\end{aligned}
$$

### Worked Example

```c
int *p;          // p is pointer to int (4 bytes per int)
p = p + 1;       // p advances by 4 bytes, not 1 byte
```

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{If } p &= 0x1000 \\
p + 1 &= 0x1000 + (1 \times 4) = 0x1004 \\
p + 2 &= 0x1000 + (2 \times 4) = 0x1008 \\
\end{aligned}
$$

In contrast, for a `char *cp`:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{If } cp &= 0x1000 \\
cp + 1 &= 0x1000 + (1 \times 1) = 0x1001 \\
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the *type-aware scaling* feature of pointer arithmetic — and the foundation of how pointers interact with arrays (covered later in Module 4).

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Operator Behavior Comparison

| Operator | Name | Operand | Operation | Result | Example |
|----------|------|---------|-----------|--------|---------|
| `&` | Address-of | Variable | Returns memory address of operand | Pointer of operand's type | `&x` → `0x7FFC5A30` |
| `*` (unary, runtime) | Dereference | Pointer | Fetches value at pointed address | Same type as base type | `*ptr` → `25` |
| `*` (declaration) | Indirection | Variable name | Marks the variable as a pointer | N/A (compile-time) | `int *p;` |
| `->` | Arrow | Pointer to struct | Accesses struct member via pointer | Member's type | `ptr->field` |
| `.` | Dot | Struct variable | Accesses struct member directly | Member's type | `s.field` |

> [!NOTE]
> The `*` symbol is **context-dependent** in C: at *declaration time* it creates a pointer; at *runtime* it dereferences. KTU examiners frequently ask this as a "trick" question worth 2–3 marks.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Memory Architecture Diagram — Pointer Declaration in Action

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the in-memory relationship between a variable, its pointer, and the CPU/compiler components during declaration and initialization.

```mermaid

        direction TB

    end

    subgraph CPU["CPU & COMPILER PIPELINE"]
        direction TB

    compDecl --> varPtr

    varX --> OUTPUT
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Multi-Variable Declaration Resolution

The following diagram clarifies the most-tested KTU pitfall: the placement of `*` in multi-variable pointer declarations.

```mermaid

    subgraph SCENARIO_A["Scenario A: int *p, q;"]
        direction LR
        aIn["Input Declaration"]
        aIn --> aParse["Compiler Tokenizer\nSplits at comma"]
        aParse --> aP1["int *p;\nptr-type, 8 bytes"]
        aParse --> aQ1["int q;\ninteger-type, 4 bytes"]
    end

    subgraph SCENARIO_B["Scenario B: int *p, *q;"]
        direction LR

        bParse --> bQ2["int *q;\nptr-type, 8 bytes"]
    end

    SCENARIO_A --> RESULT["RESULT:\nAsymmetric Memory Footprint\np: 8 bytes | q: 4 bytes\nTotal: 12 bytes"]
    SCENARIO_B --> RESULT2["RESULT:\nSymmetric Memory Footprint\np: 8 bytes | q: 8 bytes\nTotal: 16 bytes"]

```

## 4.3 Process Flow — From Source Code to Runtime Pointer

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant U as User/Programmer
    participant SRC as Source Code (.c file)
    participant CC as C Compiler
    participant STK as Stack Memory
    participant CPU as CPU Registers

    U->>SRC: Writes: int x = 25; int *ptr = &x;
    SRC->>CC: Source code submitted for compilation
    CC->>CC: Lexical analysis → tokens
    CC->>CC: Syntax analysis → parse tree
    CC->>CC: Symbol table update: x (int), ptr (int *)
    CC->>STK: Allocate 4 bytes for x at 0x7FFC5A30
    CC->>STK: Allocate 8 bytes for ptr at 0x7FFC5A38
    CC->>CPU: MOV [0x7FFC5A30], 25
    CC->>CPU: LEA RAX, [0x7FFC5A30]
    CC->>CPU: MOV [0x7FFC5A38], RAX
    CPU->>STK: Store 25 at x's location
    CPU->>STK: Store 0x7FFC5A30 at ptr's location
    STK-->>U: Memory state ready for runtime use
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Tip:** When asked to "explain pointer declaration with a diagram" in the exam, prefer a simple **variable-box-and-arrow** drawing. Even a hand-drawn diagram showing `ptr → x` with the address and value labelled will earn full marks if clear.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 Marks = 6 Marks)

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Define a pointer in C. Explain pointer declaration with a suitable example. (3 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

A pointer is a derived data type in C that stores the **memory address** of another variable. It does not hold the data directly but references the location where the data resides.

**Syntax:** `data_type *pointer_name;`

The `*` indicates that the variable is a pointer, and `data_type` specifies the type of data the pointer will point to.

**Example:**

```c
int a = 10;
int *p = &a;   // p is declared as pointer to int and initialized with address of a
```

Here, `p` holds the address of `a`, and `*p` yields the value `10`.

---

### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Differentiate between the address-of (`&`) operator and the dereference (`*`) operator. (3 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | `&` (Address-of) | `*` (Dereference) |
|---------|------------------|-------------------|
| Function | Returns the memory address of a variable | Accesses the value at a given memory address |
| Operand | Any ordinary variable (l-value) | A pointer variable |
| Result | A pointer of the variable's type | A value of the base type |
| Example | `&x` returns address of `x` | `*ptr` returns value pointed to by `ptr` |
| Category | Unary operator | Unary operator |
| Usage | To obtain an address for pointer initialization | To read/write through a pointer |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (Module Internal Choice: 14 Marks)

> KTU's 2024 ESE pattern for Module 4 awards **one 14-mark question** with an **internal choice** between two alternatives. Model answers for both options are provided below.

### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Modified]`

**(a)** Explain the concept of pointer declaration in C with a suitable example. Differentiate between the declaration `int *p, q;` and `int *p, *q;` with the help of a memory diagram. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Write a C program to demonstrate pointer declaration, initialization, and dereferencing. Show the output with a clear explanation. **(7 marks)**

#### Part (a) — Model Solution (7 marks)

**Concept (2 marks):**
Pointer declaration introduces a variable that holds a memory address rather than a direct value. The syntax is `type *ptr;` where `type` is the base type and `ptr` is the pointer name.

**Differentiation (4 marks):**

- `int *p, q;` → The `*` binds only to `p`. So `p` is a pointer to int, but `q` is a plain integer. Total memory = 8 + 4 = 12 bytes.
- `int *p, *q;` → Both `p` and `q` are pointers to int. Total memory = 8 + 8 = 16 bytes.

**Memory Diagram (1 mark):**

```
Case 1: int *p, q;           Case 2: int *p, *q;
+----------------+           +----------------+
| p (int *) |8B  |           | p (int *) |8B  |
+----------------+           +----------------+
| q (int)  |4B   |           | q (int *) |8B  |
+----------------+           +----------------+
```

#### Part (b) — Model Solution (7 marks)

```c
#include <stdio.h>

int main(void) {
    int num = 42;
    int *ptr;

    ptr = #   // pointer initialized with address of num

    printf("Value of num        : %d\n", num);
    printf("Address of num (&num): %p\n", (void *)&num);
    printf("Value of ptr        : %p\n", (void *)ptr);
    printf("Value pointed by *ptr: %d\n", *ptr);

    *ptr = 100;   // modifying num through pointer
    printf("New value of num     : %d\n", num);

    return 0;
}
```

**Sample Output:**

```
Value of num        : 42
Address of num (&num): 0x7ffd5a30
Value of ptr        : 0x7ffd5a30
Value pointed by *ptr: 42
New value of num     : 100
```

**Explanation (1 mark):** The program shows that `ptr` holds the same address as `&num`, and `*ptr` retrieves/modifies the value at that address, demonstrating indirection.

---

### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Modified]`

**(a)** What is a pointer? Explain with a neat diagram how a pointer variable is declared and used to access another variable. Discuss the size of a pointer in a 32-bit and 64-bit system. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the following pointer declarations and state whether each is valid or invalid. Justify your answer. **(7 marks)**

1. `int *p;`
2. `int *p, q;`
3. `float *x = 100;`
4. `void *vp;`
5. `int *p = NULL;`
6. `*int p;`
7. `int p*;`

#### Part (a) — Model Solution (7 marks)

**Definition (1 mark):** A pointer is a variable that stores the memory address of another variable.

**Diagram + Declaration (3 marks):**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Variable: int a = 10\nAddress: 1000\nValue: 10"] -->|address 1000| B["Pointer: int *p = &a\nAddress: 2000\nValue: 1000"]
    B -->|dereference *p| C["Returns 10"]
```

**Size Discussion (3 marks):**

- On a **32-bit** system, the address bus is 32 bits wide → every pointer occupies `32/8 = 4 bytes`.
- On a **64-bit** system, the address bus is 64 bits wide → every pointer occupies `64/8 = 8 bytes`.
- The pointer's size is independent of the base type — `char *`, `int *`, and `double *` all occupy the same number of bytes on a given machine.

#### Part (b) — Model Solution (7 marks)

| # | Declaration | Status | Justification (1 mark each) |
|---|-------------|--------|----------------------------|
| 1 | `int *p;` | ✅ Valid | Declares `p` as a pointer to an integer. |
| 2 | `int *p, q;` | ✅ Valid syntax, ⚠️ Logical issue | `p` is a pointer to int; `q` is a plain int. |
| 3 | `float *x = 100;` | ❌ Invalid (warning) | `100` is an integer literal, not a valid address — compiler issues a warning. |
| 4 | `void *vp;` | ✅ Valid | Declares a generic pointer that can hold any type's address. |
| 5 | `int *p = NULL;` | ✅ Valid | Declares and initializes `p` to NULL — best practice. |
| 6 | `*int p;` | ❌ Invalid | `*` must follow the type, not precede it. |
| 7 | `int p*;` | ❌ Invalid | `*` must precede the variable name, not follow it. |

---

## 5.3 Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU Students Lose Marks on Pointer Declaration Questions:**
>
> 1. **Asterisk Binding Misconception (–2 marks):** Writing `int *p, q;` and then claiming *both* are pointers. Only `p` is; `q` is a plain int.
> 2. **Mixing Declaration with Initialization (–1 mark):** Saying "declaring `int *p = &x;`" is a single concept. The C standard treats declaration and initialization as two distinct stages; a precise answer distinguishes them.
> 3. **Confusing `*` operator contexts (–1 mark):** Failing to clarify that `*` in `int *p;` is the *indirection* (compile-time) operator, while `*p` at runtime is the *dereference* operator. Same symbol, different roles.
> 4. **Omitting the `void *` cast in `printf` (–0.5 mark):** Writing `printf("%p", ptr)` without `(void *)` cast generates a compiler warning in strict KTU labs.
> 5. **Forgetting that pointer size is independent of base type (–1 mark):** Stating "pointer to int is 4 bytes" — it's 8 bytes on a 64-bit machine, not 4.
> 6. **Drawing poor memory diagrams (–1 mark):** A diagram without explicit address labels and arrow direction earns only partial credit.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ A **pointer** stores a **memory address**, not a value directly.
- ✅ Declaration syntax: **`type *name;`** — the `*` is part of the *variable declarator*, not the type.
- ✅ The `*` **binds only to the variable it precedes** in multi-variable declarations.
- ✅ Pointer size = **`N/8` bytes** on an `N`-bit architecture (4 B on 32-bit, 8 B on 64-bit) — *independent of base type*.
- ✅ `&` returns the address of a variable; `*` retrieves the value at an address.
- ✅ Always **initialize pointers** to either a valid address or `NULL` to avoid wild-pointer bugs.
- ✅ `void *` is a **generic pointer** — needs a type cast before dereferencing.
- ✅ Pointer arithmetic scales by `sizeof(base_type)` — this is the basis of pointer–array equivalence (next sub-topic).
- ✅ The same `*` symbol plays two roles: *indirection at declaration*, *dereference at runtime*.
- ✅ KTU expects both **theoretical clarity** (definitions, diagrams) and **practical fluency** (trace outputs, write programs).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Next Sub-Topic Preview:** *Pointer Initialization and Accessing Variables Through Pointers* — the natural continuation of this discussion, covering `int *p = &x;`, `*p = value;`, and the indirection chain `ptr → address → value`.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Operations on Pointers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GXEST204 - PROGRAMMING IN C
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Operations on Pointers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pointer Operations** are the set of legal arithmetic, relational, and assignment manipulations that may be performed on a pointer variable in the C programming language. Unlike ordinary variables, the only meaningful operations permitted on pointers are a strictly defined subset: **pointer assignment**, **pointer dereferencing (indirection)**, **address-of (&) referencing**, **pointer arithmetic (increment, decrement, addition, subtraction)**, **pointer comparison**, and **pointer subtraction (distance between two pointers)**.

In the C memory model, a pointer stores a memory address. The compiler allocates to every pointer a fixed size (typically **4 bytes** on 32-bit systems and **8 bytes** on 64-bit systems), and the **scale factor** (or unit step) for all pointer arithmetic is determined by the size of the data type it points to.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Plain English)

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: The House Number on a Street**
> Imagine a street where every house is uniformly spaced, and every house is **4 meters wide** (just like every `int` is 4 bytes, every `float` is 4 bytes, every `double` is 8 bytes, and every `char` is 1 byte). A pointer is like a slip of paper that holds the **house number (address)** of one such house. When you say "go to the next house", you don't add 1 to the number — you skip ahead by the **width of one house (4 meters)**. That is exactly what pointer arithmetic does: `ptr + 1` doesn't add 1 byte; it adds **1 × sizeof(type)** bytes to the address.

This is why pointer arithmetic is **type-aware** — the compiler always scales by the size of the base type.

## 1.3 List of Legal Operations on Pointers

| # | Operation | Symbolic Form | Legal? |
|---|-----------|---------------|--------|
| 1 | Assignment of address of same type | `p = &x;` | ✅ Yes |
| 2 | Dereferencing (indirection) | `*p` | ✅ Yes |
| 3 | Increment / Decrement | `p++`, `p--` | ✅ Yes |
| 4 | Integer addition / subtraction | `p + n`, `p - n` | ✅ Yes |
| 5 | Pointer subtraction (distance) | `p1 - p2` | ✅ Yes (same array) |
| 6 | Comparison (`==`, `!=`, `<`, `>`, `<=`, `>=`) | `p1 == p2` | ✅ Yes |
| 7 | Multiplication / Division | `p * 2` | ❌ Illegal |
| 8 | Addition of two pointers | `p1 + p2` | ❌ Illegal |
| 9 | Float offset addition | `p + 2.5` | ❌ Illegal |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Pointer Arithmetic — The Core Mechanics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Cardinal Rule of Pointer Arithmetic:** Every arithmetic operation on a pointer `p` of type `T` is internally scaled by `sizeof(T)` bytes. The C Standard guarantees this so that `p + 1` always points to the *next* element of the array, regardless of the type's physical width.

### 2.1.1 Increment and Decrement

Let `p` be a pointer of type `T*` currently holding address `A`.

$$p_{\text{new}} = A \pm 1 \times \text{sizeof}(T)$$

For example, on a typical 64-bit system:
- If `p` is `int*` and `A = 1000`, then `p + 1 = 1000 + 1 \times 4 = 1004`.
- If `p` is `double*` and `A = 1000`, then `p + 1 = 1000 + 1 \times 8 = 1008`.
- If `p` is `char*` and `A = 1000`, then `p + 1 = 1000 + 1 \times 1 = 1001`.

### 2.1.2 Integer Addition and Subtraction

Adding or subtracting an integer `n` to a pointer is equivalent to moving `n` elements forward or backward in an array of type `T`.

$$p + n \equiv p + n \times \text{sizeof}(T) \quad \text{(in bytes)}$$

$$p - n \equiv p - n \times \text{sizeof}(T) \quad \text{(in bytes)}$$

### 2.1.3 Pointer Subtraction (Difference of Two Pointers)

> [!NOTE]
> The subtraction of two pointers that point into the **same array** yields the number of elements (not bytes) between them, of type `ptrdiff_t` (a signed integer type defined in `<stddef.h>`).

If `p1` and `p2` point to elements of the same array:

$$\text{diff} = p1 - p2 \;\;(\text{measured in number of elements of } T)$$

Equivalently, in raw bytes:

$$p1 - p2 = \frac{\text{byte\_address}(p1) - \text{byte\_address}(p2)}{\text{sizeof}(T)}$$

### 2.1.4 Pointer Comparison

Two pointers may be compared using relational operators (`<`, `<=`, `>`, `>=`) and equality operators (`==`, `!=`). The result is meaningful primarily when the pointers point into the **same array** (or one-past-the-end). Comparing unrelated pointers invokes **undefined behavior** (per the C standard).

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is the high-yield reference card you should memorize for the KTU 2024 Scheme University Examination.

| Operation | Syntax | Resulting Address (Bytes) | Resulting Pointer Position | Notes |
|-----------|--------|---------------------------|----------------------------|-------|
| Increment | `p++` or `++p` | `A + sizeof(T)` | One element forward | Pre/post both valid |
| Decrement | `p--` or `--p` | `A - sizeof(T)` | One element backward | Pre/post both valid |
| Add integer | `p + n` | `A + n \cdot \text{sizeof}(T)` | `n` elements forward | `n` must be integral |
| Subtract integer | `p - n` | `A - n \cdot \text{sizeof}(T)` | `n` elements backward | `n` must be integral |
| Difference | `p1 - p2` | $\frac{\text{addr}(p1) - \text{addr}(p2)}{\text{sizeof}(T)}$ | Element count | Same array only |
| Compare | `p1 < p2` | Logical true/false | Ordering check | Same array preferred |
| Dereference | `*p` | N/A (yields value of type `T`) | Accesses pointed object | Must be non-null |
| Address-of | `&x` | N/A (yields `T*`) | Address of `x` | Only for lvalues |
| Assignment | `p = q` | `addr(p) = addr(q)` | Now points to same object | Type must match or be `void*` |

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

Pointer operations are the engine behind nearly every high-performance system written in C:

- **Embedded Systems & IoT**: Direct manipulation of memory-mapped hardware registers (e.g., `*(volatile uint32_t *)0x40021018 = 0x01;`) relies entirely on casting integer addresses to typed pointers and dereferencing.
- **Operating System Kernels**: Process scheduling, virtual memory paging, and linked-list traversal (e.g., the Linux kernel's intrusive lists) depend on pointer arithmetic and pointer-to-struct offsets.
- **Compilers and Interpreters**: Abstract syntax tree (AST) traversal, symbol table management, and runtime stack frames use pointer operations as the primary data movement mechanism.
- **Data Structures**: Arrays, linked lists, trees, hash tables — all are built and traversed through pointer arithmetic and dereferencing.
- **High-Performance Computing (HPC)**: BLAS/LAPACK numerical kernels use pointer arithmetic to stride through memory with maximum cache locality.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: How `p + 1` is Computed by the Compiler

We will rigorously derive the scaling rule of pointer arithmetic.

### Step 1 — Start with a typed pointer declaration

```c
int *p;
```

The compiler assigns `p` a slot of **8 bytes** (on a 64-bit machine) to hold a memory address.

### Step 2 — Establish the base address

Suppose `p` is initialized:

```c
int x = 10;
p = &x;   /* suppose &x == 2000 */
```

The variable `p` now stores the integer address `2000`. Let `A = 2000`.

### Step 3 — Apply the expression `p + 1`

Per the C Standard (Section 6.5.6, Additive Operators), `p + n` is defined as:

> *"If the pointer operand points to an element of an array, the result points to the element offset from the original element by `n` elements."*

For a pointer to type `T`, the byte-level offset is therefore `n × sizeof(T)`.

### Step 4 — Compute the resulting address

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{NewAddress} &= A + n \times \text{sizeof}(\text{int}) \\
&= 2000 + 1 \times 4 \\
&= 2004
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5 — Verify via dereferencing

If `p + 1` is dereferenced, the 4 bytes starting at address `2004` are interpreted as an `int`, which is precisely the next `int` in an array `int arr[ ]` containing `x` as `arr[0]`.

> **Conclusion:** `*(p + 1) ≡ arr[1]`. This is the **fundamental equivalence** between pointer arithmetic and array indexing — a favourite KTU question.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Worked Examples for All Legal Operations

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following analytical matrix uses a concrete memory layout so you can verify every formula visually. Assume a 64-bit system where `sizeof(char)=1`, `sizeof(int)=4`, `sizeof(float)=4`, `sizeof(double)=8`. Base address of array `A` is `1000`.

### Example 1 — `int *p = A;` where `A` is `int[5]`

| Operation | Code | Address (decimal) | Pointed Element | `*p` Value |
|-----------|------|-------------------|-----------------|------------|
| Initial | `p = A;` | **1000** | `A[0]` | 10 |
| Increment | `p++;` | **1004** | `A[1]` | 20 |
| Add 2 | `p = p + 2;` (after reset) | **1008** | `A[2]` | 30 |
| Pre-decrement | `--p;` (from `A[2]`) | **1004** | `A[1]` | 20 |
| Dereference | `*p` (at `A[1]`) | 1004 | `A[1]` | **20** |
| Difference | `(A+4) - A` | N/A | N/A | **4** (elements) |
| Difference in bytes | byte-form | N/A | N/A | $4 \times 4 = 16$ bytes |

### Example 2 — `double *q;` and pointer subtraction

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Let } q_1 &= \text{address of } A[2] = 1000 + 2 \times 8 = 1016 \\
\text{Let } q_2 &= \text{address of } A[5] = 1000 + 5 \times 8 = 1040 \\
q_2 - q_1 &= \frac{1040 - 1016}{\text{sizeof}(\text{double})} = \frac{24}{8} = 3 \text{ elements}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Example 3 — Illegal Operations (Why KTU asks these)

```c
int *p, *q;
p + q;      /* ILLEGAL — addition of two pointers */
p * 2;      /* ILLEGAL — multiplication */
p / q;      /* ILLEGAL — division */
p % 3;      /* ILLEGAL — modulo */
p + 2.5;    /* ILLEGAL — only integer offsets allowed */
```

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Note:** If you write `*(p + 1)` as `*p + 1` in a board exam, you will lose **2 marks** immediately. Operator precedence: `*` and `+` are both at the same level and associate left-to-right, so `*p + 1` means `(*p) + 1`, which is **value + 1**, not the next element. Always parenthesize.

## 3.3 Verification of the Equivalence `a[i] ≡ *(a + i)`

$$
\begin{aligned}
a[i] &\equiv \text{element at index } i \text{ of array } a \\
*(a + i) &\equiv \text{value at address } (\text{base} + i \times \text{sizeof}(T)) \\
&\equiv \text{value at address of } a[i] \\
&\equiv a[i] \quad \blacksquare
\end{aligned}
$$

This identity is the cornerstone of array-pointer duality and is a **guaranteed 7-mark question** topic in KTU Module 4.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Memory Layout Diagram — Pointer Arithmetic in Action

The diagram below shows an `int` array `arr[5] = {10, 20, 30, 40, 50}` laid out contiguously in memory, with pointers `p`, `p+1`, and `p+4` indicating successive increments.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A0["arr[0]\nAddr 1000\nValue 10"]
    A1["arr[1]\nAddr 1004\nValue 20"]
    A2["arr[2]\nAddr 1008\nValue 30"]
    A3["arr[3]\nAddr 1012\nValue 40"]
    A4["arr[4]\nAddr 1016\nValue 50"]
    P["Pointer p\nHolds 1000"]
    P1["Pointer p+1\nHolds 1004"]
    P4["Pointer p+4\nHolds 1016"]

    A0 --- A1 --- A2 --- A3 --- A4
    P -. points to .-> A0
    P1 -. points to .-> A1
    P4 -. points to .-> A4
```

> **Reading the diagram:** Notice how the address gap between consecutive array elements is exactly **4 bytes** (the size of `int`), and the pointer values reflect the **byte address**, not the index.

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Pointer Operation Pipeline

The following block diagram illustrates the *data path* through which the C compiler and CPU execute a pointer arithmetic expression like `*(p + 2)`:

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SRC[Source Expression]
        EXPR["*(p + 2)"]
    end

    subgraph PARSE[Compiler Frontend]
        TOK["Tokenizer\nidentifies ptr, +, 2, *"]
        SEM["Semantic Analyzer\nresolves T=int"]
        SCALE["Scale Calculator\n2 × sizeof int = 8 bytes"]
    end

    subgraph GEN[Code Generation]
        ADDR["LEA / ADD instruction\np + 8 bytes"]
        LOAD["MOV instruction\nload 4 bytes from new addr"]
        RESULT["Result in register\n(e.g., EAX)"]
    end

    subgraph CPU[CPU Execution]
        ALU["ALU computes\n1000 + 8 = 1008"]
        MEM["Memory fetch\n4 bytes at 1008"]
        REG["Store in register\nas int value"]
    end

    EXPR --> TOK --> SEM --> SCALE --> ADDR --> LOAD --> RESULT
    RESULT --> ALU --> MEM --> REG
```

## 4.3 State Transition Diagram — Pointer Lifecycle

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Uninitialized
    Uninitialized --> Initialized : p = NULL or p = &x
    Initialized --> Dereferenced : *p (read or write)
    Dereferenced --> Incremented : p++ or p = p + n
    Incremented --> Compared : p1 op p2
    Compared --> Dereferenced : *p again
    Dereferenced --> [*] : scope ends / freed
```

> [!NOTE]
> The **Uninitialized → Initialized** transition is the single most important one in C programming. Using a pointer before initialization invokes **undefined behavior** — a classic KTU pitfall.

## 4.4 Pointer-to-Array Element Access Pattern

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START["Start: ptr = arr"]
```
