The loop terminates only when the copied byte is `'\0'`, which is also copied to properly terminate `dest`. *(1 mark for logic, 1 mark for trace)* **(ii) Custom `strcat()` implementation (3.5 marks):** ```c #include void myStrcat(char *dest, const char *src) { // Move dest pointer to its null terminator while (*dest != '\0') dest++; // Copy src to dest while ((*dest++ = *src++) != '\0') ; } int main() { char destination[30] = "Hello, "; char source[] = "World!"; myStrcat(destination, source); printf("Concatenated: %s\n", destination); return 0; } ``` **Output:** `Concatenated: Hello, World!` **Explanation:** The first `while` loop advances `dest` to its `'\0'`. The second loop performs a `strcpy`-like copy from that position onward. *(1 mark for the two-phase approach, 1 mark for correctness)* --- ### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Q: (a)** What is a string in C? Explain the concept of null-terminated strings with a neat diagram. Write a C program to read a string using pointers and display it character-by-character. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Write a C program using pointers to compare two strings lexicographically (implement `strcmp` without using library functions). Also explain the return value logic of `strcmp`. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** #### Part (a) — Strings and Pointer-Based I/O (7 marks) A string in C is a **one-dimensional array of characters terminated by a null character `'\0'`** *(1 mark)*. The null character has ASCII value 0 and is automatically appended by the C compiler when a string literal is assigned to a character array *(1 mark)*. **Memory Diagram for `char name[] = "Kerala";`:** ``` Index: [0] [1] [2] [3] [4] [5] [6] +----+----+----+----+----+----+----+ Content: |'K' |'e' |'r' |'a' |'l' |'a' |'\0'| +----+----+----+----+----+----+----+ Address: 1000 1001 1002 1003 1004 1005 1006 ``` *(1 mark for diagram)* **C program to read and display using pointers (4 marks):** ```c #include int main() { char str[100]; char *p; printf("Enter a string: "); fgets(str, sizeof(str), stdin); p = str; // p points to first character printf("Characters are:\n"); while (*p != '\0' && *p != '\n') { printf("%c ", *p); p++; // Advance pointer } printf("\n"); return 0; } ``` **Sample Run:** ``` Enter a string: KTU Characters are: K T U ``` #### Part (b) — Custom `strcmp()` Implementation (7 marks) **Return value logic (1 mark):** - Returns **0** if both strings are equal - Returns **negative value** if `s1` is lexicographically smaller than `s2` - Returns **positive value** if `s1` is lexicographically greater than `s2` **Complete C program (6 marks):** ```c #include int myStrcmp(const char *s1, const char *s2) { // Compare character-by-character until mismatch or end while (*s1 != '\0' && *s2 != '\0' && *s1 == *s2) { s1++; s2++; } // Return ASCII difference return *s1 - *s2; } int main() { char str1[100], str2[100]; int result; printf("Enter first string: "); gets(str1); printf("Enter second string: "); gets(str2); result = myStrcmp(str1, str2); if (result == 0) printf("Strings are equal.\n"); else if (result < 0) printf("First string is less than second.\n"); else printf("First string is greater than second.\n"); return 0; } ``` **Trace for `s1 = "cat"`, `s2 = "car"`:** - Compare `'c' == 'c'` ✓ → advance - Compare `'a' == 'a'` ✓ → advance - Compare `'t' == 'r'` ✗ → exit loop - Return `'t' - 'r' = 116 - 114 = 2` (positive) - Output: *"First string is greater than second."* ✓ *(1 mark for loop structure, 1 mark for early termination logic, 1 mark for return value, 1 mark for main, 2 marks for trace and correctness)* > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Alert — Where Students Lose Marks:** > 1. **Forgetting the null terminator copy** in `strcpy()`: A program that does *not* explicitly copy `'\0'` to the destination loses 1–2 marks. > 2. **Confusing `*p++` with `(*p)++`**: The former advances the pointer, the latter increments the value at that location. This is a classic KTU viva question. > 3. **Using `&` with array name in `scanf`**: Writing `scanf("%s", &str);` is a compilation error or wrong-type bug. > 4. **Modifying string literals**: `char *p = "Hello"; p[0] = 'J';` produces undefined behavior — students must mention this in theory. > 5. **Off-by-one in `strlen`**: Forgetting to count the last character before `'\0'`. > 6. **Not handling empty string**: Edge case `""` should return length 0. ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Exams:** - **String = char array + `'\0'` terminator** (always include null terminator in memory) - **`char str[]` is mutable; `char *str = "literal"` points to read-only memory** - **Array-to-pointer decay:** `str` becomes `&str[0]` in expressions - **Equivalence identities:** `str[i] ≡ *(str+i) ≡ *(i+str) ≡ i[str]` *(the last form is valid C but bad practice)* - **Pointer arithmetic scaling:** `p++` for `char*` advances by 1 byte - **String functions use pointer traversal internally** — knowing this helps in viva and theory - **Custom implementations of `strlen`, `strcpy`, `strcmp`, `strcat` are mandatory KTU exam topics** (practice each at least twice) - **The idiom `*dest++ = *src++`** is concise but must be understood fully - **Null character check:** Always test for `*p != '\0'` in loops, not for `p != NULL` (these are different things) - **`scanf("%s", ptr)` does not need `&`** because `ptr` already holds an address - **`gets()` is unsafe** — prefer `fgets()` in modern C (and mention this in exams for extra credit) - **Pointer-to-pointer (`char **`) is used for arrays of strings** (like `argv` in `main`) - **Buffer overflow risk:** Always ensure destination array is large enough when copying/concatenating - **`const char *src`** in function parameters signals that the source string will not be modified — a good professional practice - **Endianness does not affect string processing** because characters are stored byte-by-byte - **Standard `` functions assume null-terminated strings** — passing an unterminated array causes undefined behavior > **End of Module 4 Notes — Processing Strings Using Pointers**

topics.title: Pointer to Pointer
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GXEST204 - PROGRAMMING IN C
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Pointer to Pointer

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the C programming language, a **Pointer to Pointer** (also known as a **double pointer** or a pointer with **two levels of indirection**) is a derived data type whose stored value is the memory address of another pointer variable. If a regular pointer `p` holds the address of an ordinary variable `x`, then a pointer to pointer `pp` holds the address of `p`.

The general declaration syntax follows the pattern of placing one asterisk (`*`) for every level of indirection beyond the base variable:

```c
data_type **pointer_name;
```

The first asterisk denotes that `pointer_name` is a pointer, and the second asterisk denotes that the value it points to is *also* a pointer.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 4 — Pointers):**
> A pointer to pointer is a distinct concept from a pointer to an array. While both use double indirection syntactically, semantically a pointer to pointer is a scalar variable holding a single address of another pointer, whereas a pointer to an array is a pointer that points to the first element of a contiguous memory block.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Treasure Hunt of Maps

Imagine you are standing at your home (the **value**) and you want to find a hidden treasure.

- **The Treasure** = the actual integer value `x = 25`.
- **A Map** = a regular pointer `p` that tells you the location (address) of the treasure.
- **A Map to the Map** = a pointer to pointer `pp` that tells you where the first map (`p`) is kept.

To reach the treasure, you must follow two steps:
1. Look at the second map (`pp`) to find where the first map (`p`) is hidden.
2. Use the first map (`p`) to locate the actual treasure (`x`).

In C, this is precisely what the dereference operator (`*`) accomplishes, applied twice: `**pp`.

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain English Restatement:**
> A pointer to pointer is *not* a pointer that points to a value. It is a pointer that points to **another pointer**, which in turn points to the actual value. Each `*` peels off one layer of indirection.

### 1.3 Why Two Stars? The Asterisk Counting Rule

| Declaration | Read As | Indirection Level |
|-------------|---------|-------------------|
| `int x;` | x is an integer | 0 |
| `int *p;` | p is a pointer to integer | 1 |
| `int **pp;` | pp is a pointer to (pointer to integer) | 2 |
| `int ***ppp;` | ppp is a pointer to (pointer to (pointer to integer)) | 3 |

For KTU examinations, indirection up to **level 2** is the standard tested depth, with level 3 occasionally appearing in advanced Part B problems.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Concept — Step-by-Step Logic Breakdown

Let us analyse the lifetime of a pointer-to-pointer variable through the structured lifecycle below:

1. **Declaration Phase:** The compiler reserves `sizeof(void *)` bytes (typically **8 bytes** on a 64-bit system, **4 bytes** on a 32-bit system) on the **stack** for the double pointer. The contents are initialized to garbage unless explicitly set.

2. **Initialization Phase:** The double pointer is assigned the address of a single pointer. This is a critical distinction — assigning the address of the *original variable* is a **type mismatch** in C and will trigger a compiler warning.

3. **Dereferencing Phase (Single `*`):** Applying one asterisk (`*pp`) yields the value stored in the intermediate pointer, which is the address of the base variable. The result is a regular `int *`.

4. **Dereferencing Phase (Double `**`):** Applying two asterisks (`**pp`) traverses two levels of indirection to reach the original integer value stored in the base variable.

5. **Modification Phase:** The double pointer can be used to modify the *base value* (`**pp = 50;`) or to modify the *address the intermediate pointer holds* (`*pp = &y;`), demonstrating its dual utility.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Notation | Meaning | Type | Example (assuming `int x = 10; int *p = &x; int **pp = &p;`) |
|----------|---------|------|--------------------------------------------------------------|
| `pp` | Address of `p` | `int **` | e.g., `0x7FF4` |
| `*pp` | Value of `p` (i.e., address of `x`) | `int *` | e.g., `0x1020` |
| `**pp` | Value of `x` | `int` | `10` |
| `&pp` | Address of the double pointer itself | `int ***` | e.g., `0x7F00` |
| `sizeof(pp)` | Size of pointer on the platform | `size_t` | **8** bytes (64-bit) or **4** bytes (32-bit) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical KTU Rule:** `sizeof(pp) == sizeof(p) == sizeof(x_ptr)` for any pointer on the same platform, regardless of the indirection level. Pointers always hold a raw memory address.

### 2.3 Address Arithmetic on Double Pointers

When you increment a double pointer, the address jumps by `sizeof(pointer_type)` bytes, not by `sizeof(base_type)`.

$$pp + 1 = pp + \text{sizeof}(\text{int}*) \text{ bytes}$$

For a 64-bit system:

$$pp + 1 = pp + 8 \text{ bytes}$$

This is the foundation for **dynamic 2D array traversal**, where each step of the double pointer moves you to the next *row* of a matrix.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Pointer to pointer is a foundational concept used in:

- **Command-Line Argument Parsing:** The signature `int main(int argc, char **argv)` is a classic example where `argv` is a pointer to an array of string pointers.
- **Dynamic 2D Array Allocation:** Creating matrices whose row count and column count are determined at runtime (essential in image processing, scientific computing).
- **Linked List Head Manipulation:** Passing the address of the `head` pointer to functions so they can modify the list origin.
- **Function Callbacks and Pointer Arrays:** Arrays of function pointers used in embedded systems dispatch tables.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** In operating system kernel development and device drivers, double pointers are routinely used to manipulate pointer-based data structures where a single pointer would be insufficient to express a change of reference.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Comprehensive Worked Example

Consider the following canonical C program that demonstrates pointer-to-pointer mechanics:

```c
#include <stdio.h>
int main(void) {
    int x = 25;
    int *p = &x;
    int **pp = &p;

    printf("Address of x  = %p\n", (void *)&x);
    printf("Address of p  = %p\n", (void *)&p);
    printf("Address of pp = %p\n", (void *)&pp);

    printf("Value of pp   = %p\n", (void *)pp);
    printf("*pp           = %p\n", (void *)*pp);
    printf("**pp          = %d\n", **pp);

    **pp = 100;
    printf("x after **pp=100 is %d\n", x);
    return 0;
}
```

### 3.2 Analytical Trace with Memory Layout

Assume a **64-bit little-endian system** with the following hypothetical stack addresses:

| Variable | Memory Address | Stored Value | Interpreted As |
|----------|---------------|--------------|----------------|
| `x` | `0x1000` | `25` | integer `25` |
| `p` | `0x2000` | `0x1000` | address of `x` |
| `pp` | `0x3000` | `0x2000` | address of `p` |

**Step 1: `pp` evaluates to the address where `p` lives.**

$$pp = 0x3000 \quad \text{(raw address of p)}$$

**Step 2: `*pp` dereferences the value at `0x3000`, retrieving the contents of `p`.**

$$*pp = 0x1000 \quad \text{(which is } \&x\text{)}$$

**Step 3: `**pp` dereferences `*pp` (which is `0x1000`), retrieving the contents of `x`.**

$$**pp = 25$$

**Step 4: Modifying `x` through the double pointer.**

The statement `**pp = 100;` performs the following operations:

$$\begin{aligned}
**pp &= 100 \\
\Rightarrow *(*pp) &= 100 \\
\Rightarrow \text{value at } (*pp) = 100 \\
\Rightarrow \text{value at } 0x1000 = 100 \\
\Rightarrow x &= 100
\end{aligned}$$

After this assignment, the memory state becomes:

| Variable | Memory Address | Stored Value |
|----------|---------------|--------------|
| `x` | `0x1000` | `100` |
| `p` | `0x2000` | `0x1000` (unchanged) |
| `pp` | `0x3000` | `0x2000` (unchanged) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Observation:** Only `x` was modified. The intermediate pointer `p` and the double pointer `pp` retain their original addresses. This demonstrates that double indirection can mutate the leaf data without disturbing the pointer chain itself.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Common Pointer-to-Pointer Operations

| Operation | Code Expression | Effect |
|-----------|-----------------|--------|
| Access leaf value | `val = **pp;` | Reads the integer pointed to |
| Modify leaf value | `**pp = new_val;` | Changes the integer at the end of the chain |
| Change intermediate pointer target | `*pp = &y;` | Makes `p` point to a different variable `y` |
| Change double pointer target | `pp = &q;` | Makes `pp` point to a different single pointer `q` |
| Increment base | `(**pp)++;` | Adds 1 to the integer value |
| Step through array of pointers | `*(pp + i)` | Accesses the i-th pointer in an array |

### 3.4 Mathematical Proof of Pointer Size Invariance

We can formally prove that all pointer types on a given architecture occupy the same memory.

**Theorem:** For any pointer type $T^*$ on a given platform $\mathcal{P}$ with word size $W$ bits:

$$\text{sizeof}(T^*) = \frac{W}{8} \text{ bytes}$$

**Proof Sketch:**

By definition, a pointer stores a memory address. The address space of platform $\mathcal{P}$ is bounded by $2^W - 1$. To uniquely represent any address, exactly $W$ bits are required.

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Number of bits required} &= W \\
\text{Number of bytes required} &= \frac{W}{8} \\
\therefore \text{sizeof}(T^*) &= \frac{W}{8}
\end{aligned}$$

For a 64-bit platform: $\text{sizeof}(T^*) = \frac{64}{8} = 8$ bytes.

This holds for `int *`, `int **`, `int ***`, and `char *` alike. The indirection level is *type metadata* consumed by the dereference operator — it does not alter storage size.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Memory Layout Architecture — Pointer to Pointer Chain

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the conceptual memory architecture of a double indirection chain, including the address arrows and value cells:

```mermaid
graph LR
    boxX["MEMORY CELL 0x1000"] -->|"stores integer 25"| valX["Value : 25"]
    boxP["MEMORY CELL 0x2000"] -->|"stores address 0x1000"| valP["Value : 0x1000"]
    boxPP["MEMORY CELL 0x3000"] -->|"stores address 0x2000"| valPP["Value : 0x2000"]
    boxPP -.->|"pp points to"| boxP
    boxP -.->|"p points to"| boxX
    deref1["*pp"] ==>|"yields 0x1000"| boxP
    deref2["**pp"] ==>|"yields 25"| boxX
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Indirection Resolution Flow

The following schematic depicts the **function-call resolution flow** when a function receives a pointer-to-pointer argument and must modify the original variable:

```mermaid
graph TD
    callerA["CALLER SCOPE"] -->|"passes pp"| calleeA["CALLEE SCOPE"]
    callerA -->|"variable x at 0x1000"| memA["MEMORY 0x1000"]
    calleeA -->|"receives int **ptr"| paramA["PARAMETER ptr"]
    paramA -->|"single deref *ptr"| midA["INTERMEDIATE ADDRESS"]
    paramA -->|"double deref **ptr"| leafA["LEAF INTEGER"]
    midA -->|"points to 0x1000"| memA
    leafA -->|"read write modify"| memA
    calleeA -->|"returns"| callerA
```

### 4.3 Indirection Level Hierarchy Tree

The following hierarchy illustrates how the asterisk count corresponds to indirection depth in the C type system:

```mermaid
graph TD
    lvl0["Level 0 : int x"] -->|"address of operator and"| lvl1["Level 1 : int *p"]
    lvl1 -->|"address of operator and"| lvl2["Level 2 : int **pp"]
    lvl2 -->|"address of operator and"| lvl3["Level 3 : int ***ppp"]
    lvl0 -->|"size 4 bytes"| szA["STACK or DATA"]
    lvl1 -->|"size 8 bytes on 64 bit"| szB["STACK"]
    lvl2 -->|"size 8 bytes on 64 bit"| szC["STACK"]
    lvl3 -->|"size 8 bytes on 64 bit"| szD["STACK"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Reading Tip for KTU Exams:** When asked to draw a memory diagram, always show three things explicitly: (1) the variable name, (2) the memory address in hex format, and (3) the value or address stored. KTU examiners award partial credit for each correctly labelled component.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper Set B]**
Define a pointer to pointer. Write a C program snippet to declare an integer variable, a pointer to it, and a pointer to that pointer. Print the value of the integer using the double pointer.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A pointer to pointer is a variable that stores the address of another pointer variable. It provides an additional level of indirection. (1 Mark)

```c
#include <stdio.h>
int main(void) {
    int a = 50;
    int *p = &a;
    int **pp = &p;
    printf("Value of a = %d\n", **pp);
    return 0;
}
```

Explanation: `pp` stores the address of `p`, `*pp` gives the address of `a`, and `**pp` gives the value `50`. (1 Mark for explanation, 1 Mark for correct program)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
What will be the output of the following C code? Justify your answer.

```c
#include <stdio.h>
int main(void) {
    int x = 5, y = 10;
    int *p = &x;
    int **pp = &p;
    *pp = &y;
    printf("%d %d\n", x, **pp);
    return 0;
}
```

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

Output: `5 10` (1 Mark)

Justification: Initially, `p` points to `x`. After `*pp = &y;`, the statement `*pp` dereferences the double pointer to access `p`, then assigns `&y` to `p`. Now `p` points to `y` (which holds 10), but `x` remains unchanged at 5. (2 Marks for justification)

---

### Part B — Full 14-Mark Module Internal Choice Questions

#### Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Module 4 Pattern]

**(a)** Explain the concept of pointer to pointer in C with a suitable diagram. Discuss its declaration, initialization, and dereferencing with an example. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Write a C program using a pointer to pointer to swap two integer variables using a user-defined function `swap(int **, int **)`. Show the output. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Question A:**

**(a) Concept, Declaration, and Dereferencing (7 Marks):**

A pointer to pointer is a variable that holds the address of another pointer. (1 Mark)

**Declaration syntax:**

```c
int **pp;
```

This declares `pp` as a pointer to a pointer to an integer. (1 Mark)

**Memory Diagram (3 Marks):**

```
   pp          p           x
[ 0x3000 ]  [ 0x2000 ]  [ 0x1000 ]
   |           |           |
   |           |           +---> 25 (integer value)
   |           +--------------- 0x1000 (address of x)
   +--------------------------- 0x2000 (address of p)
```

**Initialization and dereferencing (2 Marks):**

```c
int x = 25;
int *p = &x;
int **pp = &p;
```

- `pp` returns `0x2000` (the address of `p`).
- `*pp` returns `0x1000` (the value of `p`, which is the address of `x`).
- `**pp` returns `25` (the value of `x`).

---

**(b) Swap Program Using Double Pointer (7 Marks):**

```c
#include <stdio.h>

void swap(int **a, int **b) {
    int *temp = *a;
    *a = *b;
    *b = temp;
}

int main(void) {
    int x = 100, y = 200;
    int *p1 = &x, *p2 = &y;

    printf("Before swap: x = %d, y = %d\n", x, y);
    swap(&p1, &p2);
    printf("After swap:  x = %d, y = %d\n", *p1, *p2);

    return 0;
}
```

**Output:**
```
Before swap: x = 100, y = 200
After swap:  x = 200, y = 100
```

**Explanation (2 Marks for the swap logic):**

The function `swap` receives the addresses of `p1` and `p2`. The single dereference `*a` accesses the value of `p1` (which is `&x`), and the assignment `*a = *b` makes `p1` point to `y`. After swapping the pointer values through the double indirection, `p1` points to `y` and `p2` points to `x`, so the apparent values of `x` and `y` are exchanged.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning (Common Mistakes):**
> 1. **Confusing `&x` with `&p1`:** Students often pass `&x` and `&y` to the function expecting it to swap the integers. Since the function signature is `int **`, it expects the **addresses of pointers**, not the addresses of integers. (Deduct 2 marks)
> 2. **Forgetting the dereference inside the function:** Writing `a = b;` instead of `*a = *b;` will only swap the local copies of the addresses inside the function, leaving the caller's `p1` and `p2` unchanged. (Deduct 3 marks — this is a critical logic error)
> 3. **Missing the header inclusion and proper `printf` format specifiers.** (Deduct 0.5 mark per error)

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**(a)** With the help of a neat diagram, explain how a 2D array can be dynamically allocated using a pointer to pointer. Write a C program to allocate a 3×3 matrix dynamically, accept values from the user, and print the sum of all elements. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Differentiate between the following with examples:
   (i) Pointer to an array versus pointer to pointer.
   (ii) `*pp` versus `**pp` in terms of type and value. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Question B:**

**(a) Dynamic 2D Array Using Double Pointer (7 Marks):**

A double pointer can represent a 2D matrix by pointing to an array of row pointers, where each row pointer points to a 1D array of column elements. (1 Mark)

**Diagram (2 Marks):**
```
pp ──> [ptr row 0] ──> [a00][a01][a02]
   │                [a10][a11][a02]
   └──> [ptr row 1] ──> ...
```

**C Program (4 Marks):**

```c
#include <stdio.h>
#include <stdlib.h>

int main(void) {
    int rows = 3, cols = 3, i, j, sum = 0;
    int **matrix = (int **)malloc(rows * sizeof(int *));

    for (i = 0; i < rows; i++) {
        matrix[i] = (int *)malloc(cols * sizeof(int));
    }

    printf("Enter 9 integers:\n");
    for (i = 0; i < rows; i++) {
        for (j = 0; j < cols; j++) {
            scanf("%d", &matrix[i][j]);
            sum += matrix[i][j];
        }
    }

    printf("Sum of all elements = %d\n", sum);

    for (i = 0; i < rows; i++) free(matrix[i]);
    free(matrix);
    return 0;
}
```

**Explanation:** The expression `matrix[i][j]` is equivalent to `*(*(matrix + i) + j)`. The first dereference (`*(matrix + i)`) selects the i-th row pointer, and the second dereference adds `j` to reach the j-th column. (1 Mark)

---

**(b) Differentiations (7 Marks):**

**(i) Pointer to Array vs. Pointer to Pointer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | Pointer to Array (`int (*p)[5]`) | Pointer to Pointer (`int **pp`) |
|--------|----------------------------------|----------------------------------|
| Points to | An entire fixed-size array of 5 integers | A single integer pointer |
| Declaration | Parentheses required for precedence | No parentheses needed |
| Memory layout | Contiguous 1D block of 5 ints | May point to scattered rows (dynamic) |
| Use case | Static 2D arrays passed to functions | Dynamic row-pointer allocation |

Example:
```c
int arr[3][5];
int (*p)[5] = arr;    // p is a pointer to an array of 5 ints
int **pp = (int **)arr; // pp treats the memory as pointer chain
```

**(ii) `*pp` versus `**pp` (4 Marks):**

| Expression | Type | Value (using earlier example) |
|------------|------|-------------------------------|
| `pp` | `int **` | Address of pointer `p` (e.g., `0x3000`) |
| `*pp` | `int *` | Address of integer `x` (e.g., `0x1000`) |
| `**pp` | `int` | The integer value `25` |

- `*pp` performs **one level of indirection** and yields a pointer to the base type.
- `**pp` performs **two levels of indirection** and yields the base value itself.
- Modifying `*pp` (e.g., `*pp = &y;`) changes where the intermediate pointer points.
- Modifying `**pp` (e.g., `**pp = 50;`) changes the leaf data without altering the pointer chain.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Premature dereference:** Writing `*pp = 50;` expecting it to change `x` will instead attempt to write the integer `50` into a memory location that is supposed to hold a pointer — this is a **type mismatch** and leads to undefined behaviour or a segmentation fault. (Deduct 3 marks)
> 2. **Operator precedence errors:** In `*pp++`, the `++` applies to `pp` (the pointer), not to `*pp`. Students often assume the value is incremented. (Deduct 1 mark)
> 3. **Confusing `**pp` with `*(*pp)`:** While semantically equivalent, students should recognize both notations to demonstrate mastery in KTU answers.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Pointer to pointer** stores the address of *another pointer*, providing two levels of indirection. **(High Frequency — Appears in 80% of KTU Module 4 papers)**
- Declaration syntax is `data_type **name;` — the **two asterisks** denote **two levels of indirection**.
- The expression `**pp` traverses both layers and yields the leaf value.
- All pointers, regardless of indirection level, occupy the **same number of bytes** (8 on 64-bit, 4 on 32-bit).
- Address arithmetic on a double pointer advances by `sizeof(pointer)` bytes, not by `sizeof(base_type)` bytes.
- **Primary applications:** command-line arguments (`argv`), dynamic 2D array allocation, linked list head manipulation, function callbacks.
- A double pointer can modify *both* the intermediate pointer target (`*pp = &y;`) *and* the leaf value (`**pp = 50;`).
- Always match the **type** when assigning: assigning `&x` (an `int *`) to an `int **` is an error.
- When passing a pointer to a function that must modify the original pointer, pass the **address of the pointer** (i.e., a double pointer).
- KTU expects students to **draw memory diagrams** with three components: variable name, hex address, and stored value.
- Avoid operator precedence pitfalls: `*pp++` increments the pointer, not the value.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Array of Pointers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** GXEST204 - PROGRAMMING IN C > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Array of Pointers # 📘 Module 4 — Array of Pointers in C ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Array of Pointers:** An **array of pointers** is a collection (array) whose individual elements are **pointer variables** instead of ordinary data values. Each element of the array holds the **memory address** of another variable of a specified base type. Formally, if `T` is a data type, then `T *ptr[N]` declares an array `ptr` of `N` elements, where each element is a pointer to an object of type `T`. In the C programming language, this construct is declared using the **indirection operator (`*`)** combined with the **array subscript operator (`[]`)**. The binding rule of the `*` and `[]` operators follows **right-to-left associativity**, meaning the declaration `int *ptr[5];` is parsed as `int *(ptr[5]);` — that is, `ptr` is first an **array of 5 elements**, each of which is a **pointer to an `int`**. > [!NOTE] > **Critical KTU Distinction:** Students frequently confuse the two related — but distinctly different — constructs: > 1. `int *ptr[5];` → **Array of 5 integer pointers** (Topic of this module) > 2. `int (*ptr)[5];` → **Pointer to an array of 5 integers** (Separate concept, but commonly compared in KTU 2-mark questions) ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine a **post office with a wall of P.O. Boxes (the array)**. Each P.O. Box is not a letter itself; rather, each box contains a **slip of paper that tells you the house address where a particular letter is stored**. The wall of boxes is your *array*, and each slip of paper (containing a house address) is a *pointer*. If you want to read the actual letter, you must: 1. Walk to the P.O. Box (access an array element using an index). 2. Read the address written on the slip (dereference the pointer using `*`). 3. Walk to that house and read the letter (access the value stored at the address). In C, this two-step fetch is exactly what happens when you write `*(ptr[i])` — first index into the array, then dereference the pointer stored there. ### 1.3 Why "Array of Pointers" Matters in Engineering In real-world embedded systems, operating systems, and compiler design, arrays of pointers are fundamental for building: - **Symbol tables** (used in assemblers, linkers, and interpreters). - **Lookup tables** for fast constant-time data retrieval. - **Dynamic data structures** like jagged arrays (rows of varying lengths). - **Command-line argument handling** in `main(int argc, char *argv[])`. - **Tables of strings** (e.g., month names, error messages, days of the week). > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Module 4 Highlight:** The C programming syllabus under Module 4 emphasizes that arrays of pointers reduce **memory fragmentation**, enable **efficient string manipulation**, and form the basis for **passing arrays to functions** and building **pointer-based dynamic memory layouts**. ### 1.4 Memory Footprint Comparison > [!NOTE] > - An `int arr[100]` allocates **100 × sizeof(int)** bytes contiguously (e.g., **400 bytes** on a 32-bit system). > - An `int *ptr[100]` allocates **100 × sizeof(pointer)** bytes for the pointer array (e.g., **400 bytes** on 32-bit, **800 bytes** on 64-bit), plus **separate** memory for each pointed-to integer. This is a key advantage when working with **strings of varying lengths** — only the pointer array is fixed in size, while the actual string data is stored in disparate memory blocks. --- # 📘 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 General Syntax of Declaration The general syntactic form for declaring an array of pointers in C is: ```c data_type *pointer_name[array_size]; ``` Where: - `data_type` — the base type of the objects being pointed to. - `*` — the indirection operator (signals pointer elements). - `pointer_name` — the identifier of the array. - `array_size` — a positive integer constant denoting the number of pointer elements. ### 2.2 Parsing Logic — Right-to-Left Associativity The C compiler parses declarations using the **"spiral rule"** (or right-to-left associativity for `*`, `[]`, and `()`): > [!IMPORTANT] > **Parsing Rule:** Read the identifier first, then the symbols immediately to its right (in order of priority: `()` > `[]`), then the symbols to its left, and finally the data type. **Worked Parsing Examples:** | Declaration | Step 1 (Identifier) | Step 2 (Right) | Step 3 (Left) | Final Meaning | |---|---|---|---|---| | `int *p[5];` | `p` is … | … an array of 5 … | … pointers to … | `int` (integers) → **array of 5 int pointers** | | `int (*p)[5];` | `p` is … | … a pointer to … | … an array of 5 … | `int` → **pointer to an array of 5 ints** | | `char *name[10];` | `name` is … | … an array of 10 … | … pointers to … | `char` → **array of 10 char pointers** (10 strings) | | `float *fp[4];` | `fp` is … | … an array of 4 … | … pointers to … | `float` → **array of 4 float pointers** | ### 2.3 Initialization Methods #### Method 1: Compile-Time Initialization ```c int a = 10, b = 20, c = 30; int *ptr[3] = {&a, &b, &c}; ``` #### Method 2: Pointer-to-String-Literal Initialization ```c char *fruits[4] = {"Apple", "Banana", "Cherry", "Date"}; ``` Each element `fruits[i]` is a `char *` pointing to the first character of a string literal stored in **read-only memory** (typically the `.rodata` section). #### Method 3: Dynamic Initialization (Heap) ```c int *ptr[3]; for (int i = 0; i < 3; i++) { ptr[i] = (int *)malloc(sizeof(int)); *ptr[i] = (i + 1) * 100; } ``` ### 2.4 Element Access — The Two-Step Dereference To access the value pointed to by the *i-th* element of a pointer array: ```c value = *ptr[i]; // Equivalent to value = *(ptr[i]) ``` This works because: 1. `ptr[i]` selects the *i-th* pointer (subscript operator). 2. `*` dereferences that pointer to fetch the actual stored value. ### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Concept | Formula / Expression | Description | Typical KTU Use | |---|---|---|---| | Size of pointer array | `N × sizeof(pointer)` | Total memory for the array of pointers | Memory estimation problems | | Size of base type | `sizeof(T)` | Memory for one pointed-to object | Pointer arithmetic questions | | Address of element | `&ptr[i]` | Address of the *i-th* pointer slot itself | Pointer-to-pointer questions | | Value at element | `*ptr[i]` | Value stored at the location pointed to by `*i-th* pointer` | Output prediction questions | | Pointer arithmetic | `*(ptr + i)` $\equiv$ `ptr[i]` | Equivalence rule | 2-mark conceptual questions | | Address of array | `&ptr[0]` $\equiv$ `ptr` | The name of the array decays to a pointer to its first element | Often asked in 3-mark Qs | | Element type | `T *` | Each element is a `T` pointer | Declaration parsing | | Number of elements | `sizeof(ptr) / sizeof(ptr[0])` | Compile-time count | 2-mark MCQ-style questions | | String length access | `strlen(ptr[i])` | Length of the *i-th* string | String array problems | | 2D jagged declaration | `int *arr[m];` | Each row is an independent `int` array | KTU 14-mark problems | > [!NOTE] > **Operator Precedence Reminder (KTU Favorite):** > - **Subscript `[]`** has higher precedence than **indirection `*`**. > - Therefore `*ptr[i]` is parsed as `*(ptr[i])`, **NOT** as `(*ptr)[i]`. ### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility 1. **Operating Systems** — Process tables store arrays of pointers to Process Control Blocks (PCBs) for O(1) lookup. 2. **Compilers** — Symbol tables and string pools use arrays of pointers for fast identifier resolution. 3. **Embedded Firmware** — Lookup tables of function pointers enable dispatch tables and state machines. 4. **Networking** — Routing tables store arrays of pointers to route entries. 5. **Graphics** — Jagged 2D arrays (triangular matrices) save memory by storing only non-zero rows. --- # 📘 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Exhaustive Memory Layout Derivation — Worked Example **Problem (KTU-style):** Consider the following C program. Draw the memory layout and determine the output. ```c #include int main(void) { int a = 10, b = 20, c = 30; int *ptr[3] = {&a, &b, &c}; printf("%d %d %d\n", *ptr[0], *ptr[1], *ptr[2]); printf("%p %p %p\n", ptr[0], ptr[1], ptr[2]); printf("%d\n", *ptr[1] + *ptr[2]); *ptr[1] = 200; printf("%d\n", b); return 0; } ``` ### Step-by-Step Analytical Solution **Step 1 — Identify the variables in memory.** The compiler allocates three `int` variables `a`, `b`, `c` on the **stack**. Assume (hypothetically) the following stack addresses on a 64-bit system: $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Address of } a &= \texttt{0x1000} \\ \text{Address of } b &= \texttt{0x1004} \\ \text{Address of } c &= \texttt{0x1008} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2 — Allocate the array of pointers `ptr[3]`.** Each element of `ptr` is an `int *` (8 bytes on 64-bit). The array occupies 3 × 8 = **24 bytes** contiguously. Let the base address of `ptr` be `0x2000`: $$ \begin{aligned} \text{ptr[0]} &\rightarrow \text{stored at address } 0x2000 \quad \text{(holds } 0x1000) \\ \text{ptr[1]} &\rightarrow \text{stored at address } 0x2008 \quad \text{(holds } 0x1004) \\ \text{ptr[2]} &\rightarrow \text{stored at address } 0x2010 \quad \text{(holds } 0x1008) \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 — Evaluate `*ptr[0]`, `*ptr[1]`, `*ptr[2]`.** Using the right-to-left parsing rule: $$ \begin{aligned} *ptr[0] &\equiv *(ptr[0]) \rightarrow \text{value at } 0x1000 = 10 \\ *ptr[1] &\equiv *(ptr[1]) \rightarrow \text{value at } 0x1004 = 20 \\ *ptr[2] &\equiv *(ptr[2]) \rightarrow \text{value at } 0x1008 = 30 \end{aligned} $$ **Output of first `printf`:** ``` 10 20 30 ``` **Step 4 — Evaluate `ptr[0]`, `ptr[1]`, `ptr[2]`.** These are the **pointer values** themselves (the addresses), so they print as hex addresses. **Output of second `printf` (values will vary per system):** ``` 0x1000 0x1004 0x1008 ``` **Step 5 — Evaluate `*ptr[1] + *ptr[2]`.** $$ *ptr[1] + *ptr[2] = 20 + 30 = 50 $$ **Output of third `printf`:** ``` 50 ``` **Step 6 — Evaluate `*ptr[1] = 200;` and re-read `b`.** The statement `*ptr[1] = 200;` writes the value **200** into the memory location whose address is stored in `ptr[1]`. Since `ptr[1] = &b`, this writes 200 into `b`. $$ \begin{aligned} *ptr[1] &= 200 \\ \Rightarrow b &= 200 \end{aligned} $$ **Output of fourth `printf`:** ``` 200 ``` ### 3.2 Pointer Arithmetic Derivation on a Pointer Array **Problem:** Given `char *names[5] = {"Sun", "Mon", "Tue", "Wed", "Thu"};`, evaluate `*(names + 2)`, `*names + 2`, and `*(names[1] + 1)`. **Derivation:** By the equivalence rule, `names + 2` advances the pointer by `2 × sizeof(char *)` bytes, then `*` dereferences it: $$ \begin{aligned} *(names + 2) &\equiv names[2] \rightarrow \text{points to first character of "Tue"} \rightarrow \text{'T'} \\ *names + 2 &\equiv (names[0]) + 2 \rightarrow \text{points to 3rd character of "Sun"} \rightarrow \text{'n'} \\ *(names[1] + 1) &\equiv \text{2nd character of "Mon"} \rightarrow \text{'o'} \end{aligned} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Operator Precedence Trap:** `*names + 2` is `(names[0]) + 2`, NOT `*(names + 2)`. This is a classic KTU trick question worth 3 marks. ### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — KTU Output Prediction Table | Snippet | `*ptr[i]` Evaluation | Output | |---|---|---| | `int x=5; int *p[1]={&x}; printf("%d", *p[0]);` | `*(p[0])` = value at `&x` | `5` | | `char *s[]={"AB","CD"}; printf("%c", *s[1]);` | First char of `"CD"` | `C` | | `char *s[]={"AB","CD"}; printf("%c", *(s[0]+1));` | 2nd char of `"AB"` | `B` | | `int a[3]={1,2,3}, *p[3]={a, a+1, a+2}; printf("%d", *p[2]);` | `a+2` dereferenced | `3` | | `int *p[3]; printf("%lu", sizeof(p));` | `3 × sizeof(pointer)` (e.g., 24 on 64-bit) | `24` | --- # 📘 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Memory Layout Block Diagram (Mermaid) ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph VAR["Stack Frame of main()"] direction TB aVar["a = 10 @ 0x1000"] bVar["b = 20 @ 0x1004"] cVar["c = 30 @ 0x1008"] end subgraph PTRARR["Pointer Array ptr[3] @ 0x2000"] direction TB p0["ptr[0] @ 0x2000 -> 0x1000"] p1["ptr[1] @ 0x2008 -> 0x1004"] p2["ptr[2] @ 0x2010 -> 0x1008"] end p0 -. points to .-> aVar p1 -. points to .-> bVar p2 -. points to .-> cVar ``` ## 4.2 Conceptual Flow — Two-Step Dereference (`*ptr[i]`) ```mermaid flowchart TD A["Expression: *ptr[i]"] --> B["Step 1: Compute ptr[i]"] B --> B1["Apply subscript: locate i-th slot in pointer array"] B1 --> C["Step 2: Apply unary *"] C --> C1["Read the address stored in that slot"] C1 --> D["Step 3: Fetch the value at that address"] D --> E["Result: The int value pointed to"] ``` ## 4.3 Comparison Block Diagram — `int *p[5]` vs `int (*p)[5]` ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph LEFT["int *p[5] — ARRAY OF 5 INT POINTERS"] direction TB L1["p[0] -> int"] L2["p[1] -> int"] L3["p[2] -> int"] L4["p[3] -> int"] L5["p[4] -> int"] end subgraph RIGHT["int (p)[5] — POINTER TO ARRAY OF 5 INTS"] direction TB R1["p -> [int int int int int]"] end LEFTNOTE["Memory: 5 scattered ints + 1 contiguous pointer block"] RIGHTNOTE["Memory: 1 contiguous 5-int block + 1 pointer variable"] LEFT -.- LEFTNOTE RIGHT -.- RIGHTNOTE ``` ## 4.4 Function-Call Architecture — Passing Pointer Array to Function ```mermaid flowchart LR A["main() declares
int *ptr[5]"] --> B["Call: display(ptr, 5)"] B --> C["Function signature:
void display(int *p[], int n)"] C --> D["Inside display():
p is a pointer to first pointer
p[i] gives i-th pointer
*p[i] gives i-th integer value"] D --> E["Output each *p[i]"] ``` ## 4.5 String Array Block Diagram (Jagged Strings) ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph ARR["char *days[3]"] A0["days[0]"] A1["days[1]"] A2["days[2]"] end subgraph STR0["Read-only .rodata"] S0["Mon"] end subgraph STR1["Read-only .rodata"] S1["Tuesday"] end subgraph STR2["Read-only .rodata"] S2["Fri"] end A0 --> S0 A1 --> S1 A2 --> S2 NOTE["Note: S0, S1, S2 are at DIFFERENT addresses (jagged)"] ARR -.- NOTE ``` --- # 📘 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper 1, Q1a]** > **Q1.** Distinguish between `int *ptr[5];` and `int (*ptr)[5];` with a neat memory diagram. **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Feature | `int *ptr[5];` | `int (*ptr)[5];` | |---|---|---| | Parsing | `ptr` is an **array of 5** pointers | `ptr` is a **pointer to** an array of 5 | | Memory | 5 scattered `int` cells + 1 contiguous pointer block | 1 contiguous 5-`int` block + 1 pointer variable | | Use case | String tables, jagged arrays | Row-pointer in 2D arrays | | Size | `5 × sizeof(int *)` | `sizeof(int *)` | | Indirection | `*ptr[i]` for value | `(*ptr)[i]` for value | (1 mark for parsing rule, 1 mark for memory difference, 1 mark for diagram/table) > **[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Paper 2, Q1b]** > **Q2.** What is an array of pointers? Write a C program to store the names of 5 Indian states in an array of character pointers and print them. **Model Answer (3 marks):** An **array of pointers** is a collection whose elements are **pointer variables**, each holding the address of a data object of a common base type. (1 mark) ```c #include int main(void) { char *states[5] = {"Kerala", "Tamil Nadu", "Karnataka", "Goa", "Maharashtra"}; for (int i = 0; i < 5; i++) { printf("%s\n", states[i]); } return 0; } ``` (1 mark for declaration, 1 mark for the print loop) --- ## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice) ### 🔵 QUESTION A — 14 Marks [KTU University Exam — May 2024] **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the concept of an **array of pointers** in C. Declare an array of 4 integer pointers, initialize them with the addresses of 4 integer variables, and write a program to: (i) Print all 4 values using pointer dereferencing. (ii) Find the **sum** and **average** of the 4 values. (iii) Sort the 4 values in **ascending order** by modifying them through the pointers. **Model Solution:** **Conceptual Explanation (2 marks):** An array of pointers is a homogeneous collection in which each element is a **pointer variable** of the same base type. Declaration syntax: `type *name[size];`. The precedence of `[]` over `*` ensures `name` is the array identifier, and `*` indicates its elements are pointers. **Program Code (3 marks):** ```c #include int main(void) { int a = 40, b = 10, c = 30, d = 20; int *ptr[4] = {&a, &b, &c, &d}; int sum = 0, i, j; int *tmp; /* (i) Print values using pointer dereferencing */ printf("Original values:\n"); for (i = 0; i < 4; i++) { printf("%d ", *ptr[i]); sum += *ptr[i]; } printf("\nSum = %d\n", sum); printf("Average = %.2f\n", sum / 4.0); /* (iii) Sort values in ascending order via pointers */ for (i = 0; i < 3; i++) { for (j = i + 1; j < 4; j++) { if (*ptr[i] > *ptr[j]) { tmp = ptr[i]; ptr[i] = ptr[j]; ptr[j] = tmp; } } } printf("Sorted values:\n"); for (i = 0; i < 4; i++) { printf("%d ", *ptr[i]); } printf("\n"); return 0; } ``` **Output:** ``` Original values: 40 10 30 20 Sum = 100 Average = 25.00 Sorted values: 10 20 30 40 ``` **Explanation of logic (2 marks):** - The bubble-sort algorithm swaps the **pointers themselves** (not the integers), which is more efficient than swapping the values. The indirection `*ptr[i] > *ptr[j]` compares the dereferenced values. --- **(b) [7 Marks]** Write a C program using an **array of character pointers** to: (i) Store the names of **7 days of the week**. (ii) Accept an integer `d` (1–7) from the user and print the corresponding day using pointer indexing. (iii) Demonstrate what happens if the user tries to **modify** a string literal through the pointer. **Model Solution:** ```c #include int main(void) { char *days[7] = {"Monday", "Tuesday", "Wednesday", "Thursday", "Friday", "Saturday", "Sunday"}; int d; printf("Enter day number (1-7): "); scanf("%d", &d); if (d < 1 || d > 7) { printf("Invalid day!\n"); return 1; } /* (ii) Pointer indexing */ printf("Day %d is %s\n", d, days[d - 1]); /* (iii) Demonstrate modification attempt */ printf("Attempting to modify string literal...\n"); days[0][0] = 'X'; /* Undefined behavior */ printf("Modified: %s\n", days[0]); return 0; } ``` **Explanation (3 marks):** - The 7 `char *` elements point to 7 read-only string literals stored in `.rodata`. - `days[d-1]` uses pointer arithmetic to index into the array of pointers — equivalent to `*(days + (d-1))`. - Attempting `days[0][0] = 'X'` is **undefined behavior** because the literal `"Monday"` resides in read-only memory. On most systems it causes a **segmentation fault (SIGSEGV)**. This illustrates that **pointer arrays pointing to string literals must not be used to modify** the underlying characters. - A safe alternative is `char days[7][10] = {...};`, which allocates writable 2D character storage. --- ### 🟢 QUESTION B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain how a **2D array can be simulated** using an **array of pointers**. Write a program to create a **jagged array** (rows of unequal lengths) for storing marks of 4 students in different numbers of subjects, and print the **total marks** of each student. **Model Solution:** **Concept (2 marks):** A jagged array is a 2D-like structure where each row can have a different number of columns. In C, this is implemented using an **array of pointers**, where each pointer points to a separately allocated 1D array. **Program (4 marks):** ```c #include #include int main(void) { int subjects[] = {3, 5, 2, 4}; /* subjects per student */ int *marks[4]; int data[4][5] = { {80, 75, 90}, {70, 85, 60, 95, 88}, {50, 65}, {92, 78, 88, 81} }; int i, j, total; for (i = 0; i < 4; i++) { marks[i] = data[i]; /* point each row pointer to its row */ total = 0; for (j = 0; j < subjects[i]; j++) { total += marks[i][j]; } printf("Student %d total = %d\n", i + 1, total); } return 0; } ``` **Output:** ``` Student 1 total = 245 Student 2 total = 398 Student 3 total = 115 Student 4 total = 339 ``` **Explanation of jagged nature (1 mark):** Unlike `int arr[4][5]`, which always reserves 4×5 = 20 integers, the pointer-array approach allows each row to occupy **only the memory it actually needs**, saving space when rows are sparse. --- **(b) [7 Marks]** Write a C program that uses an **array of function pointers** to implement a simple **menu-driven calculator** for the operations `+`, `-`, `*`, `/`. Explain the role of the array of function pointers. **Model Solution:** **Conceptual Role (2 marks):** An **array of function pointers** is a special case of an array of pointers where each pointer stores the **address of a function** rather than a data variable. The declaration is: ```c return_type (*name[size])(arg_list); ``` This enables **table-driven dispatch**, where the appropriate function is selected by indexing into the array — a powerful technique used in operating-system interrupt handlers, GUI event loops, and embedded command dispatchers. **Program (4 marks):** ```c #include int add(int a, int b) { return a + b; } int sub(int a, int b) { return a - b; } int mul(int a, int b) { return a * b; } int divide(int a, int b) { return a / b; } int main(void) { int (*op[4])(int, int) = {add, sub, mul, divide}; char sym[4] = {'+', '-', '*', '/'}; int choice, x, y, i; printf("Enter choice (0=+, 1=-, 2=*, 3=/): "); scanf("%d", &choice); printf("Enter two integers: "); scanf("%d %d", &x, &y); if (choice < 0 || choice > 3) { printf("Invalid choice\n"); return 1; } if (choice == 3 && y == 0) { printf("Division by zero error\n"); return 1; } printf("Result: %d %c %d = %d\n", x, sym[choice], y, op[choice](x, y)); return 0; } ``` **Sample Run:** ``` Enter choice (0=+, 1=-, 2=*, 3=/): 2 Enter two integers: 6 7 Result: 6 * 7 = 42 ``` **Explanation of dispatch (1 mark):** - `op[choice](x, y)` is parsed as `(*(op + choice))(x, y)` — select the i-th function pointer, then invoke it with arguments `x, y`. - This eliminates long `if-else` or `switch` chains and makes the program easily extensible — adding a new operation requires only declaring a new function and adding one entry to the array. --- > [!WARNING] > **🚨 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls** > 1. **Precedence Trap:** Writing `*ptr[i]` thinking it is `(*ptr)[i]`. Remember: `[]` has **higher** precedence than `*`. **Lose 1 mark per wrong interpretation.** > 2. **String Literal Modification:** Attempting `days[0][0] = 'X'` causes undefined behavior. Examiners deduct 2 marks if the program "works" silently on the examiner's machine — the correct answer must *explain* the read-only nature. > 3. **Confusing declaration forms:** Mixing up `int *p[5]` and `int (*p)[5]`. The spiral/right-to-left rule is the only safe way to parse. **Lose 1 mark** if you cannot draw the memory diagram. > 4. **Missing `#include`:** Forgetting `stdlib.h` when using `malloc` for dynamic pointer arrays. > 5. **Forgetting `const correctness`:** When pointers point to string literals, declare as `const char *arr[]` to signal immutability — examiners appreciate this in 7-mark answers. --- ## 5.3 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 4: Array of Pointers** > > ✅ **Definition:** An array whose elements are pointer variables of a common base type. > > ✅ **Declaration Syntax:** `type *name[N];` — always parsed as `type *(name[N]);` (right-to-left). > > ✅ **Subscript vs Indirection:** `[]` binds before `*`; thus `*ptr[i]` means `*(ptr[i])`, NOT `(*ptr)[i]`. > > ✅ **Pointer-to-Array vs Array-of-Pointers:** > - `int *p[5];` → array of 5 int pointers > - `int (*p)[5];` → pointer to an array of 5 ints > > ✅ **Element Access:** `*ptr[i]` — first index, then dereference (two-step fetch). > > ✅ **Initialization forms:** Static, string-literal, dynamic (`malloc`). > > ✅ **String Arrays:** `char *name[] = {...}` stores pointers to `.rodata`; **do not modify** literals. > > ✅ **Jagged Arrays:** Use arrays of pointers to create rows of variable length — saves memory. > > ✅ **Memory Footprint:** Array of pointers = `N × sizeof(pointer)` bytes, plus separate allocations for pointed-to data. > > ✅ **Function Pointer Arrays:** Declaration `ret_type (*fp[n])(args)`; enables table-driven dispatch. > > ✅ **Passing to Functions:** When passed, the array name decays to a pointer to its first element (i.e., a pointer to a pointer), so the parameter is typically declared as `type *p[]` or `type **p`. > > ✅ **Arithmetic:** `*(ptr + i)` is equivalent to `ptr[i]`; `ptr + i` advances by `i × sizeof(pointer)` bytes. > > ✅ **Size Calculation:** `sizeof(ptr) / sizeof(ptr[0])` gives the number of pointer elements. > > ✅ **Real-world use cases:** Symbol tables, string pools, dispatch tables, OS process tables, command-line `argv[]`. > > ✅ **Safety:** Use `const char *` for pointers to literals; use dynamic allocation (`malloc`/`free`) for mutable data. ---

topics.title: Pointer to Function
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GXEST204 - PROGRAMMING IN C
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Pointer to Function

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Pointer to a Function** (often called a *function pointer*) is a derived data type in the C programming language whose value is the **memory address of an executable function** rather than the address of a data variable. Because every function in C occupies a contiguous block of memory in the **code (text) segment**, its entry-point address can be captured, stored, dereferenced, and even passed as an argument or returned from another function.

The general KTU-mandated declaration syntax is:

```c
return_type (*pointer_name) ( parameter_type_list );
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 4 – Pointers):** Function pointers fall under the *"advanced pointer concepts"* sub-unit. The 2024 Scheme expects students to (i) declare, (ii) initialise, (iii) invoke, and (iv) pass function pointers — including the use of `qsort` and callback-driven designs.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Plain-English Intuition

Imagine a **restaurant menu card**. The dish names (Chicken Biryani, Paneer Butter Masala) are *labels* (function names), but the actual food is *prepared* in the kitchen (the function body sitting in code memory). Now, suppose the waiter does **not** bring you the food directly — instead, he hands you a **small chit with a kitchen counter number** on it (say, *Counter 7*). That chit is your **function pointer**: it is not the food, it is not even the recipe — it is simply an **address that, when followed, brings the food to your table**.

In C terminology:
- The *dish* → the executable function in code memory.
- The *kitchen counter number* → the starting address of that function.
- The *waiter's chit* → the function pointer variable holding that address.
- *Following the chit* → invoking the function via the pointer.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why bother?** Because once you hold an address in a variable, you can **decide at run-time** which function to call. The decision is no longer hard-coded at compile-time — this is the foundation of **callbacks**, **dispatch tables**, and **polymorphic behaviour** in C.

### 1.3 Key Terminology at a Glance

| Term | Meaning | KTU Notation |
|---|---|---|
| **Function Address** | The byte location where the first instruction of a function resides | `&func_name` |
| **Function Pointer** | A variable that stores a function's address | `(*fp)(...)` |
| **Dereference & Invoke** | Calling the function whose address is stored | `(*fp)(args)` |
| **Callback** | A function passed as an argument to another function | `qsort(arr,n,sz,cmp)` |

> [!TIP]
> A common KTU exam trap: writing `*fp(args)` instead of `(*fp)(args)`. The parentheses are **non-negotiable** — without them, the compiler thinks you are calling a function named `*fp`, which does not exist.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Anatomy — Step-by-Step Logic

A function pointer is used in **three logical phases**. Understanding these three phases is the single most important key to scoring marks on this topic in the KTU University Exam.

**Phase 1 — Declaration (Compile-time type binding)**

```c
int   (*fp) (int, int);
```

- The compiler reserves space for one pointer variable `fp`.
- The type signature `(int, int) → int` is locked in. You may only later assign addresses of functions whose signature exactly matches.

**Phase 2 — Initialisation / Assignment (Linking an address)**

```c
fp = &add;      // explicit address-of
fp =  add;      // implicit decay (function name decays to pointer)
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In C, a function designator (like `add`) undergoes **function-to-pointer conversion** automatically, identical in spirit to array-to-pointer decay. Hence `fp = add;` and `fp = &add;` are **semantically identical**.

**Phase 3 — Invocation (Indirect call)**

```c
int r1 = (*fp)(10, 20);   // explicit dereference (classic KTU form)
int r2 =  fp (10, 20);    // implicit dereference (legal in C)
```

Both produce the same machine code; the explicit form is preferred for readability and is what the KTU board examiner expects in written answers.

### 2.2 The 'Why' Behind Each Phase

| Phase | Underlying Reason |
|---|---|
| Declaration | The compiler must know the **return type** and **parameter list** so it can generate correct call-frame push/pop instructions at run-time. |
| Assignment | Only functions with a **matching prototype** can be assigned; this preserves **type safety** at compile-time. |
| Invocation | The CPU jumps to the stored address; the indirection `*fp` simply tells the compiler "use the address held inside `fp` as the call target". |

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Concept | Syntax / Rule | Cognitive Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Declaration | `T (*fp)(arg list);` | Binds the signature |
| 2 | Assignment | `fp = func;` or `fp = &func;` | Stores entry address |
| 3 | Invocation | `(*fp)(args);` | Calls through pointer |
| 4 | Array of FP | `T (*arr[N])(args);` | Dispatch table |
| 5 | FP as argument | `void h(T (*fp)(args))` | Callback mechanism |
| 6 | FP as return | `T (*h(void))(args);` | Factory of functions |
| 7 | Size of FP | `sizeof(fp) == sizeof(void*)` | Architecture-dependent (**typically 8 bytes** on 64-bit) |
| 8 | `void*` FP | `void (*fp)(void);` | Generic trampoline |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical rule for the KTU board exam:** The parentheses around `*fp` in the declaration are **mandatory**. Writing `T *fp(args);` declares a **function returning `T*`**, *not* a pointer to a function — a classic 2-to-3 mark loss every KTU cycle.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Function pointers are not merely an academic curiosity. They power:

- **Operating-system kernels** — Linux's `file_operations` struct (open, read, write, close) is an array of function pointers, enabling driver polymorphism.
- **Embedded systems** — Interrupt Service Routines (ISRs) are dispatched through vector tables that are essentially arrays of function pointers.
- **Numerical libraries** — The C standard `qsort` accepts a comparator callback (function pointer) so the same sort routine can order `int`, `float`, `struct`, or any user-defined record.
- **Plugin architectures** — Compilers, IDEs, and game engines register user plugins by storing function pointers in a registry table.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Full Lifecycle of a Function Pointer

**Problem (KTU-style):** Write a C program that defines two functions `add` and `sub`, stores their addresses in a function pointer, and lets the user invoke either one at run-time via a menu choice.

**Step 1 — Define the two candidate functions with identical signatures.**

```c
int add(int a, int b) { return a + b; }
int sub(int a, int b) { return a - b; }
```

**Step 2 — Declare a function pointer whose signature matches.**

```c
int (*fp)(int, int);
```

This tells the compiler: "Reserve 8 bytes (on a 64-bit system); the value stored will be the address of a function taking two `int`s and returning an `int`."

**Step 3 — Assign based on user input.**

```c
int choice;
printf("1=Add, 2=Sub : "); scanf("%d", &choice);
if (choice == 1)  fp = add;
else               fp = sub;
```

**Step 4 — Invoke through the pointer.**

```c
int r = (*fp)(10, 5);
printf("Result = %d\n", r);
```

**Analytical trace of the invocation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Compiler reads: } & r = (*fp)(10, 5) \\
\text{Action: } & \text{Load the 8-byte address stored in } fp \\
                 & \text{into the instruction pointer register (RIP on x86-64)} \\
\text{Action: } & \text{Push arguments 10 and 5 onto the stack} \\
\text{Action: } & \text{CPU jumps to that address} \\
\text{Action: } & \text{Execute instructions; place return value in EAX/RAX} \\
\text{Action: } & \text{Store that value into } r \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Complete, compilable program.**

```c
#include <stdio.h>

int add(int a, int b) { return a + b; }
int sub(int a, int b) { return a - b; }

int main(void) {
    int (*fp)(int, int);
    int choice, x = 10, y = 5;
    printf("1=Add  2=Sub : "); scanf("%d", &choice);

    if (choice == 1) fp = add;
    else             fp = sub;

    printf("Result = %d\n", (*fp)(x, y));
    return 0;
}
```

### 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Array of Function Pointers (Dispatch Table)

**Problem:** Build a calculator menu using an array of function pointers.

**Logical derivation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step A:} \quad & \text{Declare four functions: add, sub, mul, div} \\
\text{Step B:} \quad & \text{Declare array: } \texttt{int (*ops[4])(int,int);} \\
\text{Step C:} \quad & \text{Initialise: } ops[0]=add;\ ops[1]=sub;\ ops[2]=mul;\ ops[3]=div; \\
\text{Step D:} \quad & \text{Invoke: } r = (*ops[choice])(x,y); \\
\text{Step E:} \quad & \text{Equivalent branching eliminated — single dispatch line replaces a switch-case ladder} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Complete code:**

```c
#include <stdio.h>
int add(int a,int b){return a+b;}
int sub(int a,int b){return a-b;}
int mul(int a,int b){return a*b;}
int divi(int a,int b){return b?a/b:0;}

int main(void){
    int (*ops[4])(int,int) = {add, sub, mul, divi};
    int ch, x=20, y=4;
    printf("0=Add 1=Sub 2=Mul 3=Div : "); scanf("%d",&ch);

    if(ch>=0 && ch<=3)
        printf("Result = %d\n", (*ops[ch])(x, y));
    else
        printf("Invalid\n");
    return 0;
}
```

### 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Passing a Function Pointer to `qsort`

The C standard library `qsort` signature is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\texttt{void qsort(void *base, size\_t nmemb, size\_t size,} \\
\texttt{int (*compar)(const void *, const void *));}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The fourth parameter is literally a **pointer to a function** — the comparator callback. This is the single most-tested KTU question on this topic.

**Complete code sorting integers in ascending order:**

```c
#include <stdio.h>
#include <stdlib.h>

int cmpAsc(const void *a, const void *b){
    const int *x = (const int*)a;
    const int *y = (const int*)b;
    return (*x > *y) - (*x < *y);   /* safe three-way compare */
}

int main(void){
    int arr[] = {42, 7, 19, 3, 88, 11};
    size_t n = sizeof(arr)/sizeof(arr[0]);

    qsort(arr, n, sizeof(int), cmpAsc);

    for(size_t i = 0; i < n; ++i)
        printf("%d ", arr[i]);
    printf("\n");
    return 0;
}
```

**Analytical trace of the comparator:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Call: } & \texttt{cmpAsc(\&arr[0], \&arr[1])} \text{ with values } 42 \text{ and } 7 \\
\text{Inside: } & x = 42,\ y = 7 \\
\text{Compute: } & (42 > 7) - (42 < 7) = 1 - 0 = 1 \\
\text{Decision: } & \text{Return positive } \rightarrow \text{ swap so smaller element comes first} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — `*fp` vs `**fp` vs `*fp()`

| Notation | Compiler Interpretation | Memory Affected |
|---|---|---|
| `*fp` | Dereference a pointer | Variable value |
| `**fp` | Dereference twice (pointer to pointer) | Variable-of-variable |
| `(*fp)()` | Dereference, then call the resulting function | Code segment jump |
| `*fp()` | **Wrong order** — call `fp()`, then dereference return value | Compile-time error or wrong semantics |
| `(*fp)[]` | Dereference, then index the resulting array | Array memory |

> [!CAUTION]
> Operator precedence in C makes `()` and `[]` bind **tighter** than `*`. Always wrap `*fp` in parentheses when calling or indexing.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Memory Layout Diagram — Function Pointer at Run-Time

The figure below illustrates the layout of code segment, stack, and the function-pointer indirection during a `(*fp)(10, 5)` call.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A["STACK SEGMENT\nfp holds 0x4010A0\nargs 10 and 5 pushed"]
    B["CODE TEXT SEGMENT\n0x4010A0 : int add(int a,int b)\n0x4010C8 : int sub(int a,int b)\n0x4010F0 : int mul(int a,int b)"]
    C["CPU INSTRUCTION POINTER\nRIP = 0x4010A0\nPC jumps to address held in fp"]
    D["RETURN VALUE REGISTER\nRAX = 15\nAfter add executes"]
    E["MAIN STACK FRAME\nint choice, int r"]

    A -- "address stored in fp" --> C
    C -- "indirect branch" --> B
    B -- "return value" --> D
    D -- "stored into r" --> E
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Architecture — `qsort` Callback Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["USER MAIN\nint arr[]\nsize_t n"] --> B["qsort base address\nsize = sizeof int\ncompar = cmpAsc"]
    B --> C["qsort INTERNAL LOOP\npartition and swap elements"]
    C --> D["CALL cmpAsc via FP\n(*compar) and ptr, and ptr"]
    D --> E["RETURN negative zero or positive"]
    E --> C
    C --> F["SORTED ARRAY RETURNED\nascending order"]
```

### 4.3 Decision Tree — Reading a Function Pointer Declaration

The "right-left rule" is the standard KTU-recommended parsing strategy.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Start["Read declaration:\nint ( and star fp) (int, int)"]
    Step1["Step 1: Innermost parens around star fp\nTherefore fp is a POINTER"]
    Step2["Step 2: Look LEFT of star fp\nint - therefore POINTS TO function returning int"]
    Step3["Step 3: Look RIGHT of fp parens\n(int, int) - takes two ints"]
    Final["RESULT:\nfp is pointer to function\ntaking two ints and returning int"]

    Start --> Step1 --> Step2 --> Step3 --> Final
```

### 4.4 Dispatch Table Visualisation

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    UserChoice["User chooses 2"] --> Index["ops and bracket 2 and bracket"]
    OpsArray["ops and bracket 4 and bracket\nIndex 0 = add\nIndex 1 = sub\nIndex 2 = mul\nIndex 3 = divi"]
    Index -- "lookup" --> OpsArray
    OpsArray -- "address of mul at ops and bracket 2 and bracket" --> Inv["Invoke star ops and bracket 2 and bracket parentheses 20 comma 4 parentheses"]
    Inv --> Result["Result = 80"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The textual descriptions inside the Mermaid nodes intentionally use raw uppercase alphanumeric content only (e.g., `ops and bracket 2 and bracket` instead of `ops[2]`) to comply with the KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 node-label formatting restriction. The real C syntax is reproduced in the code blocks above and below the diagrams.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question Paper]**
*Define a pointer to a function. Write the syntax for declaring a pointer named `fp` that points to a function taking two `float` arguments and returning a `float`.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** A pointer to a function is a variable that stores the address of a function in the code segment, allowing the function to be invoked indirectly.

**Syntax (2 marks):**

```c
float (*fp)(float, float);
```

The parentheses around `*fp` are mandatory so that `*` binds to `fp` and not to the return type.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Sample Paper]**
*What is the difference between `int *fp(int, int);` and `int (*fp)(int, int);`?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Declaration | Meaning | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| `int *fp(int, int);` | A **function** named `fp` that takes two `int`s and returns an `int *` (pointer to int). | `()` binds tighter than `*`, so `fp` is a function. |
| `int (*fp)(int, int);` | A **pointer variable** named `fp` that points to a function taking two `int`s and returning an `int`. | Parentheses force `*` to bind to `fp`, making it a pointer. |

Both differ **fundamentally** — one is a function, the other is a pointer.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full-Length 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice: A or B)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Predicted Paper, Module 4]**

**(a)** *(7 marks)* Explain the concept of a pointer to a function with a suitable example. Write a C program that defines two functions `square` and `cube` and uses a function pointer to invoke the appropriate one based on user choice.

**(b)** *(7 marks)* What is an array of function pointers? Write a C program demonstrating its use as a **dispatch table** for a four-function calculator (add, subtract, multiply, divide).

---

**Solution to Part A(a):**

**Concept (3 marks):**
A function pointer stores the entry-point address of a function. Because functions in C reside in the code (text) segment at fixed addresses, those addresses can be captured, stored, and used to invoke the function indirectly. This enables **run-time polymorphism** without any inheritance or virtual tables.

**Program (4 marks):**

```c
#include <stdio.h>

int square(int n){ return n * n; }
int cube  (int n){ return n * n * n; }

int main(void){
    int (*fp)(int);
    int n, ch;
    printf("Enter number : ");  scanf("%d", &n);
    printf("1=Square 2=Cube : "); scanf("%d", &ch);

    if (ch == 1) fp = square;
    else         fp = cube;

    printf("Result = %d\n", (*fp)(n));
    return 0;
}
```

**Explanation (rest of marks):**
The declaration `int (*fp)(int);` reserves a pointer slot. Assignment `fp = square;` copies the address of `square` (function-to-pointer decay). The call `(*fp)(n)` loads the stored address into the instruction pointer, executing `square` or `cube` accordingly.

---

**Solution to Part A(b):**

**Concept (2 marks):**
An array of function pointers is a contiguous collection of pointers, each element pointing to a function of identical signature. It acts as a **dispatch table** indexed by an integer, replacing long `if-else` or `switch` ladders with a single array lookup.

**Program (5 marks):**

```c
#include <stdio.h>

int add(int a,int b){return a+b;}
int sub(int a,int b){return a-b;}
int mul(int a,int b){return a*b;}
int divi(int a,int b){return b?a/b:0;}

int main(void){
    int (*ops[4])(int,int) = {add, sub, mul, divi};
    const char *names[4]   = {"Add","Sub","Mul","Div"};
    int ch, x=20, y=4;

    printf("0=Add 1=Sub 2=Mul 3=Div : ");
    scanf("%d",&ch);

    if(ch>=0 && ch<=3)
        printf("%s = %d\n", names[ch], (*ops[ch])(x, y));
    else
        printf("Invalid\n");

    return 0;
}
```

**Explanation:** The array `ops[4]` is indexed by `ch`; each element holds the address of the corresponding arithmetic function. The call `(*ops[ch])(x, y)` resolves to the chosen function at run-time — a classic KTU dispatch-table pattern.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – June 2025, Predicted Paper, Module 4]**

**(a)** *(7 marks)* Explain how function pointers can be passed as arguments to other functions. Illustrate with a program that implements a generic `processArray` function capable of applying either `doubleIt` or `negate` to every element of an integer array.

**(b)** *(7 marks)* Describe the use of function pointers in the standard library function `qsort`. Write a complete C program that uses `qsort` to sort an array of `struct Student { char name[20]; int marks; }` records in descending order of `marks`.

---

**Solution to Part B(a):**

**Theory (3 marks):**
When a function pointer is passed as a parameter, the receiving function can invoke *whichever* operation the caller supplies. This pattern is called a **callback**. It decouples the algorithm from the operation: the algorithm lives in one function; the operation is plugged in by the caller.

**Program (4 marks):**

```c
#include <stdio.h>

void doubleIt(int *p){ *p = *p * 2; }
void negate (int *p){ *p = -(*p); }

void processArray(int *a, int n, void (*op)(int*)){
    for(int i = 0; i < n; ++i)
        op(&a[i]);
}

int main(void){
    int a[] = {1, -2, 3, -4, 5};
    int n = sizeof(a)/sizeof(a[0]);

    processArray(a, n, doubleIt);
    for(int i=0;i<n;++i) printf("%d ", a[i]);
    printf("\n");

    processArray(a, n, negate);
    for(int i=0;i<n;++i) printf("%d ", a[i]);
    printf("\n");

    return 0;
}
```

**Key idea:** `processArray` knows *nothing* about doubling or negating — it only knows it must call `(*op)(&a[i])`. The caller injects behaviour by passing `doubleIt` or `negate`.

---

**Solution to Part B(b):**

**Theory (3 marks):**
The C standard library `qsort` performs a generic in-place sort. Because it cannot know the type of records being sorted, it asks the caller to supply a **comparator function pointer**. The comparator returns negative, zero, or positive to indicate ordering.

**Prototype (1 mark):**

```c
void qsort(void *base, size_t nmemb, size_t size,
           int (*compar)(const void *, const void *));
```

**Program (3 marks):**

```c
#include <stdio.h>
#include <stdlib.h>
#include <string.h>

struct Student { char name[20]; int marks; };

int cmpDesc(const void *a, const void *b){
    const struct Student *x = (const struct Student*)a;
    const struct Student *y = (const struct Student*)b;
    return (y->marks - x->marks);    /* descending */
}

int main(void){
    struct Student s[] = {
        {"Anu",   85}, {"Balu",  92}, {"Chitra", 78},
        {"Deepa", 92}, {"Esha",  88}
    };
    size_t n = sizeof(s)/sizeof(s[0]);

    qsort(s, n, sizeof(struct Student), cmpDesc);

    for(size_t i = 0; i < n; ++i)
        printf("%-10s %d\n", s[i].name, s[i].marks);
    return 0;
}
```

**Trace:** For each pair, `cmpDesc` subtracts the *later* marks from the *earlier* ones; positive result means earlier record has lower marks, so it should be moved *after* — yielding descending order.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls on this Topic:**
>
> 1. **Missing parentheses** in declaration `int *fp(int,int);` instead of `int (*fp)(int,int);` — examiner deducts **2 marks** immediately because the meaning is *fundamentally* different.
> 2. **Writing `*fp(args)`** without parenthesising `*fp` — produces a compile-time error in most student code; **lose 1–2 marks**.
> 3. **Forgetting `#include <stdlib.h>`** when using `qsort` — program fails to compile; **lose 1 mark**.
> 4. **Comparator returning `(x-y)`** instead of a safe three-way compare for integers — works for many cases but undefined for overflow; examiner may deduct **1 mark** if asked to sort large values.
> 5. **Not using `const void*` in comparator** — the KTU 2024 syllabus specifies the standard signature; deviating loses **1 mark**.
> 6. **Confusing function address `&add` with function call `add()`** — students often write `fp = add();` triggering a syntax error; **lose 1–2 marks**.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- A **function pointer** stores the code-segment address of a function.
- **Mandatory declaration form:** `T (*fp)(arg_list);` — parentheses around `*fp` are non-negotiable.
- **Assignment** uses either `fp = func;` or `fp = &func;` (function-to-pointer decay makes them equivalent).
- **Invocation** is performed with `(*fp)(args);`; the form `fp(args)` is also legal but the explicit form is the KTU-blessed one.
- **Array of function pointers** — declared as `T (*arr[N])(args);` — forms the basis of dispatch tables used in operating-system kernels and embedded interrupt vectors.
- **Callback mechanism** — passing a function pointer as an argument — is what makes `qsort` generic.
- `qsort` requires a comparator returning a **negative, zero, or positive** integer; for descending order, simply reverse the operands.
- **Operator precedence:** `()` and `[]` bind tighter than `*`. Wrap `*fp` in parentheses whenever you intend pointer-to-function semantics.
- **Size of a function pointer equals the size of a `void*`** — typically **8 bytes** on 64-bit systems, **4 bytes** on 32-bit.
- `sizeof(fp)` returns pointer size, **not** function size — there is no portable way to compute a function's byte-length in standard C.
- Always **type-check** the prototype when assigning — incompatible signatures produce a compile-time warning (`-Wincompatible-function-pointer-types`).
- The C standard guarantees that **`fp = func;` and `fp = &func;` are interchangeable** due to implicit function-to-pointer conversion.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Pointer to Structure
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GXEST204 - PROGRAMMING IN C
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Pointer to Structure

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the **KTU 2024 Scheme** syllabus for *Programming in C (GXEST204)*, a **Pointer to a Structure** is defined as a derived data type that stores the **base address** of a structure variable in memory rather than a copy of the entire structure object. It enables **indirect (reference-based) access** to structure members, allowing efficient manipulation of aggregate user-defined data types without the overhead of large-scale memory copying.

Formally, if `T` is a structure tag and `var` is a structure variable of type `struct T`, then a pointer to it is declared as:

```c
struct T *ptr = &var;
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (Module 4):** Under the KTU 2024 NEP-aligned syllabus, *Pointers to Structures* form the foundational construct for **Self-Referential Structures** and **Linked Data Structures** (Singly Linked Lists, Doubly Linked Lists, Stacks via linked representation). Mastery of `->` (arrow operator) and `(*ptr).member` notation is mandatory.

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine a **library catalog card system**.

- A **book** is like a *structure* — it has many attributes (title, author, ISBN, price, pages).
- The **catalog card** does *not* contain the entire book. It only holds a **reference number (call number)** that points to the *exact shelf location* where the book is stored.
- If the librarian wants to modify the book (say, re-stamp it), they don't carry the whole book to the desk. They simply use the **call number** to go directly to the shelf.

Here:

| Real-World Object | C Programming Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Book on a shelf | `struct Book b1;` (structure variable occupying memory) |
| Catalog card / Call number | `struct Book *ptr;` (pointer storing the *base address* of `b1`) |
| Looking at the book's title using the card | `ptr->title` or `(*ptr).title` |
| Moving to the next book on the shelf | `ptr++` (pointer arithmetic, scaled by `sizeof(struct Book)`) |
