# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**Q: Differentiate between text files and binary files in C. Mention the mode strings used to open each.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | Text File | Binary File |
|:-------|:----------|:------------|
| **Content** | Stores data as ASCII / UTF-8 characters | Stores data in raw binary (memory) format |
| **Newline Handling** | Translates `\n` to OS-specific line ending (e.g., CR+LF) | No translation — stored as raw `\n` |
| **EOF Detection** | Special `EOF` (`-1`) value returned | Detected by exhausting the file size |
| **Opening Mode** | `"r"`, `"w"`, `"a"`, `"r+"`, `"w+"`, `"a+"` | `"rb"`, `"wb"`, `"ab"`, `"rb+"`, `"wb+"`, `"ab+"` |
| **Readability** | Human-readable in any text editor | Not human-readable |
| **Recommended I/O** | `fprintf`, `fscanf`, `fgetc`, `fputc` | `fread`, `fwrite` |

*(1 mark for content difference, 1 mark for mode strings, 1 mark for I/O function recommendation.)*

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**Q: What is a `FILE` pointer in C? Why is it necessary to check whether `fopen()` returns `NULL`?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A **`FILE` pointer** is a pointer variable of type `FILE *` declared in `<stdio.h>`. It points to a `FILE` structure allocated by the C runtime when `fopen()` successfully opens a file. The structure contains internal I/O information such as the file position indicator, buffer pointer, error flags, and EOF indicator. The pointer is used by all subsequent file I/O functions (`fprintf`, `fread`, `fclose`, etc.) to identify which file is being operated upon.

It is **necessary to check whether `fopen()` returns `NULL`** because `fopen()` returns `NULL` in the following cases:

1. The file does not exist (in read mode `"r"`).
2. The path is invalid or the disk is full (in write mode `"w"`).
3. Insufficient file permissions or access denied.
4. Hardware I/O error.

If the returned pointer is `NULL` and the program proceeds to use it, the result is **undefined behaviour** — typically a segmentation fault. The safe pattern is:

```c
FILE *fp = fopen("data.txt", "r");
if (fp == NULL) {
    printf("Cannot open file!\n");
    return 1;  // Exit gracefully
}
```

*(1 mark for `FILE` pointer definition, 1 mark for structure contents, 1 mark for `NULL` check rationale.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU 2024 Examiner's Valuation Warning — Pitfall Callout:**
> In Module 4 Part-B questions on file handling, students **commonly lose marks** in the following ways. Memorize this list to avoid the most frequent deductions:
>
> 1. **Forgetting the `NULL` check after `fopen()`** — losing 1 to 2 marks per occurrence.
> 2. **Using `"r"` instead of `"rb"` for binary file operations** — losing 1 mark and introducing subtle bugs.
> 3. **Using `char ch = fgetc(fp);` instead of `int ch = fgetc(fp);`** — losing 1 mark for type mismatch.
> 4. **Forgetting to call `fclose(fp);`** — losing 1 mark and potentially causing data loss in evaluation.
> 5. **Confusing `fprintf` with `fscanf`** for the wrong direction (writing vs reading).
> 6. **Using `sizeof("string")` (which includes `\0`) instead of `strlen("string")`** for string lengths.
> 7. **Hardcoding record sizes (e.g., `64`) instead of using `sizeof(struct)`** — losing marks for portability.

---

### Part B — Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**Q (a)** Write a C program to create a binary file named `student.dat` and write **N** student records into it. Each record contains `roll_no` (int), `name` (char[40]), and `cgpa` (float). Use appropriate file I/O functions. **(7 Marks)**

**Q (b)** Write a C program to read the binary file `student.dat` created above and display all student records whose `cgpa` is **greater than or equal to 8.0**. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution for Question A (a) — Writing to Binary File

```c
#include <stdio.h>

struct Student {
    int roll_no;
    char name[40];
    float cgpa;
};

int main(void) {
    struct Student s;
    FILE *fp;
    int n, i;

    /* Open file in binary write mode */
    fp = fopen("student.dat", "wb");
    if (fp == NULL) {
        printf("Error: Cannot create student.dat\n");
        return 1;
    }

    /* Read number of students */
    printf("Enter number of students: ");
    scanf("%d", &n);
    getchar();  /* consume the trailing newline */

    /* Read and write each student record */
    for (i = 0; i < n; i++) {
        printf("\n--- Student %d ---\n", i + 1);
        printf("Roll Number : ");
        scanf("%d", &s.roll_no);
        getchar();
        printf("Name        : ");
        fgets(s.name, sizeof(s.name), stdin);
        /* Remove trailing newline from fgets */
        /* Strip the newline character if present */
        {
            char *nl = strchr(s.name, '\n');
            if (nl != NULL) *nl = '\0';
        }
        printf("CGPA        : ");
        scanf("%f", &s.cgpa);
        getchar();

        /* Write the record to the binary file */
        fwrite(&s, sizeof(s), 1, fp);
    }

    fclose(fp);
    printf("\n%d student record(s) successfully written to student.dat\n", n);
    return 0;
}
```

**Step-by-Step Explanation (Model Solution Walkthrough):**

1. **Line 1-8:** Include `<stdio.h>` and `<string.h>` (for `strchr`) and define the `Student` structure. The structure has three members with total size `4 + 40 + 4 = 48 bytes` (padded to 48 by most compilers).
2. **Line 14-17:** Open the file `"student.dat"` in binary write mode (`"wb"`). The `wb` mode creates the file if it does not exist, or **truncates it to zero length** if it does. The `NULL` check is mandatory.
3. **Line 20-21:** Read the number of students `N` from the user.
4. **Line 24-39:** Loop `N` times, reading each field using `scanf` and `fgets`. The `fgets` is used for the `name` to safely read strings with spaces. The `getchar()` calls consume stray newlines.
5. **Line 40-41:** Use `fwrite(&s, sizeof(s), 1, fp)` to write **one** structure of size `sizeof(s)` to the file. The `&s` is the address of the structure in memory.
6. **Line 43-44:** Close the file using `fclose()` and report success.

**Mark Distribution (7 marks):**
- Structure definition and `main` function setup: **1 mark**
- `fopen` with `"wb"` mode and `NULL` check: **1 mark**
- Reading input using `scanf` and `fgets`: **2 marks**
- Correct `fwrite` call with `&s, sizeof(s), 1, fp`: **2 marks**
- `fclose` and graceful exit: **1 mark**

#### Model Solution for Question A (b) — Reading and Filtering from Binary File

```c
#include <stdio.h>

struct Student {
    int roll_no;
    char name[40];
    float cgpa;
};

int main(void) {
    struct Student s;
    FILE *fp;
    int found = 0;

    /* Open file in binary read mode */
    fp = fopen("student.dat", "rb");
    if (fp == NULL) {
        printf("Error: Cannot open student.dat\n");
        return 1;
    }

    printf("\n=== Students with CGPA >= 8.0 ===\n");
    printf("%-10s %-40s %-6s\n", "Roll No", "Name", "CGPA");
    printf("--------------------------------------------------\n");

    /* Read records one-by-one until EOF */
    while (fread(&s, sizeof(s), 1, fp) == 1) {
        if (s.cgpa >= 8.0f) {
            printf("%-10d %-40s %-6.2f\n", s.roll_no, s.name, s.cgpa);
            found = 1;
        }
    }

    if (!found) {
        printf("No student has CGPA >= 8.0\n");
    }

    fclose(fp);
    return 0;
}
```

**Step-by-Step Explanation (Model Solution Walkthrough):**

1. **Line 11-15:** Open the file in binary read mode (`"rb"`). The `rb` mode opens the file **only if it exists**; otherwise it returns `NULL`.
2. **Line 21-23:** Print a formatted header.
3. **Line 26:** The `while` loop reads one record at a time using `fread(&s, sizeof(s), 1, fp)`. The loop continues as long as `fread` returns `1` (one item read). When EOF is reached, `fread` returns `0` and the loop exits.
4. **Line 27-30:** For each record read, check if `s.cgpa >= 8.0f`. If true, print the record in a formatted manner. The `%-10d`, `%-40s`, `%-6.2f` format specifiers left-align the output in fixed-width columns.
5. **Line 32-34:** If no matching record was found, print a message.
6. **Line 36-37:** Close the file and exit.

**Mark Distribution (7 marks):**
- `fopen` with `"rb"` mode and `NULL` check: **1 mark**
- `while` loop using `fread` with correct return check: **2 marks**
- CGPA comparison and formatted output: **2 marks**
- `feof` or return-value-based loop termination: **1 mark**
- `fclose` and clean exit: **1 mark**

---

### Part B — Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2024 — Alternative Choice]

**Q (a)** Explain the different file opening modes in C with suitable examples. Create a text file `"notes.txt"` and write five lines of text entered by the user into it. **(7 Marks)**

**Q (b)** Write a C program to count the **number of lines, words, and characters** in a text file named `"article.txt"`. Display the counts on the screen. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution for Question B (a) — Opening Modes and Writing Text File

**Theoretical Explanation (4 marks):**

The standard file opening modes in C are summarized below:

| Mode | Purpose |
|:-----|:--------|
| `"r"` | Read from existing text file. Returns `NULL` if file does not exist. |
| `"w"` | Write to text file. **Truncates** existing file or creates new one. |
| `"a"` | Append to text file. Writes start at **end of existing file**. |
| `"r+"` | Read and write existing text file. File must exist. |
| `"w+"` | Read and write — truncates existing file or creates new. |
| `"a+"` | Read and append — file is created if it does not exist. |
| `"rb"`, `"wb"`, `"ab"`, `"rb+"`, `"wb+"`, `"ab+"` | Same as above but in **binary mode** (no newline translation). |

**Program Code (3 marks):**

```c
#include <stdio.h>

int main(void) {
    FILE *fp;
    char line[200];
    int i;

    fp = fopen("notes.txt", "w");
    if (fp == NULL) {
        printf("Error opening notes.txt for writing.\n");
        return 1;
    }

    printf("Enter 5 lines of text:\n");
    for (i = 0; i < 5; i++) {
        printf("Line %d: ", i + 1);
        fgets(line, sizeof(line), stdin);
        fputs(line, fp);
    }

    fclose(fp);
    printf("5 lines successfully written to notes.txt\n");
    return 0;
}
```

**Explanation Walkthrough:**

1. **`fopen("notes.txt", "w")`**: Opens the file in text write mode. If the file exists, its contents are erased (truncated). If it does not exist, a new file is created.
2. **`fgets(line, sizeof(line), stdin)`**: Reads a line of text from standard input (keyboard) including spaces. The buffer `line` can hold up to 199 characters plus the null terminator.
3. **`fputs(line, fp)`**: Writes the string to the file pointed to by `fp`. The newline character (if present in `line`) is preserved.
4. **`fclose(fp)`**: Closes the file, ensuring the buffer is flushed to disk.

**Mark Distribution (7 marks):**
- Explanation of opening modes: **3 marks**
- Correct `fopen` mode and `NULL` check: **1 mark**
- Loop with `fgets` and `fputs`: **2 marks**
- `fclose` and proper termination: **1 mark**

#### Model Solution for Question B (b) — Counting Lines, Words, and Characters in Text File

```c
#include <stdio.h>
#include <ctype.h>  /* for isspace() */

int main(void) {
    FILE *fp;
    int ch;
    int lines = 0, words = 0, characters = 0;
    int inWord = 0;  /* flag: are we inside a word? */

    fp = fopen("article.txt", "r");
    if (fp == NULL) {
        printf("Error: article.txt not found.\n");
        return 1;
    }

    /* Read the file character by character until EOF */
    while ((ch = fgetc(fp)) != EOF) {
        characters++;

        if (ch == '\n') {
            lines++;
        }

        if (isspace(ch)) {
            inWord = 0;
        } else if (inWord == 0) {
            inWord = 1;
            words++;
        }
    }

    fclose(fp);

    printf("\n=== File Statistics for article.txt ===\n");
    printf("Characters : %d\n", characters);
    printf("Words      : %d\n", words);
    printf("Lines      : %d\n", lines);

    return 0;
}
```

**Step-by-Step Explanation (Model Solution Walkthrough):**

1. **Line 5:** Declare counters for `lines`, `words`, and `characters`, and a flag `inWord` to track word boundaries.
2. **Line 7-10:** Open the file in text read mode (`"r"`). If the file does not exist, `fopen` returns `NULL` and the program exits gracefully.
3. **Line 13:** The `while` loop reads one character at a time using `fgetc(fp)`. The expression `(ch = fgetc(fp)) != EOF` is the standard idiom for file reading — assignment inside condition is intentional.
4. **Line 14:** Every successfully read character increments `characters`.
5. **Line 16-18:** If the character is a newline (`'\n'`), increment the line counter.
6. **Line 20-26:** **Word counting logic:**
   - If the current character is whitespace (space, tab, newline, etc.) — set `inWord = 0`.
   - Else if we are NOT currently in a word (`inWord == 0`) — it means we are entering a new word, so increment `words` and set `inWord = 1`.
   - This state-machine logic correctly handles multiple consecutive spaces and punctuation.
7. **Line 29:** Close the file.
8. **Line 31-34:** Print the counts in a formatted manner.

**Mark Distribution (7 marks):**
- Correct `fopen("article.txt", "r")` and `NULL` check: **1 mark**
- `fgetc` loop with `EOF` check: **1 mark**
- Character counter logic: **1 mark**
- Line counter using `'\n'` detection: **1 mark**
- Word counter using state-machine logic with `inWord` flag: **2 marks**
- `fclose` and formatted output: **1 mark**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU 2024 — Common Mistake in Word Counting:**
> A naive approach is to count spaces and add 1, but this fails when:
> - There are multiple consecutive spaces.
> - There are leading/trailing spaces.
> - Tabs are present.
>
> The `inWord` state-machine flag pattern shown above is the **exam-safe** method that handles all edge cases. Avoid the naive approach to prevent losing 1 to 2 marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

This is a **rapid-revision checklist** for the last 10 minutes before the KTU 2024 exam. Tick these off in your head.

- [ ] A **file** in C is a named sequence of bytes stored on secondary memory, accessed via a `FILE *` pointer.
- [ ] The **two types** of files are **Text Files** and **Binary Files**.
- [ ] **Text files** store ASCII/UTF-8 characters; undergo newline translation; are human-readable; opened with `"r"`, `"w"`, `"a"`, etc.
- [ ] **Binary files** store raw memory bytes; no translation; not human-readable; opened with `"rb"`, `"wb"`, `"ab"`, etc.
- [ ] The **only technical difference** is the `"b"` in the mode string passed to `fopen()`.
- [ ] Every file operation follows the **Open → Process → Close** lifecycle.
- [ ] **Always check** if `fopen()` returns `NULL` before using the file pointer.
- [ ] **Always call `fclose()`** to flush buffers and release the OS file handle.
- [ ] Use `int ch = fgetc(fp);` — **never** `char ch = fgetc(fp);` — to handle `EOF` correctly.
- [ ] Use `fprintf`/`fscanf` (formatted) and `fgetc`/`fputc` (character) for **text** files.
- [ ] Use `fread`/`fwrite` (block) for **binary** files; pass `&structure` and `sizeof(structure)`.
- [ ] The **EOF constant** is defined as `-1` in `<stdio.h>`.
- [ ] For random access, use `fseek(fp, offset, SEEK_SET/SEEK_CUR/SEEK_END)` and `ftell(fp)`.
- [ ] The seek offset for the **N-th record** in a binary file is $(N-1) \times \text{sizeof(struct)}$.
- [ ] The number of records in a binary file is $\text{ftell at EOF} \div \text{sizeof(struct)}$.
- [ ] Standard streams `stdin`, `stdout`, `stderr` are pre-opened files.
- [ ] Default I/O is **buffered** with `BUFSIZ` bytes (typically 512 or 1024) — call `fflush()` or `fclose()` to force a write.
- [ ] Binary files are **faster**, **smaller**, and **support random access** better than text files.
- [ ] Binary files are **less portable** across architectures with different endianness or type sizes.
- [ ] For Module 4, expect a **3-mark definitional** question and a **14-mark programming** question in the ESE.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU 2024 Strategy Tip:**
> In your answer sheet, **always explicitly mention** whether the file mode is text (`"w"`) or binary (`"wb"`) — even if the question only says "write to a file." Examiners award 1 mark specifically for choosing the correct mode. Defaulting to `"w"` for a record-storage question (which expects binary) costs a mark. When in doubt, write `"wb"` for record-based data and `"w"` for character/line data.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Opening & Closing a File
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** GXEST204 - PROGRAMMING IN C > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Opening & Closing a File # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard) In the C programming language, a **file** is a named location on secondary storage (typically a hard disk) used to permanently store data. Unlike variables whose contents are lost when a program terminates, files allow **persistent storage** of information. **Opening a file** is the process of establishing a logical communication link (a stream) between the executing program and a physical file stored on disk, by associating it with a **file pointer** (of type `FILE *`). This is achieved using the standard library function `fopen()`. **Closing a file** is the process of severing this logical link, flushing any buffered data to the disk, and releasing the allocated resources. This is achieved using `fclose()`. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Definition to Memorize:** > *"A file must be opened before any read/write operation and closed after all operations are complete to ensure data integrity and prevent resource leakage."* ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Door to a Warehouse" 🚪📦 Imagine your C program is a **factory manager** sitting in his office, and the data file is a **warehouse** full of boxes located in another city. | Real-World Concept | C Programming Equivalent | |---|---| | Warehouse on disk | The physical `.dat` or `.txt` file | | Manager's office | The running C program (RAM) | | Telephone line to the warehouse | A **stream** (data pipeline) | | Manager dialing the warehouse number | Calling `fopen()` to open the file | | Hanging up the phone | Calling `fclose()` to close the file | | Phone number on speed dial | The **file pointer** (`FILE *fp`) | **Why does the manager need to "open" the warehouse first?** - He cannot ship goods to a warehouse he hasn't contacted. - The phone line must be established (`fopen`) before any conversation (read/write) can happen. - After all conversations, he **must hang up** (`fclose`), otherwise the line stays busy and other managers cannot call — this is called a **resource leak**. > [!NOTE] > **Key Insight:** In C, a file is **not automatically opened or closed**. The programmer is 100% responsible for opening, performing I/O, and closing every file. This is different from languages like Python (`with open(...)`). ## 1.3 The File Pointer — The "Handle" to the File ```c FILE *fp; ``` - `FILE` is a **structure** (defined in `stdio.h`) that stores information about the file: its name, current position, error flags, buffer addresses, etc. - You never manipulate the `FILE` structure directly. Instead, you receive a **pointer** to it, which acts as a "handle" or "remote control" for that file. - The pointer returned by `fopen()` is your **only legal way** to access the file afterwards. > [!WARNING] > Never declare `FILE *fp` and forget to assign the result of `fopen()`. An uninitialized file pointer will cause **undefined behavior** (segfaults, random data corruption) when used for I/O. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The `fopen()` Function — Opening a File ### 2.1.1 Function Prototype (Mandatory to memorize for KTU) ```c FILE *fopen(const char *filename, const char *mode); ``` - **Returns:** A `FILE *` (file pointer) on success, or `NULL` on failure. - **Parameters:** - `filename` → Path of the file (absolute or relative). Example: `"C:\\data\\marks.txt"` or `"student.dat"`. - `mode` → A string specifying the operation mode (read, write, append, etc.). ### 2.1.2 The Mode String — The "Rulebook" for the File The mode string determines **what you are allowed to do** with the file after opening. Choosing the wrong mode is the #1 source of file-handling bugs. | Mode | File Existence Required? | Behavior on Open | Allowed Operations | Stream Position | |---|---|---|---|---| | `"r"` | **Yes** (else returns NULL) | Opens existing file **as-is** | Read only | Beginning | | `"w"` | No | Creates new OR **truncates** existing file to 0 bytes | Write only | Beginning | | `"a"` | No | Creates new OR **preserves** existing file | Write only (appends) | End | | `"r+"` | **Yes** | Opens existing file **as-is** | **Read AND Write** | Beginning | | `"w+"` | No | Creates new OR **truncates** existing file | **Read AND Write** | Beginning | | `"a+"` | No | Creates new OR **preserves** existing file | **Read AND Append** | End (read anywhere) | | `"rb"`, `"wb"`, `"ab"`, `"r+b"`, `"w+b"`, `"a+b"` | Same as above | **Binary mode** (no translation of `\n`) | Same as base mode | Same as base mode | > [!NOTE] > **Binary mode (`b`)** is critical for non-text files (images, audio, `.dat` files, structs). On Windows, the default text mode translates `\n` (LF) into `\r\n` (CRLF), which corrupts binary data. On Linux/Mac, the distinction is mostly conceptual, but **always use `"rb"` or `"wb"` for portability**. ### 2.1.3 Why Does the Mode String Matter? (The "Why" Behind It) - **"r"** = Read-only. The OS opens the file in **read-share** mode. Your program cannot write. Useful for reading input data. - **"w"** = Write-only with truncation. The OS **deletes all existing content** the moment you open. Extremely dangerous on important files! - **"a"** = Append. The stream position pointer is forced to the **end of the file** on every write, so old data is never overwritten. - **"r+"** = Update existing. Both read and write are allowed, **but the file must already exist**. Failure to check the return value leads to a NULL pointer dereference. - **"w+"** = Create or recreate with both permissions. - **"a+"** = Read existing data, but writes always go to the end. ## 2.2 The `fclose()` Function — Closing a File ### 2.2.1 Function Prototype ```c int fclose(FILE *stream); ``` - **Returns:** `0` on success, `EOF` (which is `-1`) on failure. - **Parameters:** The file pointer returned by `fopen()`. ### 2.2.2 What Happens Internally When You Call `fclose()`? The `fclose()` function performs **four critical housekeeping tasks**: 1. **Flush the buffer** → Any data sitting in the memory buffer (waiting to be written) is physically written to the disk. 2. **Close the OS file handle** → The operating system is told to release the file descriptor. 3. **Deallocate the buffer** → The memory used for the I/O buffer is freed. 4. **Reset the pointer** → The `FILE *` variable becomes invalid. Using it afterwards is **undefined behavior**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Tip:** Examiners often ask: *"What happens if you forget to close a file?"* Answer: (1) Buffered data may **never reach the disk** (data loss on power failure). (2) The file remains **locked** by the OS, preventing other programs from accessing it. (3) **Resource leakage** — too many open files can crash the program (default limit is often 20–256). ### 2.2.3 `fcloseall()` — The Bulk Closer ```c int fcloseall(void); ``` - Closes **all** open streams except `stdin`, `stdout`, `stderr`. - Returns the number of streams closed, or `EOF` on error. - Rarely used in production code but worth knowing for KTU MCQs. ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield "Cheat Sheet" Table | Function | Purpose | Return on Success | Return on Failure | Header | |---|---|---|---|---| | `fopen(filename, mode)` | Open a file | `FILE *` (non-NULL) | `NULL` | `stdio.h` | | `fclose(stream)` | Close a file | `0` | `EOF` (`-1`) | `stdio.h` | | `fcloseall()` | Close all files | Number of files closed | `EOF` | `stdio.h` | | `feof(stream)` | Check end-of-file | Non-zero if EOF | `0` if not EOF | `stdio.h` | | `ferror(stream)` | Check I/O error | Non-zero if error | `0` if no error | `stdio.h` | ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Data logging in IoT:** A C program running on an Arduino/Raspberry Pi continuously appends sensor readings to `"log.txt"` using `fopen("log.txt", "a")` then `fclose()` at shutdown. - **Configuration files:** Programs read settings from `config.ini` using `"r"` mode. - **Database systems:** Even modern SQLite/PostgreSQL engines use `fopen`/`fclose` semantics under the hood for the underlying storage files. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 The Standard "Lifecycle" Algorithm of File Handling in C Every C file-handling program must follow this **6-step lifecycle**. Missing any step results in a bug, data loss, or crash. ### Step-by-Step Logical Breakdown **Step 1: Declare the file pointer variable.** - Syntax: `FILE *fp;` - Reasoning: A pointer to a `FILE` structure must exist to receive the handle from `fopen()`. Without this, there is no place to store the return value. **Step 2: Open the file using `fopen()`.** - Syntax: `fp = fopen("data.txt", "w");` - Reasoning: This is the only way to legally establish a connection with the file. The mode string dictates permissions. **Step 3: Check if `fopen()` succeeded (CRITICAL).** - Syntax: `if (fp == NULL) { printf("Cannot open file!\n"); exit(1); }` - Reasoning: `fopen()` returns `NULL` if the file does not exist (in `"r"` mode) or if the disk is full, path is wrong, or permissions are denied. Dereferencing a `NULL` pointer crashes the program. **Step 4: Perform I/O operations** (covered in subsequent topics: `fprintf`, `fscanf`, `fread`, `fwrite`, `fgetc`, `fputc`, `fgets`, `fputs`). **Step 5: Check for errors after I/O** using `ferror(fp)` or end-of-file using `feof(fp)`. **Step 6: Close the file using `fclose(fp)`.** - Reasoning: Ensures data is flushed to disk and resources are released. ## 3.2 Exhaustive Code Analysis — Opening & Closing in Multiple Modes ### Example 1: Writing to a New File (Truncation Mode) ```c #include #include int main(void) { FILE *fp; /* Open file in "w" mode */ fp = fopen("marks.txt", "w"); /* Always verify the return value */ if (fp == NULL) { printf("Error: Could not open file for writing.\n"); exit(EXIT_FAILURE); } /* Write three lines of data */ fprintf(fp, "Roll No: 101\n"); fprintf(fp, "Name: Anil Kumar\n"); fprintf(fp, "Marks: 95\n"); /* Close the file */ fclose(fp); printf("Data written successfully to marks.txt\n"); return 0; } ``` **Line-by-line evaluation of what happens at runtime:** 1. `FILE *fp;` — Allocates space for a pointer on the stack. `fp` is uninitialized (contains garbage). 2. `fopen("marks.txt", "w")` — The OS: - Searches the current working directory. - If `marks.txt` does not exist → creates an empty file. - If `marks.txt` exists → **deletes all existing content** (truncates to 0 bytes). - Allocates an I/O buffer in memory. - Returns a pointer to a `FILE` structure. 3. `if (fp == NULL)` — Defensive check. If the disk is full or path is invalid, this saves the program. 4. `fprintf(...)` — Three calls write the three lines into the buffer. 5. `fclose(fp)` — The OS: - Flushes the buffer → 3 lines physically written to disk. - Closes the OS file handle. - Frees the buffer memory. - Marks `fp` as invalid. ### Example 2: Reading from an Existing File (Read Mode) ```c #include #include int main(void) { FILE *fp; char ch; /* Open file in "r" mode */ fp = fopen("marks.txt", "r"); if (fp == NULL) { printf("Error: marks.txt does not exist or cannot be read.\n"); exit(EXIT_FAILURE); } /* Read character-by-character until EOF */ while ((ch = fgetc(fp)) != EOF) { putchar(ch); } fclose(fp); return 0; } ``` **Step-by-step analysis:** 1. `fopen("marks.txt", "r")` — The OS checks if the file exists. If yes, opens in read-only mode. If no, returns `NULL`. 2. `fgetc(fp)` — Reads one byte from the file and advances the internal file position pointer by 1. 3. The `while` loop continues until `fgetc()` returns the special sentinel value `EOF` (defined as `-1` in `stdio.h`). 4. `fclose(fp)` — Releases the file. ### Example 3: Appending to an Existing File ```c #include #include int main(void) { FILE *fp; fp = fopen("log.txt", "a"); if (fp == NULL) { printf("Cannot open log file.\n"); exit(1); } fprintf(fp, "Program started at: 10:30 AM\n"); fprintf(fp, "User logged in.\n"); fclose(fp); return 0; } ``` **Behavioral difference from `"w"` mode:** - The previous content of `log.txt` is **preserved**. - The new lines are added to the **end** of the file. - This is the standard pattern for **log files**. ### Example 4: Opening a File in Binary Mode ```c #include struct Student { int roll; char name[50]; float cgpa; }; int main(void) { FILE *fp; struct Student s1 = {101, "Rahul", 9.25}; fp = fopen("student.dat", "wb"); /* Note: 'b' for binary */ if (fp == NULL) { printf("Error opening file.\n"); return 1; } fwrite(&s1, sizeof(s1), 1, fp); fclose(fp); return 0; } ``` **Why `"wb"` and not `"w"`?** - Text mode (`"w"`) on Windows performs `\n` → `\r\n` translation. If a `char name[50]` contained a newline, the file size would change unpredictably. - Binary mode (`"wb"`) writes the **exact bytes** of the struct to disk. To read it back, you must also use `"rb"`. Otherwise, mismatched modes corrupt the data. ## 3.3 The Master Template Every KTU Student Should Memorize ```c #include #include int main(void) { FILE *fp; /* ---------- 1. DECLARE ---------- */ /* ---------- 2. OPEN ---------- */ fp = fopen("data.txt", "w"); /* ---------- 3. CHECK ---------- */ if (fp == NULL) { printf("Error: Cannot open file.\n"); exit(EXIT_FAILURE); } /* ---------- 4. I/O OPERATIONS ---------- */ /* fprintf, fscanf, fread, fwrite, etc. go here */ /* ---------- 5. CLOSE ---------- */ fclose(fp); return 0; } ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Pattern:** Almost every file-handling program in the exam uses this exact skeleton. Memorize it. The only parts that change are the **mode string**, the **filename**, and the **I/O operations** in the middle. ## 3.4 Decision Matrix — Which Mode Should I Use? | Your Goal | Correct Mode | Reason | |---|---|---| | Read a config file that already exists | `"r"` | File must exist; read-only access. | | Create a fresh report from scratch | `"w"` | Old data is irrelevant; need a clean slate. | | Add new entries to a log file | `"a"` | Preserve old log lines; append to end. | | Modify specific records in an existing file | `"r+"` | Need both read and write on the same file. | | Read a `.bmp` image into memory | `"rb"` | Binary data must not be translated. | | Store an array of structs | `"wb"` | Binary write for raw byte storage. | # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Lifecycle Flow of File Handling in C The diagram below shows the complete lifecycle — from declaration to closure — including all decision points and error paths. ```mermaid flowchart TD A([Program Start]) --> B[/Declare FILE asterisk fp/] B --> C[/Call fopen filename and mode/] C --> D{Is return value NULL?} D -- Yes --> E[/Print error message/] E --> F[/Call exit FAILURE/] F --> G([Program Terminates with Error]) D -- No --> H[/fp points to valid FILE structure/] H --> I[/Perform I/O Operations/] I --> J{I/O Successful?} J -- No --> K[/Check ferror fp/] K --> L[/Call fclose fp/] L --> G J -- Yes --> M[/Call fclose fp/] M --> N{fclose returns 0?} N -- No --> O[/Print close error/] O --> P([Program Terminates with Warning]) N -- Yes --> Q[/Buffer flushed and handle released/] Q --> R([Program Ends Successfully]) ``` ## 4.2 Mode Selection Decision Tree This decision tree helps students pick the **correct mode string** for any scenario — a common KTU viva question. ```mermaid flowchart TD Start([Need to open a file]) --> Q1{What is the goal?} Q1 -- Read existing file only --> M1[/Use mode r/] Q1 -- Create new or overwrite --> Q2{Need read too?} Q2 -- No --> M2[/Use mode w/] Q2 -- Yes --> M3[/Use mode w plus/] Q1 -- Append to existing file --> Q3{Need read too?} Q3 -- No --> M4[/Use mode a/] Q3 -- Yes --> M5[/Use mode a plus/] Q1 -- Modify existing file in place --> M6[/Use mode r plus/] Start -.-> BinaryCheck{Is it binary data?} BinaryCheck -- Yes --> AppendB[/Append b to mode string/] BinaryCheck -- No --> TextMode[/Keep text mode default/] AppendB --> Final([Example: wb, rb, r plus b]) TextMode --> Final M1 --> Final M2 --> Final M3 --> Final M4 --> Final M5 --> Final M6 --> Final ``` ## 4.3 Internal Memory Architecture During File I/O This block diagram shows what happens inside the computer's memory when `fopen`, `fprintf`, and `fclose` are called. ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph DISK["Secondary Storage (Hard Disk)"] F1["File: marks.txt
Contents: 3 lines of text"] end subgraph RAM["Primary Memory (RAM)"] subgraph PROG["C Program"] FP["FILE asterisk fp
Pointer to FILE struct"] end subgraph BUF["I/O Buffer (Allocated by fopen)"] B1["Line 1 buffer slot"] B2["Line 2 buffer slot"] B3["Line 3 buffer slot"] end subgraph FSTRUCT["FILE Structure (in stdio.h)"] FS1["Field: file descriptor"] FS2["Field: position pointer"] FS3["Field: error flags"] FS4["Field: buffer address"] end end FP -.Points to.-> FSTRUCT FSTRUCT -.Manages.-> BUF BUF <-- "fprintf fills buffer" --> B1 BUF <-- "fclose flushes all data" --> DISK FP -- "fopen creates" --> FSTRUCT FP -- "fclose destroys" --> FSTRUCT ``` ## 4.4 The "Three Doors" Analogy Diagram This is a simplified visual metaphor for the three primary modes — read, write, and append. ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph FILE["marks.txt on Disk"] DATA["Existing Content:
100, Anil, 85
101, Bina, 92"] end subgraph PROGRAM["Your C Program"] OP["fopen call"] end OP -- "Mode r" --> R["DOOR 1: READING
Read-only view of data
Cannot modify"] OP -- "Mode w" --> W["DOOR 2: WRITING
DATA WIPED CLEAN
Fresh empty file"] OP -- "Mode a" --> A["DOOR 3: APPENDING
Data preserved
New lines added at end"] R --> FILE W --> FILE A --> FILE ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]** **Explain the purpose of the `fopen()` function. List any four file opening modes supported by C.** ### Model Answer (3 Marks) The `fopen()` function is a standard library function defined in `stdio.h` used to open a file and establish a connection between the program and the file. It returns a `FILE *` pointer to the opened file, or `NULL` if the file cannot be opened. **Four file opening modes:** | Mode | Purpose | |---|---| | `"r"` | Opens an existing file for **reading only**. Returns NULL if file does not exist. | | `"w"` | Opens a file for **writing**. Creates the file if it does not exist; truncates (erases all content) if it exists. | | `"a"` | Opens a file for **appending**. Creates the file if it does not exist; existing data is preserved. | | `"r+"` | Opens an existing file for both **reading and writing**. File must exist. | **Syntax:** `FILE *fp = fopen("filename.txt", "mode");` *(Word count: ~120 words — perfectly tuned for a 3-mark answer.)* --- ### Question 2 **[KTU University Exam - June 2024 Model]** **Why is it necessary to close a file using `fclose()`? What are the consequences of forgetting to close a file?** ### Model Answer (3 Marks) The `fclose()` function is essential for three reasons: 1. **Data Integrity (Flushing the Buffer):** C uses a buffered I/O system. When you write data using `fprintf` or `fputc`, the data is first stored in a memory buffer. It is written to the disk **only when the buffer is full or when `fclose()` is called**. If the program crashes before `fclose()`, all buffered data is **lost permanently**. 2. **Resource Release:** Opening a file consumes OS resources (a file descriptor and a memory buffer). Each open file uses one of the limited file descriptors (typically 20–256). Forgetting to close files exhausts these resources, causing `fopen()` to fail for new files. 3. **Releasing File Locks:** On many operating systems, an open file is **locked** — no other program can read or modify it. Failing to close keeps the file locked unnecessarily. **Syntax:** `fclose(fp);` — returns 0 on success, EOF on failure. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > [!NOTE] > **KTU Pattern:** Part B questions in the 2024 scheme feature an **internal choice**. Students must answer EITHER "Question A" OR "Question B". Both questions below are 14 marks, divided into two 7-mark sub-parts. --- ### Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Internal Choice A]** **(a)** Write a C program to open a file named `"employee.txt"` in **write mode**, accept the name and salary of `n` employees from the user, and write them to the file using `fprintf()`. After writing, close the file and display a success message. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Explain the different file opening modes in C with a suitable example for each. Also, state the role of the `FILE` pointer. **(7 marks)** ### Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks ```c #include #include int main(void) { FILE *fp; char name[50]; float salary; int n, i; /* Step 1: Open the file in write mode */ fp = fopen("employee.txt", "w"); /* Step 2: Validate fopen return */ if (fp == NULL) { printf("Error: Cannot open employee.txt for writing.\n"); exit(EXIT_FAILURE); } /* Step 3: Read number of employees */ printf("Enter the number of employees: "); scanf("%d", &n); /* Step 4: Accept and write each record */ for (i = 0; i < n; i++) { printf("\nEnter name of employee %d: ", i + 1); scanf("%s", name); printf("Enter salary of employee %d: ", i + 1); scanf("%f", &salary); fprintf(fp, "%s %.2f\n", name, salary); } /* Step 5: Close the file */ fclose(fp); printf("\nData written successfully to employee.txt\n"); return 0; } ``` **Mark Allocation Breakdown (7 marks):** - Correct header inclusions and `FILE *fp` declaration → **1 mark** - Correct `fopen()` call with `"w"` mode and NULL check → **2 marks** - Correct loop and `fprintf()` usage → **3 marks** - Correct `fclose()` call and message → **1 mark** ### Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks The `FILE` pointer is a variable of type `FILE *` (pointer to a `FILE` structure) that acts as a **handle** to an opened file. It stores information such as the file's name, current reading/writing position, error indicators, and buffer address. The C program accesses the file **only through this pointer** — never directly by filename after opening. **File Opening Modes in C:** | Mode | Description | Example Use Case | |---|---|---| | `"r"` | Opens an existing file for reading. File must exist. Returns NULL otherwise. | Reading configuration data. | | `"w"` | Opens a file for writing. Creates if absent; **truncates** if present. | Creating a new report from scratch. | | `"a"` | Opens a file for appending. Creates if absent; preserves old data. | Writing log entries. | | `"r+"` | Opens an existing file for both reading and writing. | Modifying records in place. | | `"w+"` | Opens a file for reading and writing. Creates if absent; truncates if present. | Overwriting with verification. | | `"a+"` | Opens a file for reading and appending. | Reading a log and adding new entries. | | `"rb"`, `"wb"`, `"ab"` | Same as above, but in **binary mode** (no `\n` translation). | Storing structs, images, audio. | **Examples:** - `fopen("data.txt", "r")` → Open existing text file for reading. - `fopen("output.txt", "w")` → Create or overwrite a text file. - `fopen("log.txt", "a")` → Append to a log file. - `fopen("image.bmp", "rb")` → Read a binary image file. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice **[KTU University Exam - June 2024 Internal Choice B]** **(a)** Differentiate between the file opening modes `"w"`, `"a"`, and `"r+"` with appropriate code snippets demonstrating the behavior of each mode on a pre-existing file. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Write a C program to open a file named `"data.txt"` in **read mode**, read its contents character by character using `fgetc()`, and display each character on the screen. The program must also handle the case when the file does not exist. **(7 marks)** ### Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks **Differences between `"w"`, `"a"`, and `"r+"`:** | Aspect | `"w"` | `"a"` | `"r+"` | |---|---|---|---| | File must exist? | No | No | **Yes** | | Effect on existing data | **Truncates** (deletes all) | **Preserves** all | **Preserves** all | | Allowed operations | Write only | Write only (at end) | Read and Write | | Initial position | Beginning (of empty file) | End of file | Beginning | | Risk of data loss | **High** (overwrites) | None | Low | **Code Snippet 1 — Mode `"w"` (Truncation Behavior):** ```c #include int main(void) { FILE *fp = fopen("test.txt", "w"); if (fp == NULL) return 1; fprintf(fp, "New content\n"); fclose(fp); return 0; } ``` **Effect:** If `test.txt` previously contained `"Old data"`, running this program **deletes `"Old data"` completely** and replaces it with `"New content"`. **Code Snippet 2 — Mode `"a"` (Append Behavior):** ```c #include int main(void) { FILE *fp = fopen("test.txt", "a"); if (fp == NULL) return 1; fprintf(fp, "Appended line\n"); fclose(fp); return 0; } ``` **Effect:** If `test.txt` previously contained `"Old data"`, running this program **preserves `"Old data"`** and adds `"Appended line\n"` to the end. **Code Snippet 3 — Mode `"r+"` (Read+Write on Existing File):** ```c #include int main(void) { FILE *fp = fopen("test.txt", "r+"); if (fp == NULL) { printf("File does not exist.\n"); return 1; } /* Overwrite the first 3 characters */ fputs("NEW", fp); fclose(fp); return 0; } ``` **Effect:** The file must already exist. The first 3 characters of `test.txt` are overwritten with `"NEW"`, but the rest of the file remains intact. ### Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks ```c #include #include int main(void) { FILE *fp; char ch; /* Step 1: Open the file in read mode */ fp = fopen("data.txt", "r"); /* Step 2: Handle the case when file does not exist */ if (fp == NULL) { printf("Error: data.txt does not exist or cannot be opened.\n"); exit(EXIT_FAILURE); } /* Step 3: Read character-by-character until EOF */ printf("Contents of data.txt:\n"); printf("----------------------\n"); while ((ch = fgetc(fp)) != EOF) { putchar(ch); } printf("\n----------------------\n"); /* Step 4: Close the file */ fclose(fp); return 0; } ``` **Mark Allocation Breakdown (7 marks):** - Correct `#include` and `FILE *fp` declaration → **1 mark** - Correct `fopen("data.txt", "r")` call → **1 mark** - Proper NULL check with error message → **2 marks** - Correct `fgetc()` loop with EOF check → **2 marks** - Correct `fclose(fp)` call → **1 mark** --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:** > > 1. **Forgetting the NULL check after `fopen()`** — Examiners deduct **at least 1 mark** if you do not verify the return value. Always write `if (fp == NULL) { ... }`. > > 2. **Confusing `"w"` with `"a"`** — Many students use `"w"` when they mean "add new data". This **destroys the existing file**. Read the question carefully: *"create a new file"* → `"w"`; *"add records to existing log"* → `"a"`. > > 3. **Forgetting to include ``** — Required for the `exit()` function. Omitting it may still compile on some systems, but the examiner will notice. > > 4. **Forgetting to call `fclose()`** — If your program opens a file but never closes it, the examiner deducts **1–2 marks** depending on strictness. Always close every file you open. > > 5. **Using `fp` after `fclose()`** — Some students write `fclose(fp)` and then continue to use `fp` for further I/O. This is **undefined behavior** and will lose marks. > > 6. **Confusing `fclose()` return value** — The correct return is `0` on success, `EOF` on failure. Some students incorrectly state that it returns `1` or `NULL`. > > 7. **Writing mode as `r+` vs `r+b` vs `rb+`** — All three are valid in standard C, but `r+b` is the most portable. Pick one and stick with it for the exam. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember ### 🎯 Rapid Revision Checklist - ✅ A **file** is a named storage on disk; data persists beyond program execution. - ✅ The **standard header** for file I/O is ``. - ✅ The **type** used to reference an open file is `FILE *` (a pointer to a `FILE` structure). - ✅ The **lifecycle** is: Declare pointer → `fopen` → Check NULL → I/O → `fclose`. - ✅ `fopen()` returns a `FILE *` on success, **`NULL`** on failure. - ✅ `fclose()` returns **`0`** on success, **`EOF`** on failure. - ✅ **`"r"`** = read existing file (fails if absent). **`"w"`** = write, truncates existing file. **`"a"`** = write at end, preserves existing file. - ✅ **`"r+"`** = read and write existing file. **`"w+"`** = read and write, truncates. **`"a+"`** = read anywhere, write at end. - ✅ Append **`"b"`** for binary mode (e.g., `"rb"`, `"wb"`, `"rb+"`). - ✅ **Always check** if `fopen()` returned `NULL` before performing any I/O. - ✅ **Always call** `fclose()` to flush buffers, release resources, and unlock the file. - ✅ The maximum number of files that can be opened simultaneously is given by the macro **`FOPEN_MAX`** (defined in `stdio.h`, typically 20 on older systems, 256+ on modern ones). - ✅ Standard streams `stdin`, `stdout`, `stderr` are **automatically opened** by the runtime — never close them manually. - ✅ Using a `FILE *` after `fclose()` is **undefined behavior** — always re-open or re-assign the pointer if you need to use it again. ### 🔑 Most-Tested Concepts in KTU (Priority Order) 1. **NULL check after fopen** — asked almost every exam. 2. **Difference between `"w"` and `"a"` modes** — guaranteed 3-mark question. 3. **Purpose of `fclose()`** — concept question worth 3 marks. 4. **Writing a complete program** with `fopen`, I/O, and `fclose` — 7 to 14-mark programming question. 5. **The 6-step file handling template** — students who memorize this template score full marks on programming questions.

topics.title: Writing to and Reading from a File
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GXEST204 - PROGRAMMING IN C
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Writing to and Reading from a File

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the C programming language (standardized under **ISO/IEC 9899:2018 — C17**), **file I/O** refers to the mechanism by which a program exchanges persistent data with secondary storage devices (typically a hard disk or SSD) through the **Standard I/O Library** (`<stdio.h>`). According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme** learning outcomes for Module 4 of *GXEST204 – Programming in C*, file handling is the process of creating, opening, reading from, writing to, and closing a *stream* — an abstraction that decouples the program from the physical characteristics of the underlying storage device.

A **file** is a named collection of records stored on a secondary storage device. In C, every file is treated as a sequence of **bytes (8 bits each)**, and the language provides no built-in structure (such as records or lines); the programmer must impose structure.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 4):**
> *"File handling: opening modes, writing to a file using `fprintf()`, `fputs()`, `fputc()`, and reading from a file using `fscanf()`, `fgets()`, `fgetc()`."*

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are a **postmaster** in a busy post office.

- The **post office counter** is your C program.
- The **letter** is your data (a number, a character, or a whole sentence).
- The **mailbox outside** is the file on the disk.

When you want to **send a letter (write to a file)**, you must:
1. **Open the mailbox** (use `fopen()` with the correct key — the *mode*).
2. **Drop the letter inside** (use `fprintf()`, `fputs()`, or `fputc()`).
3. **Close the mailbox** (use `fclose()`) — otherwise, the letter might be lost, and the mailbox stays unlocked!

When you want to **read letters from the mailbox (read from a file)**, you reverse the process using `fscanf()`, `fgets()`, or `fgetc()`.

### 1.3 Key Terminology Introduced

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Vocabulary for KTU Examinations**
>
> | Term | Meaning |
> |---|---|
> | **Stream** | A logical device-independent channel of data flow (`FILE *`). |
> | **File Pointer** | A pointer of type `FILE *` that identifies an open file stream. |
> | **Buffer** | A temporary memory area (usually **4096 bytes** or **8192 bytes**) used between the program and disk. |
> | **EOF** | A macro defined as `-1` indicating **End-Of-File**. |
> | **Text Mode vs Binary Mode** | Text mode translates `\n` ↔ `\r\n` (Windows); binary mode performs no translation. |
> | **Standard Streams** | `stdin`, `stdout`, `stderr` — automatically opened streams. |

### 1.4 Why File I/O Matters in Engineering

Every real-world engineering application — from logging sensor data in an **IoT device** to storing patient records in a **hospital management system** — relies on file I/O. Variables in RAM vanish when the program terminates (**volatile storage**); only files provide **persistent storage**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The File Handling Lifecycle

Every C file operation follows a strict **5-stage lifecycle**. Deviating from this pattern is one of the most common reasons students lose marks in KTU lab evaluations.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The 5-Stage File Lifecycle (Memorize this for KTU 2-Mark Questions)**
> 1. **Declare** a `FILE *` pointer.
> 2. **Open** the file using `fopen()` — returns `NULL` on failure.
> 3. **Process** the file (read/write/append).
> 4. **Check** for errors using `ferror()` or EOF using `feof()`.
> 5. **Close** the file using `fclose()`.

### 2.2 The `fopen()` Function — Detailed Analysis

The function prototype declared in `<stdio.h>` is:

$$
\text{FILE *fopen}(\text{const char *filename}, \text{const char *mode});
$$

- `filename` — A null-terminated string giving the path (relative or absolute).
- `mode` — A null-terminated string specifying the access mode.
- **Return Value** — A `FILE *` pointer on success, or `NULL` on failure (e.g., file not found, permission denied, disk full).

### 2.3 The 12 Standard File Modes (KTU High-Yield Table)

This is the **most frequently tested table** in KTU End Semester Examinations on this topic. Memorize it completely.

| Mode | File Must Exist? | Read? | Write? | Truncate? | Create if Missing? | Position Start |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| `"r"` | **Yes** | ✅ | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ | Beginning |
| `"w"` | No | ❌ | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | Beginning |
| `"a"` | No | ❌ | ✅ | ❌ | ✅ | End |
| `"r+"` | **Yes** | ✅ | ✅ | ❌ | ❌ | Beginning |
| `"w+"` | No | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | Beginning |
| `"a+"` | No | ✅ | ✅ | ❌ | ✅ | End |
| `"rb"` | **Yes** | ✅ | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ | Beginning |
| `"wb"` | No | ❌ | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | Beginning |
| `"ab"` | No | ❌ | ✅ | ❌ | ✅ | End |
| `"rb+"` / `"r+b"` | **Yes** | ✅ | ✅ | ❌ | ❌ | Beginning |
| `"wb+"` / `"w+b"` | No | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | Beginning |
| `"ab+"` / `"a+b"` | No | ✅ | ✅ | ❌ | ✅ | End |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Pitfall:** Students often confuse `"w"` and `"a"`. The mode `"w"` **erases the entire file** if it exists, while `"a"` **preserves** existing content and adds new data at the end. Choosing the wrong mode can destroy critical data.

### 2.4 The Three Character-Level I/O Functions

| Function | Direction | Header | Prototype | Purpose |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :--- | :--- |
| `fputc()` | Program → File | `<stdio.h>` | `int fputc(int c, FILE *fp);` | Writes a **single character**. |
| `fgetc()` | File → Program | `<stdio.h>` | `int fgetc(FILE *fp);` | Reads a **single character** (returns `int` to accommodate `EOF`). |
| `ungetc()` | Program → Stream | `<stdio.h>` | `int ungetc(int c, FILE *fp);` | Pushes a character back into the stream. |

### 2.5 The Two String-Level I/O Functions

| Function | Direction | Prototype | Behavior |
| :--- | :---: | :--- | :--- |
| `fputs()` | Program → File | `int fputs(const char *s, FILE *fp);` | Writes a string; **does not automatically append `\n`**. |
| `fgets()` | File → Program | `char *fgets(char *s, int n, FILE *fp);` | Reads up to `n-1` chars or until `\n` / `EOF`; **null-terminates**; retains `\n`. |

### 2.6 The Two Formatted I/O Functions (Most Important for KTU 14-Mark Questions)

| Function | Direction | Prototype | Analogy |
| :--- | :---: | :--- | :--- |
| `fprintf()` | Program → File | `int fprintf(FILE *fp, const char *fmt, ...);` | `printf()` but to a file. |
| `fscanf()` | File → Program | `int fscanf(FILE *fp, const char *fmt, ...);` | `scanf()` but from a file. |

### 2.7 The Return Value Rule (Critical for KTU 14-Mark Programs)

> [!NOTE]
> **Why functions return `int` even when handling `char`**
>
> - `fprintf()` returns the number of characters successfully written, or a **negative value** on error.
> - `fscanf()` returns the number of input items successfully matched and assigned, or `EOF` on end-of-file.
> - `fgetc()` returns the character read as an **`unsigned char` converted to `int`**, or `EOF` (which is `-1`) on failure.
>
> If you store the result of `fgetc()` in a `char` variable, the value `0xFF` (a valid character `ÿ` in Latin-1) is indistinguishable from `EOF`. This is a **classic KTU examination trap**.

### 2.8 Engineering Utility

In the field of **embedded systems engineering**, file I/O is used to:
- **Log sensor readings** (temperature, pressure) into `.csv` files.
- **Store configuration parameters** (calibration constants) persistently.
- **Read firmware updates** from external flash memory.
- **Generate reports** in industrial automation systems.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Analytical Matrix: Choosing the Correct I/O Function

Use the following decision matrix to select the right function for your KTU programming question.

| Data Type Being Transferred | Recommended Function | Why |
| :--- | :---: | :--- |
| A single byte / character | `fputc()` / `fgetc()` | Lowest overhead, ideal for binary or character streams. |
| A whole line / raw string | `fputs()` / `fgets()` | Buffer-based, no format parsing overhead. |
| Mixed types (int, float, string) | `fprintf()` / `fscanf()` | Format specifiers allow precise control. |
| Structured `struct` blocks | `fwrite()` / `fread()` (binary) | Preserves exact memory layout, faster, portable. |

### 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation: Buffered File Write Operation

Let us derive the **complete call chain** when the C runtime executes `fprintf(fp, "%d", 42);`.

**Step 1 — Format String Parsing:**
The runtime scans the format string `"%d"`, identifies an integer specifier, and locates the variadic argument `42` in the stack/registers.

**Step 2 — Integer-to-ASCII Conversion:**
The integer value `42` is converted to the ASCII string `"42"`. This requires the division-by-base algorithm.

$$
42 \div 10 = 4 \text{ remainder } 2
$$
$$
4 \div 10 = 0 \text{ remainder } 4
$$

Reading remainders in reverse order yields the character sequence `'4'`, `'2'`.

**Step 3 — Buffer Accumulation:**
Each character is appended to the stream's internal buffer. The buffer size is implementation-defined but is typically:

$$
\text{BUFSIZ} = 8192 \text{ bytes} \quad \text{(POSIX minimum is 8192, Linux uses 4096–8192)}
$$

**Step 4 — Buffer Flush Trigger:**
The buffer is written to the OS in any of three situations:
1. The buffer becomes full.
2. A newline is encountered (only in *line-buffered* streams like `stdout` to a terminal).
3. `fflush(fp)` or `fclose(fp)` is called explicitly.

**Step 5 — OS to Disk:**
The OS schedules a `write()` syscall, transferring the buffer to the disk's page cache, which is asynchronously flushed to physical storage.

### 3.3 Exhaustive Walkthrough: `fgets()` Read Algorithm

The function `fgets(str, n, fp)` performs the following logical steps:

**Step 1 — Parameter Validation:**
- `str` must be a valid pointer to at least `n` bytes of writable memory.
- `fp` must be an open stream.

**Step 2 — Character Loop:**
Read one character at a time using the equivalent of `fgetc()` until **any** of these terminating conditions occur:

$$
\text{Loop Termination: } (c = \text{EOF}) \;\lor\; (\text{count} \geq n-1) \;\lor\; (c = \text{'\n'})
$$

**Step 3 — Null Termination:**
Append the `'\0'` character at the end so the result is a valid C string.

**Step 4 — Return Value:**
Return `str` on success, or `NULL` on failure or EOF with no characters read.

### 3.4 Worked Example 1: Writing Two Integers to a File

**Problem (KTU Pattern):** Write a C program to open a file named `"data.txt"` in write mode and write two integers entered by the user.

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

```c
#include <stdio.h>

int main(void) {
    FILE *fp;
    int a, b;

    /* Step 1: Open the file in write mode */
    fp = fopen("data.txt", "w");

    /* Step 2: Validate the file pointer */
    if (fp == NULL) {
        printf("Error: Could not open file!\n");
        return 1;
    }

    /* Step 3: Read input from the user */
    printf("Enter two integers: ");
    scanf("%d %d", &a, &b);

    /* Step 4: Write formatted data to the file */
    fprintf(fp, "%d %d\n", a, b);

    /* Step 5: Close the file */
    fclose(fp);

    printf("Data written successfully to data.txt\n");
    return 0;
}
```

**Output (when run with input `10 20`):**
The file `data.txt` will contain the bytes:

$$
\text{ASCII codes: } \texttt{'1','0',' ','2','0','\n'} \quad (6 \text{ bytes total})
$$

### 3.5 Worked Example 2: Reading a File Character by Character

**Problem (KTU Pattern):** Write a C program to read a file named `"source.txt"` and display its contents on the screen, character by character.

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

```c
#include <stdio.h>

int main(void) {
    FILE *fp;
    int ch;   /* MUST be int to hold EOF */

    fp = fopen("source.txt", "r");

    if (fp == NULL) {
        printf("Error: File not found!\n");
        return 1;
    }

    /* Read one character at a time until EOF */
    while ((ch = fgetc(fp)) != EOF) {
        putchar(ch);
    }

    fclose(fp);
    return 0;
}
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why `int ch` and not `char ch`?**
> The macro `EOF` is defined as `-1` in `<stdio.h>`. If `ch` were declared as `char` (typically 8 bits signed, range -128 to 127), the comparison `(char)-1 != EOF` would work, BUT the character `ÿ` (code `255` or `0xFF`) becomes `−1` when stored in a signed `char` and would be **incorrectly identified as EOF**. Declaring `ch` as `int` solves this.

### 3.6 Worked Example 3: Appending Data Using `fputs()`

**Problem:** Append the string `"Hello, KTU!"` to a file named `"log.txt"` without erasing its existing contents.

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

```c
#include <stdio.h>

int main(void) {
    FILE *fp;

    /* Use "a" mode to preserve existing content */
    fp = fopen("log.txt", "a");

    if (fp == NULL) {
        printf("Error opening log.txt\n");
        return 1;
    }

    fputs("Hello, KTU!\n", fp);

    fclose(fp);
    return 0;
}
```

### 3.7 Worked Example 4: Reading a Full Line with `fgets()`

**Problem:** Read a complete line (max 99 characters + null terminator) from a file.

```c
#include <stdio.h>

int main(void) {
    FILE *fp;
    char line[100];

    fp = fopen("notes.txt", "r");
    if (fp == NULL) {
        perror("Error");
        return 1;
    }

    /* fgets reads until \n, EOF, or 99 chars, then adds \0 */
    if (fgets(line, sizeof(line), fp) != NULL) {
        printf("Read line: %s", line);
    } else {
        printf("File is empty or read error.\n");
    }

    fclose(fp);
    return 0;
}
```

### 3.8 Comparison Table: `fprintf` vs `fscanf` Error Indicators

| Condition | `fprintf()` return value | `fscanf()` return value |
| :--- | :---: | :---: |
| Success | Number of chars written (≥ 0) | Number of items assigned (≥ 1) |
| End of file reached mid-read | — | `EOF` (typically `−1`) |
| Disk full / write error | Negative value | — |
| Format mismatch mid-read | — | Returns count of successful reads only |

> [!NOTE]
> **Formula for buffer size on a typical Linux GCC system:**
>
> $$
> \text{BUFSIZ} = 8192 \text{ bytes} \quad \text{(defined in } \texttt{<stdio.h>}\text{)}
> $$
>
> The program can query this at runtime using `printf("Buffer size: %d\n", BUFSIZ);`.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram: The File I/O Architecture

The following Mermaid diagram shows how a C program's `fprintf` / `fscanf` calls propagate through the C runtime, OS, and finally to the physical disk.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[C Source Code] --> B[Compiler - gcc]
    B --> C[Executable Binary]
    C --> D[Program in RAM]
    D --> E[C Standard Library - stdio.h]
    E --> F[Stream Buffer - 8192 bytes]
    F --> G[Operating System Kernel]
    G --> H[Disk Page Cache]
    H --> I[Physical HDD or SSD]

    style A fill:#1f77b4,color:#ffffff
    style B fill:#1f77b4,color:#ffffff
    style C fill:#1f77b4,color:#ffffff
    style D fill:#ff7f0e,color:#ffffff
    style E fill:#2ca02c,color:#ffffff
    style F fill:#2ca02c,color:#ffffff
    style G fill:#d62728,color:#ffffff
    style H fill:#9467bd,color:#ffffff
    style I fill:#8c564b,color:#ffffff
```

### 4.2 Flowchart: The Complete File Read-Write Lifecycle

This is the **exact sequence** the KTU examiner expects when a student writes a file handling program in the exam.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start] --> B[Declare FILE pointer fp]
    B --> C[fp = fopen filename mode]
    C --> D{Is fp NULL?}
    D -->|Yes| E[Print error and EXIT]
    D -->|No| F[Perform file operation]
    F --> G[fprintf OR fputs OR fputc]
    F --> H[fscanf OR fgets OR fgetc]
    G --> I[Check return value]
    H --> I
    I --> J[fflush fp - Optional but safe]
    J --> K[fclose fp]
    K --> L[End]
    E --> L

    style A fill:#4a90e2,color:#ffffff
    style B fill:#4a90e2,color:#ffffff
    style C fill:#4a90e2,color:#ffffff
    style D fill:#f5a623,color:#000000
    style E fill:#d0021b,color:#ffffff
    style F fill:#7ed321,color:#000000
    style G fill:#7ed321,color:#000000
    style H fill:#7ed321,color:#000000
    style I fill:#9013fe,color:#ffffff
    style J fill:#9013fe,color:#ffffff
    style K fill:#9013fe,color:#ffffff
    style L fill:#50e3c2,color:#000000
```

### 4.3 Decision Tree: Choosing the Correct I/O Function

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Need to transfer data to or from file] --> B{What is the data type?}
    B -->|Single character| C[Use fputc OR fgetc]
    B -->|String or line| D[Use fputs OR fgets]
    B -->|Mixed types - int float double| E[Use fprintf OR fscanf]
    B -->|Whole struct or binary block| F[Use fwrite OR fread]

    C --> G{Need formatted output?}
    D --> G
    E --> G
    F --> G
    G -->|Yes| E
    G -->|No| C

    style A fill:#4a90e2,color:#ffffff
    style B fill:#f5a623,color:#000000
    style C fill:#7ed321,color:#000000
    style D fill:#7ed321,color:#000000
    style E fill:#7ed321,color:#000000
    style F fill:#7ed321,color:#000000
    style G fill:#9013fe,color:#ffffff
```

### 4.4 Sequence Diagram: Buffered Write Operation

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant U as User Program
    participant L as C stdio Library
    participant B as Stream Buffer 8192 bytes
    participant K as OS Kernel
    participant D as Physical Disk

    U->>L: fprintf fp Hello World
    L->>L: Parse format string
    L->>B: Append characters H e l l o space W o r l d
    Note over B: Buffer not yet full
    U->>L: fclose fp
    L->>B: Force flush remaining data
    B->>K: write syscall with 11 bytes
    K->>D: Transfer to page cache
    D-->>K: Acknowledge write
    K-->>L: Return success
    L-->>U: Return 0
```

### 4.5 Block Diagram: Memory Layout of a `FILE` Structure (Conceptual)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[FILE struct in memory] --> B[File descriptor fd]
    A --> C[Buffer pointer]
    A --> D[Buffer size - BUFSIZ]
    A --> E[Current position pointer]
    A --> F[Flags - read, write, error, EOF]
    A --> G[Lock for thread safety]

    B --> B1[int 0, 1, 2 etc]
    C --> C1[char ptr to 8KB block]
    D --> D1[int 8192]
    E --> E1[off_t offset]
    F --> F1[bitmask]
    G --> G1[pthread_mutex_t]

    style A fill:#9013fe,color:#ffffff
```
