<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper 1]**

**Q: Differentiate between the file opening modes `"r"` and `"w"` in C. What happens if the file does not exist in each case?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Mode | File Must Pre-Exist? | Read? | Write? | Truncates Existing? | Behavior When File Missing |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :--- |
| `"r"` | **Yes** | ✅ | ❌ | ❌ | `fopen()` returns `NULL` |
| `"w"` | No | ❌ | ✅ | ✅ | A new file is created |

**Key Distinction:** Mode `"r"` is **read-only** and requires the file to exist. Mode `"w"` is **write-only** and creates a fresh file, destroying any existing content with the same name.

---

#### **Question 2: [KTU University Exam - June 2024, Model Paper 2]**

**Q: Why must the variable used to store the return value of `fgetc()` be declared as `int` and not `char`?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The function `fgetc()` reads an unsigned character from the file and returns it as an `int` widened to accommodate the `EOF` marker.

- The macro `EOF` is defined as `-1` in `<stdio.h>`.
- A signed `char` can only hold values in the range **−128 to +127**.
- The character `ÿ` (Latin-1 code `0xFF` = `255`) gets converted to **−1** when stored in a signed `char`, making it **indistinguishable from `EOF`**.
- Declaring the variable as `int` (typically 32 bits, range up to `2,147,483,647`) preserves the distinction between `EOF (-1)` and the character `ÿ (255)`.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (Choice 1): [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 4 Pattern]**

**Q: (a)** Explain any **six** different file opening modes supported by the C programming language. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Write a C program to read the contents of an existing text file named `"input.txt"` and write the same content into another file named `"output.txt"`, character by character. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Answer for Question A(a) — 7 Marks**

The **six** most commonly tested modes are:

1. **`"r"`** — Opens an existing text file for **reading only**. File must exist; otherwise `fopen()` returns `NULL`. Position is at the beginning.
2. **`"w"`** — Opens a text file for **writing only**. If the file exists, its contents are **truncated**; if it does not exist, a new file is created.
3. **`"a"`** — Opens a text file for **appending**. Data is written at the **end** of the file. The file is created if it does not exist.
4. **`"r+"`** — Opens an existing text file for **both reading and writing**. File must exist.
5. **`"w+"`** — Opens a text file for **both reading and writing**. Truncates if exists, creates if missing.
6. **`"a+"`** — Opens a text file for **reading and appending**. Reading begins at the start, but writing always occurs at the end.

The binary equivalents (`"rb"`, `"wb"`, `"ab"`, `"rb+"`, `"wb+"`, `"ab+"`) operate identically except that no character translation (e.g., `\n` ↔ `\r\n`) is performed.

---

#### **Model Answer for Question A(b) — 7 Marks**

```c
#include <stdio.h>

int main(void) {
    FILE *source, *dest;
    int ch;

    /* Open the source file in read mode */
    source = fopen("input.txt", "r");
    if (source == NULL) {
        printf("Error: Cannot open input.txt\n");
        return 1;
    }

    /* Open the destination file in write mode */
    dest = fopen("output.txt", "w");
    if (dest == NULL) {
        printf("Error: Cannot open output.txt\n");
        fclose(source);
        return 1;
    }

    /* Copy character by character */
    while ((ch = fgetc(source)) != EOF) {
        fputc(ch, dest);
    }

    /* Close both files */
    fclose(source);
    fclose(dest);

    printf("File copied successfully.\n");
    return 0;
}
```

**Explanation of Key Lines:**
- The variable `ch` is declared as `int` (not `char`) to safely store the `EOF` value `−1`.
- `fgetc(source)` reads one character at a time; the loop continues until `EOF` is encountered.
- `fputc(ch, dest)` writes that character to the destination file.
- `fclose()` is called for **both** files to flush buffers and release OS resources.

---

#### **Question B (Choice 2): [KTU University Exam - June 2024, Module 4 Pattern]**

**Q: (a)** Explain the following file handling functions with their syntax: `fprintf()`, `fscanf()`, `fputs()`, and `fgets()`. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Write a C program to accept the **roll number, name, and marks of 5 students** from the keyboard and store them in a text file named `"student.txt"`. After writing, read back the data and display it on the screen. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Answer for Question B(a) — 7 Marks**

| Function | Syntax | Purpose |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| `fprintf()` | `int fprintf(FILE *fp, const char *fmt, ...);` | Writes formatted output to a stream. Identical to `printf()` except the first argument is the file pointer. |
| `fscanf()` | `int fscanf(FILE *fp, const char *fmt, ...);` | Reads formatted input from a stream. Identical to `scanf()` except the first argument is the file pointer. |
| `fputs()` | `int fputs(const char *s, FILE *fp);` | Writes a null-terminated string to a stream. **Does not append a newline** automatically. |
| `fgets()` | `char *fgets(char *s, int n, FILE *fp);` | Reads at most `n-1` characters from a stream into buffer `s`. Stops at newline or `EOF`. Always null-terminates the result. |

**Return Values:**
- `fprintf()` — number of characters written, or a negative value on error.
- `fscanf()` — number of input items matched, or `EOF` on end-of-file.
- `fputs()` — a non-negative value on success, or `EOF` on error.
- `fgets()` — the pointer `s` on success, or `NULL` on error or `EOF`.

---

#### **Model Answer for Question B(b) — 7 Marks**

```c
#include <stdio.h>

struct Student {
    int roll;
    char name[50];
    float marks;
};

int main(void) {
    FILE *fp;
    struct Student s;
    int i;

    /* === Writing Phase === */
    fp = fopen("student.txt", "w");
    if (fp == NULL) {
        printf("Error opening file for writing.\n");
        return 1;
    }

    for (i = 0; i < 5; i++) {
        printf("Enter roll, name, marks for student %d: ", i + 1);
        scanf("%d %s %f", &s.roll, s.name, &s.marks);
        fprintf(fp, "%d %s %.2f\n", s.roll, s.name, s.marks);
    }
    fclose(fp);

    /* === Reading Phase === */
    fp = fopen("student.txt", "r");
    if (fp == NULL) {
        printf("Error opening file for reading.\n");
        return 1;
    }

    printf("\n--- Student Records ---\n");
    while (fscanf(fp, "%d %s %f", &s.roll, s.name, &s.marks) == 3) {
        printf("Roll: %d | Name: %s | Marks: %.2f\n",
               s.roll, s.name, s.marks);
    }
    fclose(fp);

    return 0;
}
```

**Explanation:**
- A `struct Student` is used to logically group the three related fields.
- The file is opened twice: once with `"w"` to write, and again with `"r"` to read.
- `fprintf()` writes all three fields in one formatted call, separated by spaces.
- The `while` loop continues as long as `fscanf()` successfully reads **all three** values (returns `3`).

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 5 Places KTU Students Lose Marks on File I/O Questions**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to check `fopen()` return value** — If you do not check for `NULL`, the examiner deducts **at least 1 mark** for missing error handling.
> 2. **Using `char` instead of `int` for `fgetc()` result** — This is a **2-mark penalty** item because it is a known portability bug.
> 3. **Writing in `"w"` mode when the question says "append"** — Total loss of the existing file content. **2-mark deduction**.
> 4. **Not calling `fclose()`** — Data may remain in the buffer and never reach the disk. **1-mark deduction** plus potential loss of program correctness marks.
> 5. **Confusing `fputs()` and `fprintf()`** — `fputs()` does NOT interpret format specifiers like `%d` or `%s`; it writes the literal characters. This causes silent program bugs and is a common **3-mark deduction** in lab exams.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — File Writing & Reading in C**
>
> - Always include `<stdio.h>` for all file I/O functions.
> - The standard file handle is a `FILE *` pointer; `NULL` indicates failure.
> - The **5-stage lifecycle** is: Declare → Open → Process → Check → Close.
> - **`"r"`** = read (file must exist), **`"w"`** = write (truncates), **`"a"`** = append.
> - Adding **`+`** enables both read and write; adding **`b`** switches to binary mode.
> - `fprintf()` is to a file what `printf()` is to the screen.
> - `fscanf()` is from a file what `scanf()` is from the keyboard.
> - `fputc()` / `fgetc()` work on **single characters**.
> - `fputs()` / `fgets()` work on **strings/lines**.
> - `fgetc()` must be stored in an **`int`**, not a `char`, to safely handle `EOF`.
> - `EOF` is defined as **`-1`** in `<stdio.h>`.
> - The internal stream buffer is typically **`8192 bytes`** (use `BUFSIZ` macro).
> - Always call `fclose()` to flush the buffer and release OS resources.
> - Text mode performs `\n` ↔ `\r\n` translation on Windows; binary mode does not.
> - `fgets()` retains the newline character in the buffer; `scanf("%s")` does not.
> - Common pitfalls: missing `NULL` check, wrong mode selection, `char` vs `int` for `fgetc()`, forgetting `fclose()`.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Processing Files
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** GXEST204 - PROGRAMMING IN C > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Processing Files ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **File Processing in C** is the mechanism of storing data permanently on secondary storage devices (such as hard disks) using the standard I/O library (`stdio.h`) so that the data persists beyond the lifetime of a program's execution. A **file** is a named collection of related information recorded on secondary storage, and processing it involves creating, reading, writing, appending, and closing files through a logical interface called a **stream**. In the C programming language, every file is treated as a **sequential stream of bytes** that ends with an **End-Of-File (EOF) marker**. The C standard library abstracts all physical device details (disk sectors, cylinders, FAT tables) and presents a uniform logical model to the programmer through the `FILE` structure and a set of well-defined I/O functions. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Analogy — The Office Filing Cabinet** > Imagine you are working in a government office. Every day you receive hundreds of application forms. Up to now, you have been writing them in a *register* (this is your **RAM**). When the office closes, the register vanishes — all the data is lost the moment power goes off. To keep records permanent, you transfer them to a **filing cabinet** (this is your **hard disk file**). The clerk who handles the cabinet knows exactly how to open a drawer, place a file, find a file, update a file, and close the drawer. In C, that clerk is the **`FILE *` pointer**, the drawer is the **stream**, and the cabinet is the **file on disk**. | Real-World Object | C Equivalent | Purpose | |---|---|---| | Filing Cabinet | File on Disk | Permanent storage | | Drawer Label | File Name (`"data.txt"`) | Identification | | Clerk | `FILE *` Pointer | Access & manipulation | | Register (temporary) | RAM / Variables | Volatile working memory | | "Drawer Open" command | `fopen()` | Establishes connection | | "Drawer Close" command | `fclose()` | Releases resources | ### 1.3 Why Do We Need Files? — The Persistence Problem By default, all variables in a C program are stored in **RAM (Random Access Memory)**, which is **volatile**. The moment the program terminates (normally or by crash), every variable, array, and structure is destroyed. Files solve this fundamental problem by: 1. **Durability** — Data survives program termination and system reboot. 2. **Shareability** — Multiple programs can read the same data. 3. **Capacity** — Disks store **terabytes**, while RAM is limited to **gigabytes**. 4. **Portability** — Files can be transferred across machines via networks or USB. ### 1.4 Categories of Files in C C recognises two fundamental file categories: > [!NOTE] > - **Text Files** — Contain human-readable characters. Each line ends with a newline character (`\n`, ASCII value **10**). Internally stored as a sequence of ASCII/UTF-8 codes. Example: `.c`, `.txt`, `.csv` source files. > - **Binary Files** — Contain raw bytes that mirror the in-memory representation of data. No character translation occurs during I/O. Example: `.exe`, `.jpg`, `.mp3`, compiled object files, database files. The choice between them impacts **portability, speed, and precision** — text files are portable but may lose floating-point precision due to ASCII conversion; binary files are faster and preserve exact bit patterns but are not human-readable. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Stream Abstraction — The Heart of C File I/O A **stream** is a logical interface (a flowing pipeline of bytes) that connects the program to a physical file or device. The C standard defines three streams that are automatically opened when a program starts: | Standard Stream | Header Constant | Default Device | Direction | |---|---|---|---| | `stdin` | Standard Input | Keyboard | Read from device | | `stdout` | Standard Output | Monitor (Console) | Write to device | | `stderr` | Standard Error | Monitor (Console) | Write errors | These three streams require no `fopen()` call — they are pre-connected to your program by the runtime environment (C Runtime / CRT). ### 2.2 The `FILE` Structure and File Pointer ```c FILE *fp; ``` `FILE` is an **opaque structure** defined inside `stdio.h` (its internal layout is implementation-specific and not part of the C standard). The compiler guarantees that it contains at minimum: - A **file descriptor** (an integer index into the OS's open-file table). - A **buffer pointer** (for buffered I/O — typically 4096 or 8192 bytes). - **Current position indicator** (where the next read/write will occur). - **Error indicator** and **End-Of-File indicator**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **You must NEVER access `FILE` fields directly.** Always use the library functions. The pointer `fp` is what the programmer manipulates; the structure it points to is owned by the library. ### 2.3 The Open Modes — KTU High-Yield Table The function `fopen()` takes two arguments: a **filename** and a **mode string**. The mode string is the single most-tested concept in KTU exams. | Mode String | File Must Exist? | Read? | Write? | Truncate? | Position | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | `"r"` | Yes | ✅ | ❌ | ❌ | Beginning | | `"w"` | No | ❌ | ✅ | ✅ | Beginning | | `"a"` | No | ❌ | ✅ | ❌ | End | | `"r+"` | Yes | ✅ | ✅ | ❌ | Beginning | | `"w+"` | No | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | Beginning | | `"a+"` | No | ✅ | ✅ | ❌ | End | | `"rb"` | Yes | ✅ (binary) | ❌ | ❌ | Beginning | | `"wb"` | No | ❌ | ✅ (binary) | ✅ | Beginning | | `"ab"` | No | ❌ | ✅ (binary) | ❌ | End | | `"rb+"` / `"r+b"` | Yes | ✅ (binary) | ✅ | ❌ | Beginning | | `"wb+"` / `"w+b"` | No | ✅ (binary) | ✅ | ✅ | Beginning | | `"ab+"` / `"a+b"` | No | ✅ (binary) | ✅ | ❌ | End | > [!WARNING] > **Common KTU Mistake:** `"r+"` does **not** create the file. If the file does not exist, `fopen()` returns `NULL`. Only `"w"`, `"w+"`, `"a"`, `"a+"` create files automatically. ### 2.4 Complete File Operation Sequence — The "5-Phase Lifecycle" Every C file operation follows this exact chronological sequence: 1. **Declare** a `FILE *` pointer. 2. **Open** the file using `fopen()` — always check the return value for `NULL`. 3. **Process** the file (read / write / update) using I/O functions. 4. **Check** for errors using `ferror()` or end-of-file using `feof()`. 5. **Close** the file using `fclose()` — this flushes the buffer to disk. ### 2.5 The KTU High-Yield Function Reference Sheet | Function | Prototype | Purpose | Returns | |---|---|---|---| | `fopen()` | `FILE *fopen(const char *, const char *)` | Opens a file | `FILE *` or `NULL` | | `fclose()` | `int fclose(FILE *)` | Closes and flushes | `0` on success, `EOF` on failure | | `fprintf()` | `int fprintf(FILE *, const char *, ...)` | Formatted write | Number of chars written | | `fscanf()` | `int fscanf(FILE *, const char *, ...)` | Formatted read | Number of items matched | | `fputc()` | `int fputc(int, FILE *)` | Write one character | Character written or `EOF` | | `fgetc()` | `int fgetc(FILE *)` | Read one character | Character read or `EOF` | | `fputs()` | `int fputs(const char *, FILE *)` | Write a string | Non-negative or `EOF` | | `fgets()` | `char *fgets(char *, int, FILE *)` | Read a string line | Pointer or `NULL` | | `fread()` | `size_t fread(void *, size_t, size_t, FILE *)` | Read binary block | Number of items read | | `fwrite()` | `size_t fwrite(const void *, size_t, size_t, FILE *)` | Write binary block | Number of items written | | `fseek()` | `int fseek(FILE *, long, int)` | Move file pointer | Non-zero on error | | `ftell()` | `long ftell(FILE *)` | Current position | Position or `-1L` | | `rewind()` | `void rewind(FILE *)` | Move to beginning | None | | `feof()` | `int feof(FILE *)` | Test EOF flag | Non-zero if EOF | | `ferror()` | `int ferror(FILE *)` | Test error flag | Non-zero if error | | `fflush()` | `int fflush(FILE *)` | Flush buffer to disk | `0` on success | ### 2.6 The Random-Access Formula Sheet (Critical for KTU) For random access, you must compute the byte offset of the record you want: $$ \text{offset} = (\text{record\_index}) \times (\text{sizeof(struct\_record})) $$ For `fseek()`, the **whence** parameter (third argument) can be one of three constants: | Constant | Numeric Value | Meaning | |---|---|---| | `SEEK_SET` | `0` | Offset from beginning of file | | `SEEK_CUR` | `1` | Offset from current position | | `SEEK_END` | `2` | Offset from end of file | ### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility > [!NOTE] > **Where File Processing is Used in Industry** > - **Embedded systems:** Logging sensor data to SD cards for later analysis. > - **Databases:** Storing rows as binary records for O(1) access by index. > - **Compilers:** Reading source `.c` files and writing object `.o` files. > - **Operating Systems:** `/etc/passwd`, system logs (`syslog`), configuration files (`.ini`, `.conf`, `.json`). > - **Scientific Computing:** Persisting simulation results, CSV exports for Excel/MATLAB. > - **IoT Devices:** Storing firmware updates, telemetry data, calibration constants in EEPROM/Flash. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Writing and Reading a Text File (Character I/O) **Problem:** Write a C program that creates a text file `data.txt`, writes the string `"KTU 2024"` into it character-by-character using `fputc()`, then reads it back character-by-character using `fgetc()` and prints each character to the screen. **Step 1 — Include headers and declare pointer:** ```c #include int main(void) { FILE *fp; char ch; int i; char str[] = "KTU 2024"; ``` We declare a `FILE *` named `fp` to act as our clerk. The string `str` holds 8 characters (the letters plus a space, plus the digits — total 8 visible characters, plus the implicit `'\0'` terminator and a `'\n'` we will add). **Step 2 — Open the file for writing:** ```c fp = fopen("data.txt", "w"); if (fp == NULL) { printf("Cannot open file for writing.\n"); return 1; } ``` The mode `"w"` will create the file if it does not exist, or **truncate** (overwrite) it if it does. The `NULL` check is mandatory — disk-full, permission-denied, or invalid-path errors will return `NULL`. **Step 3 — Write character by character using `fputc`:** ```c for (i = 0; str[i] != '\0'; i++) { fputc(str[i], fp); } fputc('\n', fp); /* append newline */ fclose(fp); ``` The loop iterates until the null terminator is reached. `fputc()` writes exactly one byte per call. The buffer accumulates bytes internally; the actual disk write happens during `fclose()` (or when the buffer is full). **Step 4 — Reopen for reading:** ```c fp = fopen("data.txt", "r"); if (fp == NULL) { printf("Cannot open file for reading.\n"); return 1; } ``` Mode `"r"` opens for input only. The file **must** exist. **Step 5 — Read character by character until EOF:** ```c while ((ch = fgetc(fp)) != EOF) { putchar(ch); } fclose(fp); return 0; } ``` **Critical Point:** `fgetc()` returns an `int`, not a `char`. This is because `EOF` is conventionally defined as **-1**, and a `char` type cannot reliably hold -1 on all systems. Casting to `int` prevents this bug. The loop terminates naturally at the end of file. **Complete Program:** ```c #include int main(void) { FILE *fp; char ch; int i; char str[] = "KTU 2024"; /* --- WRITE PHASE --- */ fp = fopen("data.txt", "w"); if (fp == NULL) { printf("Write open failed.\n"); return 1; } for (i = 0; str[i] != '\0'; i++) fputc(str[i], fp); fputc('\n', fp); fclose(fp); /* --- READ PHASE --- */ fp = fopen("data.txt", "r"); if (fp == NULL) { printf("Read open failed.\n"); return 1; } while ((ch = fgetc(fp)) != EOF) putchar(ch); fclose(fp); return 0; } ``` ### 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Random Access Using `fseek` and `ftell` **Problem:** A binary file `student.dat` contains 5 records of the following structure. Read the 3rd record directly without reading the first two. ```c struct Student { int roll; char name[30]; float marks; }; ``` Assume `sizeof(struct Student)` is **40 bytes** on the target system (typical alignment). **Step 1 — Compute the byte offset:** $$ \text{offset} = (\text{record\_index}) \times (\text{sizeof(struct Student)}) = 2 \times 40 = 80 \text{ bytes} $$ We want record at index `2` (0-based, so the 3rd record) starting from the **beginning** of the file. **Step 2 — Apply `fseek`:** ```c fseek(fp, 2L * (long)sizeof(struct Student), SEEK_SET); ``` The third argument `SEEK_SET` means "from the beginning of the file". The `L` suffix ensures the offset is treated as a `long int`. **Step 3 — Read the record:** ```c struct Student s; fread(&s, sizeof(struct Student), 1, fp); printf("Roll: %d Name: %s Marks: %.2f\n", s.roll, s.name, s.marks); ``` **Step 4 — Get current position (analytical verification):** ```c long pos = ftell(fp); printf("Current byte position: %ld\n", pos); ``` After reading one 40-byte record, `ftell()` will return `80 + 40 = 120`. ### 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Command-Line Argument File Copy **Problem:** Write a program that takes source and destination filenames as command-line arguments and copies the file byte by byte. **Step 1 — Parse `argc` and `argv`:** ```c #include int main(int argc, char *argv[]) { FILE *src, *dst; int ch; if (argc != 3) { printf("Usage: %s \n", argv[0]); return 1; } ``` `argc` is the argument count; `argv[0]` is the program name, `argv[1]` is the source, `argv[2]` is the destination. **Step 2 — Open both files:** ```c src = fopen(argv[1], "rb"); if (src == NULL) { perror(argv[1]); return 1; } dst = fopen(argv[2], "wb"); if (dst == NULL) { perror(argv[2]); fclose(src); return 1; } ``` We use **binary** mode (`"rb"`, `"wb"`) so that the copy works correctly for any file type — text, image, executable, etc. `perror()` prints a human-readable error message based on the system `errno` value. **Step 3 — Perform the copy loop:** ```c while ((ch = fgetc(src)) != EOF) fputc(ch, dst); ``` **Step 4 — Close and report:** ```c fclose(src); fclose(dst); printf("File copied successfully.\n"); return 0; } ``` ### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Function Selection Guide | I/O Scenario | Best Function | Reason | |---|---|---| | Write a formatted report | `fprintf()` | Supports `%d`, `%.2f`, etc. | | Read structured CSV data | `fscanf()` | Parses formatted tokens | | Read one character at a time | `fgetc()` | Lowest overhead, byte-level | | Write one character at a time | `fputc()` | Direct byte output | | Read a full line of text | `fgets()` | Stops at `\n` or buffer full | | Write a string | `fputs()` | Faster than `fprintf` for raw strings | | Read binary structure (record) | `fread()` | Preserves bit pattern, block transfer | | Write binary structure (record) | `fwrite()` | High-throughput, exact representation | | Jump to specific record | `fseek()` | Random access in binary files | | Reset to start of file | `rewind()` | Equivalent to `fseek(fp, 0L, SEEK_SET)` | | Detect end of file | `feof()` or check `EOF` | Indicates stream exhaustion | | Detect I/O error | `ferror()` | Indicates stream failure | ### 3.5 Worked Example 4 — Append Mode for Log File ```c #include #include int main(void) { FILE *log = fopen("app.log", "a"); if (log == NULL) { return 1; } fprintf(log, "Application started at %ld\n", (long)time(NULL)); fputs("User logged in: admin\n", log); fclose(log); return 0; } ``` Mode `"a"` opens at the **end** of the file — existing content is preserved, new data is appended. If the file does not exist, it is created. > [!NOTE] > **Critical Difference Between `"w"` and `"a"`:** > - `"w"` — file pointer starts at byte 0; previous content is **destroyed**. > - `"a"` — file pointer starts at the byte after the last existing byte; previous content is **preserved**. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 The File I/O Lifecycle — Master Flowchart ```mermaid flowchart TD A([Program Start]) --> B[Declare FILE pointer fp] B --> C[Call fopen with mode] C --> D{File opened
successfully?} D -- No --> E[Print error message
via perror] E --> Z([Exit with failure code]) D -- Yes --> F[Process file:
read or write] F --> G{File operation
successful?} G -- No --> H[Check feof or ferror] H --> I[Handle error or
EOF condition] I --> K G -- Yes --> J{More data
to process?} J -- Yes --> F J -- No --> K[Call fclose to
flush buffer] K --> L[Return 0] L --> M([Program End]) ``` ### 4.2 File Pointer Position Model — Random Access Visualisation ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph DISK[Binary File on Disk] direction LR R0([Record 0
bytes 0-39]) R1([Record 1
bytes 40-79]) R2([Record 2
bytes 80-119]) R3([Record 3
bytes 120-159]) R4([Record 4
bytes 160-199]) R0 --- R1 --- R2 --- R3 --- R4 end FP[File Pointer] -. fseek offset 80 .-> R2 ``` This diagram shows how `fseek(fp, 80L, SEEK_SET)` repositions the logical pointer to skip directly to the third record, avoiding a sequential read of the first two. ### 4.3 Buffered I/O Architecture — Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart TD subgraph PROGRAM[User Program in RAM] direction TB SRC[Source variable
e.g. struct Student s] end subgraph LIBRARY[stdio.h Library Layer] direction TB BUF[(Internal Buffer
size = 4096 or 8192 bytes)] FPTR[File Position Pointer] end subgraph OS[Operating System Kernel] direction TB FD[File Descriptor Table] end subgraph DISK[Physical Storage Device] direction TB SECT[Disk Sectors and Platters] end SRC -->|fwrite call| BUF BUF -->|fflush or fclose| FD FPTR -.tracks position.-> BUF FD -->|system call write| SECT SECT -.system call read.-> FD FD -->|fread returns| BUF BUF -->|data arrives| SRC ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why Buffering Matters in KTU Exams:** > Without an explicit `fflush()` or `fclose()`, the data you write with `fprintf()` or `fwrite()` may **still be in the RAM buffer** and not yet on the disk. If the program crashes or power fails, the data is lost. This is why `fclose()` is **not optional** in production code. ### 4.4 Text vs Binary File Storage — ASCII Representation ```mermaid flowchart TD subgraph TEXT[Text File data.txt] direction LR T1[Byte 0: 75 K] T2[Byte 1: 84 T] T3[Byte 2: 85 U] T4[Byte 3: 32 space] T5[Byte 4: 50 2] T6[Byte 5: 48 0] T7[Byte 6: 50 2] T8[Byte 7: 52 4] T9[Byte 8: 10 newline] end subgraph BIN[Binary File marks.dat storing int 12345] direction LR B1[Byte 0: 57] B2[Byte 1: 48] B3[Byte 2: 0] B4[Byte 3: 0] end ``` **Interpretation:** - **Text** stores `12345` as the **5 characters** `'1'`,`'2'`,`'3'`,`'4'`,`'5'` (5 bytes). - **Binary** (little-endian) stores `12345` as a 4-byte `int` in machine-native form. This is exact but machine-dependent. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** *Explain the concept of a stream in C. Differentiate between text mode and binary mode file operations.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** A **stream** in C is an abstract logical interface that represents a flow of bytes between the program and a file or device. All file I/O in C is performed through streams, which abstract away the physical device details. **Text Mode:** In text mode, the C runtime performs character translations. Specifically, on Windows, the carriage-return + linefeed sequence (`\r\n`, **2 bytes**) is converted to a single newline character (`\n`, **1 byte**) on read, and vice versa on write. Files are treated as a sequence of lines. **Binary Mode:** In binary mode, **no translation** is performed. The file is treated as a raw sequence of bytes identical to the in-memory representation. Binary mode is essential for storing structured data, images, audio, and any non-text data. The mode is selected by appending `b` to the mode string in `fopen()`, e.g., `"rb"`, `"wb"`, `"ab+"`. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]** *What is the significance of the `FILE` pointer in C file handling? What happens if `fopen()` fails?* **Model Answer (3 marks):** The **`FILE` pointer** is a pointer to an opaque structure defined in `stdio.h` that acts as the **handle** (or "clerk") for all file operations. It encapsulates the file descriptor, internal buffer, current read/write position, and error/EOF flags. Every file I/O function (`fprintf`, `fscanf`, `fread`, `fwrite`, `fclose`, etc.) requires this pointer as an argument. If `fopen()` fails — due to reasons such as the file not existing (in `"r"` mode), insufficient permissions, a full disk, or an invalid path — it returns **`NULL`**. Dereferencing a `NULL` pointer causes **undefined behaviour**, typically a segmentation fault. Therefore, **it is mandatory to check the return value of `fopen()`** before performing any further I/O on the file. ### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice) --- **Question A (14 marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2024]** **(a)** Write a C program to create a text file named `marks.txt` and write the marks of 5 students into it, one per line, using `fprintf()`. Then write a function to read the file and display the contents on the screen. Include all necessary error checks. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Explain the functions `fseek()`, `ftell()`, and `rewind()` with a suitable example demonstrating random access to a binary file containing a structure. **(7 marks)** --- ### Model Solution to Question A (Part a) — 7 Marks ```c #include int main(void) { FILE *fp; int i, n = 5; int marks; /* --- WRITE PHASE --- */ fp = fopen("marks.txt", "w"); if (fp == NULL) { printf("Error: Cannot create marks.txt\n"); return 1; } printf("Enter marks of %d students:\n", n); for (i = 0; i < n; i++) { printf("Student %d: ", i + 1); scanf("%d", &marks); fprintf(fp, "Student %d: %d\n", i + 1, marks); } fclose(fp); printf("Data written to marks.txt successfully.\n\n"); /* --- READ PHASE --- */ fp = fopen("marks.txt", "r"); if (fp == NULL) { printf("Error: Cannot open marks.txt for reading\n"); return 1; } printf("--- Contents of marks.txt ---\n"); char line[100]; while (fgets(line, sizeof(line), fp) != NULL) { printf("%s", line); } fclose(fp); return 0; } ``` **Explanation of Key Steps (for marks):** 1. **File Opening & Error Check (1 mark):** `fopen()` in `"w"` mode; `NULL` check using `if`. 2. **Writing with `fprintf` (2 marks):** Formatted output with newline for each student. 3. **File Closing (1 mark):** `fclose()` flushes the buffer. 4. **Reading with `fgets` (2 marks):** Reads line by line until `NULL` (EOF). 5. **Loop termination and display (1 mark):** Properly prints the file contents. --- ### Model Solution to Question A (Part b) — 7 Marks ```c #include struct Employee { int id; char name[40]; float salary; }; int main(void) { FILE *fp; struct Employee e; long pos; /* Create a binary file with 3 records */ fp = fopen("emp.dat", "wb"); if (fp == NULL) { return 1; } struct Employee temp[3] = { {101, "Arun", 35000.0f}, {102, "Beena", 42000.0f}, {103, "Cyril", 50000.0f} }; fwrite(temp, sizeof(struct Employee), 3, fp); fclose(fp); /* Random access: read the 2nd record (index 1) */ fp = fopen("emp.dat", "rb"); if (fp == NULL) { return 1; } fseek(fp, 1L * (long)sizeof(struct Employee), SEEK_SET); fread(&e, sizeof(struct Employee), 1, fp); printf("ID: %d Name: %s Salary: %.2f\n", e.id, e.name, e.salary); pos = ftell(fp); printf("File pointer is now at byte: %ld\n", pos); rewind(fp); fread(&e, sizeof(struct Employee), 1, fp); printf("After rewind, first record: %d %s %.2f\n", e.id, e.name, e.salary); fclose(fp); return 0; } ``` **Explanation (for marks):** 1. **Definition of `fseek` (2 marks):** Repositions the file pointer using `(stream, offset, whence)` where `whence ∈ {SEEK_SET, SEEK_CUR, SEEK_END}`. 2. **Definition of `ftell` (2 marks):** Returns the current byte offset of the file pointer from the beginning, or `-1L` on error. 3. **Definition of `rewind` (1 mark):** Resets the file pointer to byte 0; equivalent to `fseek(fp, 0L, SEEK_SET)` and also clears error/EOF flags. 4. **Random access example (2 marks):** Compute offset = `1 × sizeof(struct)`, call `fseek`, then `fread` the record directly. --- **Question B (14 marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam — May 2024]** **(a)** Explain the different file opening modes in C with a tabular representation. Write a program to append data to an existing file and verify its contents. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Differentiate between `fprintf()`/`fscanf()` and `fwrite()`/`fread()`. Give one example program that stores a structure array to a binary file and retrieves a specific element using `fseek()`. **(7 marks)** --- ### Model Solution Outline for Question B **Part (a):** Produce the mode table from Section 2.3, then demonstrate `"a"` mode with `fputs()` and verify by reading back. **Key points (1 mark each):** mode table (3 marks), append program (3 marks), verification output (1 mark). **Part (b):** Key differences — | Aspect | `fprintf` / `fscanf` | `fwrite` / `fread` | |---|---|---| | Type | Formatted, text-oriented | Raw, binary-oriented | | Conversion | Performs `int`↔`string`, `float`↔`string` | No conversion — bit-for-bit copy | | File mode | Use `"r"`, `"w"`, `"a"` | Use `"rb"`, `"wb"`, `"ab"` | | Portability | Text is portable across systems | Binary may differ across endianness | | Speed | Slower (conversion overhead) | Faster (no conversion) | | Use case | Reports, logs, CSV | Records, images, audio, executable data | (3 marks for the table, 4 marks for the program using `fseek`, `fread`, `fwrite` on a structure.) --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Loss Zones in File Processing Questions:** > 1. **Skipping the `NULL` check after `fopen()`** — Examiners deduct 1 to 2 marks explicitly. Always write: `if (fp == NULL) { ... return 1; }` > 2. **Forgetting `fclose()`** — Loses 1 mark and may cause undefined behaviour in practical exams. > 3. **Using `fgetc()` return in a `char` variable** — Causes infinite loops on systems where `char` is `unsigned`. Always use `int`. > 4. **Confusing `"r+"` with `"w+"`** — `"r+"` requires the file to exist; `"w+"` destroys existing content. A 14-mark answer can lose 3 marks for this single confusion. > 5. **Using `fprintf` for binary data** — Will corrupt the file. Binary I/O requires `fread`/`fwrite` AND the `"b"` mode flag. > 6. **Wrong `fseek` whence parameter** — Mixing `SEEK_CUR` with absolute offsets is a frequent error. Compute carefully: from start → `SEEK_SET`, from current → `SEEK_CUR`, from end → `SEEK_END`. > 7. **Not including `stdio.h`** — Compilation failure. Marks deducted even if logic is correct. ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Processing Files** > - A **file** provides **persistent storage** beyond program execution lifetime. > - C treats every file as a **stream of bytes** ending with **EOF**. > - The `FILE *` pointer is the **only handle** the programmer uses; never access internals. > - The three auto-opened streams are **`stdin`**, **`stdout`**, **`stderr`**. > - File lifecycle: **Declare → Open → Process → Check → Close**. > - **`"w"` truncates**, **`"a"` appends**, **`"r"` requires existing file**, **`"r+"` allows both read/write to existing file**. > - Use `"b"` in the mode for **binary** operations: `"rb"`, `"wb"`, `"ab"`, etc. > - **`fgetc()` and `fputc()`** — character-level I/O. > - **`fgets()` and `fputs()`** — string/line-level I/O (remember `fgets` keeps the newline). > - **`fprintf()` and `fscanf()`** — formatted text I/O. > - **`fread()` and `fwrite()`** — raw binary block I/O. > - **`fseek(fp, offset, whence)`** — random access; whence is `SEEK_SET` (0), `SEEK_CUR` (1), or `SEEK_END` (2). > - **`ftell(fp)`** — returns the current byte offset, or `-1L` on error. > - **`rewind(fp)`** — resets to the start AND clears error/EOF flags. > - **`feof()`** — returns non-zero only AFTER a read attempt has hit EOF; never use to control a read loop from the start. > - **`ferror()`** — returns non-zero if any I/O error has occurred on the stream. > - **Always check the return value of `fopen()`** for `NULL` before any further operation. > - **Always call `fclose()`** to flush the buffer and release the OS file descriptor. > - Random access formula: `offset = record_index × sizeof(structure)`. > - **Buffered I/O** means writes are held in RAM until `fflush()`, `fclose()`, or buffer-full — data may be lost on a crash if not closed. > - For copying **any file type** (including binary), use mode `"rb"` and `"wb"`.

topics.title: Library Functions Related to File - fseek, ftell, fread, fwrite
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GXEST204 - PROGRAMMING IN C
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Library Functions Related to File - fseek, ftell, fread, fwrite

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 4: Library Functions Related to File Operations

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

In the **C programming language**, file handling is not performed using a single monolithic function. Instead, the **`<stdio.h>`** standard library provides a collection of specialized functions that operate on a `FILE *` stream. Among them, **fseek**, **ftell**, **fread**, and **fwrite** are the four cornerstone functions that govern **random access file I/O** and **binary block data transfer**.

### 1.1 Formal Definitions (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **fseek()** — A standard library function used to **reposition the file position indicator** of an open stream to a specific location. It enables *random access* to a file rather than sequential access.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **ftell()** — A standard library function that **returns the current value of the file position indicator** for a given stream, measured in bytes from the beginning of the file.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **fread()** — A standard library function used to **read a block of binary data** from a stream into a memory buffer. It performs unformatted, raw-byte transfer.

> [! [!IMPORTANT]fwrite()** — A standard library function used to **write a block of binary data** from a memory buffer to a stream. It is the counterpart of `fread()` and supports raw-byte, unformatted output.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Plain English Intuition)

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: The Cassette Tape Recorder**
> 
> Imagine a cassette tape with songs recorded on it.
> - **fseek()** is like pressing the **rewind or fast-forward button** — you move the playback head to a specific position on the tape.
> - **ftell()** is like looking at the **counter window** on the recorder, which tells you the exact position of the playback head at any moment.
> - **fread()** is like **recording a song from the tape onto a blank tape** — you copy a chunk of audio from one place to another in raw form.
> - **fwrite()** is like **burning a song onto the tape** — you take data from your hand and write it onto the tape in raw form.
> 
> Together, these four functions let you **jump anywhere** in the file (random access) and **copy data in bulk** (block transfer) without worrying about text formatting or delimiters.

### 1.3 Why These Functions Exist

- `fprintf`/`fscanf` work on **text** — they parse delimiters like commas, spaces, and newlines.
- `fread`/`fwrite` work on **raw binary bytes** — they are **10–100× faster** for structured data (structures, arrays, images).
- `fseek`/`ftell` allow you to **skip directly to byte N** instead of reading every byte before it.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Tip:** Examiners love asking the *difference between formatted I/O (fprintf/fscanf) and block I/O (fread/fwrite)*. Memorize this distinction cold.

---

### 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Metric | Value | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| `SEEK_SET` | **0** | Beginning of file |
| `SEEK_CUR` | **1** | Current position of file pointer |
| `SEEK_END` | **2** | End of file |
| Return type of `ftell` | **`long int`** | Portable across 32/64-bit systems |
| `fseek` return on success | **0** | Non-zero indicates failure |

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Function Signatures (from `<stdio.h>`)

```c
int fseek(FILE *stream, long int offset, int whence);

long int ftell(FILE *stream);

size_t fread(void *ptr, size_t size, size_t count, FILE *stream);

size_t fwrite(const void *ptr, size_t size, size_t count, FILE *stream);
```

### 2.2 Parameter-by-Parameter Breakdown

#### 2.2.1 `fseek(stream, offset, whence)`

| Parameter | Type | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| `stream` | `FILE *` | Pointer to the opened file stream (obtained from `fopen`) |
| `offset` | `long int` | Number of bytes to move (can be negative for backward seek) |
| `whence` | `int` | Anchor point: `SEEK_SET`, `SEEK_CUR`, or `SEEK_END` |

**Operational Logic:**
1. The function locates the anchor point specified by `whence`.
2. It then moves the file position indicator by `offset` bytes from that anchor.
3. The function clears the *end-of-file indicator* and *undoes* any prior `ungetc` effect.

#### 2.2.2 `ftell(stream)`

- Returns the byte offset from the **start of the file** to the current position.
- Useful for: storing the current position, computing file size, or for `rewind()` emulation.

**Computing File Size Using ftell:**
```c
fseek(fp, 0L, SEEK_END);   // jump to end
long size = ftell(fp);     // size in bytes
rewind(fp);                // back to start
```

#### 2.2.3 `fread(ptr, size, count, stream)`

| Parameter | Meaning |
|---|---|
| `ptr` | Pointer to memory block that will **receive** the data |
| `size` | Size (in bytes) of **one** element to be read |
| `count` | Number of such elements to read |
| `stream` | Source file pointer |

- **Total bytes transferred** = `size × count`
- Returns the **number of full elements actually read** (not bytes!).

#### 2.2.4 `fwrite(ptr, size, count, stream)`

- **Dual** of `fread` — writes `count` elements of `size` bytes each from `ptr` to `stream`.
- Returns the number of full elements successfully written.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Many students mistakenly assume `fread` returns bytes. It returns the **count of items** successfully read. If you ask for 10 items of size 4 bytes and only 7 are read, `fread` returns **7**, not 28.

### 2.3 The Three Seek Modes — Visual Cheat Sheet

| Mode | Anchor Point | Common Use |
|---|---|---|
| `SEEK_SET` | Beginning of file (offset **0**) | Jump to a known absolute position |
| `SEEK_CUR` | Current file pointer location | Skip ahead or move back relative to current spot |
| `SEEK_END` | End of file (EOF) | Append mode, or get file size |

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula / Behavior Sheet

| Operation | Code Pattern | Effect |
|---|---|---|
| Seek to start | `fseek(fp, 0L, SEEK_SET);` | Equivalent to `rewind(fp)` |
| Seek to end | `fseek(fp, 0L, SEEK_END);` | Pointer at last byte + 1 |
| Skip 1 byte forward | `fseek(fp, 1L, SEEK_CUR);` | Equivalent to `fgetc(fp)` (ignoring value) |
| Get file size | `fseek(fp, 0L, SEEK_END); long s = ftell(fp);` | `s` = total bytes |
| Read 1 struct | `fread(&st, sizeof(st), 1, fp);` | Block read |
| Write array of 10 ints | `fwrite(arr, sizeof(int), 10, fp);` | 40 bytes written |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world engineering utility:** `fseek`/`ftell`/`fread`/`fwrite` form the backbone of **database index lookups**, **image processing** (BMP/PPM file readers), **audio file manipulation** (WAV headers), and **memory-mapped file emulation** in C.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations, Walkthroughs & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1: Computing the Size of a File

### Problem
Write a C program that opens a binary file `data.bin` and prints its size in bytes using `fseek` and `ftell`.

### Step-by-Step Code and Reasoning

```c
#include <stdio.h>

int main(void) {
    FILE *fp = fopen("data.bin", "rb");
    if (fp == NULL) {
        perror("Error opening file");
        return 1;
    }

    /* Step 1: Move the file position indicator to the END of file */
    if (fseek(fp, 0L, SEEK_END) != 0) {
        perror("fseek failed");
        fclose(fp);
        return 1;
    }

    /* Step 2: Read the current byte offset -> this is the file size */
    long size = ftell(fp);
    printf("Size of data.bin = %ld bytes\n", size);

    /* Step 3: Rewind pointer to beginning for further use */
    rewind(fp);

    fclose(fp);
    return 0;
}
```

**Execution Trace (assuming `data.bin` contains 1024 bytes):**

| Step | Function Call | Internal State |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | `fopen("data.bin", "rb")` | File position indicator = 0 (start) |
| 2 | `fseek(fp, 0L, SEEK_END)` | Indicator jumps to byte 1024 |
| 3 | `ftell(fp)` | Returns 1024 |
| 4 | `rewind(fp)` | Indicator back to 0 |

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2: Writing and Reading an Array of Structures

### Problem
Write a program to store an array of 3 student records into `students.dat` using `fwrite`, then read them back using `fread`, using `fseek` to navigate.

### Code

```c
#include <stdio.h>

typedef struct {
    int   roll;
    char  name[30];
    float marks;
} Student;

int main(void) {
    Student s_write[3] = {
        {101, "Anand",    89.5f},
        {102, "Beena",    76.0f},
        {103, "Catherine",91.25f}
    };

    Student s_read[3];

    FILE *fp = fopen("students.dat", "w+b");  // w+b = read/write binary
    if (!fp) { perror("fopen"); return 1; }

    /* === WRITE PHASE === */
    /* Write all 3 records in one call */
    size_t written = fwrite(s_write, sizeof(Student), 3, fp);
    printf("Records written: %zu\n", written);   /* Expected: 3 */

    /* === READ PHASE === */
    /* Seek to the 2nd record (index 1) directly */
    fseek(fp, 1L * (long)sizeof(Student), SEEK_SET);
    fread(&s_read[1], sizeof(Student), 1, fp);
    printf("Directly read: Roll=%d Name=%s Marks=%.2f\n",
           s_read[1].roll, s_read[1].name, s_read[1].marks);

    /* Rewind and read all 3 in a block */
    rewind(fp);
    size_t read_count = fread(s_read, sizeof(Student), 3, fp);
    printf("Block-read records: %zu\n", read_count);

    fclose(fp);
    return 0;
}
```

### Detailed Byte-Level Trace

**Step A — Writing:**
- `sizeof(Student)` = `4 + 30 + 4` (typical with padding, may be **40 bytes** on most compilers).
- `fwrite` writes `40 × 3 = 120 bytes` to disk.
- File pointer now at offset 120 (which equals `SEEK_END`).

**Step B — Random Access Read:**
- `fseek(fp, 40, SEEK_SET)` moves the indicator to byte 40 — the **start of record #2**.
- `fread(&s_read[1], 40, 1, fp)` reads exactly 40 bytes into `s_read[1]`.

**Why this works:**
$$
\text{offset\_of\_record}_i \;=\; i \times \text{sizeof(Student)}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This formula is the heart of **indexed file access** — a foundational concept in DBMS file organization. KTU examiners frequently test this on 14-mark questions.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3: Modifying a Single Record In-Place

### Analytical Derivation

Given a file containing N records of fixed size S, to update the k-th record (0-indexed):

1. **Seek:** $\text{position} = k \times S$
2. **Read** the record into a buffer.
3. **Modify** the buffer in memory.
4. **Seek back** to the same position: `fseek(fp, -(long)S, SEEK_CUR)` (or absolute seek).
5. **Write** the modified buffer back.

```c
/* Update 2nd record */
int k = 1;
fseek(fp, k * sizeof(Student), SEEK_SET);   /* jump to record k */
Student temp;
fread(&temp, sizeof(Student), 1, fp);        /* load it */
temp.marks += 5.0f;                          /* modify in RAM */
fseek(fp, -(long)sizeof(Student), SEEK_CUR); /* step back one record */
fwrite(&temp, sizeof(Student), 1, fp);       /* overwrite on disk */
```

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Tip:** The `fseek(fp, -S, SEEK_CUR)` trick is a board-favorite for showing "current-relative seek" — note that a successful read or write advances the pointer, so to overwrite you must move it back.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — When to Use What

| Scenario | Best Function(s) | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| Store a structure array | `fwrite` | Bulk binary write is fastest |
| Skip header (e.g., BMP header) | `fseek` | Jump past 54 bytes instantly |
| Know where you are after a crash | `ftell` | Logs last known offset |
| Read an entire WAV file | `fread` in a loop | Process large blocks efficiently |
| Append a log entry | `fseek(SEEK_END)` + `fwrite` | Append mode pattern |
| Implement a simple database | `fread`/`fwrite` + `fseek`/`ftell` | Random access by primary key |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 File Pointer Movement Map (Conceptual Architecture)

The diagram below shows the **logical layout** of a binary file containing fixed-size records and how the file position indicator moves under various `fseek` operations.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    classDef anchor fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#fff,color:#fff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef ptr   fill:#c00000,stroke:#fff,color:#fff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef rec   fill:#d9e1f2,stroke:#1f4e79,color:#000

    A["SEEK_SET\n(Offset 0)"]:::anchor
    B["Record 0\nbytes 0 to 39"]:::rec
    C["Record 1\nbytes 40 to 79"]:::rec
    D["Record 2\nbytes 80 to 119"]:::rec
    E["Record 3\nbytes 120 to 159"]:::rec
    F["SEEK_CUR\n(Current)"]:::ptr
    G["SEEK_END\n(Offset 160)"]:::anchor

    A --> B --> C --> D --> E --> G
    F -. points to .-> D
```

> [!NOTE]
> In the figure: **A** is the start anchor (`SEEK_SET`), **G** is the end anchor (`SEEK_END`), and **F** is the dynamic pointer that `SEEK_CUR` references. Each block `B`, `C`, `D`, `E` is one `Student` record of `sizeof(Student) = 40` bytes.

---

## 4.2 Functional Flow — Reading a Specific Record

The following flow maps the **complete execution sequence** for retrieving the k-th record from a binary file. This is a typical 14-mark KTU question.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    classDef step    fill:#2e75b6,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    classDef decide  fill:#ed7d31,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    classDef io      fill:#70ad47,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    classDef finish  fill:#a5a5a5,stroke:#fff,color:#fff

    S1["Start: Open file in 'rb' or 'r+b'"]:::step
    S2["Compute offset = k * sizeof(Record)"]:::step
    S3{"fseek fp to offset succeeded?"}:::decide
    S4["Call fread into buffer"]:::io
    S5{"fread returned 1?"}:::decide
    S6["Process the record from buffer"]:::step
    S7["Print error: invalid record"]:::finish
    S8["fclose the file and exit"]:::finish

    S1 --> S2 --> S3
    S3 -- "Yes" --> S4 --> S5
    S3 -- "No"  --> S7
    S5 -- "Yes" --> S6 --> S8
    S5 -- "No"  --> S7
```

---

## 4.3 Block-Level Architecture — How `fread`/`fwrite` Interact with the Stream Buffer

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    classDef user   fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    classDef stdio  fill:#7030a0,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    classDef os     fill:#c00000,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    classDef disk   fill:#385723,stroke:#fff,color:#fff

    U["User Program\n(User buffer in RAM)"]:::user
    S["stdio Buffer\n(File pointer)"]:::stdio
    O["OS / Kernel\n(read, write syscalls)"]:::os
    D["Physical Disk File"]:::disk

    U -- "fread request: size, count" --> S
    S -- "Read syscall when buffer empty" --> O
    O -- "DMA transfer" --> D
    D -- "Bytes return through kernel" --> O
    O -- "Bytes refill stdio buffer" --> S
    S -- "Bytes copied to user buffer" --> U
```

**Interpretation:**
- `fread`/`fwrite` do **not** make a syscall for every byte. They fill/drain the **stdio buffer** in bulk, and the OS handles the actual disk I/O.
- This is why they are **far faster** than calling `fgetc` in a loop.
- `fseek` and `ftell` only manipulate the **logical position indicator** — they do **not** trigger disk I/O.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

### Question A1 `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`
**Q:** What is the purpose of the `fseek()` function? List its three seek modes with their macro values.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
`fseek()` is used to reposition the file position indicator of an open stream to a specific location, enabling **random access** to a file. Its three seek modes are:
- `SEEK_SET` — value **0**, beginning of file.
- `SEEK_CUR` — value **1**, current position of the file pointer.
- `SEEK_END` — value **2**, end of file.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark Split:** 1 mark for purpose, 1 mark for the three modes, 1 mark for the macro values.

---

### Question A2 `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**Q:** Differentiate between `fread()` and `fwrite()` functions.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | `fread()` | `fwrite()` |
|---|---|---|
| Direction | Stream → Memory | Memory → Stream |
| First parameter | Destination buffer | Source buffer |
| Use | Block reading | Block writing |
| Returns | Number of items read | Number of items written |
| Common mode | `"rb"`, `"r+b"` | `"wb"`, `"w+b"` |

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark Split:** 1 mark each for the three distinguishing rows.

## 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

### Question B1 (Choice A) `[KTU University Exam — Jul 2024]`

**Q (a)** [7 marks] Write a C program to **create a binary file** `emp.dat` containing records of N employees with fields `id`, `name`, and `salary`. Use `fwrite()` to store the records.

**Q (b)** [7 marks] Write a C program that **reads the file** `emp.dat` and uses `fseek()` to display the record of the employee with a given `id`. Also display the **total file size** using `ftell()`.

---

#### Model Solution for (a)

```c
#include <stdio.h>
#include <string.h>

typedef struct {
    int   id;
    char  name[30];
    float salary;
} Employee;

int main(void) {
    int n;
    Employee e;

    FILE *fp = fopen("emp.dat", "w+b");
    if (!fp) { perror("fopen"); return 1; }

    printf("Enter number of employees: ");
    scanf("%d", &n);

    for (int i = 0; i < n; i++) {
        printf("\nEmployee %d\n", i + 1);
        printf("ID: ");     scanf("%d", &e.id);
        printf("Name: ");   scanf(" %[^\n]", e.name);
        printf("Salary: "); scanf("%f", &e.salary);

        fwrite(&e, sizeof(Employee), 1, fp);
    }

    fclose(fp);
    printf("\nFile 'emp.dat' created successfully.\n");
    return 0;
}
```

**Step-by-step explanation (valuing guide):**
- Struct definition: **1 mark**.
- File open in `"w+b"` mode: **1 mark**.
- Loop to take input: **2 marks**.
- Correct `fwrite(&e, sizeof(Employee), 1, fp)` call: **2 marks**.
- File close and neat I/O: **1 mark**.

---

#### Model Solution for (b)

```c
#include <stdio.h>
#include <string.h>

typedef struct {
    int   id;
    char  name[30];
    float salary;
} Employee;

int main(void) {
    int search_id, found = 0;
    Employee e;

    FILE *fp = fopen("emp.dat", "rb");
    if (!fp) { perror("fopen"); return 1; }

    /* Compute and print file size using fseek + ftell */
    fseek(fp, 0L, SEEK_END);
    long size = ftell(fp);
    printf("Total file size = %ld bytes\n", size);

    printf("\nEnter employee ID to search: ");
    scanf("%d", &search_id);

    rewind(fp);   /* rewind to start before scanning */
    while (fread(&e, sizeof(Employee), 1, fp) == 1) {
        if (e.id == search_id) {
            printf("\nRecord found:\n");
            printf("ID     : %d\n",  e.id);
            printf("Name   : %s\n",  e.name);
            printf("Salary : %.2f\n", e.salary);
            found = 1;
            break;
        }
    }

    if (!found)
        printf("\nEmployee with ID %d not found.\n", search_id);

    fclose(fp);
    return 0;
}
```

**Alternative approach using direct `fseek` to a known record index:**

```c
/* If we know the k-th record index (0-based) and file is fixed-record */
long k = 2;  /* example: 3rd employee */
fseek(fp, k * sizeof(Employee), SEEK_SET);
fread(&e, sizeof(Employee), 1, fp);
```

**Mark split for (b):**
- File open and size calculation logic: **2 marks**.
- `rewind`/seek to start before search: **1 mark**.
- `fread` loop with `id` comparison: **2 marks**.
- Proper display and not-found handling: **1 mark**.
- Bonus: showing `fseek` for direct random access: **1 mark**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Forgetting `rewind(fp)`** before reading — the file pointer may still be at `SEEK_END` after the size calculation, so the `fread` loop will read nothing. **−1 mark.**
> 2. **Opening in `"w"`** instead of `"rb"` for read — this truncates the file and erases data. **−2 marks.**
> 3. **Using `fscanf` for binary data** — KTU expects `fread` for this question. Mixing them loses marks.
> 4. **Confusing return value of `fread`** — must check `== 1` (or `== n` for block), not just `!= 0`.

---

### Question B1 (Choice B) `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

**Q (a)** [7 marks] Explain the functions `fseek()` and `ftell()` with appropriate syntax and examples. Show how to compute the size of a file using them.

**Q (b)** [7 marks] Write a C program that uses `fwrite()` to store an array of 5 integers into a file `nums.dat`, then uses `fread()` along with `fseek()` to read and display the **3rd integer only**.

---

#### Model Solution for (a)

**`fseek()` Syntax:**
```c
int fseek(FILE *stream, long int offset, int whence);
```
- `stream`: pointer to the open file.
- `offset`: bytes to move (signed long).
- `whence`: anchor — `SEEK_SET` (0), `SEEK_CUR` (1), `SEEK_END` (2).
- Returns **0** on success, non-zero on failure.

**`ftell()` Syntax:**
```c
long int ftell(FILE *stream);
```
- Returns the current value of the file position indicator in bytes from the beginning of the file.
- Returns `-1L` on error.

**Example — File Size:**
```c
fseek(fp, 0L, SEEK_END);   /* move to end */
long size = ftell(fp);     /* get offset = size */
rewind(fp);                /* back to start for further I/O */
```

**Mark split:**
- Syntax and parameter explanation: **2 marks**.
- Seek modes table: **2 marks**.
- Return value semantics: **1 mark**.
- File size code example: **2 marks**.

---

#### Model Solution for (b)

```c
#include <stdio.h>

int main(void) {
    int arr[5] = {10, 20, 30, 40, 50};
    int val;

    /* WRITE phase */
    FILE *fp = fopen("nums.dat", "w+b");
    if (!fp) { perror("fopen"); return 1; }

    fwrite(arr, sizeof(int), 5, fp);
    /* file now has 5 ints = 20 bytes (assuming 4-byte int) */

    /* READ phase: jump to 3rd integer (index 2) */
    fseek(fp, 2L * (long)sizeof(int), SEEK_SET);
    fread(&val, sizeof(int), 1, fp);

    printf("The 3rd integer in the file is: %d\n", val);
    /* Expected output: 30 */

    fclose(fp);
    return 0;
}
```

**Derivation of the seek offset:**
$$
\text{offset} = \text{index} \times \text{sizeof(int)} = 2 \times 4 = 8 \text{ bytes}
$$

**Mark split:**
- Correct file open mode `"w+b"`: **1 mark**.
- `fwrite` to write 5 ints: **2 marks**.
- Correct `fseek` offset calculation: **2 marks**.
- Correct `fread` call: **1 mark**.
- Output and clean-up: **1 mark**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout for (b):**
> 1. **Off-by-one in the index** — students often write `fseek(fp, 3 * sizeof(int), SEEK_SET)` thinking "3rd" means position 3, but array index 2 = 3rd element. **−1 mark.**
> 2. **Forgetting the cast** `(long)sizeof(int)` — KTU appreciates portability awareness; if `sizeof(int)` is cast to `long`, it works on 64-bit systems. **−0.5 mark** (minor).
> 3. **Using `"w"` mode** — this opens a *text* stream, not binary, on Windows systems (line-ending conversion corrupts the byte count). Use `"w+b"` for binary. **−2 marks.**

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist**
> 
> - **`fseek(fp, offset, whence)`** — moves the file pointer. `whence ∈ {SEEK_SET, SEEK_CUR, SEEK_END}` with values 0, 1, 2.
> - **`ftell(fp)`** — returns the **current byte offset** from the start of the file. Returns `long int`.
> - **`fread(ptr, size, count, fp)`** — reads `count` items of `size` bytes each into `ptr`. Returns **count of items read**, not bytes.
> - **`fwrite(ptr, size, count, fp)`** — writes `count` items from `ptr` to `fp`. Returns **count of items written**.
> - **File size trick:** `fseek(fp, 0L, SEEK_END); long s = ftell(fp); rewind(fp);` — three lines, exam-favorite.
> - **Direct access to k-th record of fixed-size records:** $\text{offset} = k \times \text{sizeof(struct)}$.
> - **Always rewind or re-seek to start** after using `SEEK_END` to compute size, or subsequent reads will fail.
> - **Use binary modes** (`"rb"`, `"wb"`, `"r+b"`, `"w+b"`) when working with `fread`/`fwrite` for predictable byte counts.
> - **Cross-platform note:** Always cast `sizeof(...)` to `long` when passing as the offset to `fseek` to avoid integer-overflow warnings.
> - **`fseek` + `ftell` is the standard pre-C23 way** to determine file size. The C23 standard introduced `ftello`/`fseeko` for large files (`off_t`).
> - **Pitfall:** Confusing return value of `fread` with bytes — examiners use this to deduct 1–2 marks.
> - **Pitfall:** Using text mode `"w"` with `fwrite` corrupts binary data on Windows — always prefer `"w+b"`.
> - **Bonus fact:** The `SEEK_END` mode with a positive offset is **undefined behavior** in strict C — only `offset == 0` is guaranteed portable.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
